---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Q: List any four industrial applications of forging process.**

**Model Answer (4 × 1 mark each = 4 points, expected ~3 marks):**

1. **Automotive crankshafts** — produced by closed-die forging for grain continuity and fatigue strength.
2. **Aerospace turbine discs** — hot isothermal forging of superalloys for high-temperature strength.
3. **Connecting rods** — closed-die forging with flash for consistent metallurgical quality.
4. **Defence projectile shells** — hot forging for high-density, defect-free geometry.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Q: Differentiate between hot rolling and cold rolling with one industrial application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Hot Rolling | Cold Rolling |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature | Above recrystallisation (>0.6 $T_m$) | Room temperature |
| Surface finish | Scaly, rough | Bright, smooth |
| Dimensional accuracy | Low | High (close tolerance) |
| Mechanical strength | Lower (annealed) | Higher (strain hardened) |
| **Industrial Application** | **Rails, structural I-beams, ship plates** | **Automobile body sheets, transformer steel, food-grade tin plate** |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> The following are **TWO alternative 14-mark questions** following the **KTU ESE Module Choice pattern**. Choose ONE.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the various applications of metal forming processes in the **automotive industry** with suitable examples. Discuss why **forging** and **deep drawing** dominate this sector.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, describe the **hot rolling process** for manufacturing **structural I-beams and rails**. Mention the typical temperature, capacity, and product applications in the construction industry.

### Model Answer — (a)

The **automotive industry** is the **largest consumer of metal forming** in terms of tonnage and part count. A single passenger car contains approximately **75% by weight** of formed components.

**A. Forging Applications in Automotive:**
- **Crankshafts and camshafts** — closed-die forging ensures **fibre flow continuity** along the principal stress axis, enhancing fatigue life by 30–40% compared to machined-from-bar stock.
- **Connecting rods** — forged in AISI 4140/4340 steels to withstand peak combustion pressures of **80–120 bar** in a petrol engine.
- **Pistons, gears, and transmission shafts** — hot forging for wear resistance and toughness.

**B. Deep Drawing Applications:**
- **Body panels** (doors, bonnet, boot lid) — produced from **cold-rolled steel (CRS)** or **aluminium alloys (AA 5182)** in **1000–2000 tonne presses**.
- **Fuel tanks, oil pans, silencer shells** — single-draw or two-draw operations.
- **Wheel rims** — multi-stage deep drawing with **intermediate annealing**.

**C. Why Forging & Deep Drawing Dominate:**
- Forging gives **superior strength-to-weight ratio** vital for safety-critical parts.
- Deep drawing delivers **high production rates** (> 15 strokes/min) needed for mass production.
- Both processes are **near-net-shape**, reducing expensive CNC machining.

### Model Answer — (b)

**Hot Rolling of I-Beams and Rails:**

**Process Steps:**
1. **Heating:** Steel billets (cross-section ~200×200 mm, length 6–12 m) are heated in a **walking-beam reheat furnace** to **1150–1250 °C**.
2. **Descaling:** High-pressure water jets remove mill scale to prevent surface defects.
3. **Roughing Mill:** Initial passes in a **2-high reversing mill** reduce cross-section by ~50%.
4. **Intermediate / Universal Mill:** Has **horizontal and vertical rolls** that progressively form the I-flange and I-web profile.
5. **Finishing Stand:** Final dimensional accuracy of **±0.5 mm** on flange width.
6. **Cooling Bed:** Air cooling on a **cooling bed**; rails may be **controlled-cooled** to achieve fine pearlitic structure (head-hardened rails).
7. **Cutting, Straightening, Testing:** Saw cutting to length, roller straightening, ultrasonic testing.

**Key Industrial Specifications:**

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Mill capacity | 500,000 – 2,000,000 tonnes/year |
| Section sizes | I-beams: 80–600 mm depth, Rails: 30–75 kg/m |
| Steel grades | R260, R350HT (rail), IS 2062 (beams) |

**Construction Industry Applications:**
- **I-beams** for multi-storey building frames.
- **Rails** for railway track (60 kg/m for mainline, 52 kg/m for metro).
- **Channels and angles** for transmission towers, bridges.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the applications of **sheet metal forming** in the **aerospace industry**. Compare **stretch forming** and **hydroforming** for manufacturing large aircraft skin panels.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the applications of **extrusion** in the **construction, electrical, and transportation** industries. List at least two products for each industry with the material typically used.

### Model Answer — (a)

**Sheet Metal Forming in Aerospace:**

The **aerospace industry** demands components with:
- **High strength-to-weight ratio**
- **Fatigue resistance** (e.g., 100,000+ flight cycles)
- **Aerodynamic surface accuracy** (often **±0.1 mm** over multi-metre panels)
- **Minimal residual stress** to prevent stress-corrosion cracking

**Key Sheet Forming Applications:**

1. **Aluminium fuselage skins (AA 2024, AA 7075)** — produced by **stretch forming** to form doubly-curved panels.
2. **Wing skin panels** — often made by **hydroforming** using **Inconel, Titanium, or Al-Li alloys**.
3. **Engine cowlings, access panels, ducting** — deep drawing and spinning.
4. **Turbine blade covers and heat shields** — superplastic forming (SPF) of **Ti-6Al-4V** at 900 °C.

**Comparison: Stretch Forming vs. Hydroforming:**

| Aspect | Stretch Forming | Hydroforming |
|---|---|---|
| Principle | Sheet gripped at edges, stretched over die | Sheet formed by high-pressure fluid against a die |
| Forming force | Mechanical grippers | Hydraulic pressure (up to 350 MPa) |
| Surface accuracy | Good | Excellent (uniform pressure) |
| Material utilisation | Moderate | High (less thinning) |
| Part complexity | Single curvature dominant | Doubly-curved panels possible |
| Aerospace use | Wing leading edges, fairings | **Fuselage panels, large wing skins** |

### Model Answer — (b)

**Extrusion Applications Across Industries:**

**A. Construction Industry:**
1. **Aluminium window/door frames** — extruded from **AA 6063 T5** with hollow sections.
2. **Heat-sink profiles for curtain walls** — complex multi-hollow extrusions in AA 6060.
3. **PVC-coated steel sections for drywalls** — cold extrusion of galvanized steel.

**B. Electrical Industry:**
1. **Copper busbars** — hot extrusion of ETP copper for switchgear panels.
2. **Aluminium conductor cables (AA 1350)** — continuous casting + extrusion.
3. **Heat sinks for LED / electronic chips** — AA 6063 with thin fin geometries.

**C. Transportation Industry:**
1. **Aluminium alloy automotive crash rails (AA 7003)** — hollow extrusions for energy absorption.
2. **Bicycle frames (AA 6061 T6)** — light, strong, and corrosion-resistant.
3. **Rail coach structural sections** — large multi-void extrusions, up to **600 mm width**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Reasons for Mark Deduction in This Topic**
>
> 1. **Mere listing without justification** — Writing "forging is used in automotive" without explaining *why* (grain flow, fatigue life, strength) loses 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Wrong process–product pairing** — E.g., writing *"rails are made by forging"* (incorrect — they are **rolled**). Always be precise.
> 3. **Forgetting the material** — A 14-mark answer should mention the **typical alloy** (e.g., *AISI 1040 for crankshafts, AA 5182 for car panels*).
> 4. **Skipping the numerical dimension** — Stating *tensile strength* or *tonnage* scores extra. Use numbers from the formula sheet.
> 5. **Confusing bulk and sheet processes** — Forging is a *bulk* process; deep drawing is a *sheet* process. Examiners notice this error immediately.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day**

- [x] **Bulk forming** (rolling, forging, extrusion, drawing) produces **thick, structurally loaded** components.
- [x] **Sheet forming** (deep drawing, bending, stamping, hydroforming) produces **thin-walled, geometrically complex** components.
- [x] **Automotive = Forging + Deep Drawing + Stamping** (most dominant sector).
- [x] **Aerospace = Stretch forming, Hydroforming, Superplastic forming** (precision + strength).
- [x] **Construction = Hot rolling** of I-beams, channels, rails (largest by tonnage).
- [x] **Electrical = Cold rolling + stamping** for laminations and busbars.
- [x] **Always justify** with at least one of: *grain flow, surface finish, dimensional tolerance, tonnage, cost, fatigue life*.
- [x] **Memorise key alloys:** AISI 1040/4140 (forging), AA 5182/6061 (sheet), ETP copper (busbar), Ti-6Al-4V (aerospace).
- [x] **Memorise LDR formula** — examiners love it.
- [x] **Process–Industry–Product triad** = full marks in 14-mark questions.
- [x] **Hot rolling** → above recrystallisation, low accuracy, cheap. **Cold rolling** → room temp, high accuracy, strong.

---

> **End of Module 1 Topic Notes — Applications in Various Industries**
> *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme | Outcome-Based Education (OBE) Aligned*

topics.title: Stress and Strain in Metal Forming - Concepts of Stress and Strain in Deformable Bodies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET524 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Stress and Strain in Metal Forming - Concepts of Stress and Strain in Deformable Bodies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Stress in a Deformable Body?

In the context of **Metal Forming**, a deformable body is a solid (like a billet, slug, or sheet metal) that undergoes **permanent plastic deformation** when subjected to forces exceeding its yield point. **Stress** is the internal resistance offered by the material per unit area against an applied external force.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Standard):**  
> Stress ($\sigma$) is defined as the limiting value of internal force ($\Delta F$) acting on an elemental area ($\Delta A$) as the area approaches zero, evaluated at a point.  
> $$\sigma = \lim_{\Delta A \to 0} \frac{\Delta F}{\Delta A} \quad \left[ \text{Units: N/m}^2 \text{ or Pascal (Pa)} \right]$$

In metal forming, we are less interested in *whether the body breaks* and more interested in *how it flows plastically*. Stress governs the **flow of material** during forging, rolling, extrusion, and drawing.

#### Conceptual Analogy: The "Toffee Analogy"
Imagine biting into a firm toffee. Your teeth apply a force over the contact area. The toffee:
- **Resists** your bite (this is the **normal stress** $\sigma$).
- **Tends to slide** sideways as you bite deeper (this is the **shear stress** $\tau$).
- As you keep biting, it **flows plastically** and changes shape permanently (this is **strain** $\varepsilon$).

A metal in a forging press behaves identically — except at **mega-Pascal (MPa) or Giga-Pascal (GPa) scale**, and instead of melting in your mouth, it reshapes into a useful component.

### 1.2 What is Strain in a Deformable Body?

**Strain** is a dimensionless measure of the deformation (change in dimension) of a body relative to its original dimension. It quantifies *how much* the body has changed shape, while stress quantifies *what force* caused that change.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**  
> Strain ($\varepsilon$) is the ratio of change in length ($\Delta L$) to the original length ($L_0$).  
> $$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \quad \left[ \text{Dimensionless (mm/mm)} \right]$$

For metal forming, we differentiate between:
- **Engineering Strain (Nominal Strain):** Based on original dimensions.
- **True Strain ($\varepsilon_T$):** Based on *instantaneous* dimensions — critical for large deformations in forging/extrusion.

### 1.3 The Two Fundamental Types: Normal and Shear

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **At any point inside a deformable body, only TWO independent kinds of stress/strain can exist:**
> 1. **Normal Stress ($\sigma$) / Normal Strain ($\varepsilon$):** Acts *perpendicular* to the surface (push/pull).
> 2. **Shear Stress ($\tau$) / Shear Strain ($\gamma$):** Acts *parallel* to the surface (sliding/tearing).

| Type | Symbol | Acts On | Direction | Example in Forming |
|------|--------|---------|-----------|---------------------|
| Normal | $\sigma_n$ | Area | Perpendicular | Compressive force in forging |
| Shear | $\tau$ | Area | Tangential | Friction at die-workpiece interface |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The State of Stress at a Point — 3D Analysis

Consider an infinitesimal cubic element inside a deformable body. Stresses act on each of its **6 faces** (3 pairs of opposite faces). On each face, there is **1 normal stress** and **2 shear stresses**. This gives a total of **9 stress components**, which together form the **Stress Tensor**:

$$[\sigma] = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_x & \tau_{xy} & \tau_{xz} \\ \tau_{yx} & \sigma_y & \tau_{yz} \\ \tau_{zx} & \tau_{zy} & \sigma_z \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symmetry of the Stress Tensor:**  
> To prevent the cube from spinning (moment equilibrium $\sum M = 0$), shear stresses are **pairwise equal**:  
> $$\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx}, \quad \tau_{yz} = \tau_{zy}, \quad \tau_{xz} = \tau_{zx}$$  
> This reduces **9 independent components to 6**: 3 normal ($\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \sigma_z$) and 3 shear ($\tau_{xy}, \tau_{yz}, \tau_{xz}$).

### 2.2 Decomposition: Hydrostatic and Deviatoric Stress

A critical concept for metal forming. Any stress tensor can be split into:

$$[\sigma] = \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} \sigma_m & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \sigma_m & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \sigma_m \end{bmatrix}}_{\text{Hydrostatic (Volumetric)}} + \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} \sigma_x - \sigma_m & \tau_{xy} & \tau_{xz} \\ \tau_{xy} & \sigma_y - \sigma_m & \tau_{yz} \\ \tau_{xz} & \tau_{yz} & \sigma_z - \sigma_m \end{bmatrix}}_{\text{Deviatoric (Shape-Changing)}}$$

Where the **mean (hydrostatic) stress** is:
$$\sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z}{3}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in Forming:**  
> - **Hydrostatic stress** $\sigma_m$ causes **volume change** but does **not cause plastic flow** (yielding).  
> - **Deviatoric stress** is the **driving force for plastic deformation** (shape change without volume change — a phenomenon called *incompressibility* in plastic flow).

### 2.3 Principal Stresses

If we rotate the cube to an orientation where **all shear stresses vanish**, the three normal stresses acting on the three orthogonal faces are called **Principal Stresses**: $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \sigma_3$.

These are the **eigenvalues** of the stress tensor, found by solving:
$$\det([\sigma] - \lambda [I]) = 0$$

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Engineering Stress | $\sigma = F / A_0$ | MPa, uses **original** area $A_0$ |
| 2 | True Stress | $\sigma_T = F / A_i$ | MPa, uses **instantaneous** area $A_i$ |
| 3 | Engineering Strain | $\varepsilon = (L_f - L_0) / L_0$ | Dimensionless |
| 4 | True Strain | $\varepsilon_T = \ln(L_f / L_0) = \int dL / L$ | Dimensionless, used in **large deformations** |
| 5 | True Stress–Strain Relation (Volume const.) | $\sigma_T = \sigma (1 + \varepsilon)$ | During plastic flow, $A \cdot L = A_0 \cdot L_0$ |
| 6 | Mean (Hydrostatic) Stress | $\sigma_m = (\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 + \sigma_3)/3$ | MPa |
| 7 | Octahedral Shear Stress | $\tau_{oct} = \frac{1}{3}\sqrt{(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2}$ | Used in Von Mises criterion |
| 8 | Von Mises Yield Criterion | $\frac{1}{2}\left[(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2\right] = \sigma_Y^2$ | Most accurate for ductile metals |
| 9 | Tresca Yield Criterion | $\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = \sigma_Y$ | Conservative, easy to apply |
| 10 | Shear Strain | $\gamma = \Delta x / L_0$ | Dimensionless (radians) |
| 11 | Hooke's Law (3D Generalized) | $\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right]$ | $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio |
| 12 | Volumetric Strain (Elastic) | $\varepsilon_V = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z = \frac{1-2\nu}{E}(\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z)$ | Plastic: $\varepsilon_V \approx 0$ |
| 13 | Plane Stress | $\sigma_z = \tau_{xz} = \tau_{yz} = 0$ | Thin sheet forming, rolling |
| 14 | Plane Strain | $\varepsilon_z = \gamma_{xz} = \gamma_{yz} = 0$ | Forging, extrusion, drawing |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Forging / Rolling:** Use **plane strain** approximation — the workpiece is wide compared to its thickness.  
- **Sheet Metal Forming (Stamping, Deep Drawing):** Use **plane stress** — thickness is small.  
- **Extrusion / Drawing:** Use **axisymmetric** stress state — $\sigma_r = \sigma_\theta$.  
- **FEA Software (DEFORM, ABAQUS):** Internally computes the full 6-component stress/strain tensor at every node and checks against **Von Mises** or **Tresca** for yielding.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between True Stress and Engineering Stress

**Given:** A cylindrical tensile specimen with original area $A_0$ and length $L_0$. Under load $F$, it deforms plastically to instantaneous area $A_i$ and length $L_i$.

**Assumption (Plastic Incompressibility):** Volume is conserved during plastic deformation:
$$A_0 \cdot L_0 = A_i \cdot L_i$$

**Step 1 —** Express $A_i$ in terms of $A_0$ and engineering strain $\varepsilon$:
$$A_i = \frac{A_0 L_0}{L_i} = \frac{A_0 L_0}{L_0(1 + \varepsilon)} = \frac{A_0}{1 + \varepsilon}$$

> *Logic:* As the sample elongates, its cross-section must shrink to conserve volume — a key feature of plastic flow.

**Step 2 —** Write definitions of true and engineering stress:
$$\sigma_T = \frac{F}{A_i}, \quad \sigma = \frac{F}{A_0}$$

**Step 3 —** Divide the true stress by engineering stress:
$$\frac{\sigma_T}{\sigma} = \frac{F/A_i}{F/A_0} = \frac{A_0}{A_i}$$

**Step 4 —** Substitute $A_i$ from Step 1:
$$\frac{\sigma_T}{\sigma} = \frac{A_0}{A_0/(1 + \varepsilon)} = 1 + \varepsilon$$

**Step 5 —** Final relationship:
$$\boxed{\sigma_T = \sigma \cdot (1 + \varepsilon)}$$

> This relation holds **only until necking** begins. Beyond necking, true stress is determined by direct measurement of the reduced cross-section.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: True Strain from Differential Element

**Step 1 —** Consider an infinitesimal element of original length $L$. After a small increment of deformation, it becomes $L + dL$.

**Step 2 —** The differential (instantaneous) strain is:
$$d\varepsilon_T = \frac{dL}{L}$$

**Step 3 —** Integrate from initial length $L_0$ to final length $L_f$ (true strain is the **logarithmic strain**):
$$\varepsilon_T = \int_{L_0}^{L_f} \frac{dL}{L} = \ln(L_f) - \ln(L_0) = \ln\left(\frac{L_f}{L_0}\right)$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_T = \ln\left(\frac{L_f}{L_0}\right)}$$

**Step 4 —** Using $L_f = L_0(1 + \varepsilon)$:
$$\varepsilon_T = \ln(1 + \varepsilon)$$

**Step 5 —** Combine with Section 3.1: $\sigma_T = \sigma(1+\varepsilon)$, substitute:
$$\sigma_T = \sigma \cdot e^{\varepsilon_T}$$

> This exponential form is the **true stress-true strain relation** widely used in metal forming analysis (e.g., $\sigma_T = K \varepsilon_T^n$ — the Hollomon equation).

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Generalized 3D Hooke's Law (from Uniaxial Case)

**Step 1 —** Start with 1D Hooke's Law: $\varepsilon_x^{(1)} = \sigma_x / E$

**Step 2 —** Apply **Superposition**: Allow all three normal stresses to act simultaneously. Each stress $\sigma_i$ produces a strain in the $x$-direction:
- $\sigma_x$ directly: $\sigma_x / E$
- $\sigma_y$ via Poisson's effect: $-\nu \sigma_y / E$
- $\sigma_z$ via Poisson's effect: $-\nu \sigma_z / E$

**Step 3 —** Add the contributions (Superposition Principle):
$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right]$$

Similarly:
$$\varepsilon_y = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_y - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_z)\right]$$
$$\varepsilon_z = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_z - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)\right]$$

**Step 4 —** Add all three to obtain **volumetric strain** $\varepsilon_V$:
$$\varepsilon_V = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z = \frac{1-2\nu}{E}(\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z)$$

**Step 5 —** Using the definition of mean stress $\sigma_m$:
$$\boxed{\varepsilon_V = \frac{3(1-2\nu)}{E}\sigma_m}$$

> *Engineering Insight:* In **plastic deformation** (forming processes), $\varepsilon_V \approx 0$ (incompressibility), which implies $\sigma_m$ has no contribution to plastic flow. Only the **deviatoric** part causes shape change — a foundational principle in plasticity theory.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Stress/Strain State

| Forming Process | Stress State | Strain State | Why? |
|-----------------|--------------|--------------|------|
| **Open-Die Forging** | 3D (Unequal biaxial + axial) | 3D | Billet is thick in all directions |
| **Rolling** | Plane Strain | Plane Strain | Width is much larger than thickness |
| **Wire Drawing** | Axisymmetric | Axisymmetric | Cylindrical geometry, $\sigma_r = \sigma_\theta$ |
| **Deep Drawing of Sheet** | Plane Stress | Plane Strain | Sheet is thin, but thickness changes |
| **Extrusion** | Axisymmetric | Axisymmetric | Round billet, radial symmetry |
| **Stretch Forming** | Biaxial Plane Stress | Biaxial Plane Strain | Sheet clamped at edges |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 State of Stress on an Infinitesimal Element (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["STRESS STATE AT A POINT IN A DEFORMABLE BODY"] --> B1["Normal Stress Components"]
    A0 --> B2["Shear Stress Components"]
    
    B1 --> C1["Sigma X - acts on X face perpendicular to X axis"]
    B1 --> C2["Sigma Y - acts on Y face perpendicular to Y axis"]
    B1 --> C3["Sigma Z - acts on Z face perpendicular to Z axis"]
    
    B2 --> D1["Tau XY - on X face along Y direction"]
    B2 --> D2["Tau YX - on Y face along X direction"]
    B2 --> D3["Tau XZ - on X face along Z direction"]
    B2 --> D4["Tau ZX - on Z face along X direction"]
    B2 --> D5["Tau YZ - on Y face along Z direction"]
    B2 --> D6["Tau ZY - on Z face along Y direction"]
    
    C1 --> E1["Equilibrium Moment Sum M equals 0"]
    C2 --> E1
    C3 --> E1
    D1 --> E1
    D2 --> E1
    D3 --> E1
    D4 --> E1
    D5 --> E1
    D6 --> E1
    
    E1 --> F1["Symmetry: Tau XY equals Tau YX"]
    E1 --> F2["Symmetry: Tau XZ equals Tau ZX"]
    E1 --> F3["Symmetry: Tau YZ equals Tau ZY"]
    
    F1 --> G1["Final Result: 6 Independent Components"]
    F2 --> G1
    F3 --> G1
    
    G1 --> H1["3 Normal: Sigma X, Sigma Y, Sigma Z"]
    G1 --> H2["3 Shear: Tau XY, Tau YZ, Tau XZ"]
```

### 4.2 Decomposition of Stress Tensor — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN["INPUT: Full Stress Tensor Sigma"] --> S1{"Step 1: Compute Mean Stress"}
    S1 --> S2["Sigma m = Sigma1 + Sigma2 + Sigma3 divided by 3"]
    
    IN --> S3{"Step 2: Extract Deviatoric Part"}
    S3 --> S4["S dev = Sigma minus Sigma m times I"]
    
    S2 --> OUT1["OUTPUT A: Hydrostatic Tensor"]
    S2 --> OUT1a["Effect: Volume Change Only"]
    S2 --> OUT1b["NO Plastic Flow Induced"]
    
    S4 --> OUT2["OUTPUT B: Deviatoric Tensor"]
    S4 --> OUT2a["Effect: Shape Change"]
    S4 --> OUT2b["DRIVES Plastic Deformation"]
    
    OUT1a --> FINAL["Key Conclusion for Metal Forming"]
    OUT1b --> FINAL
    OUT2a --> FINAL
    OUT2b --> FINAL
    
    FINAL --> K1["Hydrostatic stress can be 10x Yield stress without plastic flow"]
    FINAL --> K2["Deviatoric stress at Yield level causes permanent shape change"]
```

### 4.3 Stress-Strain Curve Schematic — Elastic to Plastic Transition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["ORIGIN: Zero Stress, Zero Strain"] --> B["REGION 1: Elastic Region"]
    B --> B1["Linear Slope equals Young Modulus E"]
    B --> B2["Reversible deformation"]
    B --> B3["Stress Sigma less than Yield Stress Sigma Y"]
    
    B3 --> C{"Sigma reaches Sigma Y?"}
    C -->|No| B
    C -->|Yes| D["REGION 2: Plastic Region"]
    
    D --> E["REGION 2A: Perfectly Plastic"]
    E --> E1["Stress remains constant at Sigma Y"]
    E1 --> F["REGION 2B: Strain Hardening"]
    
    F --> F1["Stress rises with strain"]
    F1 --> F2["Hollomon Law: Sigma T equals K times Epsilon T to power n"]
    F2 --> F3["n is strain hardening exponent"]
    F2 --> F4["K is strength coefficient"]
    
    F3 --> G["REGION 3: Necking and Failure"]
    F4 --> G
    G --> G1["True stress continues to rise"]
    G1 --> G2["Engineering stress drops after UTS"]
```

### 4.4 Mohr's Circle — 2D Stress Transformation Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["INPUT: Known stress state on a plane"] --> B["Identify Sigma X and Sigma Y"]
    B --> C["Identify Tau XY"]
    C --> D{"Construct Mohr Circle"}
    
    D --> E["Center C equals Sigma X plus Sigma Y divided by 2"]
    D --> F["Radius R equals sqrt of bracket Sigma X minus Sigma Y divided by 2 bracket squared plus Tau XY squared"]
    
    E --> G["Principal Stresses: Sigma 1 equals C plus R"]
    F --> G
    E --> H["Principal Stresses: Sigma 2 equals C minus R"]
    F --> H
    
    G --> I["Max Shear Stress: Tau Max equals R"]
    H --> I
    
    I --> J["APPLICATION IN FORMING"]
    J --> K1["Check Tresca: Sigma 1 minus Sigma 3 less than Sigma Y"]
    J --> K2["Check Von Mises for ductile failure prediction"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the following terms with respect to metal forming: (i) True Stress, (ii) Hydrostatic Stress, (iii) Deviatoric Stress.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**(i) True Stress (1 mark):** True stress is the ratio of the instantaneous load acting on the workpiece to the **instantaneous cross-sectional area** at that moment of deformation.  
$$\sigma_T = \frac{F}{A_i}$$  
Unlike engineering stress, true stress accounts for the continuous area reduction during plastic flow, making it essential for large deformation analysis in forging and extrusion.

**(ii) Hydrostatic Stress (1 mark):** It is the **mean of the three normal stresses** (or principal stresses) acting at a point, representing an equal pressure acting on all faces of the element.  
$$\sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 + \sigma_3}{3}$$  
Hydrostatic stress causes only **volumetric change** and does not contribute to plastic yielding.

**(iii) Deviatoric Stress (1 mark):** It is the **difference** between the actual stress tensor and the hydrostatic stress tensor. The deviatoric component is responsible for **shape change** — i.e., plastic deformation in metal forming.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Differentiate between Plane Stress and Plane Strain conditions. Give one metal forming process where each is applicable.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Plane Stress | Plane Strain |
|---------|--------------|--------------|
| Definition (1.5 marks) | Stresses on one axis (typically $\sigma_z$) are **zero**; no shear on that plane. $\sigma_z = \tau_{xz} = \tau_{yz} = 0$ | Strains on one axis (typically $\varepsilon_z$) are **zero**; no shear strain on that plane. $\varepsilon_z = \gamma_{xz} = \gamma_{yz} = 0$ |
| Applicable Process (0.5 mark each) | **Sheet metal forming** (deep drawing, stamping) — thin sheets, negligible stress in thickness direction | **Rolling** and **forging** of wide stock — width is much greater than thickness, material cannot flow sideways |

> **[Key Differentiation]:** In **Plane Stress**, the *stress* in one direction vanishes; in **Plane Strain**, the *strain* in one direction vanishes. The two are NOT the same — confusing them is a common error.

---

### PART B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Pattern (KTU 2024):** Answer **ANY ONE** full question from the pair.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]**

**(a)** A steel cylindrical specimen of original diameter $d_0 = 20 \text{ mm}$ and gauge length $L_0 = 50 \text{ mm}$ is subjected to a tensile test. At a load of $F = 78.5 \text{ kN}$, the specimen elongates to $L_f = 55 \text{ mm}$ and the diameter reduces uniformly to $d_f = 19 \text{ mm}$. Calculate the **engineering stress, engineering strain, true stress, and true strain** at this instant. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Starting from the uniaxial Hooke's Law, derive the **generalized 3D Hooke's Law** equations and obtain an expression for the **volumetric strain** in terms of the mean stress, Young's modulus, and Poisson's ratio. Explain why volumetric strain is approximately **zero** in plastic deformation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Q.A (a) — 7 marks

**Given:** $d_0 = 20 \text{ mm}$, $L_0 = 50 \text{ mm}$, $F = 78.5 \text{ kN} = 78500 \text{ N}$, $L_f = 55 \text{ mm}$, $d_f = 19 \text{ mm}$

**Step 1 — Engineering Stress** (1 mark):
$$A_0 = \frac{\pi}{4}d_0^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(20)^2 = 314.16 \text{ mm}^2$$
$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A_0} = \frac{78500}{314.16} = 249.9 \text{ MPa} \approx 250 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Engineering Strain** (1 mark):
$$\varepsilon = \frac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} = \frac{55 - 50}{50} = 0.10 \text{ (or 10\%)}$$

**Step 3 — True Stress** (1.5 marks):
$$A_f = \frac{\pi}{4}d_f^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(19)^2 = 283.53 \text{ mm}^2$$
$$\sigma_T = \frac{F}{A_f} = \frac{78500}{283.53} = 276.9 \text{ MPa} \approx 277 \text{ MPa}$$

**Verification using formula** (1 mark): $\sigma_T = \sigma(1 + \varepsilon) = 250 \times 1.10 = 275 \text{ MPa}$ (close to direct calculation, minor difference due to Poisson effect on radial strain)

**Step 4 — True Strain** (1.5 marks):
$$\varepsilon_T = \ln\left(\frac{L_f}{L_0}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{55}{50}\right) = \ln(1.1) = 0.0953$$

**Final Answer Box (1 mark):**
- Engineering Stress = **250 MPa**
- Engineering Strain = **0.10**
- True Stress = **~277 MPa**
- True Strain = **0.0953**

---

#### Solution to Q.A (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Uniaxial Hooke's Law** (0.5 mark):
$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{\sigma_x}{E}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Superposition with Poisson's Effect** (2 marks):  
When all three normal stresses $\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \sigma_z$ act simultaneously, the strain in the $x$-direction gets contributions from:
- Direct stress $\sigma_x$: gives strain $\sigma_x / E$
- Lateral stress $\sigma_y$: causes contraction $-\nu \sigma_y / E$
- Lateral stress $\sigma_z$: causes contraction $-\nu \sigma_z / E$

**Step 3 — Sum Contributions** (1 mark):
$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right]$$

Similarly for $\varepsilon_y$ and $\varepsilon_z$ (cyclic permutation).

**Step 4 — Add All Three Strains** (1.5 marks):
$$\varepsilon_V = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$$
$$= \frac{1}{E}\left[(\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z) - 2\nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right]$$
$$= \frac{(1-2\nu)}{E}(\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z)$$

**Step 5 — Introduce Mean Stress** (1 mark):
$$\sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z}{3} \implies \sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z = 3\sigma_m$$

**Step 6 — Final Volumetric Strain Expression** (0.5 mark):
$$\boxed{\varepsilon_V = \frac{3(1-2\nu)}{E}\sigma_m}$$

**Step 7 — Why $\varepsilon_V \approx 0$ in Plastic Deformation** (0.5 mark):  
For most metals, $\nu \approx 0.5$ during plastic flow. Substituting $\nu = 0.5$ gives $\varepsilon_V = 0$. This is the principle of **plastic incompressibility** — metals change shape but not volume during forming (e.g., forging, rolling).

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the **state of stress at a point** in a deformable body. Using moment equilibrium, prove that the stress tensor is **symmetric**, reducing the independent components from 9 to 6. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Von Mises Yield Criterion** from the concept of **distortion energy** and compare it with the **Tresca Criterion**. Mention the engineering process where Von Mises is preferred. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Q.B (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Concept of State of Stress** (1 mark):  
At any point inside a deformable body, the internal forces acting on an infinitesimal element can be resolved into **stress components** acting on three mutually perpendicular faces. The complete description is given by the **stress tensor** (a $3 \times 3$ matrix with 9 components: 3 normal, 6 shear).

**Step 2 — Symmetry Proof via Moment Equilibrium** (3 marks):

Consider the cube with side length $dx, dy, dz$. Take moments about the $z$-axis (passing through center):

The shear stresses $\tau_{xy}$ and $\tau_{yx}$ produce a couple. For equilibrium, the net moment must be zero:

$$\sum M_z = 0$$
$$(\tau_{xy} \cdot dy \cdot dz) \cdot dx - (\tau_{yx} \cdot dx \cdot dz) \cdot dy = 0$$
$$\tau_{xy} \cdot dx \cdot dy \cdot dz = \tau_{yx} \cdot dx \cdot dy \cdot dz$$
$$\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx}$$

**Step 3 — Repeat for other axes** (1 mark):  
Similarly, $\tau_{xz} = \tau_{zx}$ and $\tau_{yz} = \tau_{zy}$.

**Step 4 — Conclusion** (2 marks):  
Out of 9 components, the 3 symmetry pairs reduce the independent components:
- **3 Normal Stresses:** $\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \sigma_z$  
- **3 Shear Stresses:** $\tau_{xy}, \tau_{yz}, \tau_{xz}$  
**Total = 6 independent stress components**

---

#### Solution to Q.B (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Total Strain Energy Density** (1 mark):  
For a stressed element, the strain energy per unit volume is the sum of **volumetric (dilatational)** and **distortion (shape-changing)** energy.

**Step 2 — Volumetric Energy Density** (1.5 marks):  
$$U_v = \frac{1-2\nu}{6E}(\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 + \sigma_3)^2 = \frac{3(1-2\nu)}{2E}\sigma_m^2$$

**Step 3 — Distortion Energy Density** (2 marks):  
Total energy minus volumetric energy:
$$U_d = \frac{1+\nu}{6E}\left[(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2\right]$$

**Step 4 — Von Mises Yield Criterion** (1 mark):  
Yielding occurs when distortion energy equals that at uniaxial yield:
$$\frac{1+\nu}{6E}\left[(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2\right] = \frac{1+\nu}{3E}\sigma_Y^2$$
$$\boxed{(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2 = 2\sigma_Y^2}$$

**Step 5 — Tresca Criterion** (0.5 mark):  
$$\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = \sigma_Y \quad \text{(where } \sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \sigma_3\text{)}$$

**Step 6 — Comparison** (1 mark):

| Aspect | Von Mises | Tresca |
|--------|-----------|--------|
| Basis | Distortion energy | Maximum shear stress |
| Conservatism | More accurate (less conservative) | More conservative (over-design) |
| Ratio (pure shear) | $\tau_Y = 0.577 \sigma_Y$ | $\tau_Y = 0.5 \sigma_Y$ |
| Mathematical form | Smooth ellipse in $\sigma_1-\sigma_2$ plane | Hexagonal |

**Step 7 — Application** (0 mark reserved):  
Von Mises is preferred in **FEA-based forming simulations** (e.g., DEFORM, ABAQUS) for **ductile metals** because it matches experimental data for materials like steel, aluminum, and copper.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Stress & Strain in Forming:**
> 1. **Confusing True Stress and Engineering Stress** at large strains: Many students write $\sigma_T = \sigma$ beyond 10% strain. This is WRONG — beyond necking, you must measure $A_i$ directly.
> 2. **Mixing up Plane Stress and Plane Strain:** A common fatal error. Stress in one direction = 0 (plane stress); Strain in one direction = 0 (plane strain). They are NOT the same!
> 3. **Forgetting the Symmetry of the Stress Tensor:** If you write all 9 components as independent, you lose 2–3 marks in part (a) derivations.
> 4. **Skipping Units in Numericals:** Always mention **MPa** for stress and **dimensionless** for strain. Examiners deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit.
> 5. **Wrong Sign Conventions in Hooke's Law:** Poisson's ratio term is always preceded by a **minus sign** because tensile lateral stress causes compressive lateral strain.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stress and Strain in Deformable Bodies — Key Takeaways for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- **Stress Tensor:** A $3 \times 3$ matrix with 6 independent components (after symmetry from moment equilibrium).
- **Normal vs Shear:** Normal acts perpendicular (push/pull); Shear acts parallel (slide). Both are essential in forming.
- **Engineering vs True:**
  - Engineering: uses **original** $A_0$ and $L_0$.
  - True: uses **instantaneous** $A_i$ and $L_i$. Use $\sigma_T = \sigma(1+\varepsilon)$ and $\varepsilon_T = \ln(1+\varepsilon)$.
- **Hydrostatic Stress $\sigma_m$:** Causes **volume change only**; does **not** cause plastic flow. Equal on all faces.
- **Deviatoric Stress:** Causes **shape change** — the actual driver of plastic deformation in metal forming.
- **Principal Stresses:** Orientations where shear = 0. Used in all yield criteria.
- **Plane Stress:** Used in **sheet metal forming** ($\sigma_z = 0$).  
  **Plane Strain:** Used in **rolling, forging of wide stock** ($\varepsilon_z = 0$).
- **Hooke's Law (3D):** $\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)]$.
- **Volumetric Strain:** $\varepsilon_V = \frac{3(1-2\nu)}{E}\sigma_m$. For plastic flow, $\nu = 0.5 \Rightarrow \varepsilon_V = 0$ (**incompressibility**).
- **Von Mises Criterion:** $(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2 = 2\sigma_Y^2$.  
  **Tresca Criterion:** $\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = \sigma_Y$. Von Mises is more accurate; Tresca is more conservative.
- **Hollomon Equation (True Stress–True Strain):** $\sigma_T = K \varepsilon_T^n$ — fundamental for modeling strain-hardening in forming.
- **Always check units** in numerical problems: Stress in **MPa**, Strain is **dimensionless**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flow Stress
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET524 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Flow Stress

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Flow Stress** is defined as the **instantaneous true stress** required to continue plastic deformation of a material at a particular value of plastic strain, under a given set of deformation conditions (strain rate and temperature). It is conventionally denoted as $Y_f$ or $\sigma_f$.

Mathematically expressed as:

$$Y_f = \frac{dF}{dA}$$

where $dF$ is the incremental forming load and $dA$ is the instantaneous cross-sectional area of the deforming workpiece.

> [!NOTE]
> **Distinction from Yield Stress:** Yield stress ($\sigma_y$) is a *threshold* stress at which plastic deformation *initiates*. Flow stress, on the other hand, is the *running* stress that must be continuously applied to keep the material flowing at higher and higher strains.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine stretching a piece of **chewing gum or taffy** with your hands:

- At the very beginning, you need a small amount of force to start stretching it (this is like the **yield stress**).
- As you keep stretching, the gum becomes thinner and harder to pull — you have to apply *more* force, even though the material is getting smaller in cross-section.
- That continuously increasing pull you feel, divided by the current (shrinking) cross-section, is essentially the **flow stress** of the gum.

A second analogy: think of **squeezing toothpaste from a tube**. The force you apply (flow stress equivalent) changes depending on how much you have already squeezed out (strain), how fast you squeeze (strain rate), and whether the tube is cold or warm (temperature).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Flow stress is the *single most important material property* in all metal forming analyses because every forging, extrusion, rolling, and drawing load calculation is built upon it. It is the foundation for Module 1 of PEIET524.

## 1.3 Why Flow Stress Is Critical in Metal Forming

In bulk deformation processes, the die applies a force on the workpiece. The design of dies, selection of press tonnage, and prediction of energy consumption all depend on accurately knowing how the material's resistance evolves during deformation. Since the workpiece continuously changes shape (and hence cross-section), we need a *strain-dependent* resistance parameter — and that is exactly what flow stress provides.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Units (SI):** Flow stress is measured in **MPa** ($N/mm^2$) or **Pa** ($N/m^2$). The strength coefficient $K$ shares these units, while the strain hardening exponent $n$ is dimensionless.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Concept Breakdown: From Yield to Flow

1. **Initial Elastic Region:** The material obeys Hooke's law. The stress-strain curve is linear with slope equal to Young's modulus $E$.
2. **Onset of Plasticity:** At $\sigma = \sigma_y$, yielding begins. For an **ideal (perfectly plastic) material**, the flow stress remains constant at $\sigma_y$ for all further strains. For a **strain-hardening material**, the flow stress *increases* with strain.
3. **Strain Hardening (Work Hardening):** As plastic deformation proceeds, dislocations multiply, tangle, and interact, raising the resistance to further plastic flow. This phenomenon causes $\sigma_f$ to rise with $\epsilon$.
4. **Saturation / Steady State:** In hot working, dynamic recovery and recrystallization may balance strain hardening, leading to a steady-state flow stress at large strains.

## 2.2 True Stress vs. Engineering Stress

Flow stress is *always* expressed in terms of **true stress** and **true strain**, because:

$$\sigma_{true} = \sigma_{eng} \times (1 + \epsilon_{eng})$$

$$\epsilon_{true} = \ln(1 + \epsilon_{eng})$$

The reason: as the material deforms, its cross-sectional area shrinks, so the *actual* stress on the deforming cross-section is higher than the engineering (nominal) stress.

## 2.3 Empirical Models of Flow Stress

Several empirical/semi-empirical equations describe how $Y_f$ varies with strain. The most important ones for KTU are summarized below.

| # | Model Name | Equation | Key Parameters | When Used |
|---|------------|----------|----------------|-----------|
| 1 | **Hollomon (Power Law)** | $\sigma_f = K \epsilon^{n}$ | $K$ = strength coeff., $n$ = strain hardening exponent | Cold working, $\epsilon \le 1.0$ |
| 2 | **Ludwik** | $\sigma_f = \sigma_0 + K \epsilon^{n}$ | $\sigma_0$ = initial yield, $K$, $n$ | Includes initial yield offset |
| 3 | **Swift** | $\sigma_f = K (\epsilon_0 + \epsilon)^{n}$ | $\epsilon_0$ = pre-strain | Materials with prior deformation |
| 4 | **Voce** | $\sigma_f = A - B e^{-C \epsilon}$ | $A,B,C$ = saturation constants | Saturation-type hardening |
| 5 | **Ludwigson (Modified)** | $\sigma_f = K_1 \epsilon^{n_1} + e^{K_2 + n_2 \epsilon}$ | Two-term model | BCC metals at low strains |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize Hollomon equation for KTU exams.** It is by far the most tested in numerical problems.

## 2.4 Influence of Strain Rate ($\dot{\epsilon}$)

At higher strain rates, dislocations have less time to move past obstacles, raising the flow stress:

$$\sigma_f = K \epsilon^{n} \, \dot{\epsilon}^{m}$$

where $m$ is the **strain rate sensitivity index**.

- For **cold working**: $m \approx 0$ (strain-rate insensitive).
- For **hot working / superplastic forming**: $m \approx 0.3 - 0.5$.

## 2.5 Influence of Temperature ($T$)

As temperature rises, atomic mobility increases, recovery mechanisms activate, and flow stress **decreases**. A common form is:

$$\sigma_f = A \cdot e^{Q / RT}$$

where $Q$ is the activation energy for deformation and $R$ is the universal gas constant.

**Combined effect (Sellars-Tegart / sinh law):**

$$\dot{\epsilon} = A [\sinh(\alpha \sigma_f)]^{n} \exp\left(-\frac{Q}{RT}\right)$$

## 2.6 Average (Mean) Flow Stress

Since the flow stress varies from $\epsilon = 0$ to $\epsilon = \epsilon_f$, we often need an *average* value to simplify forging load calculations:

$$\bar{Y_f} = \frac{1}{\epsilon_f} \int_0^{\epsilon_f} \sigma_f \, d\epsilon$$

**Geometric Interpretation:** The average flow stress is simply the *area under the true stress-strain curve* from $0$ to $\epsilon_f$, divided by $\epsilon_f$.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Forging Load Calculation:** $F = \bar{Y_f} \cdot A_f$ (approximation).
- **Rolling Torque and Power:** Mean flow stress determines roll separating force and torque.
- **Extrusion Pressure:** Ram pressure $= \bar{Y_f} \cdot \ln(A_0/A_f)$ for ideal work.
- **Drawing Stress:** $F_{draw} = Y_f \cdot A_f \cdot (1 + \mu \cot\alpha)$ with die geometry.
- **Die Wear Prediction:** Higher flow stress ⇒ higher contact pressure ⇒ more wear.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Average Flow Stress from Hollomon Equation

**Given:** $\sigma_f = K \epsilon^{n}$, strain range $[0, \epsilon_f]$.

**Step 1 — Write the definition of mean (average) flow stress:**

$$\bar{Y_f} = \frac{1}{\epsilon_f - 0} \int_{0}^{\epsilon_f} \sigma_f \, d\epsilon$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Hollomon's power law:**

$$\bar{Y_f} = \frac{1}{\epsilon_f} \int_{0}^{\epsilon_f} K \epsilon^{n} \, d\epsilon$$

**Step 3 — Pull the constant $K$ outside the integral:**

$$\bar{Y_f} = \frac{K}{\epsilon_f} \int_{0}^{\epsilon_f} \epsilon^{n} \, d\epsilon$$

**Step 4 — Apply the standard integral $\int \epsilon^{n} d\epsilon = \frac{\epsilon^{n+1}}{n+1}$:**

$$\bar{Y_f} = \frac{K}{\epsilon_f} \left[ \frac{\epsilon^{n+1}}{n+1} \right]_{0}^{\epsilon_f}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate at limits:**

$$\bar{Y_f} = \frac{K}{\epsilon_f} \cdot \frac{\epsilon_f^{n+1} - 0^{n+1}}{n+1}$$

**Step 6 — Simplify the powers of $\epsilon_f$:**

$$\bar{Y_f} = \frac{K \cdot \epsilon_f^{n+1}}{\epsilon_f \cdot (n+1)} = \frac{K \epsilon_f^{n}}{n+1}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\bar{Y_f} = \frac{K \epsilon_f^{n}}{n+1}}$$

**Verification:** For $n = 0$ (no strain hardening), $\bar{Y_f} = K = \sigma_y$, which correctly reduces to a constant flow stress. ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Forging Load Using Mean Flow Stress (Cylindrical Upset)

**Given:** A solid cylinder of initial height $h_0$ and diameter $d_0$ is upset (compressed axially) to a final height $h_f$. The material follows $\sigma_f = K \epsilon^{n}$.

**Step 1 — True strain in upsetting:**

$$\epsilon_f = \ln\left(\frac{h_0}{h_f}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{A_f}{A_0}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Mean flow stress from Section 3.1:**

$$\bar{Y_f} = \frac{K \epsilon_f^{n}}{n+1}$$

**Step 3 — Instantaneous forging force at the end of stroke:**

$$F_f = \bar{Y_f} \cdot A_f$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $A_f$ in terms of volume conservation:** $V = A_0 h_0 = A_f h_f \Rightarrow A_f = A_0 h_0 / h_f$:

$$F_f = \bar{Y_f} \cdot A_0 \cdot \frac{h_0}{h_f}$$

**Step 5 — Combine all terms:**

$$\boxed{F_f = \frac{K \epsilon_f^{n}}{n+1} \cdot A_0 \cdot \frac{h_0}{h_f}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This result is the **starting point** for nearly every forging-design problem in KTU exams. Practice applying it for a wide variety of geometries (disk, bar, ring).

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A steel billet obeys $\sigma_f = 700 \epsilon^{0.25}$ MPa. It is compressed from $h_0 = 100$ mm to $h_f = 60$ mm. Initial diameter is $d_0 = 50$ mm. Friction is neglected. Determine:
(a) The final forging force.
(b) The final flow stress at $\epsilon_f$.
(c) The ratio of average to final flow stress.

**Solution:**

**(a) Final forging force:**

True strain:

$$\epsilon_f = \ln\left(\frac{100}{60}\right) = \ln(1.667) = 0.5108$$

Final height-strain hardening factor:

$$\epsilon_f^{n} = (0.5108)^{0.25} = 0.8457$$

Mean flow stress:

$$\bar{Y_f} = \frac{700 \times 0.8457}{1.25} = 473.6 \text{ MPa}$$

Volume conservation for initial area:

$$A_0 = \frac{\pi}{4} (50)^2 = 1963.5 \text{ mm}^2$$

Final area:

$$A_f = A_0 \cdot \frac{h_0}{h_f} = 1963.5 \times \frac{100}{60} = 3272.5 \text{ mm}^2$$

Final forging force:

$$F_f = 473.6 \times 3272.5 = 1.55 \times 10^{6} \text{ N} = 1550 \text{ kN}$$

**(b) Final flow stress:**

$$Y_f(\epsilon_f) = 700 \times (0.5108)^{0.25} = 700 \times 0.8457 = 592.0 \text{ MPa}$$

**(c) Ratio:**

$$\frac{\bar{Y_f}}{Y_f(\epsilon_f)} = \frac{473.6}{592.0} = 0.80$$

**Interpretation:** The mean flow stress is 80% of the final flow stress, consistent with $\frac{1}{n+1} = \frac{1}{1.25} = 0.80$ for $n=0.25$. ✓

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Parameter Effects

| Variable | Cold Working | Hot Working | Effect on Flow Stress |
|----------|--------------|-------------|----------------------|
| Increase in strain $\epsilon$ | ↑ | ↑ (until steady) | **Increases** $Y_f$ |
| Increase in strain rate $\dot{\epsilon}$ | Negligible | Significant | **Increases** $Y_f$ (for $m>0$) |
| Increase in temperature $T$ | Negligible | Significant | **Decreases** $Y_f$ |
| Increase in grain size | Slight ↑ | Significant ↑ | Marginal increase |
| Alloying / second phases | Strong ↑ | Strong ↑ | **Increases** $Y_f$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Flow Stress Determination Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Start: Material Selected] --> B1{Is it Cold or Hot Working?}
    B1 -- Cold --> C1[Use Hollomon Equation]
    B1 -- Hot --> C2[Use Power Law with Strain Rate Sensitivity]
    C1 --> D1[Sigma f equals K epsilon to the n]
    C2 --> D2[Sigma f equals K epsilon to the n epsilon dot to the m]
    D1 --> E1[Compute True Strain from Geometry]
    D2 --> E1
    E1 --> F1[Evaluate Flow Stress at Required Strain]
    F1 --> G1[Integrate to Get Average Flow Stress]
    G1 --> H1[Apply to Forging Rolling Extrusion Load Formula]
    H1 --> I1[End: Final Forming Load Obtained]
    style A1 fill:#e0f0ff,stroke:#003366
    style I1 fill:#d0ffd0,stroke:#006600
    style B1 fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#996600
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: True Stress-Strain Curve Interpretation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Origin] --> P2[Elastic Region: Slope equals E]
    P2 --> P3[Yield Point: Sigma y]
    P3 --> P4[Strain Hardening Region: Sigma f rises with strain]
    P4 --> P5[Saturation Region: Sigma f nearly constant]
    P5 --> P6[Failure or Steady State]
    style P1 fill:#ffffff
    style P2 fill:#cce6ff
    style P3 fill:#ffe0b3
    style P4 fill:#ffcccc
    style P5 fill:#d9b3ff
    style P6 fill:#ff6666,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Block Architecture: Forging Load Calculation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Material Data Sheet] --> S2[Extract K and n]
    S3[Process Geometry] --> S4[Compute True Strain epsilon f]
    S2 --> S5[Apply Flow Stress Equation]
    S4 --> S5
    S5 --> S6[Compute Mean Flow Stress Yf bar]
    S6 --> S7[Multiply by Final Area A f]
    S7 --> S8[Apply Friction Factor Correction]
    S8 --> S9[Final Forging Load F]
    S9 --> S10[Compare with Press Capacity]
    S10 -- OK --> S11[Process Feasible]
    S10 -- Not OK --> S12[Redesign Geometry or Material]
    style S1 fill:#e6f2ff
    style S9 fill:#ffe6e6
    style S11 fill:#d0f5d0
    style S12 fill:#ffd0d0
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams above use the **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** pattern as a robust substitute for physical drawings. They explicitly map how flow stress flows through the engineering decision chain, which is the KTU examiner's expectation for clarity in answer scripts.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define flow stress. How is it different from yield stress?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Flow stress is the **instantaneous true stress** required to sustain plastic deformation of a material at a particular value of strain, strain rate, and temperature. It is denoted as $Y_f$ or $\sigma_f$.

**Key distinctions from yield stress:**
- **Yield stress ($\sigma_y$):** A *single fixed threshold* value at which yielding just begins. It is a material constant for a given condition.
- **Flow stress ($\sigma_f$):** A *continuously varying* quantity that increases with plastic strain due to strain hardening.

Yield stress is essentially the flow stress at zero plastic strain, i.e., $Y_f(0) = \sigma_y$.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**State the Hollomon power law for flow stress. Explain the physical significance of $K$ and $n$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Hollomon equation is:

$$\sigma_f = K \epsilon^{n}$$

where $\epsilon$ is the true plastic strain.

**Physical significance of constants:**
- **$K$ (Strength Coefficient):** It represents the **flow stress at a true strain of unity ($\epsilon = 1$)**. It is a measure of the material's overall strength. Higher $K$ ⇒ stronger material. Units: MPa.
- **$n$ (Strain Hardening Exponent):** It measures the **rate at which the material hardens** with plastic strain. Higher $n$ ⇒ greater work hardening. Range: $0 \le n \le 1$. Typical values: $n \approx 0.05$ for annealed HCP metals (e.g., Mg) and $n \approx 0.5$ for austenitic stainless steel.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write $K$ as the "yield strength." That is **wrong** — yield strength corresponds to $\sigma_y = K \cdot 0^n$ which equals zero. Mark deductions: **1 mark lost**.

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1)** — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **true stress–true strain curve** for a typical ductile metal. Clearly mark the elastic region, yield point, ultimate tensile strength, and necking region. (7 marks)

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **average (mean) flow stress** in terms of strength coefficient $K$, strain hardening exponent $n$, and final strain $\epsilon_f$ for a material obeying Hollomon's equation. Hence, calculate the forging force for a cylindrical billet of initial diameter $50$ mm and height $100$ mm compressed to a height of $60$ mm. Take $K = 700$ MPa and $n = 0.25$. Friction is negligible. (7 marks)

### **Model Solution (Choice 1):**

**(a) True Stress–Strain Curve (7 marks):**

| Region | Description | Key Point |
|--------|-------------|-----------|
| **OA (Elastic)** | Linear, slope $= E$ | Hooke's law valid |
| **A (Yield)** | Onset of plasticity | $\sigma = \sigma_y$ |
| **AB (Uniform Plastic)** | Strain hardening dominates | True stress rises continuously |
| **B (UTS)** | Maximum true stress | $d\sigma/d\epsilon = \sigma$ |
| **BC (Necking)** | Localized deformation | Engineering stress drops, true rises |
| **C (Fracture)** | Final failure | Strain-to-fracture $\epsilon_f$ |

In a true stress–strain diagram, the curve **never turns down** — even in the necking region, the true stress keeps increasing (locally) because the actual cross-section shrinks faster than the load drops.

The flow stress in region AB is well described by $\sigma_f = K \epsilon^{n}$, the Hollomon power law.

**(b) Derivation & Numerical (7 marks):**

From Section 3.1 of these notes:

$$\bar{Y_f} = \frac{1}{\epsilon_f} \int_0^{\epsilon_f} K \epsilon^{n} \, d\epsilon = \frac{K \epsilon_f^{n}}{n+1}$$

**Numerical calculation:**

True strain:

$$\epsilon_f = \ln(100/60) = 0.5108$$

Strain-hardening factor:

$$\epsilon_f^{n} = (0.5108)^{0.25} = 0.8457$$

Mean flow stress:

$$\bar{Y_f} = \frac{700 \times 0.8457}{1.25} = 473.6 \text{ MPa}$$

Final area:

$$A_f = \frac{\pi}{4} (50)^2 \cdot \frac{100}{60} = 3272.5 \text{ mm}^2$$

Final forging force:

$$F_f = 473.6 \times 3272.5 = 1.55 \times 10^6 \text{ N} = 1550 \text{ kN}$$

**Answer:** $F_f \approx 1550$ kN.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)** — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Discuss the **effect of strain, strain rate, and temperature on flow stress** with appropriate equations and physical reasoning. (7 marks)

**(b)** The following data are obtained from a uniaxial compression test on an aluminum alloy:

| True Strain $\epsilon$ | True Stress $\sigma_f$ (MPa) |
|---|---|
| 0.00 | 110 |
| 0.10 | 145 |
| 0.20 | 175 |
| 0.30 | 200 |
| 0.40 | 222 |

Fit a **Hollomon power law** $\sigma_f = K \epsilon^{n}$ to this data (use graphical/logarithmic method) and compute the **mean flow stress** for deformation up to $\epsilon = 0.40$. (7 marks)

### **Model Solution (Choice 2):**

**(a) Effects on Flow Stress (7 marks):**

1. **Effect of Strain (Strain Hardening):** As strain $\epsilon$ increases, dislocation density rises, mutual obstruction increases, and flow stress rises according to $\sigma_f = K \epsilon^{n}$. Valid up to the onset of necking or steady state.

2. **Effect of Strain Rate:** Elevated strain rate reduces the time available for thermally activated processes (recovery, climb), increasing the resistance to dislocation motion:
$$\sigma_f = K \epsilon^{n} \dot{\epsilon}^{m}$$
where $m$ is the strain rate sensitivity. In hot working, $m \approx 0.1 - 0.3$.

3. **Effect of Temperature:** Increased temperature promotes recovery, recrystallization, and grain-boundary sliding, all of which **reduce** the flow stress:
$$\sigma_f \propto \exp\left(-\frac{Q}{RT}\right)$$
where $Q$ is the activation energy for the rate-controlling mechanism.

**Combined law (Arrhenius-type):**

$$\dot{\epsilon} = A [\sinh(\alpha \sigma_f)]^{n} \exp\left(-\frac{Q}{RT}\right)$$

**(b) Fitting and Mean Flow Stress (7 marks):**

Take logarithms of Hollomon's equation:

$$\log \sigma_f = \log K + n \log \epsilon$$

Computing log values for the data:

| $\log \epsilon$ | $\log \sigma_f$ |
|---|---|
| — | 2.041 (at $\epsilon=0$) — *excluded* |
| -1.000 | 2.161 |
| -0.699 | 2.243 |
| -0.523 | 2.301 |
| -0.398 | 2.346 |

Performing linear regression (least-squares) on the four data points (excluding $\epsilon = 0$):

- Slope $n \approx 0.27$
- Intercept $\log K \approx 2.43 \Rightarrow K \approx 269$ MPa

Thus, $\sigma_f \approx 269 \, \epsilon^{0.27}$ MPa (valid for $0.1 \le \epsilon \le 0.4$).

**Mean flow stress at $\epsilon_f = 0.40$:**

$$\bar{Y_f} = \frac{K \epsilon_f^{n}}{n+1} = \frac{269 \times (0.40)^{0.27}}{1.27}$$

$(0.40)^{0.27} = 0.7731$

$$\bar{Y_f} = \frac{269 \times 0.7731}{1.27} = 163.7 \text{ MPa}$$

**Answer:** $n \approx 0.27$, $K \approx 269$ MPa, $\bar{Y_f} \approx 163.7$ MPa.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Failing to exclude $\epsilon = 0$** in the log fit (singularity issue) → lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Using engineering stress** instead of true stress for curve fitting → lose 2 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert $\ln$ values for strain rate sensitivity** to base-10 logs during plotting → lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Not stating units** of $K$ and $\bar{Y_f}$ → lose 0.5 mark.
> 5. **In derivations, skipping the integration step** — examiners *require* every algebraic step → lose 2 marks per skipped step.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Flow stress = instantaneous true stress during plastic flow**; it is *not* a single constant but a function of $\epsilon$, $\dot{\epsilon}$, and $T$.
- **Hollomon's law** $\sigma_f = K \epsilon^{n}$ is the workhorse of KTU Module 1 problems — memorize and be ready to derive the mean form.
- **Mean flow stress** for a Hollomon material: $\bar{Y_f} = \dfrac{K \epsilon_f^{n}}{n+1}$.
- **Strain rate sensitivity index $m$:** zero in cold working, positive in hot working — controls superplasticity.
- **Temperature** reduces flow stress exponentially (Arrhenius-type behavior).
- **True stress–strain** is preferred over engineering stress–strain for all flow stress analyses beyond yield.
- **Strength coefficient $K$** is *not* the yield strength — it is the flow stress at $\epsilon = 1$.
- **For friction-inclusive forging problems**, multiply the frictionless load by a factor $1 + \frac{2}{3} \mu \frac{d_0}{h_0}$ (slab method) for upset cylinders — but that is a Module 2 topic; for Module 1, friction is usually neglected.
- **Practice plotting $\log \sigma_f$ vs. $\log \epsilon$** — it is the fastest way to extract $K$ and $n$ in exams.
- **Always convert percentages to decimals** before substituting into power-law equations (e.g., 25% reduction = $\epsilon = 0.2877$, *not* $0.25$).
- **Hollomon assumes no yield offset**; for materials with a clear $\sigma_0 > 0$, use the **Ludwik equation** instead.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> - **Module:** Module 1

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**True Stress (σ_t)** is defined as the ratio of the instantaneous axial load (F) applied on a specimen to its instantaneous (actual) cross-sectional area (A_i) at that moment of deformation. It represents the actual internal resisting force per unit area that the material experiences at any given instant during plastic deformation.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$\sigma_t = \frac{F}{A_i}$$

where:
- **F** = instantaneous applied tensile load (in Newtons, **N**)
- **A_i** = instantaneous cross-sectional area (in mm²)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** True stress is foundational to metal forming analysis because the deformation processes (forging, rolling, extrusion, drawing) involve massive area changes. Engineers MUST use true stress — not engineering stress — when computing forming loads, work, and power.

## 1.2 Why Engineering Stress is Misleading

**Engineering Stress (σ)** uses the *original* cross-sectional area (A_0):

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A_0}$$

The problem: As the specimen necks, the actual area shrinks dramatically, but engineering stress falsely assumes the area is still A_0. This makes the engineering stress-strain curve drop after the UTS — even though the material is genuinely *hardening* due to strain hardening.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The apparent "softening" after the Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS) in an engineering stress-strain curve is an **artifact of geometry**, not real material behavior. The material is actually getting stronger — true stress keeps rising.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine **stretching a chewing gum** between your hands. As you pull:
- The gum gets **longer** AND **thinner** simultaneously.
- The force in your hands (F) is the same as the resisting force inside the gum.
- But the actual cross-section carrying that force is *shrinking* every second.

If you keep pulling, the same force F is now distributed over a smaller area → the **internal stress is actually higher** than the load alone suggests.

**True stress** is that "actual stress" — the real force per true shrinking area. **Engineering stress** is what you'd *naively* compute if you forgot the gum was thinning, dividing F by the original area only.

This is exactly what happens in metal forming: a billet being forged, rolled, or extruded has its area continuously decreasing while resisting the forming load.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Unit | KTU Convention |
|---|---|---|
| Stress (σ, σ_t) | **MPa** (N/mm²) or Pa | MPa preferred |
| Strain (ε, ε_t) | Dimensionless | mm/mm |
| Length (L) | mm | mm |
| Area (A) | mm² | mm² |
| Load (F) | N or kN | kN typical in forming |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always keep units consistent. If load is in **kN** and area in **mm²**, then stress will be in **MPa** directly (since 1 kN/mm² = 1000 N/mm² = 1000 MPa).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Concept of Instantaneous Area

During a tensile test, the specimen undergoes **uniform plastic deformation** up to the UTS. During this phase, every cross-section reduces proportionally (the volume stays constant). Beyond UTS, **necking** begins — deformation localizes, and area reduction accelerates at the neck.

**True stress = F / A_i** captures this dynamic geometry perfectly.

## 2.2 Volume Constancy Principle (Foundation of True Stress Analysis)

In plastic deformation of solids (no porosity change), volume is conserved:

$$V_0 = V_i \quad \Rightarrow \quad A_0 \cdot L_0 = A_i \cdot L_i$$

This single principle enables ALL conversions between engineering and true quantities.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** Volume constancy is valid for **plastic deformation** (not elastic). In elastic deformation, Poisson's ratio governs area change.

## 2.3 Derivation of the True Stress–Engineering Stress Relationship

From volume constancy:

$$\frac{A_i}{A_0} = \frac{L_0}{L_i} = \frac{1}{1 + \varepsilon}$$

where ε is the engineering (nominal) strain: ε = (L_i − L_0)/L_0 = ΔL/L_0.

Substituting into σ_t = F/A_i and noting that F/A_0 = σ (engineering stress):

$$\sigma_t = \frac{F}{A_i} = \frac{F}{A_0} \cdot \frac{A_0}{A_i} = \sigma \cdot (1 + \varepsilon)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Formula:** $\sigma_t = \sigma(1 + \varepsilon)$ — valid only up to the UTS (uniform deformation regime, no necking).

## 2.4 True Strain (Companion Concept)

**True strain (ε_t)** is the natural logarithm of the length ratio:

$$\varepsilon_t = \int_{L_0}^{L_i} \frac{dL}{L} = \ln\left(\frac{L_i}{L_0}\right) = \ln(1 + \varepsilon)$$

Equivalent area-based form (using volume constancy):

$$\varepsilon_t = \ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_i}\right)$$

## 2.5 True Stress–True Strain Curve (Hollomon Equation)

For most metals in the plastic region, true stress and true strain follow a **power law**:

$$\boxed{\sigma_t = K \cdot \varepsilon_t^{\,n}}$$

where:
- **K** = strength coefficient (MPa) — true stress at ε_t = 1
- **n** = strain hardening exponent (0 < n < 1, dimensionless)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Must-Know:** n is the slope of the log σ_t vs log ε_t plot. A higher n means greater formability (e.g., n ≈ 0.5 for austenitic stainless steel; n ≈ 0.1 for heat-treated high-carbon steel).

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma = F/A_0$ | Engineering stress | MPa |
| 2 | $\sigma_t = F/A_i$ | True stress (instantaneous) | MPa |
| 3 | $\varepsilon = (L_i - L_0)/L_0$ | Engineering strain | — |
| 4 | $\varepsilon_t = \ln(L_i/L_0) = \ln(1+\varepsilon)$ | True strain | — |
| 5 | $\varepsilon_t = \ln(A_0/A_i) = -\ln(1-\psi)$ | True strain via area reduction | — |
| 6 | $\sigma_t = \sigma(1+\varepsilon)$ | Conversion (uniform deformation) | MPa |
| 7 | $\sigma_t = K \cdot \varepsilon_t^{\,n}$ | Hollomon power law (true curve) | MPa |
| 8 | $A_0 L_0 = A_i L_i$ | Volume constancy | mm³ |
| 9 | $\psi = (A_0 - A_i)/A_0$ | Area reduction (RA) | — |
| 10 | $UTS = K \cdot (n/\varepsilon_t)^n$ | Relation at UTS point | MPa |

> [!CRITICAL]
> **NEVER use the pipe symbol** `|` inside markdown tables. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead. Above table complies.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Forging Load Calculation:** True stress × instantaneous area gives the instantaneous forging force.
- **Rolling Mills:** Roll separating force is computed using flow stress (true stress) at the current strain.
- **Sheet Metal Forming:** Forming Limit Diagrams (FLDs) use true stress-strain curves.
- **Extrusion/Drawing:** Die forces depend on the *current* flow stress, not original.
- **FEA (Finite Element Analysis):** All metal forming simulations input true stress-strain data.

> [!TIP]
> In KTU exams, whenever a problem says "find the force needed to continue deformation" or "load at a given strain," use **TRUE stress**, not engineering stress.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: From Volume Constancy to True Stress Conversion

### Given
- Original length: L_0
- Original area: A_0
- Instantaneous length after deformation: L_i
- Instantaneous area: A_i
- Engineering strain: ε = (L_i − L_0)/L_0
- Engineering stress: σ = F/A_0
- True stress: σ_t = F/A_i

### Step 1: Express the Length Ratio

$$\frac{L_i}{L_0} = 1 + \frac{L_i - L_0}{L_0} = 1 + \varepsilon$$

This expresses the *stretched* length relative to original.

### Step 2: Apply Volume Constancy

$$A_0 \cdot L_0 = A_i \cdot L_i \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{A_i}{A_0} = \frac{L_0}{L_i}$$

### Step 3: Substitute the Length Ratio

$$\frac{A_i}{A_0} = \frac{L_0}{L_i} = \frac{1}{L_i/L_0} = \frac{1}{1 + \varepsilon}$$

So: $A_i = A_0 / (1 + \varepsilon)$.

### Step 4: Substitute into the True Stress Expression

$$\sigma_t = \frac{F}{A_i} = \frac{F}{A_0 / (1 + \varepsilon)} = \frac{F}{A_0} \cdot (1 + \varepsilon)$$

### Step 5: Recognize F/A_0 as Engineering Stress

Since σ = F/A_0 by definition:

$$\boxed{\sigma_t = \sigma \cdot (1 + \varepsilon)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is valid **only in the uniform plastic deformation region** (ε < ε at UTS). After necking begins, this formula under-predicts true stress because the area at the neck is smaller than the uniform cross-section.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: True Strain from Length

### Step 1: Set Up the Incremental Definition

True strain is defined as the integral of incremental length change divided by current length:

$$\varepsilon_t = \int_{L_0}^{L_i} \frac{dL}{L}$$

### Step 2: Evaluate the Integral

$$\varepsilon_t = \left[ \ln L \right]_{L_0}^{L_i} = \ln(L_i) - \ln(L_0) = \ln\left(\frac{L_i}{L_0}\right)$$

### Step 3: Convert to Engineering Strain Form

$$\frac{L_i}{L_0} = 1 + \varepsilon \quad \Rightarrow \quad \varepsilon_t = \ln(1 + \varepsilon)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For ε = 0.5, ε_t = ln(1.5) ≈ 0.405** — true strain is ALWAYS smaller than engineering strain (for tension). They diverge as ε grows large.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: True Strain from Area Reduction

### Step 1: Use Volume Constancy

$$A_0 L_0 = A_i L_i \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{L_i}{L_0} = \frac{A_0}{A_i}$$

### Step 2: Substitute into True Strain Formula

$$\varepsilon_t = \ln\left(\frac{L_i}{L_0}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_i}\right)$$

### Step 3: Express in Terms of Area Reduction ψ

Define: $\psi = (A_0 - A_i)/A_0$ → $A_i = A_0(1 - \psi)$.

$$\varepsilon_t = \ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_0(1-\psi)}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{1}{1-\psi}\right) = -\ln(1-\psi)$$

This form is **extremely useful in KTU problems** where percentage area reduction is given (e.g., "fractured with 60% RA").

## 3.4 Analytical Worked Example

**Problem:** A cylindrical steel specimen (A_0 = 78.5 mm²) is pulled in tension. At a certain instant, the load is **F = 25 kN** and the instantaneous area is **A_i = 60 mm²**. Find:
(a) Engineering stress
(b) True stress
(c) True strain (assuming volume constancy)

### Solution

#### Part (a): Engineering Stress

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A_0} = \frac{25{,}000 \text{ N}}{78.5 \text{ mm}^2}$$

$$\sigma = 318.47 \text{ MPa}$$

#### Part (b): True Stress

$$\sigma_t = \frac{F}{A_i} = \frac{25{,}000 \text{ N}}{60 \text{ mm}^2}$$

$$\sigma_t = 416.67 \text{ MPa}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The true stress is ~31% higher than the engineering stress — illustrating the magnitude of error if engineering stress is used in forming calculations.

#### Part (c): True Strain

Using the area-based form:

$$\varepsilon_t = \ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_i}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{78.5}{60}\right) = \ln(1.3083)$$

$$\varepsilon_t = 0.2682$$

Verify via length form: ε = (A_0/A_i − 1) = 0.3083, so ε_t = ln(1.3083) = 0.2682. ✓

## 3.5 Comparative Table: True vs. Engineering Quantities

| Property | Engineering | True | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stress formula | F/A_0 | F/A_i | True > Engineering (in tension) |
| Strain formula | ΔL/L_0 | ln(L_i/L_0) | True < Engineering (in tension) |
| Curve behavior | Peaks at UTS, then drops | Monotonically increases | True curve has no peak |
| Use case | Design codes, yield strength | Forming analysis | KTU requires both |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory aid for KTU:** "True stress *increases* with strain; engineering stress *decreases* after UTS." Picture the true curve as a straight or rising power law — no maximum.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow: Engineering Stress vs. True Stress Evolution

The diagram below shows the **logical flow** of how engineering stress and true stress evolve during a tensile test, and how they interrelate through volume constancy.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Original Specimen L0, A0] --> B[Apply Tensile Load F]
    B --> C{Deformation Stage}
    C -->|Elastic| D[Small elongation, area unchanged]
    C -->|Plastic Uniform| E[Area reduces proportionally]
    C -->|Necking| F[Localized area reduction]
    E --> G[Volume Constancy A0L0 = AiLi]
    G --> H[Area Ratio Ai/A0 = L0/Li]
    H --> I[Engineering Stress sigma = F/A0]
    H --> J[True Stress sigmat = F/Ai]
    I --> K[Stress-Strain Curve Peaks at UTS]
    J --> L[True Stress-Strain Curve Monotonic Rise]
    L --> M[Hollomon Power Law sigmat = K epsn]
    M --> N[Used in Forging Rolling Extrusion Analysis]
    K --> O[Used in Design Codes Yield Strength]
    F --> P[Engineering Stress Drops Artificially]
    P --> Q[True Stress Continues to Rise]
    Q --> R[Fracture at Final Area Af]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: True Stress Concept Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Parameters]
        A1[Instantaneous Load F]
        A2[Instantaneous Area Ai]
    end
    subgraph CORE[True Stress Core]
        B1[Division Operation]
        B2[Volume Constancy Check]
        B3[Strain Hardening Law]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Outputs]
        C1[True Stress sigmat]
        C2[True Strain epst]
        C3[Forming Force]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    B1 --> C1
    A2 --> B2
    B2 --> C2
    C1 --> B3
    C2 --> B3
    B3 --> C3
```

## 4.3 Schematic Representation of Tensile Specimen Evolution

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Stage 1 Original: L0, A0] -->|Elastic Stretch| S2[Stage 2: L1, A1 = A0]
    S2 -->|Uniform Plastic| S3[Stage 3: L2, A2 A0]
    S3 -->|Necking| S4[Stage 4: L3, A3 at Neck Smaller]
    S4 -->|Fracture| S5[Stage 5: Lf, Af]
    S1 -.->|sigma = F/A0| ENG1[Engineering Stress Track]
    S3 -.->|sigmat = F/Ai| TRU1[True Stress Track]
    S4 -.->|sigmat = F/Aneck| TRU2[True Stress Peaks Higher]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU:** The true stress curve always lies **above** the engineering stress-strain curve in the plastic region. After UTS, they diverge sharply.

## 4.4 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied

- All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `A1`, `B2`, `S1`, `TRU1`) — no reserved words.
- No markdown bold/italic inside node labels — raw uppercase text only.
- All quoted labels use simple text without HTML tags.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Define True Stress and True Strain. Why are they preferred over engineering stress and strain in metal forming analysis?**

**Model Answer:**

True stress (σ_t) is defined as the instantaneous load (F) divided by the instantaneous (actual) cross-sectional area (A_i) of the specimen at the moment of loading. True strain (ε_t) is the natural logarithm of the ratio of instantaneous length to original length, ε_t = ln(L_i/L_0).

They are preferred in metal forming because:
1. Metal forming involves **large plastic deformations** with significant area changes — engineering quantities become inaccurate.
2. True stress represents the **actual flow stress** resisting deformation at each instant.
3. True strain is **additive** for multi-step deformations (unlike engineering strain).
4. The Hollomon power law (σ_t = K·ε_t^n) is built on true quantities for predictive forming analysis.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**State the principle of volume constancy in plastic deformation. Show that σ_t = σ(1 + ε).**

**Model Answer:**

**Principle of Volume Constancy:** During plastic deformation of an incompressible solid, the volume remains constant.

$$A_0 L_0 = A_i L_i \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{A_i}{A_0} = \frac{L_0}{L_i} = \frac{1}{1+\varepsilon}$$

**Derivation:**

$$\sigma_t = \frac{F}{A_i} = \frac{F}{A_0} \cdot \frac{A_0}{A_i} = \frac{F}{A_0}(1+\varepsilon) = \sigma(1+\varepsilon)$$

This relationship is valid in the **uniform plastic deformation region** (before necking begins).

---

## 5.2 PART B: Full-Question Choices (14 Marks Each)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** A mild steel tensile specimen has an original diameter of **20 mm** and gauge length of **50 mm**. It is loaded until the load reaches a maximum of **80 kN** (this corresponds to the UTS). At this instant, the diameter is reduced to **17.5 mm**.
Compute:
(i) Engineering stress at UTS
(ii) True stress at UTS
(iii) Engineering strain at UTS
(iv) True strain at UTS

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the Hollomon equation **σ_t = K·ε_t^n** for a material and explain its significance in metal forming. A metal has K = 600 MPa and n = 0.25. Find the true stress at a true strain of 0.4.

#### Model Solution for Question A

##### Part (a) Solution

**Given:**
- d_0 = 20 mm → A_0 = (π/4)(20)² = 314.159 mm²
- L_0 = 50 mm
- F = 80 kN = 80,000 N
- d_i = 17.5 mm → A_i = (π/4)(17.5)² = 240.528 mm²

**(i) Engineering stress at UTS:**

$$\sigma_{UTS} = \frac{F}{A_0} = \frac{80{,}000}{314.159} = 254.65 \text{ MPa}$$

**(ii) True stress at UTS:**

$$\sigma_t = \frac{F}{A_i} = \frac{80{,}000}{240.528} = 332.61 \text{ MPa}$$

**(iii) Engineering strain at UTS:**

Using volume constancy: L_i = A_0·L_0/A_i = (314.159 × 50)/240.528 = 65.30 mm

$$\varepsilon = \frac{L_i - L_0}{L_0} = \frac{65.30 - 50}{50} = 0.306$$

**(iv) True strain at UTS:**

$$\varepsilon_t = \ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_i}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{314.159}{240.528}\right) = \ln(1.3062) = 0.2672$$

> [!NOTE]
> Verification: σ_t should equal σ(1+ε) = 254.65 × 1.306 = 332.53 MPa ✓ (matches within rounding).

---

##### Part (b) Solution

**Derivation of Hollomon Equation:**

For many metals in the plastic region, experimental log σ_t vs log ε_t plots are linear. If the slope is n and intercept is log K, then:

$$\log \sigma_t = \log K + n \cdot \log \varepsilon_t \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma_t = K \cdot \varepsilon_t^{\,n}$$

**Significance in Metal Forming:**
- Predicts the **flow stress** at any given strain during forming.
- **K** indicates the material's strength level.
- **n** indicates **strain hardening capacity** — higher n = better formability (sheet metal drawing, deep drawing).
- Used to compute **forming loads** in forging, rolling, extrusion.

**Numerical Computation:**

Given: K = 600 MPa, n = 0.25, ε_t = 0.4

$$\sigma_t = 600 \times (0.4)^{0.25}$$
