Compute (0.4)^0.25: First (0.4)^0.5 = √0.4 = 0.6325; then (0.4)^0.25 = √0.6325 = 0.7953.

$$\sigma_t = 600 \times 0.7953 = 477.18 \text{ MPa}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Memory aid:** Hollomon constants can be found from log-log slope: n = slope, log K = intercept.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the difference between the **engineering stress-strain curve** and the **true stress-strain curve** with neat sketches. Why does the engineering curve show a drop after UTS while the true curve continues to rise?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A cylindrical specimen of aluminum (original length 80 mm, diameter 16 mm) is subjected to tension. At fracture, the final length is 96 mm and final diameter is 11.0 mm. Determine:
(i) Engineering stress at fracture
(ii) True stress at fracture
(iii) Engineering strain at fracture
(iv) True strain at fracture
(v) Percentage area reduction

#### Model Solution for Question B

##### Part (a) Solution

**Engineering Stress-Strain Curve:**
- Plots σ = F/A_0 versus ε = (L − L_0)/L_0
- Rises linearly in elastic region, then plastically until UTS.
- After UTS, the curve **drops** because the load F decreases during necking — but A_0 is kept fixed in the formula, creating the false impression of "softening."

**True Stress-Strain Curve:**
- Plots σ_t = F/A_i versus ε_t = ln(L_i/L_0).
- Uses instantaneous area, so no artificial drop.
- Continues to **rise** monotonically due to strain hardening.
- Follows Hollomon power law σ_t = K·ε_t^n in the plastic region.

**Why engineering curve drops after UTS:**
The cross-sectional area at the neck reduces rapidly, so the actual load F the specimen can support also reduces. Dividing this smaller F by the constant original area A_0 makes σ appear to fall — but the material is *not* softening. The true stress σ_t = F/A_i captures the real, increasing flow stress.

**Sketch (described):**
- X-axis: Strain (both curves share)
- Engineering curve: linear rise → yield → plastic rise to UTS peak → drop → fracture
- True curve: linear rise → yield → continuous rise (no peak) → fracture (higher final stress)

---

##### Part (b) Solution

**Given:**
- L_0 = 80 mm, d_0 = 16 mm → A_0 = (π/4)(16)² = 201.062 mm²
- L_f = 96 mm, d_f = 11.0 mm → A_f = (π/4)(11)² = 95.033 mm²
- Assume F_f at fracture ≈ load at UTS or measured. **(Use F_f = 45 kN for this problem if not given.)** 

For completeness, we'll express the area-based answers (i, iii, iv, v) without needing F, and (ii) will require F. Let's assume F_f = 38 kN.

**(i) Engineering stress at fracture:**

$$\sigma_f = \frac{F_f}{A_0} = \frac{38{,}000}{201.062} = 188.99 \text{ MPa}$$

**(ii) True stress at fracture:**

$$\sigma_{tf} = \frac{F_f}{A_f} = \frac{38{,}000}{95.033} = 399.89 \text{ MPa}$$

**(iii) Engineering strain at fracture:**

$$\varepsilon_f = \frac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} = \frac{96 - 80}{80} = 0.20$$

**(iv) True strain at fracture:**

$$\varepsilon_{tf} = \ln\left(\frac{L_f}{L_0}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{96}{80}\right) = \ln(1.2) = 0.1823$$

**Verify via area:** ε_t = ln(A_0/A_f) = ln(201.062/95.033) = ln(2.1157) = 0.7495

> [!WARNING]
> **Discrepancy Alert:** Length-based and area-based true strains differ when fracture involves non-uniform deformation (necking) — area-based is more accurate post-necking. The discrepancy here signals that necking occurred.

> [!NOTE]
> **Correction:** For post-necking analysis, use the area-based formula. ε_t = ln(2.1157) = 0.7495.

**(v) Percentage area reduction:**

$$\psi = \frac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100 = \frac{201.062 - 95.033}{201.062} \times 100$$

$$\psi = \frac{106.029}{201.062} \times 100 = 52.74\%$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Mixing units:** Using cm² with N instead of mm² with N (always keep load in N, area in mm² to get MPa directly).
> 2. **Forgetting ln in true strain:** Writing ε_t = L_i/L_0 instead of ln(L_i/L_0). This is a **3-mark killer**.
> 3. **Applying σ_t = σ(1+ε) past UTS:** This formula is **invalid** after necking begins.
> 4. **Forgetting volume constancy:** Many derivations fail because students don't recognize A_0L_0 = A_iL_i.
> 5. **Confusing ψ (area reduction) with strain:** ψ is dimensionless *reduction*, not strain. True strain via area is ε_t = −ln(1−ψ).
> 6. **Sign error:** Using A_i/A_0 instead of A_0/A_i in ε_t = ln(A_0/A_i) — true strain in tension must be **positive**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **True Stress Definition:** $\sigma_t = F/A_i$ — instantaneous load over instantaneous area. ✅
- **Engineering Stress:** $\sigma = F/A_0$ — original area (fails to capture geometry change). ✅
- **True Strain:** $\varepsilon_t = \ln(L_i/L_0) = \ln(1+\varepsilon)$ — additive across deformation steps. ✅
- **Volume Constancy:** $A_0 L_0 = A_i L_i$ — foundation of all true-stress derivations. ✅
- **Master Conversion:** $\sigma_t = \sigma(1+\varepsilon)$ — **valid only up to UTS**. ✅
- **Area-Based True Strain:** $\varepsilon_t = \ln(A_0/A_i) = -\ln(1-\psi)$ — best post-necking. ✅
- **Hollomon Equation:** $\sigma_t = K \cdot \varepsilon_t^{\,n}$ — describes true stress-strain in plastic region. ✅
- **K = strength coefficient (MPa)**, **n = strain hardening exponent (0 to 1).** ✅
- **Engineering curve peaks at UTS, then drops; true curve is monotonically increasing.** ✅
- **Forming operations (forging, rolling, extrusion) ALWAYS use true stress.** ✅
- **High n → high formability (good for sheet metal drawing).** ✅
- **True strain is always smaller than engineering strain in tension.** ✅
- **KTU Common Mistake:** Forgetting the `ln` in true strain; mixing up A_0 and A_i in formulas. ✅

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy:** When a problem gives load F, original area A_0, and asks for "stress during deformation," always compute σ_t = F/A_i using the *current* area. If only A_0 is given but a strain is mentioned, derive A_i from A_0/(1+ε) first, then compute σ_t.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: True Strain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET524 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** True Strain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In metal forming operations, the deformation experienced by a workpiece is not uniform across its geometry. When a cylindrical specimen of initial length $L_0$ is plastically deformed to a final length $L_f$, the conventional **engineering strain** ($e$ or $\varepsilon_{eng}$) assumes a linear, uniform distribution of deformation and is defined as:

$$e = \frac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0}$$

However, in actual plastic deformation processes, the material undergoes continuous geometric change. The instant an element of length $dL$ is stretched to $L + dL$, the ratio $dL/L$ represents the **instantaneous strain increment**. Summing (integrating) these infinitesimal strain increments from the original length to the final length yields the mathematically rigorous and physically accurate measure of deformation, known as the **True Strain** (also termed **Natural Strain** or **Logarithmic Strain**), denoted by $\varepsilon$ or $\varepsilon_T$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** True strain is defined as the natural logarithm of the ratio of instantaneous length to original length. It is the integral of instantaneous strain increments and is the only physically valid measure of large plastic deformations encountered in bulk metal forming.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine stretching a rubber band slowly while marking equally spaced ink dots on it. If you stretch it to **twice its original length**, the engineering strain says "50% increase" ($e = 1.0$), which feels misleading. Now, consider stretching the same rubber band step-by-step in tiny increments — first to 1.1 times, then to 1.2 times, then 1.3 times its original length, and so on. Each step is an instantaneous change relative to the *current* length, not the original.

**True strain is exactly this "step-by-step" or "memoryless" measure** — it always asks, "How much did the length change *right now* relative to *where it is right now*?" It is what you get when you multiply infinitely many tiny stretching steps. The natural logarithm captures this compounding effect, which is why true strain is indispensable in metal forming where strains routinely exceed 1.0 (i.e., 100% elongation).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Logarithms?** In nature, processes that compound multiplicatively (like population growth, radioactive decay, and progressive plastic elongation) are governed by exponential functions. The natural logarithm is the inverse of the exponential function and is therefore the natural mathematical tool to linearize such compounding phenomena.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Metrics

- The **base of the natural logarithm** is **Euler's number, $e \approx 2.71828$**.
- True strain is a **dimensionless quantity** (mm/mm or m/m — units cancel out).
- In tension, true strain is **positive**; in compression, it is **negative** by sign convention.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Logic Behind True Strain

To rigorously understand true strain, consider an elemental slice of material of instantaneous length $L$ that is plastically elongated to $L + dL$:

1. **Instantaneous Strain Increment:** The infinitesimal strain at the current state is:
   $$d\varepsilon = \frac{dL}{L}$$

2. **Assumption of Homogeneous Deformation:** Bulk metal forming processes (forging, extrusion, drawing) generally assume **homogeneous** strain distribution within the deformation zone, allowing the integral to be evaluated analytically.

3. **Integration Over the Entire Deformation History:** To obtain the *total* accumulated strain, integrate the increments from the initial state ($L_0$) to the final state ($L_f$):
   $$\varepsilon = \int_{L_0}^{L_f} \frac{dL}{L}$$

4. **Evaluation of the Integral:** The integral of $1/L$ is the natural logarithm, yielding:
   $$\varepsilon = \ln\left(\frac{L_f}{L_0}\right)$$

5. **Sign Convention:** Elongation gives positive $\varepsilon$; compression gives negative $\varepsilon$.

### 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' — Engineering Strain vs. True Strain

| Aspect | Engineering Strain ($e$) | True Strain ($\varepsilon$) |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition basis** | Change relative to *original* length | Change relative to *instantaneous* length |
| **Mathematical form** | $e = \dfrac{L_f - L_0}{L_0}$ | $\varepsilon = \ln\left(\dfrac{L_f}{L_0}\right)$ |
| **Validity range** | Only accurate for small strains ($e < 0.1$) | Valid for *any* magnitude of strain |
| **Physical accuracy** | Inaccurate for large plastic deformation | Physically rigorous for all deformation |
| **Volumetric constancy** | Not inherently built-in | Naturally couples with volume conservation |
| **Use in KTU problems** | Quick estimates, elastic region | Plastic flow analysis, forming loads, FEA |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in nearly every Module 1 KTU question.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\varepsilon = \ln\left(\dfrac{L_f}{L_0}\right)$ | True strain (axial) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\varepsilon = \ln(1 + e)$ | True strain from engineering strain | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z = 0$ | Incompressibility (volume constancy) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\varepsilon_h = -\left(\varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y\right)$ | For axisymmetric deformation, true hoop strain | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\varepsilon_h = -\dfrac{1}{2}\varepsilon_x$ | For *axisymmetric* (e.g., wire drawing) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $L_f = L_0 \cdot e^{\varepsilon}$ | Final length from true strain | m or mm |
| 7 | $\ln\left(\dfrac{A_0}{A_f}\right)$ | True strain from area ratio (using volume constancy) | dimensionless |

**Critical Engineering Utility:**
- True strain is the *only* valid input to **constitutive flow curves** (e.g., $\sigma = K\varepsilon^n$) used to compute forming loads in forging, extrusion, rolling, and drawing.
- It is required for the **Levy-Mises** and **Prandtl-Reuss** flow rules used in plasticity analysis.
- It is fundamental to **Forming Limit Diagrams (FLD)** and **Fracture Strain** calculations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the True Strain Formula

**Starting Premise:** Consider a tensile specimen (or a deforming workpiece element) of original gauge length $L_0$ that is plastically stretched to a final length $L_f$.

**Step 1 — Define an Elementary Strain Increment:**
At any intermediate instant when the current length is $L$, an additional infinitesimal elongation $dL$ produces the elementary true strain increment:

$$d\varepsilon = \frac{dL}{L}$$

**Step 2 — Justify the Form of the Increment:**
For small increments, the engineering-strain-like ratio $dL/L$ *is* the true strain increment, because it is measured against the *current* length, not the original. This is the foundational postulate of natural strain.

**Step 3 — Set Up the Definite Integral:**
To find the *total* true strain from the initial to the final state, integrate the increment from $L = L_0$ to $L = L_f$:

$$\varepsilon = \int_{L_0}^{L_f} d\varepsilon = \int_{L_0}^{L_f} \frac{dL}{L}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the Integral:**
The antiderivative of $\dfrac{1}{L}$ with respect to $L$ is $\ln \vert L \vert$. Applying the limits:

$$\varepsilon = \Big[\ln \vert L \vert\Big]_{L_0}^{L_f} = \ln(L_f) - \ln(L_0) = \ln\!\left(\frac{L_f}{L_0}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Final Closed-Form Expression:**

$$\boxed{\varepsilon = \ln\!\left(\frac{L_f}{L_0}\right)}$$

This is the **primary KTU result** for true strain under uniaxial tension.

**Step 6 — Derive the Engineering-to-True Conversion:**
Starting from $e = \dfrac{L_f - L_0}{L_0}$, rearrange to find $L_f$ in terms of $L_0$ and $e$:

$$L_f = L_0(1 + e)$$

Substitute this into the true strain expression:

$$\varepsilon = \ln\!\left(\frac{L_0(1 + e)}{L_0}\right) = \ln(1 + e)$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\varepsilon = \ln(1 + e)}$$

**Step 7 — Derive the Area-Ratio Form (using Volume Constancy):**
In plastic deformation of an incompressible material, volume is conserved:

$$A_0 \cdot L_0 = A_f \cdot L_f \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \frac{L_f}{L_0} = \frac{A_0}{A_f}$$

Substitute into the true strain equation:

$$\boxed{\varepsilon = \ln\!\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right)}$$

This is exceptionally useful in metal forming problems where cross-sectional areas are easier to measure (or are given) than lengths.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A cylindrical billet in an extrusion press is reduced in length from $L_0 = 100 \text{ mm}$ to $L_f = 35 \text{ mm}$ under plastic compression. Calculate: (a) the engineering strain, (b) the true strain, and (c) verify the sign convention.

**Solution:**

**(a) Engineering Strain:**

$$e = \frac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} = \frac{35 - 100}{100} = -0.65 \; \text{or} \; -65\%$$

**(b) True Strain:**

$$\varepsilon = \ln\!\left(\frac{L_f}{L_0}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{35}{100}\right) = \ln(0.35) = -1.0498$$

**(c) Sign Verification:** The negative sign correctly indicates **compressive deformation**, consistent with the KTU sign convention (tensile positive, compressive negative).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Note that for the *same* physical deformation, the engineering strain ($|e| = 0.65$) and the true strain ($|\varepsilon| = 1.05$) differ substantially — by over 60%. This is precisely why KTU problems on metal forming mandate true strain as the correct measure.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Strain Level | Engineering $e$ | True $\varepsilon = \ln(1+e)$ | % Difference |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0.01 (1% elongation) | 0.0100 | 0.00995 | 0.5% |
| 0.10 (10% elongation) | 0.1000 | 0.0953 | 4.7% |
| 0.50 (50% elongation) | 0.5000 | 0.4055 | 18.9% |
| 1.00 (100% elongation) | 1.0000 | 0.6931 | 30.7% |
| 2.00 (200% elongation) | 2.0000 | 1.0986 | 45.1% |
| 4.00 (400% elongation) | 4.0000 | 1.6094 | 59.8% |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Takeaway:** The divergence grows exponentially. For any plastic strain exceeding ~10%, true strain *must* be used in forming analysis.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow: From Incremental Strain to True Strain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Initial Workpiece of Length L0"] --> B["Apply Infinitesimal Stretch dL"]
    B --> C["Current Length Becomes L plus dL"]
    C --> D["Compute Incremental Strain d epsilon equals dL over L"]
    D --> E["Repeat Step Incrementally until L reaches Lf"]
    E --> F["Integrate d epsilon from L0 to Lf"]
    F --> G["Evaluate the Natural Logarithm Integral"]
    G --> H["Final Result epsilon equals ln of Lf over L0"]
    H --> I["Equivalent Area Form epsilon equals ln of A0 over Af"]
    I --> J["Apply to Forming Load Analysis via Flow Curve"]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: True Strain in Metal Forming Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Data Layer]
        L0["Original Length L0"]
        LF["Final Length Lf"]
        A0["Original Area A0"]
        AF["Final Area Af"]
        E["Engineering Strain e"]
    end

    subgraph COMPUTE[True Strain Computation Engine]
        MOD1["Length Ratio Method ln of Lf over L0"]
        MOD2["Area Ratio Method ln of A0 over Af"]
        MOD3["Engineering Conversion ln of 1 plus e"]
        VAL["Cross-Validation Block"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Layer for Forming Analysis]
        FLOW["Flow Stress sigma equals K epsilon power n"]
        LOAD["Forming Load Computation"]
        FLD["Forming Limit Diagram Entry"]
        ANISO["Anisotropy and Failure Analysis"]
    end

    L0 --> MOD1
    LF --> MOD1
    A0 --> MOD2
    AF --> MOD2
    E --> MOD3
    MOD1 --> VAL
    MOD2 --> VAL
    MOD3 --> VAL
    VAL --> FLOW
    VAL --> LOAD
    VAL --> FLD
    VAL --> ANISO
```

### 4.3 Decision Logic: When to Use Engineering vs. True Strain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["Identify the Problem Context"] --> Q1{"Is the Strain Less Than 10 Percent?"}
    Q1 -- Yes --> USE_E["Use Engineering Strain e for Simplicity"]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{"Is the Process Plastic Forming Forging Rolling Extrusion Drawing?"}
    Q2 -- Yes --> USE_T["Use True Strain epsilon equals ln of Lf over L0"]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{"Are Flow Stress Curves sigma versus epsilon Required?"}
    Q3 -- Yes --> USE_T
    Q3 -- No --> USE_E
    USE_T --> FINAL["Apply in Constitutive Equation sigma equals K epsilon power n"]
    USE_E --> FINAL2["Apply in Elastic Hooke Law for Small Deformation"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Pattern]**
**Define true strain. Why is it preferred over engineering strain in metal forming analysis?**

**Model Answer:**
True strain is defined as the natural logarithm of the ratio of the instantaneous length to the original length of a deforming element, given by $\varepsilon = \ln(L_f / L_0)$. It measures the accumulated instantaneous strain increments $d\varepsilon = dL/L$ integrated from the initial to the final configuration.

It is preferred over engineering strain in metal forming because:
1. Metal forming involves large plastic deformations (often $> 0.5$), where engineering strain becomes physically inaccurate.
2. True strain is the mathematically rigorous measure consistent with the assumption of **homogeneous deformation** and **incompressibility** (volume constancy).
3. It is the *only* valid input to the constitutive flow equation $\sigma = K\varepsilon^n$, which is used to compute forming loads.

*(3 marks — definition 1 mark, three reasons 1 mark each, or proportional split per marking rubric)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary Pattern]**
**A copper rod of initial length 200 mm is drawn through a die to a final length of 500 mm. Calculate the true strain experienced by the rod.**

**Model Answer:**
Given: $L_0 = 200 \text{ mm}$, $L_f = 500 \text{ mm}$.

Apply the true strain formula:

$$\varepsilon = \ln\!\left(\frac{L_f}{L_0}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{500}{200}\right) = \ln(2.5)$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon = 0.9163 \text{ (dimensionless)}}$$

The positive value confirms tensile elongation, as expected in a wire drawing operation.

*(3 marks — formula 1 mark, substitution 1 mark, final answer with units 1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern, Dec 2024 Expected Style]**

**Q.A (a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for true strain starting from the definition of an elementary strain increment $d\varepsilon = dL/L$. Show clearly that the integral reduces to the natural logarithm of the length ratio.

**Q.A (b) [7 Marks]** In a forging operation, a cylindrical steel billet of original diameter 50 mm and length 150 mm is compressed (under volume constancy) to a final diameter of 30 mm. Calculate the true strain along the axial and hoop directions. Take the lateral direction (width) to remain unchanged.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A (a):**

**Step 1:** State the elementary increment:

$$d\varepsilon = \frac{dL}{L}$$

**Step 2:** Set up the integral from the original length $L_0$ to the final length $L_f$:

$$\varepsilon = \int_{L_0}^{L_f} \frac{dL}{L}$$

**Step 3:** Integrate (antiderivative is $\ln L$):

$$\varepsilon = \Big[\ln \vert L \vert\Big]_{L_0}^{L_f}$$

**Step 4:** Apply the limits:

$$\varepsilon = \ln(L_f) - \ln(L_0) = \ln\!\left(\frac{L_f}{L_0}\right)$$

**Step 5:** Note the conversion to engineering strain form by substituting $L_f = L_0(1 + e)$:

$$\varepsilon = \ln(1 + e)$$

**Step 6:** Discuss physical interpretation and limitation (engineering strain valid only for $e \ll 1$).

*(7 marks: increment definition 1, integral setup 2, evaluation 2, conversion 1, physical interpretation 1)*

---

**Model Solution for Q.A (b):**

**Given:**
- $D_0 = 50 \text{ mm} \Rightarrow A_0 = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(50)^2 = 1963.5 \text{ mm}^2$
- $D_f = 30 \text{ mm} \Rightarrow A_f = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(30)^2 = 706.86 \text{ mm}^2$
- $L_0 = 150 \text{ mm}$, lateral dimension unchanged.

**Step 1 — Apply volume constancy to find final length $L_f$:**

$$A_0 L_0 = A_f L_f \quad\Longrightarrow\quad L_f = \frac{A_0 L_0}{A_f} = \frac{1963.5 \times 150}{706.86} = 416.67 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate axial true strain $\varepsilon_z$:**

$$\varepsilon_z = \ln\!\left(\frac{L_f}{L_0}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{416.67}{150}\right) = \ln(2.778) = 1.0217$$

**Step 3 — Calculate hoop (circumferential) true strain $\varepsilon_h$:**

Using the area ratio equivalence (area change is driven by diameter change, not length, since length is in the axial direction):

$$\varepsilon_h = \ln\!\left(\frac{D_f}{D_0}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{30}{50}\right) = \ln(0.6) = -0.5108$$

**Step 4 — Verify with the incompressibility relation** $\varepsilon_z + \varepsilon_r + \varepsilon_h = 0$:

For an axisymmetric cylinder, the radial strain equals the hoop strain: $\varepsilon_r = \varepsilon_h = -0.5108$.

$$1.0217 + (-0.5108) + (-0.5108) = 0.0001 \approx 0 \;\checkmark$$

**Step 5 — Final Answers:**
- Axial true strain: $\varepsilon_z = +1.022$ (tensile along the forging direction — material flows axially).
- Hoop true strain: $\varepsilon_h = -0.511$ (compressive circumferentially — diameter shrinks).

*(7 marks: volume constancy application 2, axial strain 2, hoop strain 2, verification 1)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)**

**[KTU University Exam – Sessional Pattern, May 2024 Expected Style]**

**Q.B (a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat sketch the concept of true strain as the limit of accumulated incremental strains. Discuss why the natural logarithm appears naturally in the final expression.

**Q.B (b) [7 Marks]** A brass wire is cold-drawn from an initial diameter of 8 mm to a final diameter of 5 mm. Using volume constancy: (i) calculate the true strain, (ii) if the original gauge length was 200 mm, find the final length, and (iii) compute the engineering strain and compare with the true strain.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B (a):**

**Step 1:** Describe an incremental plastic deformation scenario — a workpiece element is stretched in infinitesimal steps, each producing a strain increment $d\varepsilon = dL/L$.

**Step 2:** The total deformation is the *sum* (integral) of these increments, which mathematically yields a logarithmic expression.

**Step 3:** Justify the logarithm — when a quantity changes by a factor that depends on its *current* value (rather than its initial value), the resulting accumulation is governed by an exponential, whose inverse is the natural logarithm.

**Step 4:** Mention the limit concept:

$$\varepsilon = \lim_{n \to \infty} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{\Delta L_i}{L_{i-1}} = \int_{L_0}^{L_f} \frac{dL}{L} = \ln\!\left(\frac{L_f}{L_0}\right)$$

**Step 5:** Conclude by contrasting with engineering strain, which sums the same increments but always uses the *original* length as the denominator.

*(7 marks: incremental concept 2, integral 2, logarithm justification 2, conclusion 1)*

---

**Model Solution for Q.B (b):**

**Given:** $D_0 = 8 \text{ mm}$, $D_f = 5 \text{ mm}$, $L_0 = 200 \text{ mm}$.

**(i) True Strain:**
Using the area-ratio form (volume constancy $\Rightarrow A_0 L_0 = A_f L_f$):

$$\varepsilon = \ln\!\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{D_0^2}{D_f^2}\right) = 2\ln\!\left(\frac{D_0}{D_f}\right)$$

$$\varepsilon = 2\ln\!\left(\frac{8}{5}\right) = 2\ln(1.6) = 2 \times 0.4700 = 0.9400$$

**(ii) Final Length:**

$$L_f = L_0 \cdot e^{\varepsilon} = 200 \cdot e^{0.9400} = 200 \times 2.5600 = 512.0 \text{ mm}$$

**Alternative check via area ratio:**

$$L_f = L_0 \cdot \frac{A_0}{A_f} = 200 \times \frac{64}{25} = 200 \times 2.56 = 512.0 \text{ mm} \;\checkmark$$

**(iii) Engineering Strain:**

$$e = \frac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} = \frac{512 - 200}{200} = 1.56 \;\text{or}\; 156\%$$

**Comparison:** $\varepsilon = 0.94$ vs. $e = 1.56$. The engineering strain *overestimates* the true strain by approximately 66%, confirming that true strain must be used for accurate forming analysis.

*(7 marks: true strain calculation 2, final length 2, engineering strain 1, comparison 2)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting the sign convention** — Students often report the magnitude only and lose 1 mark. Always state that compression gives negative true strain.
> 2. **Using the wrong length** — In wire drawing, the wire *lengthens*; in forging, it *shortens*. Read the question carefully to determine which dimension is $L_0$ and which is $L_f$.
> 3. **Mixing up $A_0/A_f$ vs. $A_f/A_0$** — True strain is $\ln(A_0/A_f)$, NOT $\ln(A_f/A_0)$. Reversing these gives a sign error and is the most common single mistake.
> 4. **Not verifying with volume constancy** — Always cross-check your axial and lateral true strains sum to zero (within rounding). Examiners reward this verification step.
> 5. **Quoting strain without units** — True strain is dimensionless; you may still write "(dimensionless)" or "no units" to demonstrate full rigor.
> 6. **Using engineering strain in flow stress equations** — Substituting $e$ directly into $\sigma = K\varepsilon^n$ is a guaranteed loss of 2–3 marks. Always convert to true strain first.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** True strain is the natural logarithm of the ratio of instantaneous to original length: $\varepsilon = \ln(L_f / L_0)$.
- **Physical Meaning:** It is the *accumulated instantaneous* strain increment, integrating $d\varepsilon = dL/L$ from $L_0$ to $L_f$.
- **Conversion Identity:** $\varepsilon = \ln(1 + e)$ — the bridge between engineering and true strain.
- **Area Form (Volume Constancy):** $\varepsilon = \ln(A_0 / A_f)$ — extremely useful in forming problems.
- **Sign Convention:** Tensile deformation $\Rightarrow$ positive $\varepsilon$; compressive $\Rightarrow$ negative $\varepsilon$.
- **Incompressibility Check:** For 3D deformation, $\varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z = 0$.
- **Final Length Formula:** $L_f = L_0 \cdot e^{\varepsilon}$ — useful for reverse calculations.
- **Why It Matters in Metal Forming:** True strain is the *only* valid measure for large plastic deformations and is the input to flow stress equations $\sigma = K\varepsilon^n$ used in forging, rolling, extrusion, and drawing load calculations.
- **Threshold Rule of Thumb:** For strains below ~10% (small elastic or mildly plastic deformation), engineering strain is acceptable. Beyond 10%, always use true strain.
- **KTU's Most-Tested Variants:** (1) Length-ratio form, (2) Area-ratio form, (3) Conversion from engineering to true, (4) Sign convention in compression, (5) Application to flow stress for load computation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Yield Criteria for Ductile Materials (Von Mises and Tresca)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET524 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Yield Criteria for Ductile Materials (Von Mises and Tresca)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the mechanics of metal forming, a **Yield Criterion** (also called a yield condition or yield locus) is a mathematical relationship that defines the limiting combination of stresses at which a ductile material transitions from the elastic to the plastic state. For an isotropic ductile metal, the yield criterion is expressed as a function of the three principal stresses $\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \sigma_3$ (with $\sigma_1 \ge \sigma_2 \ge \sigma_3$ by convention).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A yield criterion specifies the condition under which plastic deformation begins. For ductile metals, it is independent of the hydrostatic (mean) stress component, depending only on the **deviatoric stress** (the part responsible for shape change).

The two classical yield criteria prescribed in the KTU Module 1 syllabus are:

1. **Tresca Yield Criterion (1864):** Based on the **Maximum Shear Stress Theory** — yielding begins when the maximum shear stress in the material reaches a critical value equal to the shear yield stress in a uniaxial tension test.

2. **Von Mises Yield Criterion (1913):** Based on the **Distortion Energy Theory** (also called the octahedral shear stress theory) — yielding begins when the elastic distortion energy stored in the material per unit volume reaches a critical value equal to that stored at the yield point in uniaxial tension.

Both criteria are **phenomenological**, meaning they are derived from observed mechanical behaviour of ductile metals rather than from atomic-level theory. They reduce to identical predictions under uniaxial tension and pure shear is the principal state where they diverge.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Inflating Balloon"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture:** Imagine a fully inflated rectangular balloon that is at the verge of bursting.
> - **Tresca** is like saying *the balloon bursts as soon as the largest single "stretch difference" across it exceeds a threshold.* Only the maximum stress difference matters.
> - **Von Mises** is like saying *the balloon bursts when the overall "distortion energy" — a combined measure of all three stretch differences — reaches a critical total.* It considers all the shear-like distortions acting together.

In simpler terms, **Tresca** watches one number (the worst shear), while **Von Mises** watches the *total* distortion energy. Since metals deform by atomic slip on multiple planes simultaneously, Von Mises (which averages over all slip systems) is generally more accurate, while Tresca is mathematically simpler and *always conservative* (predicts yielding sooner).

## 1.3 Key Engineering Constants (to be memorized)

- **Uniaxial Yield Stress:** $\sigma_Y$ (also written $\sigma_0$, $Y$, or $S_y$) — measured from a standard tensile test.
- **Pure Shear Yield Stress:** $k$ (or $\tau_Y$) — the shear stress at which the material yields.
- **Critical Relationships:**
  - For **Tresca**: $k = \dfrac{\sigma_Y}{2}$
  - For **Von Mises**: $k = \dfrac{\sigma_Y}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.577\,\sigma_Y$

These two constants are fundamental — every KTU numerical will involve one of them.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Logical Foundation of Yield Criteria

The development of any yield criterion rests on a few **postulated axioms** (originally proposed by **Bridgman, Drucker, and Hill**):

- **Isotropy:** The yield behaviour is the same in all directions (valid for annealed, fine-grained ductile metals).
- **Independence of Hydrostatic Pressure:** Yielding is unaffected by an equal superimposed hydrostatic stress $\sigma_m = (\sigma_1+\sigma_2+\sigma_3)/3$. This is **strictly true only for fully dense, non-porous ductile metals** (not for soils, concrete, or powder compacts — which need a separate criterion like Drucker–Prager or Gurson).
- **Smoothness & Convexity:** The yield surface in principal stress space is a closed, smooth, convex surface enclosing the elastic region.
- **Symmetry:** The yield surface is symmetric about the hydrostatic axis $\sigma_1=\sigma_2=\sigma_3$.

Any valid yield criterion must satisfy these axioms. Both Tresca and Von Mises do.

## 2.2 Why Two Criteria? The "Why" and "How"

- **Tresca** captures the **physical** fact that slip (shear failure) initiates on a plane of **maximum shear stress**. It is intuitive and gives a closed-form bound used in limit analysis of metal forming (e.g., upper-bound slip-line field theory).
- **Von Mises** captures the **energetic** fact that a small volume element yields when the energy tied up in distorting its shape (excluding the part that just changes its volume) reaches a critical level. It is mathematically smoother (no sharp edges in the yield surface) and matches **polycrystalline metal experiments** better (typically within 10–15%).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table must be memorized.** Every Part-B numerical in KTU Module 1 is solved by applying one of these equations.

| # | Concept | Mathematical Form | Remarks |
|---|---------|-------------------|---------|
| 1 | Tresca — General 3D form | $\max\bigl[\vert \sigma_1-\sigma_2\vert,\ \vert \sigma_2-\sigma_3\vert,\ \vert \sigma_3-\sigma_1\vert\bigr] = \sigma_Y$ | Always conservative |
| 2 | Tresca — General 3D simplified | $\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = \sigma_Y$ (with $\sigma_1 \ge \sigma_2 \ge \sigma_3$) | The "main" inequality |
| 3 | Tresca — Pure shear $\Rightarrow$ | $\tau = \dfrac{\sigma_Y}{2} = k$ | **Important numerical** |
| 4 | Von Mises — General 3D form | $(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2 = 2\sigma_Y^{\,2}$ | Derived from $J_2 = \dfrac{\sigma_Y^{\,2}}{3}$ |
| 5 | Von Mises — Pure shear $\Rightarrow$ | $\tau = \dfrac{\sigma_Y}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.577\,\sigma_Y$ | $\sqrt{3}\approx 1.732$ |
| 6 | Von Mises — Plane stress ($\sigma_3=0$) | $\sigma_1^{\,2} - \sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2^{\,2} = \sigma_Y^{\,2}$ | Ellipse in $\sigma_1$–$\sigma_2$ plane |
| 7 | Tresca — Plane stress hexagon | Six linear inequalities: $\vert\sigma_1\vert=\sigma_Y$, $\vert\sigma_2\vert=\sigma_Y$, $\sigma_1-\sigma_2=\pm\sigma_Y$ | Hexagon inscribed in the ellipse |
| 8 | Octahedral shear stress | $\tau_{oct} = \dfrac{1}{3}\sqrt{(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2+(\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2+(\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2}$ | Equivalent in Von Mises to $\tau_{oct} = \dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{3}\sigma_Y$ |
| 9 | Deviatoric stress tensor | $s_{ij} = \sigma_{ij} - \sigma_m\,\delta_{ij}$ | $\sigma_m = \dfrac{\sigma_{ii}}{3}$ |
| 10 | Second deviatoric invariant | $J_2 = \dfrac{1}{2}\,s_{ij}\,s_{ij}$ | Von Mises ⇒ $J_2 = k^2$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **K-T Notation Reminder (no vertical bars in tables):** Throughout these notes, $\vert x \vert$ and $\vert \sigma \vert$ are written using `\vert` and `\mid` to avoid breaking the markdown table layout. **In your answer book, write them as absolute value signs as usual.**

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Sheet Metal Forming:** Von Mises defines the **Forming Limit Diagram (FLD)** boundary — when a point in strain space crosses the FLD curve, necking and failure occur. Tresca gives the **simpler bound** for conservative die design.
- **Forging & Extrusion:** Upper-bound and slip-line field analyses use the Tresca criterion for tractable limit-load calculations.
- **Finite Element Analysis (FEA):** Every modern elasto-plastic FEA code (ANSYS, Abaqus, DEFORM, AutoForm) requires a yield criterion. Von Mises is the default for ductile metals; Tresca is selected for safety-critical, conservative assessments.
- **Pressure Vessel & Piping Codes (ASME, EN 13445):** Tresca-based maximum shear stress is used historically; Von Mises-based equivalent stress (also called the Von Mises or Huber stress) is now standard.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Tresca Yield Criterion (3D Form)

### Step 1 — Start with the Uniaxial Tension Reference

In a uniaxial tensile test, the principal stresses are:

$$\sigma_1 = \sigma_Y,\quad \sigma_2 = 0,\quad \sigma_3 = 0$$

The maximum shear stress on any plane is half the maximum principal-stress difference:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2} = \frac{\sigma_Y - 0}{2} = \frac{\sigma_Y}{2} = k$$

This defines the material constant $k$ (the shear yield stress).

### Step 2 — Postulate the Generalisation

Tresca proposed that yielding begins whenever the **largest** of the three principal-shear stresses equals the critical value $k$. The three principal shear stresses are:

$$\tau_{12} = \frac{\vert \sigma_1 - \sigma_2 \vert}{2},\quad \tau_{23} = \frac{\vert \sigma_2 - \sigma_3 \vert}{2},\quad \tau_{31} = \frac{\vert \sigma_3 - \sigma_1 \vert}{2}$$

### Step 3 — Equate the Maximum to $k$

Setting $\tau_{max} = k$:

$$\frac{1}{2}\,\max\!\left[\, \vert \sigma_1 - \sigma_2 \vert,\ \vert \sigma_2 - \sigma_3 \vert,\ \vert \sigma_3 - \sigma_1 \vert \,\right] = k = \frac{\sigma_Y}{2}$$

Multiplying both sides by 2 gives the compact Tresca condition:

$$\max\!\left[\, \vert \sigma_1 - \sigma_2 \vert,\ \vert \sigma_2 - \sigma_3 \vert,\ \vert \sigma_3 - \sigma_1 \vert \,\right] = \sigma_Y$$

> When the principal stresses are ordered $\sigma_1 \ge \sigma_2 \ge \sigma_3$, only the third absolute term $\vert \sigma_3 - \sigma_1 \vert$ can be the largest, so this reduces to the **canonical 2D form**:
> $$\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = \sigma_Y \quad \text{(Tresca, principal-stress ordered)}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Von Mises Yield Criterion (3D Form)

### Step 1 — Distortion Energy Concept

The strain energy per unit volume stored in an elastic body under a 3D stress state splits into two parts:

- **Volumetric (dilatational) energy** $U_v$ — associated with uniform compression/expansion (no shape change).
- **Distortion (shape-change) energy** $U_d$ — associated with the deviatoric stress that *distorts* the element.

Von Mises hypothesised that **yielding begins when $U_d$ reaches the value attained at yielding in a uniaxial tensile test**.

### Step 2 — Express $U_d$ in Principal Stresses

The deviatoric principal stresses are:

$$s_1 = \sigma_1 - \sigma_m,\quad s_2 = \sigma_2 - \sigma_m,\quad s_3 = \sigma_3 - \sigma_m$$

where the mean stress is $\sigma_m = (\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 + \sigma_3)/3$.

The distortion energy density is:

$$U_d = \frac{1+\nu}{6E}\Big[(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2\Big]$$

(derivation uses $U_d = U_{total} - U_v$ and elastic stiffness relations).

### Step 3 — Evaluate in Uniaxial Tension at Yield

Substitute $\sigma_1 = \sigma_Y$, $\sigma_2 = \sigma_3 = 0$:

$$U_{d,\text{ref}} = \frac{1+\nu}{6E}\Big[(\sigma_Y)^2 + (0)^2 + (-\sigma_Y)^2\Big] = \frac{1+\nu}{6E}\,(2\sigma_Y^{\,2}) = \frac{1+\nu}{3E}\,\sigma_Y^{\,2}$$

### Step 4 — Equate General to Reference

Setting $U_d = U_{d,\text{ref}}$ and cancelling the common factor $\dfrac{1+\nu}{6E}$:

$$(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2 = 2\,\sigma_Y^{\,2}$$

> This is the **Von Mises yield criterion in 3D principal-stress form**. It can be written more compactly using the second deviatoric invariant $J_2$:
> $$J_2 = \frac{1}{6}\Big[(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2+(\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2+(\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2\Big] = \frac{\sigma_Y^{\,2}}{3} = k^2$$

## 3.3 Specialisation to Pure Shear

**Setup:** In pure shear, the principal stresses are $\sigma_1 = \tau$, $\sigma_2 = 0$, $\sigma_3 = -\tau$.

### Tresca under Pure Shear

Apply $\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = \sigma_Y$:

$$\tau - (-\tau) = \sigma_Y \;\Rightarrow\; 2\tau = \sigma_Y \;\Rightarrow\; \boxed{\tau = \frac{\sigma_Y}{2} = k}$$

### Von Mises under Pure Shear

Substitute into the 3D equation:

$$(\tau-0)^2 + (0-(-\tau))^2 + (-\tau-\tau)^2 = 2\sigma_Y^{\,2}$$

Expand term by term:

$$\tau^2 + \tau^2 + 4\tau^2 = 2\sigma_Y^{\,2}$$

$$6\,\tau^2 = 2\,\sigma_Y^{\,2}$$

$$\boxed{\tau = \frac{\sigma_Y}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.577\,\sigma_Y}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Result:** For the **same** uniaxial yield stress $\sigma_Y$, the Von Mises criterion predicts a **higher** pure-shear yield ($\approx 15.5\%$ higher than Tresca). This is why experimental torsion tests on ductile metals confirm Von Mises more closely.

## 3.4 Plane Stress Yield Loci ($\sigma_3 = 0$)

Setting $\sigma_3 = 0$ in each criterion:

### Von Mises — Plane Stress Derivation

Start from the 3D form:

$$(\sigma_1-0)^2 + (0-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_1)^2 = 2\sigma_Y^{\,2}$$

Expand and simplify:

$$\sigma_1^{\,2} + \sigma_2^{\,2} + (\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 = 2\sigma_Y^{\,2}$$

$$2\sigma_1^{\,2} + 2\sigma_2^{\,2} - 2\sigma_1\sigma_2 = 2\sigma_Y^{\,2}$$

Dividing by 2:

$$\boxed{\sigma_1^{\,2} - \sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2^{\,2} = \sigma_Y^{\,2}}$$

This is an **ellipse** in the $\sigma_1$–$\sigma_2$ plane, with major axis along $\sigma_1 = \sigma_2$ and intercepts on the axes at $\sigma_1 = \pm\sigma_Y$ and $\sigma_2 = \pm\sigma_Y$.

### Tresca — Plane Stress Derivation

The plane-stress Tresca surface is a **regular hexagon** inscribed inside the Von Mises ellipse. Its six sides are:

- $\sigma_1 = \sigma_Y$ (uniaxial tension along axis-1)
- $\sigma_1 = -\sigma_Y$ (uniaxial compression along axis-1)
- $\sigma_2 = \sigma_Y$ (uniaxial tension along axis-2)
- $\sigma_2 = -\sigma_Y$ (uniaxial compression along axis-2)
- $\sigma_1 - \sigma_2 = \sigma_Y$ (positive quadrant biaxial shear)
- $\sigma_1 - \sigma_2 = -\sigma_Y$ (negative quadrant biaxial shear)

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Step-by-Step)

> **Problem:** A steel shaft carries an axial tensile stress $\sigma_x = 200$ MPa and a torsional shear stress $\tau_{xy} = 80$ MPa. The uniaxial yield stress of the material is $\sigma_Y = 320$ MPa. Determine the safety factor using both Tresca and Von Mises.

### Step 1 — Compute Principal Stresses (2D stress)

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^{\,2}}$$

With $\sigma_y = 0$:

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{200}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{200}{2}\right)^2 + 80^2}$$

$$\sigma_{1,2} = 100 \pm \sqrt{100^2 + 80^2} = 100 \pm \sqrt{10000 + 6400} = 100 \pm \sqrt{16400}$$

$$\sigma_{1,2} = 100 \pm 128.06\ \text{MPa}$$

So $\sigma_1 = 228.06$ MPa, $\sigma_2 = -28.06$ MPa, and $\sigma_3 = 0$ (plane stress).

### Step 2 — Apply Tresca

Order the principal stresses: $228.06 \ge 0 \ge -28.06$, so $\sigma_1 = 228.06$, $\sigma_3 = -28.06$.

$$\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = 228.06 - (-28.06) = 256.12\ \text{MPa}$$

Tresca safety factor:

$$N_T = \frac{\sigma_Y}{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3} = \frac{320}{256.12} \approx 1.249$$

### Step 3 — Apply Von Mises

Compute the equivalent Von Mises stress in plane stress:

$$\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\sigma_x^{\,2} + 3\,\tau_{xy}^{\,2}} = \sqrt{200^2 + 3(80)^2} = \sqrt{40000 + 19200} = \sqrt{59200}$$

$$\sigma_{VM} = 243.31\ \text{MPa}$$

Von Mises safety factor:

$$N_{VM} = \frac{\sigma_Y}{\sigma_{VM}} = \frac{320}{243.31} \approx 1.315$$

### Step 4 — Compare and Conclude

- **Tresca** $N_T \approx 1.25$ (conservative, smaller safety factor).
- **Von Mises** $N_{VM} \approx 1.32$ (larger safety factor, more realistic for ductile steel).
- The Von Mises prediction is approximately **5.3% higher** than Tresca — well within the typical 10–15% spread between the two.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Student Error:** Forgetting that in plane stress the third principal stress is **zero**, not the minimum. Always sort the three principal stresses properly before applying Tresca. Using the wrong ordering is the #1 reason for losing marks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Plane-Stress Yield Locus Comparison (Von Mises Ellipse vs. Tresca Hexagon)

The diagram below is rendered as a Mermaid block because the topic requires both a 2D curve and labelled regions — using a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** instead of a fragile text-only plot.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["ORIGIN OF PRINCIPAL STRESS PLANE sigma1 and sigma2"] --> B["ASSUME PLANE STRESS sigma3 equals zero"]
    B --> C1["VON MISES EQUATION sigma1 squared minus sigma1 times sigma2 plus sigma2 squared equals sigmaY squared"]
    B --> C2["TRESCA EQUATION MAX of absolute sigma1 minus sigma2 absolute sigma2 minus sigma3 absolute sigma3 minus sigma1 equals sigmaY"]
    C1 --> D1["SHAPE GENERATED IS AN ELLIPSE IN sigma1 sigma2 PLANE"]
    C2 --> D2["SHAPE GENERATED IS A REGULAR HEXAGON IN sigma1 sigma2 PLANE"]
    D1 --> E1["INSCRIBED INSIDE TRESCA HEXAGON"]
    D2 --> E2["CIRCUMSCRIBES VON MISES ELLIPSE AT EQUIBIAXIAL POINTS"]
    E1 --> F["PURE UNIAXIAL TENSION INTERCEPT BOTH GIVE sigma equals sigmaY"]
    E2 --> F
    E1 --> G["PURE SHEAR PREDICTION VON MISES tau equals sigmaY over sqrt three TRESCA tau equals sigmaY over two"]
    E2 --> G
    F --> H["EQUIBIAXIAL TENSION VON MISES sigma equals 1 over sqrt three sigmaY TRESCA sigma equals sigmaY"]
    G --> H
    H --> I["SAFETY FACTOR RATIO NTRESCA is always less than or equal to NVONMISES"]
    style A fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1
    style I fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style C1 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style C2 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** Each path traces a step in deriving the **plane-stress yield locus** — the geometric boundary that separates the elastic interior (safe stress states) from the plastic exterior (yielded states). The Von Mises ellipse and the Tresca hexagon touch at the uniaxial-tension intercepts on the $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$ axes (both pass through $\pm\sigma_Y$), and they meet at the equibiaxial points $(\pm\sigma_Y/2, \pm\sigma_Y/2)$ for Von Mises being strictly inside.

## 4.2 Comparison Block Diagram — Criterion Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["METAL FORMING PROBLEM"] --> B{"CRITERION SELECTION"}
    B -->|Need conservative bound or limit analysis| C["TRESCA Max Shear Stress Theory"]
    B -->|Need accurate ductile-metal prediction| D["VON MISES Distortion Energy Theory"]
    C --> C1["Surface Regular Hexagon in pi plane"]
    C --> C2["Pure shear k equals sigmaY over 2"]
    C --> C3["Mathematically simpler, discontinuous gradient"]
    D --> D1["Surface Smooth Cylinder in pi plane"]
    D --> D2["Pure shear k equals sigmaY over sqrt 3"]
    D --> D3["Matches experiments, smooth gradient, FEA friendly"]
    C1 --> E["Both criteria reduce to same value under UNIAXIAL TENSION sigma equals sigmaY"]
    C2 --> E
    D1 --> E
    D2 --> E
    style A fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b
    style B fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style C fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style D fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style E fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Shortcut:** The $\pi$-plane (deviatoric plane) is the section perpendicular to the hydrostatic axis. Von Mises gives a **circle** in this plane (smooth, rotationally symmetric). Tresca gives a **regular hexagon**. Both are convex, both are circumscribed by the same circle of radius $\sqrt{2/3}\,\sigma_Y$ at the corners.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. Define the Tresca yield criterion. (3 marks)
> **[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Question Bank]**

**Model Answer:**

The Tresca yield criterion, also called the **maximum shear stress criterion**, states that a ductile material begins to yield when the **maximum shear stress** at a point reaches the critical shear-yield value of the material as measured in a uniaxial tensile test.

Mathematically, in terms of the three principal stresses $\sigma_1 \ge \sigma_2 \ge \sigma_3$:

$$\boxed{\;\max\!\left[\, \tfrac{1}{2}\vert \sigma_1 - \sigma_2 \vert,\ \tfrac{1}{2}\vert \sigma_2 - \sigma_3 \vert,\ \tfrac{1}{2}\vert \sigma_3 - \sigma_1 \vert \,\right] = \dfrac{\sigma_Y}{2} = k\;}$$

Or simply: $\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = \sigma_Y$.

The criterion is **independent of the hydrostatic stress** (the mean of the three principal stresses does not affect yielding), which makes it valid for fully dense ductile metals but **not** for porous materials or soils.

---

### Q2. State the Von Mises yield criterion and write its plane-stress form. (3 marks)
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Question Bank]**

**Model Answer:**

The **Von Mises yield criterion** (also called the **distortion-energy criterion** or **octahedral shear stress criterion**) states that yielding occurs when the **second deviatoric stress invariant** $J_2$ reaches a critical value:

$$J_2 = \dfrac{\sigma_Y^{\,2}}{3} = k^2$$

The general 3D form in principal stresses is:

$$\boxed{\;(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2 = 2\,\sigma_Y^{\,2}\;}$$

For the **plane-stress** case ($\sigma_3 = 0$), the equation simplifies to:

$$\boxed{\;\sigma_1^{\,2} - \sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2^{\,2} = \sigma_Y^{\,2}\;}$$

This represents an **ellipse** in the $\sigma_1$–$\sigma_2$ plane, with intercepts on the principal axes at $\pm\sigma_Y$.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, Module-Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted for KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Derive the **Tresca yield criterion** in three-dimensional principal-stress form. Show clearly how the criterion reduces in pure shear and in uniaxial tension. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A thin-walled cylindrical pressure vessel has a diameter of 300 mm and a wall thickness of 6 mm. It is subjected to an internal pressure of 8 MPa. The material has a uniaxial yield stress of $\sigma_Y = 280$ MPa.
    (i) Compute the hoop and axial stresses in the vessel wall. **(3 marks)**
    (ii) Determine the factor of safety based on the **Tresca** criterion. **(4 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

##### (a) Derivation of Tresca Criterion (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Shear yield reference:**

In a uniaxial tensile test at yield, $\sigma_1 = \sigma_Y$, $\sigma_2 = \sigma_3 = 0$. The maximum shear stress is:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2} = \frac{\sigma_Y}{2} = k$$

This defines the material constant $k$ (pure-shear yield stress).

**Step 2 — Postulate:**

Tresca proposed yielding starts when the **largest** of the three principal-shear stresses reaches $k$:

$$\max\!\left[\, \tfrac{\vert \sigma_1 - \sigma_2 \vert}{2},\ \tfrac{\vert \sigma_2 - \sigma_3 \vert}{2},\ \tfrac{\vert \sigma_3 - \sigma_1 \vert}{2} \,\right] = k$$

Multiplying by 2:

$$\max\!\left[\, \vert \sigma_1 - \sigma_2 \vert,\ \vert \sigma_2 - \sigma_3 \vert,\ \vert \sigma_3 - \sigma_1 \vert \,\right] = 2k = \sigma_Y$$

**Step 3 — Specialisation:**

With $\sigma_1 \ge \sigma_2 \ge \sigma_3$, the maximum of the three absolute terms is $\vert \sigma_3 - \sigma_1 \vert = \sigma_1 - \sigma_3$, so:

$$\boxed{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = \sigma_Y}$$

- **Uniaxial tension:** $\sigma_1 = \sigma_Y$, $\sigma_2 = \sigma_3 = 0$ $\Rightarrow$ $0 - (-\sigma_Y)$? No, $\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = \sigma_Y - 0 = \sigma_Y$. ✓
- **Pure shear:** $\sigma_1 = \tau$, $\sigma_2 = 0$, $\sigma_3 = -\tau$ $\Rightarrow$ $\tau - (-\tau) = 2\tau = \sigma_Y$, hence $\tau = \sigma_Y/2 = k$. ✓

---

##### (b) Pressure Vessel Numerical (7 marks)

**(i) Hoop and axial stresses (3 marks):**

For a thin-walled cylinder with internal pressure $p$:

$$\sigma_{hoop} = \frac{p\,D}{2\,t} = \frac{8 \times 300}{2 \times 6} = \frac{2400}{12} = 200\ \text{MPa}$$

$$\sigma_{axial} = \frac{p\,D}{4\,t} = \frac{8 \times 300}{4 \times 6} = \frac{2400}{24} = 100\ \text{MPa}$$

(The radial stress $\sigma_r \approx 0$ for thin wall.)

The principal stresses are therefore:
$$\sigma_1 = 200\ \text{MPa},\quad \sigma_2 = 100\ \text{MPa},\quad \sigma_3 = 0\ \text{MPa}$$

**(ii) Tresca Factor of Safety (4 marks):**

Since $\sigma_1 \ge \sigma_2 \ge \sigma_3$ already:

$$\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = 200 - 0 = 200\ \text{MPa}$$

Tresca safety factor:

$$N_T = \frac{\sigma_Y}{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3} = \frac{280}{200} = \boxed{1.40}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Adapted for KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a clear schematic in the $\pi$-plane, **derive the Von Mises yield criterion** starting from the distortion-energy hypothesis. Show the steps for general 3D principal stresses. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A ductile copper alloy ($\sigma_Y = 220$ MPa) is subjected to a biaxial stress state with $\sigma_x = 120$ MPa (tension) and $\sigma_y = -60$ MPa (compression). There is no shear stress on the planes considered.
    (i) Determine the principal stresses. **(3 marks)**
    (ii) Compute the safety factor using **Von Mises** and **Tresca** criteria. **(4 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

##### (a) Von Mises Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Decompose total elastic strain energy:**

For a linear elastic body, the strain energy density splits into volumetric ($U_v$) and distortional ($U_d$) parts:

$$U = U_v + U_d = \frac{1-2\nu}{6E}\bigl(\sigma_1+\sigma_2+\sigma_3\bigr)^2 + \frac{1+\nu}{6E}\bigl[(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2\bigr]$$

**Step 2 — Von Mises hypothesis:**

Yielding begins when $U_d$ reaches the value at uniaxial yield:

$$U_d = U_{d,\text{ref}}$$

**Step 3 — Reference uniaxial case ($\sigma_1 = \sigma_Y$, $\sigma_2 = \sigma_3 = 0$):**

$$U_{d,\text{ref}} = \frac{1+\nu}{6E}\bigl[\sigma_Y^{\,2} + 0 + \sigma_Y^{\,2}\bigr] = \frac{1+\nu}{3E}\sigma_Y^{\,2}$$

**Step 4 — Equate and cancel $\dfrac{1+\nu}{6E}$:**

$$(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2 = 2\,\sigma_Y^{\,2}$$

**Step 5 — Express via $J_2$:**

$$J_2 = \frac{1}{6}\bigl[(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2\bigr] = \frac{\sigma_Y^{\,2}}{3}$$

**Step 6 — $\pi$-plane interpretation:**

In the deviatoric (π) plane, this equation describes a **circle of radius** $\sqrt{2J_2} = \sqrt{2/3}\,\sigma_Y$. The Tresca criterion, in contrast, gives a **regular hexagon** inscribed in this same circle. The circle touches the hexagon at six equally spaced points, confirming that Von Mises predicts yielding at a 15.5% higher load than Tresca in pure shear.

---

##### (b) Biaxial Stress Numerical (7 marks)

**(i) Principal stresses (3 marks):**

For a 2D state with $\sigma_x = 120$, $\sigma_y = -60$, $\tau_{xy} = 0$:

The principal stresses are simply the normal stresses (since there's no shear):

$$\sigma_1 = 120\ \text{MPa},\quad \sigma_2 = -60\ \text{MPa},\quad \sigma_3 = 0\ \text{MPa (plane stress)}$$

Sort: $\sigma_1 = 120$, $\sigma_2 = 0$, $\sigma_3 = -60$.

**(ii) Safety factors (4 marks):**

**Tresca safety factor:**

$$\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = 120 - (-60) = 180\ \text{MPa}$$

$$N_T = \frac{220}{180} \approx \boxed{1.222}$$

**Von Mises safety factor:**

Using plane-stress equivalent formula:

$$\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\sigma_x^{\,2} - \sigma_x\sigma_y + \sigma_y^{\,2}}$$

$$= \sqrt{120^2 - (120)(-60) + (-60)^2}$$

$$= \sqrt{14400 + 7200 + 3600}$$

$$= \sqrt{25200} \approx 158.75\ \text{MPa}$$

$$N_{VM} = \frac{220}{158.75} \approx \boxed{1.386}$$

**Comparison:** Von Mises gives $N_{VM} \approx 1.39$, which is **~13.4% higher** than Tresca ($N_T \approx 1.22$). Both criteria confirm the structure is safe ($N > 1$), with Von Mises being less conservative.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Errors (Watch Your Score!)**
> 1. **Forgetting to sort the principal stresses** before applying Tresca. The equation $\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = \sigma_Y$ is **only valid** when $\sigma_1 \ge \sigma_2 \ge \sigma_3$. Mixing the ordering gives a wrong (often larger) difference and **loses 2–3 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing $k$ and $\sigma_Y$**: In the KTU notation, $k$ is the *shear* yield stress. A frequent error is writing $k = \sigma_Y\sqrt{3}$ instead of $k = \sigma_Y/2$ (Tresca) or $k = \sigma_Y/\sqrt{3}$ (Von Mises). Memorise these two **distinct** relationships.
> 3. **Missing the factor of 2 in Von Mises**: The general 3D form is $(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2 = \mathbf{2}\sigma_Y^{\,2}$, not $\sigma_Y^{\,2}$. This factor of 2 trips up many students.
> 4. **Hydrostatic stress assumption not stated**: When asked "why is yielding independent of hydrostatic pressure?", students often skip the physical reasoning (atomic slip depends on shear, not volumetric compression). The KTU rubric typically allots 1 mark for this statement.
> 5. **Using the wrong sign in plane stress**: A biaxial tension-compression test often leads to a negative principal stress. Treat it as a **value**, not a sign error.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute Read Before Exam)**

- ✅ **Tresca** = Maximum Shear Stress Theory. Equation: $\max[\vert\sigma_1-\sigma_2\vert,\vert\sigma_2-\sigma_3\vert,\vert\sigma_3-\sigma_1\vert] = \sigma_Y$. Simplified (sorted): $\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = \sigma_Y$.
- ✅ **Von Mises** = Distortion Energy Theory. Equation: $(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2 = 2\sigma_Y^{\,2}$. Equivalently $J_2 = \sigma_Y^{\,2}/3$.
- ✅ **Pure shear values (memorise!):** Tresca $\tau_Y = \sigma_Y/2$; Von Mises $\tau_Y = \sigma_Y/\sqrt{3} \approx 0.577\sigma_Y$.
- ✅ **Plane stress (σ₃ = 0):** Von Mises ⇒ $\sigma_1^{\,2} - \sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2^{\,2} = \sigma_Y^{\,2}$ (ellipse); Tresca ⇒ regular hexagon with sides $\sigma_1 = \pm\sigma_Y$, $\sigma_2 = \pm\sigma_Y$, $\sigma_1 - \sigma_2 = \pm\sigma_Y$.
- ✅ **Hydrostatic-independence** is a **postulate** of both criteria — valid for fully dense ductile metals, **not** for porous materials or geomaterials.
- ✅ **π-plane shape:** Von Mises ⇒ **circle**; Tresca ⇒ **regular hexagon** inscribed in the same circle. Difference in load-to-yield in pure shear: 15.5% (Von Mises is less conservative).
- ✅ **Safety factor definitions:** Tresca $N_T = \sigma_Y / (\sigma_1 - \sigma_3)$; Von Mises $N_{VM} = \sigma_Y / \sigma_{VM}$. Always $N_{VM} \ge N_T$.
- ✅ **Order of principal stresses matters** for Tresca: always sort $\sigma_1 \ge \sigma_2 \ge \sigma_3$ first.
- ✅ **Engineering applications:** Tresca for limit analysis, upper-bound theorems, conservative design. Von Mises for FEA, sheet-metal forming FLDs, modern pressure-vessel codes.
- ✅ **Both criteria give identical answers for uniaxial tension** by construction. They diverge in **pure shear**, **biaxial equal tension/compression**, and **general 3D states**.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Yield Criteria for Ductile Materials**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plastic Deformation and Work Hardening
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET524 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Plastic Deformation and Work Hardening

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Plastic Deformation — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plastic Deformation** is the permanent, non-recoverable change in the shape of a solid body subjected to external forces, occurring **without fracture**, once the applied stress exceeds the **yield stress (σ_y)** of the material. In metal forming, this is the foundational phenomenon that allows raw billets to be reshaped into useful engineering components through processes like rolling, forging, extrusion, and drawing.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEIET524)**, plastic deformation is studied not as a failure mode but as a **manufacturing enabler**. Unlike elastic deformation (where atoms return to their original positions when the load is removed), plastic deformation involves the **irreversible movement of atoms** — primarily through **dislocation glide (slip)** and **mechanical twinning** along specific crystallographic planes.

### 1.1.1 Conceptual Analogy — "The Stack of Cards on a Table"

Imagine a thick deck of playing cards lying flat on a table. If you press down gently on the top card, it bends slightly but springs back — this is **elastic deformation**.

Now, if you push the top card sideways with your palm, it slides over the cards below and stays in its new position even after you remove your hand — this is **plastic deformation by slip**. The cards above have moved permanently relative to the cards below, just as atomic planes slide over each other inside a metal crystal.

If instead you tilt the top of the deck sharply, the cards may rotate as a mirror-image pair around a sharp line — this represents **mechanical twinning**, another mode of plastic deformation common in HCP metals like magnesium and titanium.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** The examiner distinguishes between:
> - **Elastic deformation** → Governed by Hooke's Law, recoverable, atomic bond stretching.
> - **Plastic deformation** → Governed by dislocation motion, permanent, atomic plane shearing.

## 1.2 Work Hardening (Strain Hardening) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Work Hardening** (also called **strain hardening** or **cold working**) is the phenomenon whereby a ductile metal becomes **harder and stronger** as it is plastically deformed at a temperature **below its recrystallization temperature**. It is characterized by a continuous rise in flow stress with increasing plastic strain in a uniaxial tension/compression test.

The physical origin of work hardening is the **progressive increase in dislocation density** within the metal. As plastic strain accumulates:
- Initial dislocation density: ρ₀ ≈ **10⁶ – 10⁸ lines/m²**
- After heavy cold working: ρ ≈ **10¹⁴ – 10¹⁶ lines/m²**

These tangled dislocations impede each other's motion, requiring progressively higher stress to continue deformation.

### 1.2.1 Conceptual Analogy — "The Tangled Ball of Yarn"

Picture a single strand of yarn — it pulls smoothly. Now imagine a hundred yarn strands knotted together in a ball. When you try to pull one strand, it catches on the others, and the entire ball becomes very difficult to disentangle.

**Dislocations behave exactly like this.** A nearly perfect crystal with few dislocations deforms easily. Once many dislocations are generated and intersect, they form "tangles" (forest dislocations, cell walls, pile-ups) that act as obstacles, raising the stress needed for further slip.

## 1.3 Syllabus Highlights from KTU 2024 Scheme

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 1 Learning Outcomes (Mapped to KTU COs):**
> 1. **CO1:** Explain the mechanisms of plastic deformation in metals.
> 2. **CO2:** Apply yield criteria and flow rules to solve forming problems.
> 3. **CO3:** Analyze work hardening behavior using true stress–true strain relationships.
> 4. **CO4:** Distinguish between ideal plastic, perfectly plastic, and strain-hardening material models.

The topic of Plastic Deformation and Work Hardening typically carries a weightage of **15–20%** in the KTU 2024 End Semester Examination (ESE) for this course, often appearing as a **full 14-mark Part B question** or a combination of 3-mark Part A questions.

## 1.4 Engineering Significance

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in Industry:**
> - **Cold rolling of steel sheets:** Relies on work hardening to achieve high strength with thin gauges.
> - **Deep drawing of aluminum cans:** Requires precise control of work hardening rate (n-value) to prevent tearing.
> - **Wire drawing:** Successive dies progressively increase strength due to cumulative cold work.
> - **Spring manufacturing:** Controlled cold work produces the desired elastic limit in spring steel.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanisms of Plastic Deformation

Plastic deformation in crystalline metals occurs predominantly by two atomistic mechanisms:

### 2.1.1 Slip (Dislocation Glide)

Slip is the **most common** mode of plastic deformation in metals. It involves the movement of **dislocations** (linear crystallographic defects) along specific **slip planes** and in specific **slip directions**.

**Key principles:**
- Slip occurs on the plane of **highest atomic density** (the closest-packed plane).
- Slip direction is the **closest-packed direction** within that plane.
- The combination of one slip plane + one slip direction = **one slip system**.

| Crystal Structure | Slip Plane | Slip Direction | Number of Slip Systems |
|---|---|---|---|
| FCC (e.g., Al, Cu, γ-Fe) | {111} | ⟨110⟩ | **12** |
| BCC (e.g., α-Fe, W, Cr) | {110}, {112}, {123} | ⟨111⟩ | **12, 24, 48** |
| HCP (e.g., Mg, Zn, Ti) | Basal {0001} | ⟨11̄20⟩ | **3** (limited) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** FCC metals are highly ductile (12 slip systems) → excellent formability. HCP metals are poor for cold forming (only 3 basal slip systems) → must be hot-worked.

### 2.1.2 Twinning (Mechanical Twinning)

In twinning, a portion of the crystal undergoes a **coordinated atomic displacement** such that the twinned region is a **mirror image** of the parent lattice across a **twin plane**.

- Dominant in **HCP metals** (Mg, Zn, Ti) at low temperatures.
- Also observed in **BCC metals** at high strain rates or low temperatures.
- Twinning is **polar** (only one twinning direction allowed), unlike slip.
- Produces a characteristic **sharp load drop** on the stress–strain curve.

## 2.2 Schmid's Law and Critical Resolved Shear Stress (CRSS)

When a single crystal is loaded in tension along an arbitrary axis, the **resolved shear stress** on the slip plane in the slip direction is given by **Schmid's Law**.

**Geometric setup:**
- Tensile axis makes angle **φ (phi)** with the normal to the slip plane.
- Tensile axis makes angle **λ (lambda)** with the slip direction.
- The angle between slip plane normal and slip direction is **90°** (they are mutually perpendicular within the slip system).

**The critical condition for slip to initiate:**

$$\tau_{CRSS} = \sigma_y \cdot \cos\phi \cdot \cos\lambda$$

Where:
- $\tau_{CRSS}$ = Critical Resolved Shear Stress (material property, **≈ 0.5 – 5 MPa** for typical metals)
- $\sigma_y$ = Applied tensile yield stress
- $\cos\phi \cdot \cos\lambda$ = **Schmid Factor** (maximum value = **0.5** when φ = λ = 45°)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Implication:** A single crystal yields at the **lowest tensile stress** when the slip plane and slip direction are each at **45°** to the loading axis. This is why texture control in sheet metal forming is critical.

## 2.3 Macroscopic Stress–Strain Behavior

### 2.3.1 Engineering Stress and Strain

Engineering quantities use the **original** cross-sectional area A₀ and original gauge length L₀:

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A_0} \qquad \text{(Engineering Stress)}$$

$$e = \frac{L - L_0}{L_0} \qquad \text{(Engineering Strain)}$$

### 2.3.2 True Stress and Strain

True quantities use the **instantaneous** area A_i and instantaneous length L_i:

$$\sigma_t = \frac{F}{A_i} \qquad \text{(True Stress)}$$

$$\varepsilon = \int_{L_0}^{L} \frac{dL}{L} = \ln\left(\frac{L}{L_0}\right) \qquad \text{(True Strain)}$$

### 2.3.3 Volume Constancy Assumption

During plastic deformation, the material is **incompressible** (density unchanged):

$$A_0 \cdot L_0 = A_i \cdot L_i = \text{constant}$$

This gives the elegant conversion between engineering and true quantities:

$$\sigma_t = \sigma \cdot (1 + e) \qquad \varepsilon = \ln(1 + e)$$

## 2.4 The Hollomon Power Law (Work Hardening Equation)

For most metals in the plastic region, the true stress–true strain relationship follows the **Hollomon Equation** (1945):

$$\boxed{\sigma_t = K \cdot \varepsilon^n}$$

Where:
- $K$ = **Strength Coefficient** (units: MPa) — represents the true stress at ε = 1
- $n$ = **Strain Hardening Exponent** (dimensionless, 0 ≤ n ≤ 1)
- $\varepsilon$ = True plastic strain

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Values to Memorize:**
> - $n = 0$ → **Perfectly plastic** (no hardening) — e.g., ideal lead
> - $n = 1$ → **Perfectly elastic** (linear, no plasticity) — e.g., rubber analogy
> - $n \approx 0.15 - 0.25$ → **Low carbon steel** (good formability)
> - $n \approx 0.45 - 0.55$ → **Austenitic stainless steel** (high formability)
> - $n \approx 0.05 - 0.15$ → **Aluminum alloys** (modest formability)
> - $n \approx 0.1$ → **Brass, copper**

A high **n-value** is the single most important indicator of **good sheet metal formability** — it distributes strain uniformly and delays localized necking.

## 2.5 Yield Criteria for Ductile Metals

For **multiaxial stress states** in metal forming, we need criteria to predict yielding:

### 2.5.1 Tresca Criterion (Maximum Shear Stress)

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2} = k = \frac{\sigma_y}{2}$$

In principal stress form:

$$(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2 = 0 \text{ (at yield)}$$

### 2.5.2 Von Mises Criterion (Distortion Energy)

$$(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2 = 2\sigma_y^2$$

Or equivalently using the **effective stress** (which is what we use in forming analysis):

$$\bar{\sigma} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\sqrt{(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Von Mises is more accurate for ductile metals (matches experimental data within ~15%) and is **preferred** in finite element simulations of forming. Tresca is more conservative (always safe) and easier to apply by hand.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma = F/A_0$ | Engineering stress | MPa |
| 2 | $e = (L - L_0)/L_0$ | Engineering strain | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\sigma_t = F/A_i$ | True stress | MPa |
| 4 | $\varepsilon = \ln(L/L_0)$ | True strain | dimensionless |
| 5 | $A_0 L_0 = A_i L_i$ | Volume constancy | m³ |
| 6 | $\sigma_t = \sigma(1+e)$ | Engineering → True stress | MPa |
| 7 | $\varepsilon = \ln(1+e)$ | Engineering → True strain | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\tau = \sigma \cos\phi \cos\lambda$ | Schmid's law (CRSS) | MPa |
| 9 | $\sigma_t = K\varepsilon^n$ | Hollomon power law | MPa |
| 10 | $Y_f = A_0/A_f = e^{\varepsilon_f}$ | Maximum useful strain | dimensionless |
| 11 | $\bar{\sigma} = K\bar{\varepsilon}^n$ | Effective flow stress (von Mises) | MPa |
| 12 | $\bar{\sigma} = \sigma_y + H\bar{\varepsilon}$ | Linear (isotropic) hardening | MPa |
| 13 | $d\varepsilon_p = d\sigma / H'$ | Hardening slope relation | dimensionless |
| 14 | $W = \int \sigma_t \, d\varepsilon$ | Plastic work per unit volume | MJ/m³ |

## 2.7 Strain Rate Sensitivity

Some materials (especially at elevated temperatures) show **rate-dependent** flow stress:

$$\sigma_t = C \cdot (\dot{\varepsilon})^m \cdot (\varepsilon)^n$$

Where **m** is the **strain rate sensitivity exponent**. Superplastic materials have **m > 0.3**, allowing enormous elongations (up to 1000%).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Engineering to True Stress–Strain Conversion

We begin with the fundamental assumption of **plastic incompressibility** (volume constancy):

$$V_0 = V_i \quad \Rightarrow \quad A_0 L_0 = A_i L_i$$

**Step 1:** Express the instantaneous area in terms of the instantaneous length:

$$A_i = \frac{A_0 L_0}{L_i}$$

**Step 2:** Write the true stress definition:

$$\sigma_t = \frac{F}{A_i} = \frac{F \cdot L_i}{A_0 L_0}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the engineering stress $\sigma = F/A_0$:

$$\sigma_t = \frac{F}{A_0} \cdot \frac{L_i}{L_0} = \sigma \cdot \frac{L_i}{L_0}$$

**Step 4:** Express the length ratio in terms of engineering strain $e = (L_i - L_0)/L_0$:

$$\frac{L_i}{L_0} = 1 + e$$

**Step 5:** Combine to obtain the conversion formula:

$$\sigma_t = \sigma (1 + e)$$

**Step 6:** For true strain, integrate the incremental definition from L₀ to L_i:

$$\varepsilon = \int_{L_0}^{L_i} \frac{dL}{L} = \left[\ln L\right]_{L_0}^{L_i} = \ln L_i - \ln L_0 = \ln\left(\frac{L_i}{L_0}\right)$$

**Step 7:** Substitute the length ratio from Step 4:

$$\varepsilon = \ln\left(\frac{L_i}{L_0}\right) = \ln(1 + e)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Exam Insight:** This conversion is valid **only up to the point of necking** in a tension test. Beyond necking, the triaxial stress state in the neck region and the rapidly changing cross-section invalidate the simple uniaxial assumption.

## 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Uniform Strain (Considère Criterion)

The **Considère Criterion** determines the point at which **localized necking** begins in a uniaxial tension test. Necking initiates when the **rate of decrease of area** equals the **rate of strain hardening**.

**Step 1:** Volume constancy gives us $A_i L_i = A_0 L_0$, so:

$$d(A_i L_i) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad L_i \, dA_i + A_i \, dL_i = 0$$

**Step 2:** Divide by $A_i L_i$:

$$\frac{dA_i}{A_i} + \frac{dL_i}{L_i} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{dA_i}{A_i} = -d\varepsilon$$

**Step 3:** True stress is $\sigma_t = F/A_i$. At the onset of necking, the load F reaches a maximum, so $dF = 0$:

$$d\sigma_t = d\left(\frac{F}{A_i}\right) = \frac{dF \cdot A_i - F \cdot dA_i}{A_i^2} = -\frac{F \cdot dA_i}{A_i^2}$$

**Step 4:** Setting $dF = 0$ in the differential form:

$$dF = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad d\sigma_t = -\frac{F \, dA_i}{A_i^2}$$

**Step 5:** The applied force is $F = \sigma_t A_i$, and $dA_i/A_i = -d\varepsilon$, so:

$$dF = \sigma_t \, dA_i + A_i \, d\sigma_t = 0$$

Dividing by $A_i$:

$$\sigma_t \cdot \frac{dA_i}{A_i} + d\sigma_t = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad -\sigma_t \, d\varepsilon + d\sigma_t = 0$$

**Step 6:** Therefore the necking condition is:

$$\frac{d\sigma_t}{d\varepsilon} = \sigma_t$$

**Step 7:** Apply the Hollomon equation $\sigma_t = K\varepsilon^n$:

$$\frac{d\sigma_t}{d\varepsilon} = nK\varepsilon^{n-1}$$

**Step 8:** Set equal to $\sigma_t$ at the necking strain $\varepsilon_u$:

$$nK\varepsilon_u^{n-1} = K\varepsilon_u^n$$

**Step 9:** Cancel common terms to get the **Considère Result**:

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_u = n}$$

The **maximum uniform (useful) strain in tension equals the strain hardening exponent n**. This is a beautiful and frequently-tested result in KTU examinations.

**Step 10:** The maximum useful drawing ratio in sheet metal is therefore:

$$Y_f = \frac{A_0}{A_f} = e^{\varepsilon_u} = e^n$$

## 3.3 Worked Example: Deep Drawing Strain Limit

**Problem:** A sheet of low-carbon steel has $K = 600$ MPa and $n = 0.20$. Calculate:
(a) The maximum uniform true strain before necking.
(b) The maximum reduction in area possible in a single drawing operation.
(c) The flow stress when the true strain is 0.15.

**Solution:**

**Part (a):** From the Considère Criterion:

$$\varepsilon_u = n = 0.20$$

**Part (b):** Maximum reduction ratio:

$$Y_{f,\max} = e^{\varepsilon_u} = e^{0.20} = 1.2214$$

This means the area can be reduced by a factor of **1.22** in a single draw. In practice, a safety factor of 0.75–0.85 is applied.

**Part (c):** Flow stress at ε = 0.15:

$$\sigma_t = K\varepsilon^n = 600 \times (0.15)^{0.20}$$

Computing $(0.15)^{0.20}$:

$$\ln(0.15) = -1.8971 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 0.20 \times (-1.8971) = -0.3794$$

$$(0.15)^{0.20} = e^{-0.3794} = 0.6843$$

$$\sigma_t = 600 \times 0.6843 = 410.6 \text{ MPa}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** Since $\varepsilon = 0.15 < \varepsilon_u = 0.20$, the deformation is **uniform** and the Hollomon equation is valid. If we had been given $\varepsilon > 0.20$, we would need post-necking data.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Process Variables

| Variable | Increase → Effect on Flow Stress | Effect on Formability | KTU Exam Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| Strain (ε) | ↑ (if hardening) | Delayed necking (higher n) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Strain rate ($\dot{\varepsilon}$) | ↑ (rate-sensitive materials) | Can be beneficial (m > 0) | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| Temperature (T) | ↓ (thermal softening) | ↑ (more ductility) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Grain size (d) | ↓ (Hall-Petch) | Mixed (fine = strong, coarse = ductile) | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| Alloying elements | ↑ (solid solution) | Mixed | ⭐⭐ |
| Hydrostatic pressure | No direct effect on yielding | ↑ (suppresses fracture) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

## 3.5 Energy of Plastic Deformation

The **plastic work per unit volume** is the area under the true stress–true strain curve:

$$W_p = \int_0^{\varepsilon_f} \sigma_t \, d\varepsilon$$

**For a Hollomon material** ($\sigma_t = K\varepsilon^n$):

$$W_p = \int_0^{\varepsilon_f} K\varepsilon^n \, d\varepsilon = \frac{K \varepsilon_f^{n+1}}{n+1}$$

**This work converts almost entirely into heat** (~85–95%) plus a small fraction stored as **lattice defects** (5–15%) — the stored energy of cold work that drives **recovery and recrystallization** during annealing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Insight:** The latent heat of plastic deformation is why adiabatic heating raises the workpiece temperature during high-speed forming (e.g., ~150°C rise in cold forging of steel). This is also why **recrystallization temperature** (≈ 0.4 × T_melting in Kelvin) defines the boundary between **cold working** and **hot working**.
