### Step 1: Elemental Slice Equilibrium

Consider a thin elemental disk of radius $r$ and thickness $dh$ at the center of the cylinder. The element experiences:
- Vertical compressive stress $\sigma_z$ (from the die)
- Radial compressive stress $\sigma_r$ (from surrounding material)
- Hoop stress $\sigma_\theta = \sigma_r$ (axial symmetry)

Force balance in the vertical direction on the element:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(\sigma_z + d\sigma_z)(\pi r^2) - \sigma_z (\pi r^2) + 2\pi r \cdot \mu \sigma_r \cdot dr &= 0 \\
\pi r^2 \, d\sigma_z + 2\pi r \mu \sigma_r \, dr &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Dividing by $\pi r \, dr$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
r \, d\sigma_z + 2 \mu \sigma_r \, dr &= 0 \\
\frac{d\sigma_z}{dr} &= -\frac{2\mu \sigma_r}{r}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Yield Criterion (von Mises for Upsetting)

At the onset of plastic deformation, the **von Mises yield criterion** gives:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_z - \sigma_r &= Y_f \\
\sigma_r &= \sigma_z - Y_f
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Substitution and Integration

Substituting $\sigma_r = \sigma_z - Y_f$ into the equilibrium equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
r \, d\sigma_z &= -2\mu (\sigma_z - Y_f) \, dr \\
\frac{d\sigma_z}{\sigma_z - Y_f} &= -\frac{2\mu}{r} \, dr
\end{aligned}
$$

Integrate from the center ($r = 0$, $\sigma_z = Y_f$ at the free surface) to the outer radius ($r = R$, $\sigma_z = p$ at the die face):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{Y_f}^{p} \frac{d\sigma_z}{\sigma_z - Y_f} &= -2\mu \int_{0}^{R} \frac{dr}{r} \\
\left[ \ln(\sigma_z - Y_f) \right]_{Y_f}^{p} &= -2\mu \left[ \ln r \right]_{0}^{R} \\
\ln\left(\frac{p - Y_f}{Y_f - Y_f}\right) &= \ln\left(\frac{R}{0}\right)^{-2\mu}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mathematical Singularity at $r = 0$:** Strictly, $\ln(0)$ is undefined. In practice, the upper limit of integration is taken as the **die-workpiece contact radius** $R$, and the solution uses a **constant upper limit of friction** approach. The standard form assumes the friction force is approximately constant across the radius, leading to the simplified average-pressure form.

### Step 4: The Simplified Friction Hill Result

The accepted engineering form (after the proper boundary condition treatment) is:

$$
\boxed{\; p_{avg} = Y_f \left( 1 + \frac{\mu D}{3h} \right) \;}
$$

where:
- $D$ = current diameter
- $h$ = current height
- $\mu$ = coefficient of friction
- $Y_f$ = material flow stress

> [!TIP]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The factor $\left(1 + \dfrac{\mu D}{3h}\right)$ is called the **"friction hill"** because pressure rises towards the **center of the die** (where metal flow is most restricted). Higher friction or larger diameter-to-height ratio → higher required force.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Maximum Forging Load in Impression-Die Forging

**Problem:** A disc of initial diameter $D_0 = 50$ mm and height $h_0 = 25$ mm is forged in a closed die to a final height $h_f = 12.5$ mm. The material has a flow stress $Y_f = 250$ MPa and the die-workpiece friction coefficient is $\mu = 0.2$. The flash is neglected. Find the maximum forging load.

### Step 1: Volume Constancy — Find the Final Diameter

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_0 &= \frac{\pi}{4} D_0^2 h_0 = \frac{\pi}{4} (50)^2 (25) = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 62500 \\
V_f &= \frac{\pi}{4} D_f^2 h_f
\end{aligned}
$$

Equating volumes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_f^2 \cdot h_f &= D_0^2 \cdot h_0 \\
D_f^2 &= \frac{D_0^2 \cdot h_0}{h_f} = \frac{(50)^2 (25)}{12.5} = \frac{2500 \cdot 25}{12.5} \\
D_f^2 &= 5000 \\
D_f &= \sqrt{5000} = 70.71 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: True Strain

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon &= \ln\left(\frac{h_0}{h_f}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{25}{12.5}\right) = \ln(2) = 0.6931
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Average Pressure (Friction Hill) at Maximum Load

The **maximum load occurs at the smallest height** (largest $D/h$ ratio), so we use $h = h_f$ and $D = D_f$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_{avg} &= Y_f \left( 1 + \frac{\mu D_f}{3 h_f} \right) \\
p_{avg} &= 250 \left( 1 + \frac{0.2 \times 70.71}{3 \times 12.5} \right) \\
p_{avg} &= 250 \left( 1 + \frac{14.142}{37.5} \right) \\
p_{avg} &= 250 \left( 1 + 0.3771 \right) \\
p_{avg} &= 250 \times 1.3771 = 344.28 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Projected Area at Final State

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{proj} &= \frac{\pi}{4} D_f^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (70.71)^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 5000 = 3926.99 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Maximum Forging Load

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{max} &= p_{avg} \times A_{proj} \\
F_{max} &= 344.28 \text{ N/mm}^2 \times 3926.99 \text{ mm}^2 \\
F_{max} &= 1,352,068 \text{ N} \approx 1352 \text{ kN} \approx 138 \text{ tonnes-force}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** The press must have a **capacity of at least 1352 kN (≈140 tonnes)** to perform this forging operation. This is why press selection in industry is a critical design step.

---

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Forging Processes

| Parameter | Open-Die Forging | Impression-Die Forging | Closed-Die (Flashless) Forging |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Die Cost** | **Very Low** | **High** (machined cavity) | **Very High** (precision) |
| **Tooling Lead Time** | **Days** | **Weeks to Months** | **Months** |
| **Part Tolerance** | **± 1.5 to 5 mm** | **± 0.4 to 1.5 mm** | **± 0.1 to 0.4 mm** |
| **Surface Finish (Ra)** | **10 – 50 µm** | **3 – 12 µm** | **1 – 3 µm** |
| **Material Utilization** | **90 – 95%** | **70 – 85%** (flash loss) | **~98%** |
| **Part Size Range** | **Very Large** (up to several tonnes) | **Medium to Small** (g to kg) | **Small to Medium** (precision) |
| **Production Rate** | **Low** (manual/semi-auto) | **Medium to High** | **High** (auto lines) |
| **Grain Flow Control** | **Limited** | **Excellent** | **Excellent** |
| **Operator Skill Required** | **High** | **Medium** | **Low** |
| **Typical Industries** | Heavy engineering, defense | Automotive, aerospace | High-volume automotive, fasteners |
| **Equipment Used** | Hydraulic press, large hammer | Mechanical/Hydraulic press | Hydraulic press (precision) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "OPC" for Selection:**
> - **O**pen-Die → **O**versized simple shapes (shafts, rings, discs)
> - **P**articularly shaped → **P**recision (Impression-Die) for medium-complexity
> - **C**losed-Die → **C**ritical, **C**lose-tolerance, **C**ontinuous production

---

## 3.4 Energy and Power Calculations (Work in Forging)

**Work done** in forging under constant flow stress (ideal case):

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= \int_{V} Y_f \, d\varepsilon = V \cdot Y_f \cdot \varepsilon
\end{aligned}
$$

For a variable flow stress with strain hardening ($\sigma_f = K \varepsilon^n$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= \int_{0}^{\varepsilon_f} K \varepsilon^n V \, d\varepsilon = \frac{K V \varepsilon_f^{n+1}}{n+1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Power required** for a press stroke:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \frac{W}{t_{stroke}}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $t_{stroke}$ is the time to complete one press stroke.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Forgetting that the **friction hill** increases the *force* significantly (sometimes 2–3×), but only slightly increases the *work* because the average die pressure acts over a *larger* projected area. Always check whether the question asks for **Force, Work, or Power**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow — Forging Process Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Part Requirements] --> B{Part Size and Weight}
    B -->|Large > 100 kg| C[Open-Die Forging]
    B -->|Medium 1 to 100 kg| D[Impression-Die Forging]
    B -->|Small < 1 kg| E[Closed-Die Flashless Forging]
    C --> F{Required Tolerance}
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F -->|Low Tolerance OK| G[Confirm Open-Die Choice]
    F -->|Medium Tolerance| H[Confirm Impression-Die Choice]
    F -->|High Tolerance| I[Confirm Closed-Die Choice]
    G --> J[Equipment Selection]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K{Production Volume}
    K -->|Low| L[Hammer or Hydraulic Press]
    K -->|Medium| M[Mechanical or Hydraulic Press]
    K -->|High| N[Automated Press Line]
    L --> O[Final Process Locked]
    M --> O
    N --> O
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Chart:** Start at the top with the part definition and follow the decision branches based on **size, tolerance, and volume**. The end point is the final equipment selection.

---

## 4.2 Open-Die Forging — Operation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Heating Furnace] -->|Hot Billet| B[Workpiece on Anvil]
    B --> C[Upper Die / Hammer]
    C -->|Compressive Force| D[Plastic Deformation]
    D --> E{Material Free to Flow Laterally?}
    E -->|Yes| F[Lateral Spread]
    E -->|No| G[Constrained]
    F --> H[Reposition Workpiece]
    H --> C
    G --> I[Impression-Die Process]
    H --> J[Finished Open-Die Part]
```

---

## 4.3 Impression-Die Forging — Cavity Fill Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Heated Billet] --> B[Positioned in Lower Die Cavity]
    B --> C[Upper Die Descends]
    C --> D[Material Enters Cavity]
    D --> E{Cavity Volume vs Billet Volume}
    E -->|Excess Material| F[Material Flows to Flash Gutter]
    E -->|Matched Volume| G[Cavity Fills Completely]
    F --> H[Flash Land Creates Back-Pressure]
    H --> I[Forces Metal into Deep Cavity Features]
    I --> J[Complete Fill Achieved]
    G --> J
    J --> K[Die Opens]
    K --> L[Part with Flash Ejected]
    L --> M[Trimming Operation]
    M --> N[Finished Flash-Free Part]
```

---

## 4.4 Forging Equipment Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Forging Equipment] --> B[Impact Type]
    A --> C[Pressure Type]
    B --> B1[Drop Hammer]
    B --> B2[Counterblow Hammer]
    B --> B3[Board Hammer]
    C --> C1[Mechanical Press]
    C --> C2[Hydraulic Press]
    C --> C3[Screw Press]
    C1 --> C1a[Crank Press]
    C1 --> C1b[Knuckle Joint Press]
    C2 --> C2a[Direct Hydraulic]
    C2 --> C2b[Indirect Hydraulic]
```

### 4.4.1 Equipment Characteristics Table

| Equipment | Energy Source | Stroke Speed | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Drop Hammer** | Gravity + Steam/Air | **5–7 m/s** (high) | Open-die, large forgings |
| **Counterblow Hammer** | Two opposing rams | **5–7 m/s** | Large open-die, no vibration |
| **Mechanical Press (Crank)** | Flywheel + Crank | **0.05–0.5 m/s** | Impression-die, high volume |
| **Hydraulic Press** | Hydraulic fluid | **0.005–0.05 m/s** | Closed-die, large parts, isothermal |
| **Screw Press** | Flywheel + Screw | **0.5–1.0 m/s** | Precision impression-die |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strain Rate Principle:** Hammers deliver force in **milliseconds** (high strain rate, $\dot{\varepsilon} > 100$ s⁻¹), causing adiabatic heating. Hydraulic presses deliver force in **seconds** (low strain rate, $\dot{\varepsilon} < 5$ s⁻¹), allowing isothermal conditions — essential for **superalloys** like Inconel and titanium.

---

## 4.5 Flash Formation in Impression-Die Forging

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Upper Die] --> B[Flash Land]
    B --> C[Flash Gutter]
    C --> D[Parting Line]
    A --> E[Impression Cavity]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Lower Die]
    B -.->|Generates Back-Pressure| G[Material Forced into Cavity]
    E --> G
```

> [!TIP]
> **Understanding Flash Land vs. Flash Gutter:** The **flash land** is a *narrow gap* (typically 0.5–3 mm) between the upper and lower die at the parting line. The **flash gutter** is the *reservoir* beyond the land that collects the squeezed-out excess material. The narrow land creates the back-pressure (similar to a flow restriction in fluid mechanics).

---

## 4.6 Closed-Die (Flashless) Forging Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Precision Billet - Volume Controlled] --> B[Heating to Forge Temperature]
    B --> C[Placed in Die Cavity]
    C --> D[Press Stroke Initiated]
    D --> E{Die Cavity Volume = Billet Volume?}
    E -->|Yes - No Flash| F[Complete Cavity Fill]
    E -->|No - Excess| G[Flash Forms]
    F --> H[Die Opens]
    H --> I[Part Ejected - Near-Net Shape]
    I --> J[Minimal Machining Required]
    G --> K[Trimming Required]
```

---

## 4.7 Hot Forging Temperature Zones for Steel

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Room Temperature 25 C] --> B[Pre-heat 200 to 500 C]
    B --> C[Forge Range 900 to 1250 C]
    C --> D[Upper Limit 1250 C - Below Solidus]
    D --> E[Cooling in Air / Oil / Water]
    E --> F[Hot Forged Part]
    C -.->|If Temperature Drops Below 900 C| G[Work Hardening - Difficult to Forge]
    G --> H[Reheat Required]
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Forging **above** the upper limit causes **burning** (oxidation along grain boundaries) and **hot shortness** (loss of strength at high temperature). The safe range is **0.6 $T_m$ to 0.8 $T_m$**, which for steel is **900 °C – 1250 °C**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define forging. List any three advantages of forged components over cast components.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Forging is a metal forming process in which compressive forces are applied to a solid metal workpiece, causing it to plastically deform and take the shape of the dies without any material removal or addition.

**Three Advantages of Forged vs. Cast Components:**

1. **Superior Mechanical Properties:** Forging produces a **continuous grain flow** that follows the contour of the part, resulting in higher tensile strength, fatigue resistance, and impact toughness compared to cast parts where grain structure is random.

2. **Better Dimensional Accuracy & Repeatability:** Forged parts have tighter tolerances and consistent properties from one batch to another. Cast parts often have porosity, shrinkage cavities, and inclusions.

3. **Higher Reliability for Critical Applications:** Forged components fail in a more **predictable and ductile** manner, making them preferred for **safety-critical** parts like crankshafts, connecting rods, and landing gear. (3 marks)

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Distinguish between open-die forging and impression-die forging in any four aspects.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Open-Die Forging | Impression-Die Forging |
|---|---|---|
| **Die Shape** | Flat or simple contour dies | Dies have machined impressions/cavities |
| **Material Flow** | Free flow in lateral directions | Constrained within the die cavity |
| **Part Complexity** | Simple shapes (shafts, rings, discs) | Complex 3D shapes (gears, connecting rods) |
| **Tolerance / Finish** | Poor (±1.5 to 5 mm), rough surface | Better (±0.4 to 1.5 mm), smoother surface |
| **Production Volume** | Low to medium (job shop) | High volume (mass production) |
| **Die Cost** | Very low | High |

(3 marks for any four points)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **impression-die forging process** and discuss the role of **flash** in cavity filling. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cylindrical steel billet of diameter 60 mm and height 40 mm is hot forged in an impression die to a height of 20 mm. The flow stress of the material is $Y_f = 200$ MPa and the coefficient of friction at the die-workpiece interface is $\mu = 0.15$. Calculate:
- (i) The final diameter of the forged part. **(2 marks)**
- (ii) The true strain. **(2 marks)**
- (iii) The maximum forging load using the friction hill equation. **(3 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a): Impression-Die Forging Process

Impression-die forging (also called closed-die forging with flash) uses a pair of dies that contain a **pre-machined cavity** matching the final shape of the part. The process is illustrated in the diagrams in Section 4.5.

**Process Steps:**
1. **Billet Preparation:** A cylindrical or pre-shaped billet is cut to a volume slightly greater than the part volume.
2. **Heating:** The billet is heated to the forging temperature (e.g., 1200 °C for steel).
3. **Lubrication:** Die surfaces are coated with a graphite-based lubricant to reduce friction and wear.
4. **Placement:** The hot billet is positioned in the lower die cavity.
5. **Die Closure:** The upper die descends, compressing the billet. Material flows into the cavity.
6. **Flash Formation:** Excess material squeezes out through the narrow **flash land** into the **flash gutter** at the parting line.
7. **Ejection & Trimming:** The die opens, the part (with flash attached) is ejected, and the flash is removed in a trimming press.

**Role of Flash in Cavity Filling:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The flash is **not waste** — it is an **engineering feature** that ensures complete die fill. As material squeezes through the **narrow flash land** (typically 0.5–3 mm thick), the restricted flow creates a **back-pressure** that forces the remaining material to flow into the **deeper and more complex regions** of the die cavity. This phenomenon is analogous to the **venturi effect** in fluid flow — the constriction increases local pressure. Without the flash, the metal would simply spread out at the parting line and the cavity would remain partially empty.

(7 marks: 4 for process + 3 for flash role)

---

#### Part (b): Numerical Solution

**Given:**
- $D_0 = 60$ mm, $h_0 = 40$ mm, $h_f = 20$ mm
- $Y_f = 200$ MPa, $\mu = 0.15$

**Step (i): Final Diameter (Volume Constancy)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\pi}{4} D_0^2 h_0 &= \frac{\pi}{4} D_f^2 h_f \\
D_f^2 &= \frac{D_0^2 h_0}{h_f} = \frac{(60)^2 (40)}{20} \\
D_f^2 &= \frac{144000}{20} = 7200 \text{ mm}^2 \\
D_f &= \sqrt{7200} = 84.85 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**∴ Final diameter $D_f \approx 84.85$ mm** (2 marks)

---

**Step (ii): True Strain**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon &= \ln\left(\frac{h_0}{h_f}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{40}{20}\right) = \ln(2) \\
\varepsilon &= 0.6931
\end{aligned}
$$

**∴ True strain $\varepsilon = 0.693$** (2 marks)

---

**Step (iii): Maximum Forging Load**

The maximum load occurs at the **smallest height** (largest $D/h$ ratio), so we use $h = h_f = 20$ mm and $D = D_f = 84.85$ mm.

Average die pressure (friction hill equation):

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_{avg} &= Y_f \left( 1 + \frac{\mu D_f}{3 h_f} \right) \\
p_{avg} &= 200 \left( 1 + \frac{0.15 \times 84.85}{3 \times 20} \right) \\
p_{avg} &= 200 \left( 1 + \frac{12.7275}{60} \right) \\
p_{avg} &= 200 \left( 1 + 0.2121 \right) \\
p_{avg} &= 200 \times 1.2121 = 242.42 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

Projected area at final state:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{proj} &= \frac{\pi}{4} D_f^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 7200 = 5654.87 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Maximum forging load:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{max} &= p_{avg} \times A_{proj} \\
F_{max} &= 242.42 \text{ N/mm}^2 \times 5654.87 \text{ mm}^2 \\
F_{max} &= 1,370,940 \text{ N} \approx 1371 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**∴ Maximum forging load $F_{max} \approx 1371$ kN (≈ 140 tonnes-force)** (3 marks)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]

**(a)** Compare **hammers and presses** as forging equipment. Discuss the strain rate effects on the forging process and the resulting microstructural differences. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An aluminum alloy billet of diameter 50 mm and height 30 mm is cold-forged between two flat dies in an open-die forging operation. The material has a strength coefficient $K = 600$ MPa and a strain hardening exponent $n = 0.25$. The friction coefficient is $\mu = 0.1$. The final height is 15 mm. Determine:
- (i) The true strain. **(2 marks)**
- (ii) The flow stress at the final state. **(2 marks)**
- (iii) The maximum forging load. **(3 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a): Hammers vs. Presses

| Aspect | Forging Hammers | Forging Presses |
|---|---|---|
| **Force Application** | Impact (sudden blow) | Sustained pressure (gradual) |
| **Strain Rate** | **High** (10 to 300 s⁻¹) | **Low** (0.05 to 5 s⁻¹) |
| **Energy Source** | Gravity + Steam/Air/Hydraulic lift | Mechanical (crank) or Hydraulic |
| **Cycle Time** | Short (multiple blows per second) | Longer (1 stroke per 2-30 s) |
| **Temperature Effect** | Adiabatic heating (temperature rises during stroke) | Isothermal (heat dissipates during stroke) |
| **Best For** | Large parts, open-die, difficult materials | Precision parts, impression-die, superalloys |
| **Vibration/Noise** | High | Low |
| **Capacity Range** | Up to 50,000 kg-m (energy) | Up to 50,000 tonnes (force) |

**Strain Rate and Microstructure Effects:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **In Hammers (High Strain Rate):**
> - Deformation occurs so fast that **heat cannot escape** — this is **adiabatic heating**.
> - The local temperature rise **softens** the material momentarily, but the overall forging temperature drops rapidly.
> - The microstructure shows **finer grain size** due to dynamic recrystallization and rapid cooling.
> - Risk of **cracking** at grain boundaries if too cold.

> [!TIP]
> **In Hydraulic Presses (Low Strain Rate):**
> - The slow compression allows **heat to dissipate** to the dies and surroundings, maintaining an **isothermal** condition.
> - This is **essential for superalloys** (Inconel, Waspaloy, titanium) that are strain-rate sensitive — high strain rates cause localized shear and cracking.
> - The microstructure shows **uniform grain flow** with controlled recrystallization.

(7 marks: 4 for comparison table + 3 for strain rate analysis)

---

#### Part (b): Numerical Solution

**Given:**
- $D_0 = 50$ mm, $h_0 = 30$ mm, $h_f = 15$ mm
- $K = 600$ MPa, $n = 0.25$, $\mu = 0.1$

**Step (i): True Strain**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon &= \ln\left(\frac{h_0}{h_f}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{30}{15}\right) = \ln(2) = 0.6931
\end{aligned}
$$

**∴ True strain $\varepsilon = 0.693$** (2 marks)

---

**Step (ii): Flow Stress at Final State**

Using the Hollomon equation $\sigma_f = K \varepsilon^n$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_f &= 600 \times (0.6931)^{0.25} \\
\sigma_f &= 600 \times 0.9128 \\
\sigma_f &= 547.7 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**∴ Flow stress $\sigma_f \approx 547.7$ MPa** (2 marks)

---

**Step (iii): Maximum Forging Load**

First, find the final diameter using volume constancy:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_f^2 &= \frac{D_0^2 h_0}{h_f} = \frac{(50)^2 (30)}{15} = 5000 \text{ mm}^2 \\
D_f &= \sqrt{5000} = 70.71 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Apply the friction hill equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_{avg} &= Y_f \left( 1 + \frac{\mu D_f}{3 h_f} \right) \\
p_{avg} &= 547.7 \left( 1 + \frac{0.1 \times 70.71}{3 \times 15} \right) \\
p_{avg} &= 547.7 \left( 1 + \frac{7.071}{45} \right) \\
p_{avg} &= 547.7 \left( 1 + 0.1571 \right) \\
p_{avg} &= 547.7 \times 1.1571 = 633.79 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

Projected area at final state:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{proj} &= \frac{\pi}{4} D_f^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 5000 = 3926.99 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Maximum forging load:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{max} &= p_{avg} \times A_{proj} \\
F_{max} &= 633.79 \times 3926.99 \\
F_{max} &= 2,489,121 \text{ N} \approx 2489 \text{ kN} \approx 254 \text{ tonnes-force}
\end{aligned}
$$

**∴ Maximum forging load $F_{max} \approx 2489$ kN (≈ 254 tonnes-force)** (3 marks)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in Forging Problems (Based on KTU Valuation Patterns):**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to use $D_f$ in the friction hill equation** — Many students use $D_0$ instead of $D_f$ at the final state. Maximum friction hill occurs at **maximum diameter, minimum height**. **−2 marks**
> 
> 2. **Mixing up "flash land" and "flash gutter"** — Flash **land** is the narrow gap; flash **gutter** is the reservoir. Examiners expect this distinction. **−1 mark**
> 
> 3. **Not stating units in the final answer** — "Maximum load is 1371" loses 0.5 marks. Always write "1371 kN" or "1.371 MN". **−0.5 marks**
> 
> 4. **Confusing "impression-die" with "closed-die"** — In KTU 2024 Scheme, **impression-die forging has flash**; **true closed-die (flashless) forging has no flash**. This distinction is frequently tested. **−2 marks**
> 
> 5. **Using the engineering strain formula** $e = (h_0 - h_f)/h_0$ instead of the **true strain** formula $\varepsilon = \ln(h_0/h_f)$ for large deformations. Forging always uses true strain. **−2 marks**
> 
> 6. **Forgetting to convert $\mu D / h$ correctly** — The dimensionless ratio $\mu D / 3h$ is the *friction hill factor*. Students often compute $D/h$ without multiplying by $\mu/3$. **−1 mark**
> 
> 7. **In Hammer vs. Press comparison** — Writing "press is better than hammer" or vice versa without specifying the *application context* loses 2–3 marks. The correct answer is **"hammers for large parts and high temperatures; presses for precision and superalloys."**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Forging (Module 2)**

- **Forging** is a **bulk deformation** process using **compressive forces** on a solid workpiece.
- **Volume is conserved:** $A_0 h_0 = A_f h_f$ — always true in plastic deformation.
- **True strain in compression:** $\varepsilon = \ln(h_0/h_f)$ — always use this, not engineering strain.
- **Three Main Types:**
  - **Open-Die** → Flat dies, free material flow, large simple parts.
  - **Impression-Die** → Cavity dies, flash present, medium-complexity parts.
  - **Closed-Die (Flashless)** → Precision cavity, no flash, high-tolerance parts.
- **Flash** is essential in impression-die forging because it creates **back-pressure** to fill deep cavity features.
- **Friction Hill Equation:** $p_{avg} = Y_f(1 + \mu D/3h)$ — the term $(1 + \mu D/3h)$ is the **friction hill factor**.
- **Maximum forging load** = $p_{avg} \times A_{proj}$ where $A_{proj} = (\pi/4)D_f^2$ for axisymmetric parts.
- **Forging temperature range:** **0.6 $T_m$ to 0.8 $T_m$** (≈ 900–1250 °C for steel).
- **Hot forging** = above recrystallization temperature → no work hardening.
- **Cold forging** = below 0.3 $T_m$ → significant work hardening, higher forces.
- **Hammers** → High strain rate (10–300 s⁻¹), impact, adiabatic heating, open-die use.
- **Presses** → Low strain rate (0.05–5 s⁻¹), sustained, isothermal, impression/closed-die use.
- **Hollomon equation for cold work:** $\sigma_f = K \varepsilon^n$.
- **Hot flow stress:** $\sigma_f = K \varepsilon^n \dot{\varepsilon}^m \exp(Q/RT)$ (with strain-rate sensitivity $m$).
- **Grain flow follows the part contour** in forged parts → superior fatigue and toughness.
- **Forging load** always uses **final dimensions** in the friction hill (max $D$, min $h$).
- **Selection rule:** Large + simple → Open-Die; Medium + complex + flashable → Impression-Die; Small + precision + high-volume → Closed-Die.
- **Equipment selection:** Large parts → Hydraulic press or counterblow hammer; high-volume → Mechanical crank press; superalloys → Hydraulic press (isothermal).
- **Flash land thickness** is typically 0.5–3 mm; **flash gutter** holds the excess.
- **Adiabatic heating** in hammers can raise local temperature by 50–150 °C during a single blow.
- **Isothermal forging** (press) is mandatory for nickel and titanium superalloys to prevent shear cracking.
- **Forged parts vs. cast parts:** Forging gives **better mechanical properties, no porosity, no shrinkage cavities, better fatigue life**.
- **Strain rate sensitivity exponent $m$** is high for hot working (good for superplastic forming).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rolling - Types (Hot and Cold Rolling), Rolling Mills and Products
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET524 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Rolling - Types (Hot and Cold Rolling), Rolling Mills and Products

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rolling** is a **bulk metal forming process** in which the **workpiece (stock)** is passed between **two opposing rotating rolls** (or a pair of contoured rolls) that exert compressive forces, thereby causing **plastic deformation** and a **reduction in cross-sectional area** while simultaneously **increasing the length** of the workpiece.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (Course Code: **PEIET524 – Metal Forming and Tool Design**, Module 2), rolling is classified as a **continuous, steady-state, compression-dominant** forming operation. It is the **most widely used hot-working and cold-working process** in the metallurgical and manufacturing industries because of its ability to produce uniform cross-sections at very high production rates.

**Terminological Anchors:**
- **Stock (Workpiece):** The incoming metal piece (e.g., an ingot, bloom, billet, or slab).
- **Rolls:** Hardened cylindrical tools (made of cast iron, cast steel, or forged steel) that rotate about their axes to draw the stock in.
- **Draft ($\Delta h$):** The change in thickness per pass: $\Delta h = h_0 - h_1$.
- **Reduction ($r$):** The fractional decrease in thickness: $r = (h_0 - h_1)/h_0$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture – The Dough Roller Analogy**
> Imagine you are flattening a ball of dough with a wooden rolling pin. As you push the pin forward, the dough becomes **thinner and longer**. The pin **grips** the dough and **pulls it in** automatically — you do not have to shove the dough into the pin. The two surfaces of the pin rotate **toward each other** and squeeze the dough. That is *exactly* what happens in a rolling mill — the rolls act as the "rolling pin," the metal ingot acts as the "dough," and the rotation of the rolls **drags the stock into the gap** by virtue of **friction** at the roll–workpiece interface.

A more engineering-flavored analogy is the **cloth wringer**: two counter-rotating rollers squeeze water out of wet cloth. Replace the cloth with hot steel and the water with the metal's own thickness — and you have a rolling mill.

### 1.3 Classification Overview

| Basis | Category |
|---|---|
| Temperature of Workpiece | **Hot Rolling** vs **Cold Rolling** |
| Number of Rolls | Two-high, Three-high, Four-high, Cluster (Sendzimir / Z-mill) |
| Arrangement of Stands | Single-stand, **Tandem mill**, Reversing mill |
| Product Geometry | Flat-rolling (sheets, plates, strips) vs Shape-rolling (rails, I-beams) |
| Direction of Roll Rotation | Conventional (both rolls same direction) vs Cross-rolling |

### 1.4 High-Yield Highlights

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Favourite Lines (must memorize):**
> 1. Rolling is a **2-roll, 2-friction, 2-compression** process — the rolls compress the metal and friction pulls it in.
> 2. The **angle of contact** ($\alpha$) and the **coefficient of friction** ($\mu$) decide whether the roll can "bite" the stock.
> 3. The **bite condition** or **no-slip condition** for successful rolling is: $\mu \geq \tan \alpha$ (or equivalently, $\alpha \leq \beta$, where $\beta = \tan^{-1}\mu$ is the **friction angle**).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hot Rolling – The Industrial Workhorse

Hot rolling is performed **above the recrystallization temperature** of the metal (typically **0.6 T_m** to **0.7 T_m** in absolute Kelvin scale, where T_m is the melting point). For carbon steel, the hot-rolling temperature range is roughly **900 °C to 1300 °C**.

**Why Hot Roll?**
- **Flow stress is dramatically lower** (steel at 1000 °C has a flow stress ~10–50 MPa vs. ~250–500 MPa at room temperature).
- **Large reductions per pass** (drafts of 30–50 mm on heavy ingots are common).
- **Ductility is high**; brittle materials (e.g., cast structures) can be broken down.
- **Recrystallization during deformation** means no work-hardening accumulates.
- **Lower roll-separating force and torque** → smaller, cheaper mills suffice.

**Typical Hot-Rolled Products:** Blooms, billets, slabs, plates, structural sections (I-beams, channels, rails, angles), hot-rolled sheets and coils, seamless tube shells (piercing mills).

> [!NOTE]
> **Disadvantages of Hot Rolling:**
> - Poor surface finish (oxide scale, decarburization).
> - Dimensional tolerance is loose (±0.5 mm to ±2 mm).
> - Grain structure is coarse; mechanical properties are isotropic but not as strong as cold-worked equivalents.

### 2.2 Cold Rolling – The Precision Process

Cold rolling is performed **below the recrystallization temperature**, usually at **room temperature** (occasionally slightly elevated to ~200 °C for difficult-to-form alloys like stainless steel and titanium).

**Why Cold Roll?**
- **Excellent surface finish** (mirror-like, Ra < 0.4 µm possible).
- **Tight dimensional tolerances** (±0.01 mm to ±0.05 mm).
- **Strengthening by strain hardening** — yield strength can double or triple.
- **Improved mechanical anisotropy** (preferred crystallographic texture) for deep-drawing applications.
- **Full control of mechanical properties** through reduction schedule and annealing.

**Typical Cold-Rolled Products:** Cold-rolled sheets and strips, automobile body panels, appliance housings, tin-plate (for food cans), galvanizing stock, electrical steel (silicon steel) laminations, foils, cold-rolled bars, precision tubes.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students confuse **warm rolling** with **cold rolling**. Warm rolling is done **between 0.3 T_m and 0.5 T_m** (e.g., 200–600 °C for steel), which is **above room temperature** but **below recrystallization**. It is a separate, distinct category.

### 2.3 The Mechanics of Rolling – Step-by-Step Logic

Let us follow what physically happens when a slab of thickness $h_0$ enters the roll gap and exits as a strip of thickness $h_1$.

**Step 1 — Entry of the Stock:** The leading edge contacts the rolls. The vertical compression (draft) $\Delta h$ is the difference $h_0 - h_1$.

**Step 2 — Generation of Contact Arc:** The metal touches the roll surface over a **circular arc** subtending an angle $\alpha$ (the **angle of contact** or **bite angle**) at the roll center.

**Step 3 — Frictional Drag (Biting):** For the roll to draw the stock in (rather than slip upward), the **horizontal component of the friction force** must exceed the **horizontal component of the vertical compressive force**. This yields the bite condition:

$$\mu \geq \tan \alpha \quad \text{(Bite Condition)}$$

**Step 4 — Plastic Deformation Zone:** The arc is divided into two sub-zones by the **neutral point** (no-slip point) where the roll surface velocity $V_r$ exactly equals the workpiece velocity $V_x$.
- **Entry zone (lag zone):** $V_r > V_x$ → friction acts in the **rolling direction**, pulling the metal in.
- **Exit zone (forward slip zone):** $V_r < V_x$ → friction acts **opposite to the rolling direction**, slowing the metal to enforce continuity.

**Step 5 — Exit and Forward Slip:** Because the strip is moving faster than the roll at exit, the **forward slip** $s$ is defined as:

$$s = \frac{V_x - V_r}{V_r}$$

This is a critical parameter — it controls the **coiling tension**, the **mill speed set-point**, and the **roll-bite lubrication regime**.

**Step 6 — Force and Power Demand:** The **roll separating force** $F$ acts vertically and is balanced by the **rolls' elastic deflection**, the **mill-stretch**, and the **housing load cells**. The **rolling torque** $T$ overcomes friction and the deformation work, and the **power** is $P = 2\pi N T$.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table. Every formula appears in KTU University Exams.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes / Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Draft | $\Delta h = h_0 - h_1$ | mm | Per-pass reduction |
| 2 | Fractional Reduction | $r = \Delta h / h_0$ | dimensionless | Often expressed as % |
| 3 | Contact Length (Projected) | $L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}$ | mm | Valid for small $\alpha$ ($\alpha < 15°$) |
| 4 | True Contact Arc Length | $\ell = R \cdot \alpha$ | mm | $\alpha = \cos^{-1}\!\left(1 - \dfrac{\Delta h}{2R}\right)$ |
| 5 | Angle of Contact (approx.) | $\alpha \approx \sqrt{\Delta h / R}$ | radians | Small-angle approximation |
| 6 | Bite (No-Slip) Condition | $\mu \geq \tan \alpha$ or $\alpha \leq \tan^{-1}\mu$ | — | $\mu$ is roll–stock friction coefficient |
| 7 | Maximum Draft | $\Delta h_{max} = \mu^2 R$ | mm | Limiting case at $\alpha = \tan^{-1}\mu$ |
| 8 | Roll Separating Force | $F = \bar{\sigma}_f \cdot L \cdot w$ | N | $\bar{\sigma}_f$ = avg. flow stress, $w$ = strip width |
| 9 | Average Flow Stress (for strain-hardening) | $\bar{\sigma}_f = \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon}\!\int_{0}^{\varepsilon}\!K \varepsilon^{n}\,d\varepsilon = \dfrac{K\varepsilon^{n}}{1+n}$ | MPa | $\sigma_f = K \varepsilon^n$ (Hollomon) |
| 10 | Neutral Angle (Siebel's) | $\tan \phi_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{h_1}{R}} \cdot \tan\!\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\tan^{-1}\mu - \dfrac{1}{2}\sin^{-1}\!\dfrac{\mu R - \Delta h/2}{R}\right)$ | radians | Complicated; often given graphically |
| 11 | Neutral Angle (Approx., Ekelund) | $\phi_n \approx \dfrac{1}{2}\sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\mu R}{h_1}\right) \cdot \dfrac{1}{2}$ | radians | Valid when $\mu R \ll h_1$ |
| 12 | Forward Slip (Hitchcock) | $s = \dfrac{R \tan \phi_n}{h_1} - \dfrac{1}{2}\tan^2 \phi_n$ | dimensionless | Or simplified $s \approx R\phi_n^2 / h_1$ |
| 13 | Rolling Torque (per roll) | $T = 0.5 \cdot F \cdot L$ | N·m | Assumes uniform pressure along $L$ |
| 14 | Rolling Power | $P = 2\pi N T$ | W | $N$ = roll speed (rev/s); factor of 2 for 2 rolls |
| 15 | Power (alt. form) | $P = F \cdot L \cdot 2\pi N$ | W | Substituting $T$ above |
| 16 | Strain in Rolling | $\varepsilon = \ln(h_0/h_1) = -\ln(1-r)$ | dimensionless | True strain |
| 17 | Strain Rate (mean) | $\dot{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{R}{h_0 - h_1}} \cdot \ln(h_0/h_1)$ | s⁻¹ | Important for hot-rolling flow stress |
| 18 | Spread (Brower–Beynon) | $\Delta w = 0.0014 \cdot w \cdot (\Delta h / h_0)^{0.42} \cdot (R/h_1)^{0.16}$ | mm | Empirical; usually < 2% for wide flat rolling |
| 19 | Hill's Specific Roll Pressure (avg.) | $p = \bar{\sigma}_f \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{\mu L}{2 h_{avg}}\right)$ | MPa | Where $h_{avg} = (h_0 + h_1)/2$ |
| 20 | Coefficient of Friction (typical) | Hot: 0.2–0.5; Cold: 0.05–0.15 | — | Lubrication-dependent |

> [!NOTE]
> **Where the LaTeX in the table above uses `|x|`-style bars, the symbol `\vert` is used to preserve Markdown rendering.** For example, $\mu \geq \tan \alpha$ was deliberately written without a `\vert` — but in expressions like $\bar{\sigma}_f = \dfrac{K\varepsilon^{n}}{1+n}$, the modulus-style notation should never use the pipe character `|`.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility of Rolling

Rolling is **the backbone of every steel plant in the world** — over **1.8 billion tonnes of steel** are rolled annually. Beyond steel, rolling is used for:
- **Aluminium** beverage-can stock (cold-rolled to 0.25 mm, then ironed to 0.07 mm).
- **Copper and brass** strips for electrical and plumbing applications.
- **Titanium and zirconium** plates for nuclear and aerospace use.
- **Magnesium and high-entropy alloys** for lightweight vehicles.

The **rolling mill is the single largest capital investment** in any primary metal-producing country, making its design and optimization an economically strategic activity.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Bite Condition $\mu \geq \tan \alpha$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this derivation matters:** It is the single most-tested derivation in any rolling chapter. KTU examiners will give full marks only if the **free-body diagram (FBD) reasoning** is shown explicitly.

**Setting up the FBD at the entry of the roll bite:**

Consider a small element of width $w$ and infinitesimal length $d\ell$ at angle $\theta$ measured from the **roll center line to the neutral plane** (entry side). The roll surface pushes the metal with a **normal force** $dN$ along the radial direction and a **frictional force** $dF_f = \mu \, dN$ along the **tangential direction** (in the direction of roll rotation, which opposes the metal's relative motion on the entry side).

**Resolving the forces horizontally** (the metal must be pulled *into* the roll):

The horizontal component of the normal force $dN$ (acting radially inward, i.e., toward the roll center) has a horizontal projection $dN \sin \theta$ pointing *away* from the roll bite (it tries to push the metal *back out*). The horizontal component of the friction force $dF_f = \mu \, dN$ has a projection $\mu \, dN \cos \theta$ pointing *into* the roll bite.

For the roll to "bite" the metal (drag it in), the inward horizontal friction component must exceed the outward horizontal normal component:

$$\mu \, dN \cos \theta \geq dN \sin \theta$$

Dividing both sides by $dN \cos \theta$ (which is non-zero):

$$\mu \geq \tan \theta$$

Since this must hold for **all** $\theta$ from 0 to $\alpha$ (the full bite angle), the **worst case** is at $\theta = \alpha$. Therefore:

$$\boxed{\mu \geq \tan \alpha \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \alpha \leq \tan^{-1}\mu = \beta \text{ (friction angle)}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Maximum Draft $\Delta h_{max} = \mu^2 R$

**Step 1 — Geometric relation between $\alpha$, $R$, and $\Delta h$:**

From the geometry of the roll bite (drop a perpendicular from the roll center to the strip), the contact chord is related to the draft by:

$$\cos \alpha = 1 - \frac{\Delta h}{2R}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $\Delta h$:**

$$\Delta h = 2R (1 - \cos \alpha)$$

Using the identity $1 - \cos \alpha = 2 \sin^2(\alpha/2)$:

$$\Delta h = 4R \sin^2\!\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Apply the bite condition $\alpha = \beta = \tan^{-1}\mu$:**

At the **limiting condition** (roll just barely bites), $\alpha = \beta$, so $\tan \alpha = \mu$. For **small bite angles** (a reasonable assumption since $\alpha$ is typically < 15°), $\sin \alpha \approx \tan \alpha \approx \alpha \approx \mu$. Substituting $\sin(\alpha/2) \approx \mu/2$:

$$\Delta h_{max} \approx 4R \left(\frac{\mu}{2}\right)^2 = 4R \cdot \frac{\mu^2}{4} = \mu^2 R$$

$$\boxed{\Delta h_{max} = \mu^2 R}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical sanity check:** If $R = 250$ mm (typical for a 4-high cold mill work roll) and $\mu = 0.08$ (cold rolling with lubrication):

$$\Delta h_{max} \approx (0.08)^2 \cdot 250 = 0.0064 \cdot 250 = 1.6 \text{ mm}$$

This is realistic — cold-rolling drafts are typically 0.1 to 2 mm per pass.

### 3.3 Derivation of the Projected Contact Length $L = \sqrt{R \Delta h}$

**Step 1 — Use the small-angle geometric relation:** From Step 2 of the previous derivation:

$$\Delta h = 2R(1 - \cos \alpha)$$

**Step 2 — Use the small-angle approximation:** For $\alpha$ small, $\cos \alpha \approx 1 - \alpha^2/2$:

$$\Delta h \approx 2R \cdot \frac{\alpha^2}{2} = R \alpha^2$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $\alpha$:**

$$\alpha \approx \sqrt{\frac{\Delta h}{R}}$$

**Step 4 — The projected contact length is the chord** of the roll bite arc, which for small $\alpha$ equals the arc length $\ell = R \alpha$:

$$L = R \alpha = R \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\Delta h}{R}} = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}$$

$$\boxed{L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}}$$

This is the **most-used formula in rolling-mill design** and appears in nearly every numerical problem.

### 3.4 Derivation of the Forward Slip (Hitchcock's Equation) — Simplified

**Step 1 — Define velocities.** Let $V_r$ be the tangential (peripheral) velocity of the roll surface. By **volume constancy** (incompressibility in plastic flow), the strip velocity $V_x$ at any cross-section in the bite satisfies:

$$V_x \, h_x = V_r \, h_1 \quad \text{(at exit: thickness is } h_1 \text{)}$$

Wait — more precisely, at the entry $V_x h_x$ at any point and at the exit $V_x h_1$ must equal the volumetric rate. But the standard Hitchcock analysis compares $V_x$ at exit with $V_r$:

**Step 2 — Neutral point analysis.** At the neutral point, $V_x = V_r$. The strip thickness at the neutral point is $h_n$, found from the slip-line field or from the Ekelund–Siebel friction-hill analysis. The key result is:

$$h_n = h_1 + R \phi_n^2$$

where $\phi_n$ is the neutral angle (measured from the roll exit plane to the neutral point).

**Step 3 — Forward slip definition:**

$$s = \frac{V_x^{exit} - V_r}{V_r}$$

**Step 4 — Substituting** $V_x^{exit} = V_r \cdot (h_n / h_1)$ (from volume constancy between the neutral point and the exit):

$$s = \frac{h_n}{h_1} - 1 = \frac{h_n - h_1}{h_1} = \frac{R \phi_n^2}{h_1}$$

For small $\phi_n$, the second-order correction is negligible, and the **Hitchcock formula** becomes:

$$\boxed{s \approx \frac{R \phi_n^2}{h_1}}$$

with the more accurate version:

$$s = \frac{R \tan \phi_n}{h_1} - \frac{1}{2} \tan^2 \phi_n$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example.** Take $R = 300$ mm, $h_1 = 5$ mm, $\phi_n = 0.05$ rad:

$$s \approx \frac{300 \times (0.05)^2}{5} = \frac{300 \times 0.0025}{5} = \frac{0.75}{5} = 0.15 = 15\%$$

This is a typical hot-rolling forward slip; cold-rolling values are usually 1–5%.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Hot vs. Cold vs. Warm Rolling

| Feature | Hot Rolling | Warm Rolling | Cold Rolling |
|---|---|---|---|
| Temperature | $> 0.6\,T_m$ | $0.3\,T_m$ to $0.5\,T_m$ | $< 0.3\,T_m$ (often RT) |
| Flow Stress (steel) | 10 – 50 MPa | 80 – 200 MPa | 250 – 800 MPa |
| Draft per pass | 25 – 50 mm | 5 – 15 mm | 0.1 – 2 mm |
| Reduction per pass | 20 – 50 % | 10 – 25 % | 10 – 60 % (multi-pass) |
| Total reduction | 80 – 95 % | 30 – 60 % | 50 – 90 % |
| Surface finish | Rough (oxide scale) | Semi-smooth | Mirror-grade |
| Tolerance | ±0.5 to ±2 mm | ±0.1 to ±0.5 mm | ±0.01 to ±0.05 mm |
| Friction coefficient $\mu$ | 0.2 – 0.5 | 0.1 – 0.3 | 0.05 – 0.15 |
| Lubrication | Usually none (or water) | Oil / emulsion | Oil, emulsion, or dry-film |
| Mill type | 2-high reversing, 3-high | 2-high, 4-high | 4-high, 6-high, Sendzimir (cluster) |
| Products | Blooms, slabs, plates, rails | Wire rods, bars | Sheets, strips, foils |
| Energy per tonne | Low | Medium | High |
| Capital cost | Medium | Medium | Very high |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Roll Bite Geometry – Detailed Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Workpiece h0 approaches rolls] --> B[Entry contact at angle alpha]
    B --> C{Friction check: mu >= tan alpha?}
    C -- NO --> D[Roll slips - NO BITE - Roll back the stock]
    C -- YES --> E[Lag Zone: Vroll > Vstrip]
    E --> F[Neutral Point: Vroll = Vstrip]
    F --> G[Forward Slip Zone: Vroll < Vstrip]
    G --> H[Strip exits at h1 with velocity Vx > Vroll]
    H --> I[Output: Reduced thickness strip with forward slip s]
    D --> J[Engineer must increase mu or decrease alpha or both]
    J --> B
```

### 4.2 Block Architecture of a Complete Rolling Mill Line

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph REHEAT[Reheating Furnace]
        RF1[Walking-beam furnace 1200 C]
    end
    ROUGHMILL[Roughing Mill - 2 high reversible, vertical edger] --> INTER1[Descaler - high pressure water]
    INTER1 --> INTERMEDIATE[Intermediate Mill - 4 high tandem 6 stands]
    INTERMEDIATE --> FINISHMILL[Finishing Mill - 7 stand tandem hot strip mill]
    FINISHMILL --> RUNOUT[Run-out table with laminar cooling]
    RUNOUT --> COILER[Down-coiler for hot rolled coil]
    REHEAT --> ROUGHMILL
```

### 4.3 Rolling Mill Type Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    RM[Rolling Mill Classification] --> R1[Two-high Mill]
    RM --> R2[Three-high Mill]
    RM --> R3[Four-high Mill]
    RM --> R4[Cluster Mill - Sendzimir Z mill]
    RM --> R5[Tandem Mill]
    R1 --> R1A[Non-reversing: light sections]
    R1 --> R1B[Reversing: heavy plates, slabs]
    R2 --> R2A[Uses elevators between passes]
    R2 --> R2B[Roughing in primary mills]
    R3 --> R3A[Small work rolls backed by large backup rolls]
    R3 --> R3B[Used for cold rolling of sheets]
    R4 --> R4A[20 roll Sendzimir for foil and stainless]
    R4 --> R4B[Extremely flat thin strip]
    R5 --> R5A[4 to 7 stands in series]
    R5 --> R5B[Continuous production of strip]
```

### 4.4 Hot Rolling vs. Cold Rolling Process Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Steel Mill Slab 250 mm thick] --> B[Reheat Furnace 1250 C]
    B --> C[Roughing Mill to 30 mm]
    C --> D[Finishing Mill to 2 to 6 mm]
    D --> E[Hot Rolled Coil - black surface]
    E --> F{Pickle and Oil}
    F --> G[5 stand Cold Mill to 0.3 to 2 mm]
    G --> H[Continuous Annealing Line]
    H --> I[Temper Mill for shape control]
    I --> J[Cold Rolled Full Hard or Annealed Coil]
    J --> K[Customer: Automotive White Body]
```

### 4.5 Schematic of Forces in the Roll Bite (2D Cross-Section)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ROLL[Roll]
        direction TB
        ROLL_C[Roll center]
        ROLL_C --> RC1[Radius R]
        RC1 --> ARC[Contact arc with alpha degrees]
    end
    subgraph STOCK[Workpiece]
        direction TB
        STOCK_E[Entry thickness h0]
        STOCK_M[Deformation zone]
        STOCK_X[Exit thickness h1]
    end
    ARC -. "Normal force dN radial inward" .-> STOCK_M
    ARC -. "Friction force mu dN tangential" .-> STOCK_M
    STOCK_M --> F1[Horizontal compressive stress sigma_x at entry]
    STOCK_M --> F2[Horizontal tensile stress at neutral point]
    STOCK_M --> F3[Vertical roll pressure p]
    F1 --> COND[Equilibrium: d sigma_x by d theta relation yields friction hill]
    F2 --> COND
    F3 --> COND
    COND --> RESULT[Total separating force F = integral of p dA over contact area]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagrams above are **functional block-architecture flowcharts** (per the KTU protocol) that map the process logic, since physical 2D drafting of a roll-bite geometry is best rendered on a CAD/sketchpad. The key insight from Diagram 4.5 is that the **friction-hill equation** is the heart of rolling analysis: $\sigma_x = \bar{\sigma}_f - \dfrac{2k}{\Delta h}\!\left[\tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\mu - \tan\theta}{1 + \mu\tan\theta}\right) - \tan^{-1}\mu + \theta\right]$, where $k$ is the yield shear stress.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Part A format (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Each question carries **3 marks**, expects a crisp answer of 80–120 words, possibly a short derivation, a labeled sketch description, or a direct comparison table.

#### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Differentiate clearly between hot rolling and cold rolling. List any three advantages of cold rolling over hot rolling.**

**Model Answer:**
Hot rolling is performed **above the recrystallization temperature** (typically $> 0.6 T_m$, e.g., 900–1300 °C for steel), whereas cold rolling is performed **below it**, usually at room temperature. In hot rolling, the metal is soft, the flow stress is low, and the surface is covered with oxide scale. In cold rolling, the metal work-hardens, the flow stress is high, and the surface finish is mirror-grade.

| S.No. | Aspect | Hot Rolling | Cold Rolling |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Temperature | $> 0.6 T_m$ | RT to 0.3 T_m |
| 2 | Surface | Scale, rough | Bright, smooth |
| 3 | Tolerance | Loose | Tight |
| 4 | Strength | Lower | Higher (work-hardened) |

**Three advantages of cold rolling:** (i) Superior surface finish, (ii) tighter dimensional tolerance, (iii) increased strength and hardness due to strain hardening.

---

#### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**State and explain the "bite condition" for successful rolling. What is the role of friction in rolling?**

**Model Answer:**
The **bite condition** (or **no-slip condition**) is the fundamental requirement for the rolls to **drag the stock into the roll gap** without slippage. Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$\mu \geq \tan \alpha \quad \text{or} \quad \alpha \leq \tan^{-1}\mu = \beta$$

where $\mu$ is the coefficient of friction at the roll–stock interface, $\alpha$ is the angle of contact (bite angle), and $\beta$ is the friction angle.

**Role of friction:** Friction at the roll–stock contact surface provides the **horizontal traction** that pulls the workpiece into the roll bite. Without sufficient friction, the rolls will simply "skid" on the metal surface and the metal will be pushed back out. The limiting maximum draft is $\Delta h_{max} = \mu^2 R$.

---

### 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Module-Internal Choice (Choose either A or B)

#### ⭐ OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the **classification of rolling mills** based on the number and arrangement of rolls. Compare the **two-high, three-high, four-high, and cluster (Sendzimir) mills** with respect to (i) work-roll diameter, (ii) product thickness range, (iii) stiffness, and (iv) typical applications.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

Rolling mills are classified primarily by the **number of rolls** in a stand. The configuration determines the **stiffness, product range, and accuracy**.

| Mill Type | Rolls | Work-Roll $\varnothing$ | Strip Thickness | Stiffness | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Two-High** | 2 | 300 – 1400 mm | > 3 mm | Low | Slabs, plates, blooms |
| **Three-High** | 3 (top, middle, bottom) | 400 – 900 mm | 5 – 50 mm | Medium | Rail, beam, heavy section |
| **Four-High** | 2 small + 2 large backup | 50 – 600 mm | 0.15 – 6 mm | High | Cold-rolled sheet |
| **Cluster (Sendzimir / Z-mill)** | 20 (1 work + 2 IMR + 4 ASM + 13 backup) | 6 – 130 mm | 0.002 – 2 mm | Very high | Stainless, silicon, foil |

**Schematic of four-high mill (text description for exam):** Two small work rolls in contact with the strip; each work roll is backed by an intermediate backup roll, which in turn is backed by two larger backup rolls. This "pyramid" arrangement gives **high stiffness with small-diameter work rolls** — small rolls are needed to reduce the bending moment and achieve a **flat strip profile**.

**Two-high mills** are of two sub-types: **reversing** (the strip travels back and forth, the rolls reverse direction each pass, used for plates) and **non-reversing** (continuous, used for light sections in a "looping" or "cross-country" layout).

**Three-high mills** have a middle roll that rotates in the opposite direction to the top and bottom rolls. The strip passes alternately between top–middle and middle–bottom — no reversing needed; elevators and roller tables guide the strip between passes.

**Cluster (Sendzimir / Z-mill)** uses a **20-roll arrangement** that gives extremely high stiffness (mill modulus > 5000 t/mm) and allows rolling of foils as thin as 20 µm with **flatness tolerance of < 1 I-unit**.

**(b)** A steel strip is cold-rolled from **2.5 mm to 2.0 mm** thickness in a **4-high mill** with work-roll diameter of **300 mm**. The strip width is **1000 mm**, the average flow stress is **350 MPa**, and the coefficient of friction is **0.08**. The roll speed is **30 rev/min**. Calculate:
1. The **projected contact length** $L$.
2. The **maximum possible draft** $\Delta h_{max}$.
3. The **roll separating force** $F$.
4. The **rolling power** $P$.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $h_0 = 2.5$ mm, $h_1 = 2.0$ mm, $R = 150$ mm (work-roll radius), $w = 1000$ mm, $\bar{\sigma}_f = 350$ MPa, $\mu = 0.08$, $N = 30$ rev/min.

**Step 1 — Projected contact length:**

$$L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h} = \sqrt{150 \times (2.5 - 2.0)} = \sqrt{150 \times 0.5} = \sqrt{75}$$

$$\boxed{L \approx 8.66 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 2 — Maximum possible draft:**

$$\Delta h_{max} = \mu^2 R = (0.08)^2 \times 150 = 0.0064 \times 150 = 0.96 \text{ mm}$$

Since the actual draft (0.5 mm) is **less than** the maximum (0.96 mm), the roll **can successfully bite** the stock. ✓

**Step 3 — Roll separating force** (using the simple flat-die approximation):

$$F = \bar{\sigma}_f \cdot L \cdot w = 350 \text{ MPa} \times 8.66 \text{ mm} \times 1000 \text{ mm}$$

$$F = 350 \text{ N/mm}^2 \times 8660 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$\boxed{F = 3.031 \times 10^6 \text{ N} \approx 3.03 \text{ MN} \approx 309 \text{ tonnes-force}}$$

**Step 4 — Rolling power:**

Rolling torque per roll (uniform pressure assumption):

$$T = 0.5 \cdot F \cdot L = 0.5 \times 3.031 \times 10^6 \text{ N} \times 8.66 \text{ mm}$$

$$T = 1.5155 \times 10^6 \text{ N} \times 8.66 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 13124 \text{ N·m}$$

Total power for 2 rolls:

$$P = 2 \pi N T = 2 \pi \times (30/60) \text{ rev/s} \times 13124 \text{ N·m}$$

$$P = 2 \pi \times 0.5 \times 13124 = 41225 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{P \approx 41.2 \text{ kW}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:** Students forget the factor of **2** for the two rolls when computing power. Marks deducted: **2/14**. Always state "total power for 2 rolls."
> **Pitfall #2:** Students confuse **roll diameter** with **roll radius** — if 300 mm is given as diameter, then $R = 150$ mm. Marks deducted: **2/14** if used directly.
> **Pitfall #3:** The **average flow stress** must be quoted in **N/mm² = MPa** consistently with $L$ and $w$ in mm — DO NOT convert $L$ to metres unless you also convert $F$ to N first.

---

#### ⭐ OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Derive the **bite condition** ($\mu \geq \tan\alpha$) for rolling, and hence obtain an expression for the **maximum draft** $\Delta h_{max}$ in terms of the roll radius $R$ and the coefficient of friction $\mu$.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

*(Follow Section 3.1 and 3.2 of these notes verbatim — Examiner expects the FBD reasoning + the small-angle trigonometric substitution. Mark split: 3 marks for FBD, 2 marks for trig, 1 mark for final boxed answer, 1 mark for numerical interpretation.)*

**Final result:**

$$\boxed{\mu \geq \tan \alpha \quad \text{and} \quad \Delta h_{max} = \mu^2 R}$$

**Numerical example for completeness:** If $R = 200$ mm, $\mu = 0.1$, then $\Delta h_{max} = (0.1)^2 \times 200 = 2$ mm per pass.

**(b)** With the aid of a labeled diagram, describe the **slip-line field** (or friction-hill) approach to determine the **roll separating force** in flat rolling. How is the **neutral point** located?

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Slip-Line Field (Friction-Hill) Approach:**

In plane-strain rolling, the deformation zone can be analyzed using **slip-line field theory** (for perfectly plastic material). The horizontal pressure $\sigma_x$ along the contact arc is **not uniform** — it rises from entry, reaches a maximum, and then falls at exit. This is called the **friction hill**.

**Friction-Hill Equation (Ekelund):**

$$\sigma_x = \bar{\sigma}_f - \frac{2k}{\Delta h}\!\left[\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\mu - \tan\theta}{1 + \mu\tan\theta}\right) - \tan^{-1}\mu + \theta\right]$$

where $k = \sigma_y / \sqrt{3}$ is the yield shear stress and $\theta$ is the angle measured from the entry.

**Locating the Neutral Point:**

The neutral point (no-slip point) is where the strip velocity $V_x$ equals the roll tangential velocity $V_r$. By the **friction-hill symmetry**, this occurs **just past the peak** of the friction hill. Mathematically, the condition is:

$$\sigma_x \text{ (at neutral point)} = 2k = \frac{2\sigma_y}{\sqrt{3}}$$

This equation, solved numerically or graphically, gives the **neutral angle** $\phi_n$. Hitchcock's equation then yields the **forward slip**:

$$s \approx \frac{R \phi_n^2}{h_1}$$

**Diagram description (for the exam answer):** Draw two rolls in cross-section with the strip between them; mark (i) entry thickness $h_0$, (ii) exit thickness $h_1$, (iii) the angle of contact $\alpha$, (iv) the neutral point N, (v) the entry zone (with friction acting inward), (vi) the exit zone (with friction acting outward), and (vii) a small graph beneath showing the **friction hill** (plot of $\sigma_x$ vs $\theta$) with its peak at the neutral point.

**Roll Separating Force:**

$$F = w \int_{0}^{\alpha} p(\theta) \, R \, d\theta = w R \int_{0}^{\alpha}\!\left(\sigma_x \cdot \frac{d\theta}{\cos\theta}\right) \approx w \cdot L \cdot \bar{p}$$

where $L = \sqrt{R \Delta h}$ is the projected contact length and $\bar{p}$ is the average roll pressure (often taken as $\bar{\sigma}_f$ for a first estimate, or $1.15\,\bar{\sigma}_f$ to account for the friction hill, or even higher for cold rolling with work-hardening).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Universal Pitfalls in Rolling Problems (lose 2–4 marks each):**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert roll diameter to radius** before applying $L = \sqrt{R\Delta h}$.
> 2. **Mixing up units** — keep $L$ in mm, $w$ in mm, $\bar{\sigma}_f$ in N/mm² (= MPa), and you get $F$ in **N** directly. Do **not** convert to SI and back.
> 3. **Not verifying the bite condition** before computing force — if $\mu < \tan\alpha$, the roll cannot bite and the process is impossible. Examiners **love** this check.
> 4. **Using $F$ instead of $2F$** for the total mill load — a 4-high mill has **2 work rolls**, each contributing $F/2$ vertically to the housing, but the total rolling power is $P = 2\pi N T$ with $T$ for one roll.
> 5. **Neglecting strain-hardening** in cold rolling — if the problem gives $K$ and $n$ (Hollomon constants), use $\bar{\sigma}_f = K \varepsilon^n / (1+n)$, NOT just $K \varepsilon^n$.
> 6. **Confusing forward slip $s$** with neutral angle $\phi_n$ — they are different concepts; $s$ is dimensionless, $\phi_n$ is in radians.
> 7. **Drawing no sketch** when the question says "with neat schematic" — examiners deduct **1–2 marks** even if the math is right.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden 30 — Must-Memorize Items for KTU 2024 ESE (Module 2)**

1. **Rolling** = bulk forming by compression between two rotating rolls.
2. **Hot rolling** = above recrystallization temperature ($> 0.6 T_m$); **cold rolling** = below it (RT typical).
3. **Draft** $\Delta h = h_0 - h_1$; **Reduction** $r = \Delta h / h_0$.
4. **Bite condition** $\mu \geq \tan\alpha$ — the foundation of rolling theory.
5. **Max draft** $\Delta h_{max} = \mu^2 R$ — derived from bite condition.
6. **Contact length** $L = \sqrt{R \Delta h}$ — small-angle approximation.
7. **Angle of contact** $\alpha \approx \sqrt{\Delta h / R}$ rad.
8. **Roll separating force** $F = \bar{\sigma}_f L w$ — simplest engineering form.
9. **Average flow stress** $\bar{\sigma}_f = K\varepsilon^n / (1+n)$ for strain-hardening material.
10. **Neutral point** = where $V_{roll} = V_{strip}$ — divides lag and forward-slip zones.
11. **Forward slip** $s = R\phi_n^2 / h_1$ (Hitchcock's simplified form).
12. **True strain** $\varepsilon = \ln(h_0 / h_1)$.
13. **Rolling torque per roll** $T = 0.5 F L$; **Power** $P = 2\pi N T$ for both rolls.
14. **Two-high mill** — simplest, 2 rolls; **reversing** for plates, **non-reversing** for light sections.
15. **Three-high mill** — middle roll reverses direction; no reversing motor needed.
16. **Four-high mill** — small work rolls + large backup rolls; backbone of cold rolling.
17. **Cluster (Sendzimir / Z-mill)** — 20-roll pyramid; foil and stainless steel.
18. **Tandem mill** — 4 to 7 stands in series; hot-strip mill has 7 finishing stands.
19. **Bloom** = 150 × 150 mm² to 400 × 400 mm²; **Billet** = 50 × 50 to 125 × 125 mm²; **Slab** = thickness 50–250 mm, width 500–2500 mm.
20. **Plate** = thickness $> 6$ mm; **Sheet** = 0.2 to 6 mm; **Strip** = width < 600 mm; **Foil** = thickness < 0.2 mm.
21. **Typical products of hot rolling** — blooms, billets, slabs, plates, rails, I-beams, channels, hot-rolled coils.
22. **Typical products of cold rolling** — cold-rolled sheets, tin-plate, galvanizing stock, electrical steel, foils.
23. **Friction coefficient** — Hot: 0.2–0.5, Cold: 0.05–0.15.
24. **Strain rate in rolling** $\dot{\varepsilon} = 2\pi N/60 \cdot \sqrt{R/\Delta h} \cdot \ln(h_0/h_1)$ — high values require dynamic recrystallization modelling.
25. **Spread (lateral flow)** is usually < 2% for wide flat rolling — often neglected in first analysis.
26. **Forward slip $s$** controls the **coiler tension set-point** at the exit of a tandem mill.
27. **Friction hill** is the plot of $\sigma_x$ vs $\theta$ along the bite — peaks at the neutral point.
28. **Neutral angle $\phi_n$** is the angle from the exit plane to the neutral point.
29. **Bite condition** must always be **checked first** in numerical problems.
30. **Schematic + equations + units = full marks** in KTU 2024 ESE.

---

> **END OF MODULE 2 NOTES — ROLLING: TYPES, MILLS, AND PRODUCTS**
> *(Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme — Course PEIET524)*
