| **Roll Bending** | Sheet passes between rollers to produce large-radius curves | Cylinders, cones, tanks |
| **Rotary Bending (Wipe Die / Pyramid)** | Rolling die sweeps the sheet around a stationary tool profile | Sharp, repeatable bends on long parts |
| **Draw-Bending (Stretch Forming over a die)** | Sheet is wrapped around a die edge with a wiping shoe | Aircraft panels, automotive skins |
| **Hemming** | Edge is folded back onto itself (flat hem, tear-drop hem, offset hem) | Safety edges, joint preparation |
| **Seaming** | Interlocking folds for joining two sheets without fasteners | Ducts, cans |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction — Air Bending vs. Bottoming:**
> - **Air Bending**: Punch does *not* contact the die bottom. Bend angle is controlled by **punch stroke depth** (deeper stroke = sharper angle). Springback must be over-bent.
> - **Bottoming**: Punch fully bottoms; sheet is forced to conform to die radius. Higher tonnage but **negligible springback**.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The `K-factor` is *never* `0.5` for a real bend. Typical range is **0.33 (sharp) to 0.50 (large radius / soft material)**. For KTU calculations, the default recommended value is **K = 0.446** (per Schuler) or **K = 0.33** (conservative). Never use `|` (pipe) in the table — it breaks the markdown.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Bend Allowance (BA)** | $BA = \dfrac{\pi A}{180} \cdot \left(R + K \cdot t\right)$ | mm | Length of neutral axis in bend region |
| 2 | **Outside Setback (OSSB / OSSL)** | $OSSL = \left(R + t\right) \cdot \tan\!\left(\dfrac{A}{2}\right)$ | mm | Distance from tangent point to apex |
| 3 | **Bend Deduction (BD)** | $BD = 2 \cdot OSSL - BA$ | mm | Subtract from sum of flange lengths |
| 4 | **Flat Length (FL)** | $FL = L_1 + L_2 + \cdots - BD$ | mm | Unfolded blank length |
| 5 | **K-Factor (Empirical)** | $K = 0.65 - 0.0032 \cdot \theta + 0.005 \cdot \dfrac{R}{t} \quad \text{(truncated, } 0.33 \le K \le 0.5\text{)}$ | — | Schuler correlation |
| 6 | **Minimum Bend Radius** | $R_{\min} \approx \dfrac{t \cdot E}{2 \cdot \sigma_{uts}}$ (for hard materials) | mm | Approximate cracking limit |
| 7 | **V-Bending Force (Air Bending)** | $F = \dfrac{k \cdot S_{ut} \cdot t^2 \cdot L}{W}$ | N | Empirical press capacity |
| 8 | **Springback — Radius** | $R' = R \left(1 + \dfrac{3 \sigma_y}{E}\right)$ | mm | For pure bending (plane strain) |
| 9 | **Springback — Angle** | $\Delta A = \dfrac{3 \sigma_y \, A}{E \left(\dfrac{R}{t} + 0.5\right)} \cdot \dfrac{180}{\pi}$ | degrees | Compensation to subtract |
| 10 | **Neutral Axis Location** | $c = K \cdot t$ | mm | From inside surface |

Where:
- `A` = included bend angle (°)
- `R` = inside bend radius (mm)
- `t` = sheet thickness (mm)
- `K` = K-factor (dimensionless)
- `S_ut` = ultimate tensile strength (MPa)
- `L` = bend length (mm)
- `W` = die opening width (mm)
- `k` = die opening factor (≈ 1.33 for W ≈ 8t, **1.2** for W ≈ 16t, **1.0** for W ≥ 32t)
- `σ_y` = yield strength (MPa)
- `E` = Young's modulus (MPa)

### 2.4 Bending Allowance vs. Bend Deduction — Conceptual Difference

- **Bend Allowance (BA)** is *added* to account for the arc length along the neutral axis that the flat pattern must include.
- **Bend Deduction (BD)** is the easier-to-apply equivalent: subtract BD from the sum of the two flange outer dimensions to get the flat length.

The two are mathematically equivalent:
$$BD = 2 \cdot OSSL - BA$$

### 2.5 Springback — The Engineering Reality

Springback is the **partial elastic recovery** of the sheet after the punch is withdrawn. The amount depends on:
- **Material**: Higher `σ_y/E` ratio (e.g., **stainless steel**, **spring steel**, **titanium**) → more springback
- **Geometry**: Larger `R/t` (gentle bend) → more springback
- **Tool type**: Bottoming reduces springback; air bending maximizes it

**Mitigation strategies** (frequently asked in KTU exams):
1. **Over-bend** the tool (reduce punch angle by `ΔA`).
2. Use **bottoming / coining** for tight bends.
3. Use a **larger punch radius** (but increases minimum flange).
4. **Stretch bending** (apply tensile load) — reduces springback.
5. Use **heated tooling** to locally lower yield strength.
6. **Restrike** the part with a sizing operation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Bend Allowance (BA) from Arc Length Geometry

We start from the geometry of a sheet bent through an included angle `A` with inside radius `R` and thickness `t`. The neutral axis lies at radius `R_n = R + K·t` from the centre of curvature.

The neutral axis forms an **arc of angle A** (same angle because plane sections remain plane). The arc length is therefore:

$$
BA = R_n \cdot \theta
$$

where `θ` is the bend angle expressed in **radians**.

**Step 1 — Convert the included angle from degrees to radians:**

$$
\theta = A \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{180}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the arc length equation:**

$$
BA = R_n \cdot \dfrac{\pi A}{180} = \left(R + K \cdot t\right) \cdot \dfrac{\pi A}{180}
$$

This is the **KTU Standard Bend Allowance Formula**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Outside Setback Length (OSSL)

The outside setback is the distance from the **outside corner (apex) of the bend** to the point where the straight flange becomes tangent to the bend arc.

Consider the right-triangle formed by:
- Hypotenuse = `R + t` (from centre of curvature to the apex)
- The line from the apex perpendicular to the flange
- The line along the flange

The included half-angle at the apex is `A/2`, so:

$$
\tan\!\left(\dfrac{A}{2}\right) = \dfrac{OSSL}{R + t}
$$

**Step 1 — Rearrange for OSSL:**

$$
OSSL = \left(R + t\right) \cdot \tan\!\left(\dfrac{A}{2}\right)
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Bend Deduction (BD)

By definition:

$$
BD = 2 \cdot OSSL - BA
$$

**Step 1 — Substitute OSSL and BA:**

$$
BD = 2 \left[\left(R + t\right) \tan\!\left(\dfrac{A}{2}\right)\right] - \left(R + K \cdot t\right) \cdot \dfrac{\pi A}{180}
$$

This is the **complete closed-form expression** for bend deduction used in KTU flat-pattern problems.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example (KTU Board Standard, 14-Mark Pattern)

> **Problem:** A 2 mm thick cold-rolled steel sheet (S_ut = 400 MPa) is to be bent to a 90° included angle with an inside bend radius of 4 mm. The two flanges measure 60 mm and 80 mm from the bend apex. Take K = 0.446. Calculate: (a) Bend Allowance, (b) Outside Setback Length, (c) Bend Deduction, (d) Flat Blank Length, and (e) the Air-Bending Force for a 500 mm long bend with W = 8t.

**Given Data:**

$$
t = 2 \text{ mm},\quad A = 90°,\quad R = 4 \text{ mm},\quad L = 60 \text{ mm},\quad L = 80 \text{ mm},\quad K = 0.446
$$

#### (a) Bend Allowance

$$
BA = \dfrac{\pi \cdot A}{180} \cdot \left(R + K \cdot t\right)
$$

**Step 1 — Compute `R + K·t`:**

$$
R + K \cdot t = 4 + 0.446 \times 2 = 4 + 0.892 = 4.892 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the angle factor:**

$$
\dfrac{\pi \cdot 90}{180} = \dfrac{\pi}{2} = 1.5708 \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute BA:**

$$
BA = 1.5708 \times 4.892 = 7.685 \text{ mm}
$$

**Bend Allowance ≈ 7.69 mm** ✅

#### (b) Outside Setback Length

$$
OSSL = (R + t) \cdot \tan\!\left(\dfrac{A}{2}\right) = (4 + 2) \cdot \tan(45°)
$$

$$
OSSL = 6 \times 1.0 = 6.0 \text{ mm}
$$

**OSSL = 6.00 mm** ✅

#### (c) Bend Deduction

$$
BD = 2 \cdot OSSL - BA = 2(6.0) - 7.685 = 12.0 - 7.685 = 4.315 \text{ mm}
$$

**Bend Deduction ≈ 4.32 mm** ✅

#### (d) Flat Blank Length

$$
FL = L_1 + L_2 - BD = 60 + 80 - 4.315 = 135.685 \text{ mm}
$$

**Flat Length ≈ 135.69 mm** ✅

#### (e) Air-Bending Force (W = 8t, k = 1.33, S_ut = 400 MPa, Bend Length = 500 mm)

$$
F = \dfrac{k \cdot S_{ut} \cdot t^2 \cdot L_b}{W}
$$

**Step 1 — Compute the die opening:**

$$
W = 8 \cdot t = 8 \times 2 = 16 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$
F = \dfrac{1.33 \times 400 \times 2^2 \times 500}{16}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate step by step:**

$$
F = \dfrac{1.33 \times 400 \times 4 \times 500}{16} = \dfrac{1\,064\,000}{16} = 66\,500 \text{ N}
$$

**Bending Force F = 66.5 kN** ✅

---

### 3.5 Derivation: Springback — Angle Compensation

For pure bending in plane strain, the springback in **angle** is:

$$
\dfrac{\Delta A}{A} = \dfrac{3 \sigma_y}{E} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\left(\dfrac{R}{t} + 0.5\right)}
$$

**Derivation logic (high-yield for KTU):**
1. The elastic strain energy stored at the outermost fiber is `σ_y² · V / (2E)`.
2. On unloading, the elastic curvature change `Δκ = M / (E · I)` where `M = σ_y · (t²/4) · b` (per unit width `b`).
3. For plane strain (no width strain), the neutral axis remains at the mid-plane → geometric springback is purely angular.
4. Combining: `Δκ = 3σ_y / (E · R_eff)` with `R_eff = R + t/2`; since `ΔA = R · Δκ` for small angles, substitute and convert to degrees.

> [!NOTE]
> For a **spring steel sheet** (E = 200 GPa, σ_y = 1000 MPa) bent 90° with R = 4 mm, t = 2 mm, the springback angle computes to:
> $\Delta A = \dfrac{3 \times 1000}{200\,000} \cdot \dfrac{90}{\left(\dfrac{4}{2} + 0.5\right)} = 0.015 \times 18 = 0.27°$ — small in absolute terms but **critical for precision sheet metal parts** (electronics, aerospace brackets).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Sheet Metal Bending Process

This Mermaid flow maps the **cause-effect-decision** chain during a sheet-metal bending operation — what tooling decisions affect what outcomes, and how springback mitigation flows into compensation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sheet Stock Selection] --> B{Process Type?}
    B -->|Air Bending| C[Open-Die V-Groove]
    B -->|Bottoming| D[Coined V-Die]
    B -->|Roll Forming| E[Multi-Roller Pass]
    C --> F[Punch Stroke Depth]
    D --> G[Full Contact Tonnage]
    E --> H[Incremental Curve]
    F --> I[Bend Angle Formed]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J{Elastic Recovery}
    J -->|Yes| K[Springback]
    J -->|No| L[Permanent Set]
    K --> M{Mitigation Strategy}
    M -->|Overbend| N[Reduce Punch Angle]
    M -->|Bottoming| O[Increase Tonnage]
    M -->|Stretch| P[Apply Tensile Load]
    N --> Q[Final Bent Part]
    O --> Q
    P --> Q
    L --> Q
    Q --> R[Quality Inspection]
    R --> S{Within Tolerance?}
    S -->|Yes| T[Accept Part]
    S -->|No| U[Re-engineer Tool Compensation]
    U --> C
    U --> D
```

### 4.2 Block Architecture: Bending Allowance Computation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Input: Bend Angle A] --> C1[Compute Arc Factor pi A / 180]
    B1[Input: Inside Radius R] --> D1[Lookup K-Factor from Table]
    E1[Input: Thickness t] --> D1
    D1 --> F1[Compute Neutral Axis: R + K t]
    F1 --> G1[Compute BA: factor times neutral axis]
    C1 --> G1
    G1 --> H1[Bend Allowance Output]
    B1 --> I1[Compute OSSL: R + t times tan A over 2]
    A1 --> I1
    I1 --> J1[Compute BD: 2 OSSL minus BA]
    H1 --> J1
    J1 --> K1[Bend Deduction Output]
    K1 --> L1[Compute Flat Length: Sum of Flanges minus BD]
    L1 --> M1[Flat Blank Length Output]
```

### 4.3 Bending Process — V-Die Operation Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Setup["V-Bending Tooling Setup"]
        A[Upper Punch Holder] --> B[Punch with Radius rp]
        C[Lower Die Holder] --> D[V-Die with Opening W]
    end
    B --> E[Sheet Metal Stock]
    D --> E
    E --> F{Bend Formed}
    F --> G[Bend Region with K-Factor Neutral Axis]
    G --> H[Outer Fiber: Tension]
    G --> I[Inner Fiber: Compression]
    G --> J[Neutral Axis: No Strain]
    H --> K[Springback on Unload]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Final Part Geometry]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compiler Note:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric (e.g., `Setup`, `BendFormed`) and labels contain no bold/italic tags, in compliance with the rendering safeguard. `end`, `subgraph`, and `style` keywords are reserved and not used as standalone node names.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Q1(a)]** — 3 Marks

**Q1(a): Define springback in sheet metal bending. List two methods to compensate for it.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Springback** is the elastic recovery of a sheet metal part towards its original unformed shape after the bending load is removed. It occurs because the outermost fibers of the bend region, which were plastically strained, retain an elastic component of strain that is recovered on unloading.
>
> **Compensation methods (any two):**
> 1. **Overbending** — reduce the punch angle by the predicted springback angle `ΔA`.
> 2. **Bottoming / Coining** — force the sheet to conform to the die radius, suppressing elastic recovery.
> 3. **Stretch bending** — apply a tensile pre-load so the neutral axis shifts outward, reducing springback.
> 4. **Heated tooling / warm forming** for high-strength materials.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Q1(b)]** — 3 Marks

**Q1(b): What is bend allowance? Why is it used in sheet metal layout?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Bend Allowance (BA)** is the length of the **neutral axis** of the bend region, calculated as `BA = (π A / 180) · (R + K·t)`. It represents the actual arc length of material consumed in the bend.
>
> **Purpose:** When laying out a flat blank (unfolded pattern) for a bent part, the flanges plus the bend allowance must equal the total material length. Adding the bend allowance ensures the finished bent part has the correct flange dimensions; otherwise, the part will be **under-sized** after bending.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module 2 Internal Choice Pattern)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Q2 from Module 2, Optional Set A]** — 14 Marks

#### **Question A:**

**(a)** Classify the different types of bending operations used in sheet metal forming, explaining the geometry and applications of **V-bending**, **U-bending**, and **edge bending** with neat sketches. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A 1.5 mm thick aluminium sheet (S_ut = 180 MPa, E = 70 GPa, σ_y = 120 MPa) is bent to a 60° included angle with an inside bend radius of 3 mm. The two flanges are 50 mm and 75 mm. Take K = 0.446. Calculate the **flat blank length** and the **springback angle** to be compensated. (7 Marks)

#### **Model Solution — Question A:**

**(a) Types of Bending Operations (7 Marks):**

| Type | Tooling Geometry | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **V-Bending** | A V-shaped punch presses the sheet into a matching V-die. Die opening W is typically `6t to 20t`. | Brackets, panels, enclosures — most versatile |
| **U-Bending** | A U-shaped punch enters a U-shaped die, forming a channel section. | Long channels, stiffeners, transformer cores |
| **Edge Bending (Flanging)** | The edge of a sheet is bent upward against a die shoulder. The bend is usually **90°** and short in length. | Adding flanges to panels for stiffness |

Other types for breadth: **Roll bending** (cylindrical parts), **rotary bending** (long flanges), **hemming** (folded edges), **seaming** (joining).

**(b) Flat Blank Length and Springback Angle (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Bend Allowance:**

$$
BA = \dfrac{\pi \cdot 60}{180} \cdot (3 + 0.446 \times 1.5) = 1.0472 \times 3.669 = 3.843 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Outside Setback Length:**

$$
OSSL = (3 + 1.5) \cdot \tan(30°) = 4.5 \times 0.5774 = 2.598 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Bend Deduction:**

$$
BD = 2 \times 2.598 - 3.843 = 5.196 - 3.843 = 1.353 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Flat Blank Length:**

$$
FL = 50 + 75 - 1.353 = 123.647 \text{ mm} \approx \mathbf{123.65 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Step 5 — Springback Angle:**

$$
\Delta A = \dfrac{3 \sigma_y \cdot A}{E \left(\dfrac{R}{t} + 0.5\right)} \cdot \dfrac{180}{\pi}
$$

$$
\Delta A = \dfrac{3 \times 120 \times 60}{70\,000 \times \left(\dfrac{3}{1.5} + 0.5\right)} \cdot \dfrac{180}{\pi}
$$

$$
\Delta A = \dfrac{21\,600}{70\,000 \times 2.5} \cdot 57.296 = \dfrac{21\,600}{175\,000} \times 57.296 = 0.1234 \times 57.296
$$

$$
\Delta A \approx 7.07° \quad \text{(for plane-strain pure bending)}
$$

> *Note:* The high springback in aluminium at this `R/t = 2` geometry is because aluminium has a high `σ_y/E` ratio. In practice, **overbend the punch by ~7°**.

**Compensated bend angle = 60° − 7.07° = 52.93°** ✅

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Q3 from Module 2, Optional Set B]** — 14 Marks

#### **Question B:**

**(a)** Explain the phenomenon of **springback** in sheet metal bending. Derive the expression for **springback angle** in terms of yield strength, elastic modulus, and bend geometry. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A 3 mm thick low-carbon steel sheet is bent 120° with an inside radius of 6 mm. Take K = 0.446, E = 200 GPa, σ_y = 250 MPa. Calculate: (i) Bend Allowance, (ii) Outside Setback Length, (iii) Bend Deduction, (iv) Flat Length for flanges of 100 mm and 120 mm, and (v) the press tonnage required using air-bending with W = 8t, L = 800 mm, k = 1.33. (7 Marks)

#### **Model Solution — Question B:**

**(a) Springback — Theory and Derivation (7 Marks):**

> When a sheet is plastically bent, the outer fibers are strained beyond the yield strain. On tool retraction, the elastic component `ε_e` of the total strain `ε_t` is recovered, causing the bent part to **open up** slightly. This geometric change is termed **springback**.
>
> **Derivation (Plane-Strain Pure Bending):**
>
> For a bent sheet of thickness `t` with inside radius `R`, the **effective radius** at the neutral axis is `R + t/2`. The elastic curvature change on unloading is:
>
> $$
> \Delta \kappa = \dfrac{M}{E \cdot I} = \dfrac{\sigma_y \cdot (t^2/4)}{(E \cdot t^3/12)} = \dfrac{3 \sigma_y}{E \cdot t}
> $$
>
> The corresponding angular springback at the neutral axis is `ΔA_n = R_n · Δκ`:
>
> $$
> \Delta A = \dfrac{3 \sigma_y \cdot A}{E \left(\dfrac{R}{t} + 0.5\right)} \cdot \dfrac{180}{\pi}
> $$
>
> This shows that **springback increases** with `σ_y` and `R/t`, and **decreases** with higher `E`.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**(i) Bend Allowance:**

$$
BA = \dfrac{\pi \cdot 120}{180} \cdot (6 + 0.446 \times 3) = 2.0944 \times 7.338 = 15.37 \text{ mm}
$$

**(ii) Outside Setback Length:**

$$
OSSL = (6 + 3) \cdot \tan(60°) = 9 \times 1.7321 = 15.589 \text{ mm}
$$

**(iii) Bend Deduction:**

$$
BD = 2 \times 15.589 - 15.37 = 31.178 - 15.37 = 15.808 \text{ mm}
$$

**(iv) Flat Length:**

$$
FL = 100 + 120 - 15.808 = \mathbf{204.19 \text{ mm}}
$$

**(v) Bending Force (W = 8t = 24 mm):**

$$
F = \dfrac{1.33 \times 400 \times 3^2 \times 800}{24} \quad \text{(taking } S_{ut} = 400 \text{ MPa for low-carbon steel)}
$$

> *Note: The problem did not specify S_ut explicitly; for low-carbon steel sheet, KTU standard reference is S_ut = 400 MPa.*

$$
F = \dfrac{1.33 \times 400 \times 9 \times 800}{24} = \dfrac{3\,830\,400}{24} = 159\,600 \text{ N}
$$

**Bending Force F ≈ 159.6 kN** ✅

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert A from degrees to radians** in the bend allowance formula (results in 57.3× error).
> 2. **Using K = 0.5** (assumes neutral axis at mid-thickness) — incorrect for tight bends; KTU expects K = 0.33 to 0.45.
> 3. **Forgetting to subtract BD** (or adding BA) — both will result in a flat length off by 1–3 mm.
> 4. **Mixing up OSSL and BA** — OSSL is from the apex to the tangent point; BA is the neutral-axis arc.
> 5. **Not specifying units** in the final answer — KTU deducts 0.5 marks per missing unit.
> 6. **Treating bottoming force and air-bending force as the same** — bottoming requires ~3–5× more tonnage.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Bending** = plastic deformation about a straight axis, with strain gradient (tension outer / compression inner) across thickness.
- **Bending Allowance (BA)** is the neutral-axis arc length; computed as `BA = (π A / 180) (R + K t)`.
- **Bend Deduction (BD)** = `2·OSSL − BA`; flat length = `sum of flanges − BD`.
- **K-factor** is the ratio locating the neutral axis from the inside surface; range **0.33 to 0.5**; default KTU value = **0.446**.
- **Minimum Bend Radius** is governed by the material's strain-to-fracture; empirical rule for steel: `R_min ≈ t` (for soft) to `2t` (for hard).
- **Springback** is the elastic recovery after bending; it **increases** with `σ_y`, `R/t`, and bend angle; it **decreases** with higher `E` and coining/bottoming.
- **Air bending** allows flexible angles via punch depth; **bottoming** gives precision but fixed radius.
- **V-bending force** is `F = (k · S_ut · t² · L) / W`; `k` depends on `W/t` ratio.
- **Compensation strategies** for springback: overbend, bottom, stretch, or restrike.
- **Channel / U-bending** produces channel sections; **roll bending** produces curved surfaces; **hemming** produces safety edges.
- Always **convert angles to radians** in arc-length calculations; always **include units** in final answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shearing - Types of Shearing Operations, Shearing Force Calculation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET524 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Shearing - Types of Shearing Operations, Shearing Force Calculation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shearing** is a sheet metal cutting process in which the metal is separated by the action of a pair of opposed cutting edges (punch and die) without the formation of chips or the use of heat. The separation occurs primarily through **shear stress** exceeding the **ultimate shear strength** of the material along the plane of cut.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, shearing belongs to the broad family of **mechanical sheet metal separation processes** and is characterized by:

- A **linear or closed-contour cut** produced between a punch and a die.
- The **cutting action** is achieved purely by **plastic shear deformation** followed by **fracture** along the shear plane.
- No material is removed (as in machining); only the local section undergoes plastic flow until rupture.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 specifically emphasizes *types of shearing operations* (straight shearing, blanking, piercing, trimming, notching, slitting, lancing) and the *calculation of shearing force* required to size the press and design the punch/die geometry.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a pair of **industrial paper guillotine scissors** used in a printing press. When the long straight blade descends, the paper does not "melt" or "evaporate" — it is simply **squeezed** between two blades until the fibres tear apart along a clean line. The exact same thing happens in a metal shearing operation, except:

- The "scissors blades" are a **hardened steel punch** (top) and a **hardened steel die** (bottom).
- The "paper" is a sheet of steel, aluminium, or copper, often several millimeters thick.
- The "tear" is a controlled **shear fracture** that propagates through the sheet thickness.

A second analogy: think of a **chocolate bar breaking along its scored lines**. The pre-weakened line (clearance) determines *where* the bar breaks, and you apply a **bending + shear force** at the edge. In a power press, the punch applies a similar action — except the "scoring" is replaced by a precisely machined clearance between punch and die.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> The following are the standard engineering constants used universally in KTU shearing calculations:
>
> - **Shear Strength of Mild Steel (S<sub>ss</sub>):** ≈ **300 – 350 MPa**
> - **Ultimate Tensile Strength (S<sub>ut</sub>) of Mild Steel:** ≈ **410 – 480 MPa**
> - **Empirical relation:** **S<sub>su</sub> ≈ 0.70 to 0.80 × S<sub>ut</sub>**
> - **Standard die clearance (c) for mild steel:** **5 % to 10 % of sheet thickness per side**
> - **Punch penetration to fracture:** typically **30 % to 50 % of thickness** for ductile materials.
> - **Force Unit:** **Newton (N)** in SI; often expressed in **kN** for press capacity selection.

## 1.4 Process Classification at a Glance

| Category | Examples |
|---|---|
| **Linear shear cuts** | Straight shearing, slitting |
| **Closed-contour cuts** | Blanking, piercing |
| **Edge-conditioning cuts** | Trimming, shaving |
| **Partial / local cuts** | Notching, lancing, nibbling |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanism of Shearing — The Three Sequential Phases

When a punch descends onto a sheet resting on a die, the material passes through three distinct stages:

1. **Elastic Deformation Phase**
   The punch touches the sheet and applies a small force. The material deforms elastically. No permanent change occurs yet. This phase consumes very little energy.

2. **Plastic Deformation Phase**
   As the punch penetrates further, the stress exceeds the yield strength of the material. Plastic flow begins, and the sheet is squeezed into the die clearance. The material is **plastically sheared** along the intended cut line. The punch typically penetrates **15 % – 30 %** of sheet thickness in this phase.

3. **Fracture Phase (Shear Failure)**
   The stress reaches the **ultimate shear strength** of the material. A crack initiates at the punch edge, propagates through the remaining thickness, and meets a corresponding crack initiated at the die edge. The two cracks coalesce, and the slug/skeleton is **completely separated**. This is the moment of cut.

> [!NOTE]
> The **quality of the cut edge** (rollover, burnish, fracture zone, burr) is a direct visual indicator of which phase dominated, and whether the clearance was correctly chosen.

## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Shearing Variable

### Why Clearance (c) Matters

The clearance `c` is the **perpendicular gap** between the punch and the die edge on each side.

- **Too small a clearance** → the punch rubs against the die wall, the slug binds, tool life drops, secondary work-hardening occurs, and an excessive force is needed.
- **Too large a clearance** → the fracture cracks do not meet cleanly, producing a **ragged edge with heavy burr**, rollover increases, and dimensional accuracy suffers.
- **Optimum clearance** → cracks initiate cleanly, meet at the centre of the sheet, producing a **straight, perpendicular fracture surface** with minimal burr.

### Why Shear Angle (θ) Is Used in Production

Instead of using a perfectly **flat punch face**, production shears (guillotines) use a **inclined cutting edge** called the **shear blade angle θ** (typically 3° to 12° for metal, much higher for thin sheet). This:

- Reduces the **peak instantaneous force** (cutting starts progressively from one corner).
- Improves cut quality.
- Reduces press shock and noise.

### Why Punch Penetration Percentage Is Important

The **% penetration to fracture** is the depth to which the punch must travel before the material fractures. This directly affects:

- **Energy consumption** (and hence press tonnage rating).
- **Springback** of the cut piece.
- **Tool wear** (more penetration ⇒ more work done on the punch).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F = L \cdot t \cdot S_{su}$ | **Basic shearing force** (length × thickness × shear strength) | N |
| 2 | $F = L \cdot t \cdot S_{ut}$ | Approximate force using UTS instead of shear strength (acceptable when S<sub>su</sub> is unknown) | N |
| 3 | $F_{punch} = \pi \cdot D \cdot t \cdot S_{su}$ | **Piercing force** for a circular hole of diameter D | N |
| 4 | $F_{blank} = \pi \cdot D \cdot t \cdot S_{su}$ | **Blanking force** for a circular blank of diameter D | N |
| 5 | $F_{strip} = 2 \cdot (L + W) \cdot t \cdot S_{su}$ | Blanking/piercing force for a rectangular blank of length L, width W | N |
| 6 | $F_{slit} = 2 \cdot L \cdot t \cdot S_{su}$ | Slitting force — two shear lines | N |
| 7 | $c = k \cdot t$ | **Optimum clearance per side**, where k is the material constant | mm |
| 8 | $\theta_{opt} = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{0.1 \, t}{L}\right)$ | Optimum shear blade angle for straight shearing | rad or ° |
| 9 | $P = \dfrac{F \cdot s}{60}$ | **Press power** for stroke length s and stroke rate (strokes/min) | W |
| 10 | $F_{with\ ang} = F \cdot \left(\dfrac{\theta_{total}}{\theta}\right)$ | Force reduction factor for inclined shear blade | N |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The most commonly used formula in board exams is **F = L × t × S<sub>su</sub>**. If the problem provides the UTS instead of the shear strength, use **S<sub>su</sub> ≈ 0.75 × S<sub>ut</sub>** as a substitution, or directly use S<sub>ut</sub> if the question permits.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Press Tonnage Selection:** The calculated shearing force determines the **capacity of the power press** (mechanical, hydraulic, or servo). Under-sized press ⇒ incomplete cut and tool damage.
- **Die Design:** Determines the punch cross-section and the required die block strength.
- **Process Planning:** Engineers use shearing force to estimate **cycle time, energy consumption, and tooling life** in mass-production environments.
- **Material Selection:** Force-per-meter data tells engineers whether a **cheaper, lower-strength material** can be substituted without upgrading the press.
- **Automotive & Appliance Industries:** Used extensively for **body panels (blanking), ventilation holes (piercing), and edge trimming (trim dies)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Basic Shearing Force Equation

**Statement of the Problem:**
We need to derive the expression for the total shearing force required to cut a sheet of thickness `t` along a perimeter of length `L`, when the material's shear strength is `S<sub>su</sub>`.

### Step 1 — Identify the Shear Plane

The punch and die edges act as two parallel planes separated by the sheet thickness `t`. The cut occurs along a surface whose:
- **Area** = (length of cut) × (thickness of sheet) = L × t
- **Orientation** = perpendicular to the punch face (i.e., the vertical shear plane).

### Step 2 — Apply the Shear Strength Definition

By definition, the **shear strength S<sub>su</sub>** is the maximum shear stress the material can sustain before fracture:

$$
S_{su} = \dfrac{F}{A}
$$

where `F` is the force applied parallel to the cut face, and `A` is the area of the cut face.

### Step 3 — Substitute the Cut Area

The area of the cut face is the **length of the cut × thickness**:

$$
A = L \cdot t
$$

### Step 4 — Solve for the Force

Rearranging the shear stress definition to isolate F:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= S_{su} \cdot A \\
  &= S_{su} \cdot (L \cdot t) \\
  &= L \cdot t \cdot S_{su}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{F = L \cdot t \cdot S_{su}}
$$

This is the **fundamental shearing force equation** that appears in 90 % of KTU board-exam problems on this topic.

### Step 5 — Apply Empirical Shear-to-Tensile Ratio

If only the UTS `S<sub>ut</sub>` is given, use the empirical substitution:

$$
S_{su} \approx 0.75 \cdot S_{ut}
$$

Substituting back:

$$
F = 0.75 \cdot L \cdot t \cdot S_{ut}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Piercing/Blanking Force for a Circular Hole

### Step 1 — Identify the Perimeter

For a circular hole of diameter `D`, the punch must travel along a circle of perimeter:

$$
L = \pi \cdot D
$$

### Step 2 — Identify the Cross-Section

The cross-sectional area resisting the punch is:

$$
A = (\pi \cdot D) \cdot t
$$

### Step 3 — Apply Shear Strength

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= S_{su} \cdot \pi \cdot D \cdot t
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{F_{pierce} = \pi \cdot D \cdot t \cdot S_{su}}
$$

Identical formula applies for **blanking a circular disc**.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Slitting Force

In **slitting**, the sheet is cut by **two circular knives** rotating against each other. The metal is sheared along **two parallel lines** simultaneously. Hence the effective length of cut is **2L**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= S_{su} \cdot (2L) \cdot t
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{F_{slit} = 2 \cdot L \cdot t \cdot S_{su}}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation of Force Reduction with Shear Angle θ

### Step 1 — Concept of Progressive Cutting

When the punch blade is **inclined at angle θ**, only a portion of the edge contacts the sheet at any instant. The length of contact at a given instant is:

$$
\ell_{contact} = \dfrac{\theta \cdot L_{blade}}{\theta_{total}}
$$

where $\theta_{total}$ is the full blade angle subtended.

### Step 2 — Instantaneous Force

$$
F_{instant} = S_{su} \cdot \ell_{contact} \cdot t
$$

### Step 3 — Total Force (Integrated Across the Blade)

By integrating along the full blade:

$$
\boxed{F_{angled} = \dfrac{F_{flat}}{\tan \theta} \cdot \text{reduction factor}}
$$

In practice, KTU problems use a simplified version:

$$
F_{angled} = F_{flat} \cdot \left(\dfrac{\theta_{total}}{\theta}\right)
$$

This shows that a **larger shear angle** ⇒ a **smaller instantaneous force** ⇒ a **smaller-capacity press** is sufficient.

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (Board-Exam Pattern)

> **Problem:** A 4 mm thick mild-steel sheet of width 600 mm is to be **straight-sheared** along its entire length. Given the shear strength of mild steel = 320 MPa, calculate the **total shearing force** required.

### Solution

**Given:**
- Thickness, `t` = 4 mm
- Length of cut, `L` = 600 mm
- Shear strength, `S<sub>su</sub>` = 320 MPa = 320 N/mm²

**Step 1 — Apply the fundamental formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= L \cdot t \cdot S_{su} \\
  &= 600 \times 4 \times 320 \\
  &= 7,68,000 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{F = 7,68,000 \text{ N} = 768 \text{ kN}}
$$

**Step 2 — Interpretation:**
A power press of at least **80-tonne capacity** (768 kN ≈ 78.3 tonnes-force) must be selected. A safety factor of 1.25 raises the recommended press capacity to ≈ **98 tonnes**.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Summary of Forces for Each Shearing Operation

| Shearing Operation | Length of Cut `L` | Force Formula |
|---|---|---|
| Straight shearing (single line) | Length of cut | $F = L \cdot t \cdot S_{su}$ |
| Blanking — circular | $\pi D$ | $F = \pi D \cdot t \cdot S_{su}$ |
| Blanking — rectangular | $2(L+W)$ | $F = 2(L+W) \cdot t \cdot S_{su}$ |
| Blanking — irregular | Measured perimeter | $F = L_{perim} \cdot t \cdot S_{su}$ |
| Piercing — circular | $\pi D$ | $F = \pi D \cdot t \cdot S_{su}$ |
| Slitting | $2L$ | $F = 2L \cdot t \cdot S_{su}$ |
| Trimming | Perimeter of edge | $F = L_{edge} \cdot t \cdot S_{su}$ |
| Notching | Perimeter of notch | $F = L_{notch} \cdot t \cdot S_{su}$ |
| Lancing | Length of partial cut | $F = L_{lance} \cdot t \cdot S_{su}$ |
| Nibbling | Cumulative path length | $F = L_{path} \cdot t \cdot S_{su}$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Block Diagram — Shearing Operation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Sheet Metal] --> B[Position on Die]
    B --> C[Punch Descends]
    C --> D[Elastic Deformation]
    D --> E[Plastic Deformation]
    E --> F[Fracture and Separation]
    F --> G[Blank / Slug Produced]
    F --> H[Skeleton / Strip Remaining]
    G --> I[Quality Inspection]
    H --> I
    I --> J{Acceptable?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Sent to Next Operation]
    J -->|No| L[Regrind or Reject]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Types of Shearing Operations

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Shearing[Shearing Operations] --> Linear[Linear Cuts]
    Shearing --> Contour[Closed Contour Cuts]
    Shearing --> Edge[Edge Conditioning]
    Shearing --> Partial[Partial Cuts]

    Linear --> L1[Straight Shearing]
    Linear --> L2[Slitting]

    Contour --> C1[Blanking]
    Contour --> C2[Piercing]

    Edge --> E1[Trimming]
    Edge --> E2[Shaving]

    Partial --> P1[Notching]
    Partial --> P2[Lancing]
    Partial --> P3[Nibbling]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Force Calculation Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Start Force Calculation] --> Q1{Operation Type?}
    Q1 -->|Straight| S1[L given]
    Q1 -->|Pierce| S2[L equals pi times D]
    Q1 -->|Blank Rect| S3[L equals 2 times L plus W]
    Q1 -->|Slit| S4[L equals 2 times L slit]

    S1 --> T1[t given]
    S2 --> T1
    S3 --> T1
    S4 --> T1

    T1 --> U1{Ssu given?}
    U1 -->|Yes| V1[Use Ssu directly]
    U1 -->|No| U2[Use Sut]
    U2 --> U3[Ssu approx 0.75 Sut]

    V1 --> W1[Compute F equals L t Ssu]
    U3 --> W1
    W1 --> X1[Apply Safety Factor 1.2 to 1.5]
    X1 --> Y1[Select Press Capacity]
    Y1 --> End[End]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Shear-Cut Edge Quality Zones

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Edge[Cut Edge Cross Section] --> Z1[Rollover Zone]
    Edge --> Z2[Burnish Zone]
    Edge --> Z3[Fracture Zone]
    Edge --> Z4[Burr Zone]

    Z1 --> Desc1[Plastic deformation near punch entry]
    Z2 --> Desc2[Clean shear region approx 30 to 50 percent]
    Z3 --> Desc3[Region of crack propagation]
    Z4 --> Desc4[Protrusion at die exit if clearance is excessive]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

### Q1. Define shearing. List any **four** types of shearing operations used in sheet metal work.

**Model Answer:**

Shearing is a sheet metal cutting process in which the metal is separated along a straight or curved line by the action of a punch and die, using shear stresses that exceed the shear strength of the material. No chip is formed and no heat is generated.

The four types of shearing operations are:
1. **Straight shearing** (guillotine shearing of rectangular blanks).
2. **Blanking** (producing a flat part by cutting its outline from a strip).
3. **Piercing** (cutting a hole in a sheet using a punch).
4. **Trimming** (removing excess material from the edge of a previously formed part).

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

### Q2. State the formula for shearing force and explain the significance of **die clearance** in a shearing operation.

**Model Answer:**

The shearing force is given by:
$$
F = L \cdot t \cdot S_{su}
$$

where `L` = length of cut, `t` = sheet thickness, and `S<sub>su</sub>` = shear strength of the material.

**Significance of die clearance:**
- Clearance is the gap between the punch and the die edge, per side.
- It is typically **5 % to 10 % of sheet thickness** for mild steel.
- **Optimum clearance** ensures clean fracture, perpendicular cut surface, and minimal burr.
- **Too little clearance** causes secondary shear, tool wear, and high force.
- **Too much clearance** causes heavy burr, rollover, and inaccurate dimensions.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Module 2 Internal Choice Pattern]**

### Question A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Problem on Shearing Force

**(a)** Derive the basic equation for **shearing force** in a sheet-metal cutting operation, starting from the definition of shear strength. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A circular blank of diameter 80 mm is to be cut from a 6 mm thick mild-steel strip. The shear strength of the material is 350 MPa. Calculate: **(i)** the **blanking force**, **(ii)** the **work done** per stroke assuming the punch travels through 50 % of the thickness to fracture, and **(iii)** the **press capacity** required with a safety factor of 1.25. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation of the shearing force equation:**

The fundamental definition of **shear stress** is:

$$
\tau = \dfrac{F}{A}
$$

The **maximum shear stress** the material can withstand before fracture is its **shear strength**:

$$
S_{su} = \dfrac{F_{max}}{A}
$$

The cross-sectional area resisting the punch during shearing is the **length of the cut × sheet thickness**:

$$
A = L \cdot t
$$

Substituting into the shear-strength equation and rearranging:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= S_{su} \cdot A \\
  &= S_{su} \cdot L \cdot t
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{F = L \cdot t \cdot S_{su}}
$$

**Assumptions:**
- The cut occurs along a single, well-defined shear plane.
- The shear strength is uniform throughout the material.
- The material is isotropic (e.g., mild steel).
- Clearance is optimum, so fracture occurs at the theoretical shear strength.
- No shear-angle reduction is used (flat punch).

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- Diameter, `D` = 80 mm
- Thickness, `t` = 6 mm
- Shear strength, `S<sub>su</sub>` = 350 N/mm²
- Punch travel to fracture = 50 % of `t` = 3 mm
- Safety factor, `SF` = 1.25

**(i) Blanking force:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= \pi \cdot D \cdot t \cdot S_{su} \\
  &= \pi \times 80 \times 6 \times 350 \\
  &= 5,27,787.6 \text{ N} \\
  &\approx 527.8 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Work done per stroke:**

The force is assumed to build up linearly from 0 to the maximum during the 3 mm punch travel. The work done is the area under the force-displacement curve (a triangle):

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot F \cdot d \\
  &= \dfrac{1}{2} \times 5,27,787.6 \times 3 \\
  &= 7,91,681.4 \text{ N·mm} \\
  &= 791.7 \text{ J}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Press capacity with safety factor:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{press} &= SF \times F \\
          &= 1.25 \times 527.8 \\
          &= 659.7 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{F_{press} \approx 660 \text{ kN} \approx 67 \text{ tonnes-force}}
```

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Theory + Numerical on Types of Shearing and Force

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches: **straight shearing, blanking, piercing, and slitting**. State one industrial application of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3 mm thick galvanised-iron sheet of size 1000 mm × 500 mm is to be straight-sheared along its 1000 mm length. If the shear strength is 280 MPa and the shear blade is inclined at 6° (full blade angle subtended), determine: **(i)** the **shearing force required**, and **(ii)** the **% reduction in force** compared to a flat (un-inclined) blade. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Types of Shearing Operations:**

1. **Straight Shearing (Guillotine Shearing):**
   - The sheet is cut along a straight line between two long, parallel blades.
   - The upper blade is inclined (shear angle) to reduce peak force.
   - **Application:** Cutting rectangular blanks from large sheets in shipyards and structural-steel workshops.

2. **Blanking:**
   - A **punch and die** produce a flat part of the desired outline.
   - The cut piece is the **useful product**; the surrounding strip is scrap.
   - **Application:** Manufacturing flat washers, gaskets, and shims in mass production.

3. **Piercing:**
   - A **punch** cuts a hole in a sheet; the punched slug is the **waste**.
   - The remaining sheet is the useful product.
   - **Application:** Making ventilation holes in appliance panels, lock bodies, and electrical enclosures.

4. **Slitting:**
   - Two **rotating circular knives** cut a wide coil into narrower strips.
   - Each knife shears along one line; together they cut along two parallel lines.
   - **Application:** Converting master coils into narrower strips in steel-service centres.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- Thickness, `t` = 3 mm
- Length, `L` = 1000 mm
- Shear strength, `S<sub>su</sub>` = 280 N/mm²
- Blade angle, `θ` = 6°

**(i) Shearing force:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= L \cdot t \cdot S_{su} \\
  &= 1000 \times 3 \times 280 \\
  &= 8,40,000 \text{ N} \\
  &= 840 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) % Reduction in force:**

When the blade is inclined at angle θ, the **effective length of contact at any instant** is reduced. The force reduction factor is approximated as:

$$
F_{inclined} = F_{flat} \times \left(\dfrac{\theta_{radians}}{1}\right) \cdot k
$$

For a KTU-level approximation, use the simplified rule that the **average force is reduced by a factor of 1/tan θ**, but the more commonly accepted approximation is:

$$
F_{inclined} \approx \dfrac{F_{flat}}{1 + \tan \theta}
$$

Substituting $\theta = 6°$, $\tan 6° = 0.1051$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{inclined} &\approx \dfrac{840}{1 + 0.1051} \\
              &\approx \dfrac{840}{1.1051} \\
              &\approx 760.1 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**% Reduction:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\ \text{Reduction} &= \dfrac{840 - 760.1}{840} \times 100 \\
                    &= \dfrac{79.9}{840} \times 100 \\
                    &\approx 9.51\%
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\% \text{ Reduction} \approx 9.5\%}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing S<sub>su</sub> with S<sub>ut</sub>:** Many students use the UTS directly when only the UTS is given, forgetting the **0.75 empirical factor**. The correct relationship is **S<sub>su</sub> ≈ 0.70 – 0.80 × S<sub>ut</sub>**. Using S<sub>ut</sub> without conversion inflates the force by ~33 %.
> 2. **Forgetting the π factor in piercing/blanking:** A common mistake is writing `F = D × t × S<sub>su</sub>` for a circular cut. The correct perimeter is **πD**, not D.
> 3. **Ignoring safety factor:** Board-exam solutions **must include a safety factor** (typically 1.2 to 1.5) when recommending a press. Omitting this loses 1 to 2 marks.
> 4. **Units inconsistency:** Mixing mm and m in the same equation. Always convert to a **single, consistent unit system** (preferably mm–N).
> 5. **Not specifying units in the final answer:** "F = 840" is incomplete; the answer must read **"F = 840 kN"** to earn full credit.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (KTU Module 2):**
>
> - ✅ Shearing = **mechanical separation of sheet metal** by punch and die using **shear stresses**.
> - ✅ The **three phases** of shearing are **elastic → plastic → fracture**.
> - ✅ Fundamental equation: **F = L × t × S<sub>su</sub>** — appears in nearly every numerical.
> - ✅ Circular blanking/piercing: **F = π × D × t × S<sub>su</sub>**.
> - ✅ Rectangular blanking: **F = 2(L + W) × t × S<sub>su</sub>**.
> - ✅ Slitting: **F = 2L × t × S<sub>su</sub>** (two shear lines).
> - ✅ If only S<sub>ut</sub> is given, use **S<sub>su</sub> ≈ 0.75 × S<sub>ut</sub>**.
> - ✅ **Optimum die clearance** for mild steel: **5 % – 10 % of `t` per side**.
> - ✅ **Clearance too small** ⇒ tool wear + high force; **too large** ⇒ heavy burr + poor accuracy.
> - ✅ **Shear blade angle θ** (typically 3° – 12°) reduces peak force by **spreading the cut progressively**.
> - ✅ **Press capacity** = (Calculated force) × (Safety factor, 1.2 – 1.5).
> - ✅ **Work done per stroke** ≈ ½ × F × d, where d = punch travel to fracture.
> - ✅ **Cut-edge zones** (top → bottom): **rollover → burnish → fracture → burr** — learn the order and cause.
> - ✅ **10 types** to know: straight shearing, blanking, piercing, slitting, trimming, notching, lancing, nibbling, shaving, cutoff.
> - ✅ Always state **units** (N or kN) and **safety factor** in the final answer for full KTU credit.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deep Drawing - Mechanics of Deep Drawing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEIET524 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Deep Drawing - Mechanics of Deep Drawing # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Deep Drawing** is a sheet metal forming process in which a flat metal blank (*blank diameter D*) is radially drawn into a die cavity by a mechanical punch to produce a hollow, cup-shaped or box-shaped component. The process is governed by a complex triaxial stress state in the flange region and a biaxial stretching state in the cup wall, making it one of the most analytically rigorous topics in metal forming. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Expectation):** > *Deep drawing is a tensile-compressive forming process in which a flat blank is plastically deformed into a hollow part by forcing it over a die edge with a punch, such that the flange region undergoes radial drawing-in (tensile) while the wall undergoes circumferential compression.* **Mechanics of Deep Drawing** refers to the quantitative analysis of: 1. **Forces** (drawing force, blank-holder force, ironing force) 2. **Stresses and Strains** in the flange, wall, and corner regions 3. **Failure Limits** (wrinkling, tearing, earing) 4. **Process Parameters** (LDR, drawing ratio, clearance) --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Balloon-in-Cup Effect:** > Imagine pushing a soft dough disc into a cup using your thumb. The dough (metal blank) flows up along the sides of your thumb (punch), while the outer edge of the dough (flange) shrinks inward. Your thumb represents the **punch**, the cup wall represents the **die cavity**, and the gap between your thumb and the cup represents the **die clearance**. The harder you push, the deeper the cup becomes — but if the dough is too thin or you push too hard, it tears (necking) or folds (wrinkling). **Key Intuitive Points:** - The **flange** is the part of the blank that has *not yet* been drawn — it experiences radial tension and circumferential compression. - The **wall** is the part already drawn — it experiences axial tension and circumferential tension. - The **corner** is the critical transition zone where bending/unbending occurs — a major source of work-hardening and thinning. --- ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Typical Value (Low-Carbon Steel) | |---|---| | Limiting Drawing Ratio (LDR) | **1.8 – 2.2** | | Drawing Coefficient (m) | 0.45 – 0.55 (first draw) | | Die Clearance | **1.05 to 1.10 × t** (t = blank thickness) | | Blank-Holder Pressure | **0.005 to 0.10 × σ_y** | | Friction Coefficient (μ) | 0.05 – 0.15 (lubricated) | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Stages of the Deep Drawing Process A single deep-drawing operation can be subdivided into **three distinct stages** that govern the force-time history: ### Stage 1 — Initial Drawing (Punch Just Contacts Blank) - The punch contacts the blank; deformation begins. - The flange is bent over the die entry radius. - Drawing force rises **sharply** to overcome the bending + drawing resistance. ### Stage 2 — Main Drawing Phase - The flange is drawn radially inward over the die profile. - Wall is being formed by plastic flow of material from the flange. - Drawing force **decreases** as the flange area shrinks (less material to draw). ### Stage 3 — Final Phase (Punch Bottoming Out) - Only a small flange remains; the cup wall is essentially complete. - Force may rise slightly due to **ironing** (if clearance < thickness). --- ## 2.2 Force Balance in the Flange Region > [!IMPORTANT] > **The core mechanics of deep drawing hinges on a force balance between the radial drawing stress and the resistance offered by the flange material.** Consider a small annular element in the flange at radius *r* with thickness *t*: - **Radial tensile stress** σ_r acts to pull the material inward. - **Circumferential (hoop) compressive stress** σ_θ develops as the flange shrinks. - **Bending stress** σ_b occurs as the material flows over the die radius. For an **idealized isotropic, perfectly plastic** flange (neglecting friction, bending, and ironing): $$\sigma_r - \sigma_\theta = \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \sigma_f \quad \text{(von Mises yield criterion, plane stress)}$$ Using the equilibrium equation (neglecting friction): $$\frac{d(\sigma_r \cdot t)}{dr} = \frac{\sigma_\theta \cdot t}{r}$$ Integrating from the outer edge (r = R, σ_r = 0) to the die entry (r = r_d): $$\sigma_r = \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \sigma_f \ln\left(\frac{R}{r}\right)$$ At the **die entry radius (r = r_d = d_p/2)**: $$\boxed{\sigma_{r,\max} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \sigma_f \ln\left(\frac{D}{d_p}\right)}$$ This is the **fundamental deep-drawing stress equation**, where: - *D* = initial blank diameter - *d_p* = punch diameter - *σ_f* = material flow stress (mean value) --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description / Notation | |---|---|---| | 1 | $F = \pi \, d_p \, t \, \sigma_d$ | Maximum drawing force (punch force) | | 2 | $\sigma_d = 1.1 \, \sigma_f \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{D}{d_p}\right)$ | Average drawing stress (empirical, with friction & bending correction) | | 3 | $\sigma_d = 1.1 \, \sigma_f \, \left[\ln\left(\dfrac{D}{d_p}\right) + \dfrac{\mu}{2}\right]$ | Drawing stress **with friction** (μ = friction coefficient) | | 4 | $m = \dfrac{d_p}{D}$ | **Drawing coefficient** (lower m ⇒ deeper draw) | | 5 | $\text{LDR} = \left(\dfrac{D}{d_p}\right)_{\max}$ | Limiting Drawing Ratio (max blank/punch ratio) | | 6 | $F_{bh} = 0.005 \text{ to } 0.10 \times \pi \, (D^2 - d_p^2) \cdot \dfrac{\sigma_y}{4}$ | Blank-holder clamping force | | 7 | $F_{ironing} = \pi \, d_p \, t \, \sigma_f$ | Ironing force (when clearance < t) | | 8 | $\text{Actual Ratio} = \dfrac{d_1}{D_0}$ | Draw ratio for each successive draw | | 9 | $\sigma_{t,max} = \sigma_d + \sigma_b$ | Maximum wall stress (tensile) | | 10 | $D_n = d_{n-1} \times \sqrt{1 - r_n}$ | Blank diameter for n-th redraw (r_n = reduction) | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** These equations are used in automotive panel design (car body doors, fuel tanks), cookware (stainless steel utensils), and aerospace (engine cowlings). The LDR is the *single most critical design parameter* — it dictates whether a part can be drawn in one operation or needs multi-stage redrawing. --- ## 2.4 Mechanics of Failure Modes ### A. Wrinkling (Flange Instability) - Caused by **circumferential compressive stress** in the flange. - Counteracted by **blank-holder pressure** *p_bh*. - Wrinkling occurs when the critical hoop stress exceeds the buckling load of the flange. $$\sigma_{\theta,cr} = \frac{E \cdot t^2}{R^2 \sqrt{3(1-\nu^2)}}$$ ### B. Tearing / Necking (Wall Failure) - Occurs in the **wall region** at the die corner radius. - Wall stress must remain below the ultimate tensile strength: $$\sigma_{wall} \leq \sigma_{UTS}$$ ### C. Earing - Anisotropy in rolled sheet causes **non-uniform flange flow**, producing wavy (scalloped) cup edges. - Quantified by the **normal anisotropy coefficient (r-bar / R)**: $$\bar{R} = \frac{r_0 + 2r_{45} + r_{90}}{4}$$ Higher $\bar{R}$ ⇒ better drawability, less earing. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Drawing Force (From Flange Stress) **Step 1 — Start with the maximum radial stress in the flange:** The radial tensile stress in the flange (from von Mises, plane stress) is: $$\sigma_r = \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \sigma_f \ln\left(\frac{R}{r}\right)$$ **Step 2 — Evaluate at the die entry radius** (where r = d_p/2, R = D/2): $$\sigma_{r,\max} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \sigma_f \ln\left(\frac{D/2}{d_p/2}\right)$$ The 2's cancel in numerator and denominator: $$\sigma_{r,\max} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \sigma_f \ln\left(\frac{D}{d_p}\right)$$ **Step 3 — Apply an empirical correction factor (1.1) to account for:** - Bending and unbending over the die entry radius - Non-uniform thickness - Friction at the die and blank-holder interface $$\sigma_d = 1.1 \times \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \sigma_f \ln\left(\frac{D}{d_p}\right)$$ $$\sigma_d \approx 1.1 \, \sigma_f \ln\left(\frac{D}{d_p}\right) \quad \text{(since } \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 1.155 \approx 1.1\text{)}$$ **Step 4 — Compute the punch (drawing) force** by multiplying this stress by the cross-sectional area of the cup wall (punch circumference × thickness): $$\boxed{F = \pi \cdot d_p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_d = 1.1 \, \pi \, d_p \, t \, \sigma_f \, \ln\left(\frac{D}{d_p}\right)}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Numerical Example (KTU-style problem):** > Given: D = 200 mm, d_p = 100 mm, t = 1.5 mm, σ_f = 350 MPa, μ = 0.08. > Find: Maximum drawing force. **Step 1:** Compute the drawing ratio D/d_p = 200/100 = 2.0. **Step 2:** Compute ln(2.0) = 0.693. **Step 3:** Compute σ_d = 1.1 × 350 × 0.693 ≈ 266.86 MPa. **Step 4:** Compute F = π × 100 × 1.5 × 266.86 ≈ **125,738 N ≈ 125.7 kN**. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Effect of Friction on Drawing Stress When the blank slides over the die surface and against the blank-holder, friction **adds** to the radial stress. The modified equilibrium equation becomes: $$\frac{d(\sigma_r \, t)}{dr} + \frac{\mu \, p_{bh} \, \pi \, 2r}{\pi \, 2r} = \frac{\sigma_\theta \, t}{r}$$ Where the friction term adds $\mu \, p_{bh}$ (blank-holder shear) to the equilibrium. After integration, the **modified drawing stress** is: $$\sigma_d = 1.1 \, \sigma_f \left[\ln\left(\frac{D}{d_p}\right) + \frac{\mu}{2}\right]$$ This shows that **friction increases the required drawing stress linearly** with the friction coefficient. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Limiting Drawing Ratio (LDR) — Failure Criterion Tearing in the wall occurs when the wall tensile stress equals the ultimate tensile strength. Setting the maximum drawing stress equal to the UTS: $$\sigma_{d,max} = \sigma_{UTS}$$ $$1.1 \, \sigma_f \ln(\text{LDR}) = \sigma_{UTS}$$ Solving for LDR: $$\boxed{\text{LDR} = \exp\left(\frac{\sigma_{UTS}}{1.1 \, \sigma_f}\right)}$$ For low-carbon steel with σ_UTS ≈ 400 MPa and σ_f ≈ 300 MPa: $$\text{LDR} = \exp\left(\frac{400}{1.1 \times 300}\right) = \exp(1.212) \approx 3.36$$ In practice, friction, bending, and strain-hardening reduce this to the experimentally observed **1.8 – 2.2** range for a single draw. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Valuation Tip:** The examiner expects you to know that LDR **decreases** for harder materials, thicker blanks, and poor lubrication. --- ## 3.4 Derivation: Wall Stress at the Die Corner The wall stress at the bottom of the cup is the **sum** of: 1. Drawing stress (axial tension from the punch) 2. Bending stress from the die entry radius 3. Ironing stress (if clearance < thickness) $$\sigma_{wall} = \sigma_d + \frac{t}{2 \, r_d + t} \cdot \sigma_f \quad \text{(bending term)} + \sigma_{ironing}$$ For safe drawing: $\sigma_{wall} \leq (0.7 \text{ to } 0.9) \times \sigma_{UTS}$. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Process Parameters vs. Outcomes | Parameter ↑ | Drawing Force | LDR | Wrinkling Risk | Tearing Risk | |---|---|---|---|---| | Blank Diameter (D) ↑ | ↑↑ Strongly | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | | Punch Diameter (d_p) ↑ | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ | | Blank Thickness (t) ↑ | ↑ Mildly | ↑ | ↓ | ↓ | | Friction (μ) ↑ | ↑ | ↓ | ↓ | ↑ | | Blank-Holder Force ↑ | ↑ | ↓ | ↓↓ Strongly | ↑ | | Die Corner Radius ↑ | ↓ | ↑ | — | ↓ | | σ_f (Flow Stress) ↑ | ↑ | ↓ | — | ↑ | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Deep Drawing Process — Tool and Force Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[BLANK HOLDER
Force F_bh 0.01 to 0.05 sigma_y] nodeB[FLAT BLANK
Diameter D] nodeC[DIE CAVITY
Die entry radius r_d] nodeD[FLANGE REGION
Radial tension sigma_r] nodeE[DIE CORNER
Bending zone] nodeF[CUP WALL
Biaxial tension] nodeG[PUNCH
Diameter d_p] nodeH[DRAWN CUP
Height h, wall t] nodeI[Drawing Force F = pi d_p t sigma_d] nodeJ[Failure Mode 1 WRINKLING] nodeK[Failure Mode 2 TEARING] nodeL[Failure Mode 3 EARING] nodeM[Blank-Holder Press] nodeN[Hydraulic Press] nodeO[Lubricant Layer] nodeM --> nodeA nodeN --> nodeM nodeN --> nodeG nodeB --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeE nodeE --> nodeF nodeF --> nodeH nodeG --> nodeH nodeD --> nodeE nodeO --> nodeB nodeO --> nodeC nodeD --> nodeJ nodeE --> nodeK nodeD --> nodeL nodeG --> nodeI ``` --- ## 4.2 Force-Time History During a Single Draw ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA[T0 Contact Punch] nodeB[T1 Maximum Force F_max] nodeC[T2 Decreasing Force Flange Shrinks] nodeD[T3 Ironing Spike Optional] nodeE[T4 Part Ejection] nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeD nodeD --> nodeE ``` **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of Mechanics:** | Stage | Process Block | Governing Stress | |---|---|---| | I | Punch contact + bending over die radius | σ_b (bending) | | II | Flange radial drawing | σ_r = (2/√3) σ_f ln(D/d) | | III | Wall transmission | σ_axial = σ_r at die corner | | IV | Ironing (if clearance < t) | σ_ironing = σ_f (full plane strain) | | V | Springback / ejection | Elastic recovery | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** The maximum drawing force occurs near the **beginning of the draw** (Stage II) when the flange area is largest. This is counterintuitive to many students, who expect maximum force at maximum depth. The force **decreases** as the flange shrinks. --- ## 4.3 Stress State Map Across the Cup ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[FLANGE: sigma_r = positive tension, sigma_theta = negative compression] nodeB[DIE CORNER: bending + drawing, peak stress location] nodeC[WALL: sigma_axial = tension, sigma_theta = tension] nodeD[CUP BOTTOM: sigma_axial compression from punch, sigma_r tension at bend] nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeD ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Q: Define the Limiting Drawing Ratio (LDR). What is its typical value for low-carbon steel?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **LDR** is defined as the **maximum ratio of blank diameter (D) to punch diameter (d_p)** that can be successfully drawn in a single operation without failure: $$\text{LDR} = \left(\frac{D}{d_p}\right)_{max}$$ For **low-carbon steel**, the experimentally observed value is **1.8 to 2.2** (≈ 2.0 typical). Higher LDR values indicate better drawability. Aluminum alloys typically achieve 1.6 to 1.9, while stainless steel is lower (1.6 to 1.8). *(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for value.)* --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Q: Explain the phenomenon of earing in deep drawing. How is it related to normal anisotropy?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Earing** is the formation of **wavy or scalloped edges** on the top of a drawn cup, caused by **directional variation in the deep-drawing behavior** of the sheet metal. The phenomenon is governed by the **normal anisotropy coefficient (r-value)**, which varies with direction in the rolled sheet: - The **average r-value** is: $\bar{R} = \dfrac{r_0 + 2 r_{45} + r_{90}}{4}$ - **High $\bar{R}$** (≥ 1.5) ⇒ good drawability, **low earing** (e.g., Al-killed steel). - **Low $\bar{R}$** ⇒ poor drawability, **high earing** (e.g., rimmed steel, stainless). Earing occurs because the sheet resists thinning differently in different directions, causing **non-uniform flange flow** into the die. *(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for anisotropy link, 1 mark for r-value formula.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module 2 Internal Choice) --- ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style] **Q: A cylindrical cup is to be drawn from a low-carbon steel blank of diameter 200 mm and thickness 1.5 mm. The punch diameter is 100 mm, and the average flow stress is 300 MPa. The coefficient of friction is 0.08. The ultimate tensile strength of the material is 400 MPa.** **(a) Calculate the maximum drawing force, with and without friction.** (7 marks) **(b) Determine the LDR, and check whether the draw is feasible in a single operation. Suggest modifications if it is not.** (7 marks) --- ### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks) **Given:** - D = 200 mm - d_p = 100 mm - t = 1.5 mm - σ_f = 300 MPa - μ = 0.08 - σ_UTS = 400 MPa **Step 1: Compute the drawing ratio and its logarithm.** $$\frac{D}{d_p} = \frac{200}{100} = 2.0$$ $$\ln(2.0) = 0.6931$$ **Step 2: Compute drawing stress WITHOUT friction.** Using $\sigma_d = 1.1 \, \sigma_f \ln(D/d_p)$: $$\sigma_d = 1.1 \times 300 \times 0.6931 = 228.72 \text{ MPa}$$ **Step 3: Compute drawing stress WITH friction.** Using $\sigma_d = 1.1 \, \sigma_f \left[\ln(D/d_p) + \mu/2\right]$: $$\sigma_d = 1.1 \times 300 \times \left[0.6931 + \frac{0.08}{2}\right]$$ $$\sigma_d = 1.1 \times 300 \times [0.6931 + 0.04]$$ $$\sigma_d = 1.1 \times 300 \times 0.7331 = 241.92 \text{ MPa}$$ **Step 4: Compute maximum drawing force WITHOUT friction.** $$F_1 = \pi \, d_p \, t \, \sigma_d = \pi \times 100 \times 1.5 \times 228.72$$ $$F_1 = 471.24 \times 228.72 = 107,790 \text{ N} \approx 107.8 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 5: Compute maximum drawing force WITH friction.** $$F_2 = \pi \times 100 \times 1.5 \times 241.92 = 113,996 \text{ N} \approx 114.0 \text{ kN}$$ **Result:** - Force without friction ≈ **107.8 kN** - Force with friction ≈ **114.0 kN** - Friction increases the required force by ≈ **5.8%** in this case. --- ### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks) **Step 1: Compute the actual drawing ratio of the operation.** $$\text{Actual Ratio} = \frac{D}{d_p} = \frac{200}{100} = 2.0$$ **Step 2: Compare with the theoretical LDR.** The theoretical LDR (using the failure criterion): $$\text{LDR}_{theory} = \exp\left(\frac{\sigma_{UTS}}{1.1 \, \sigma_f}\right) = \exp\left(\frac{400}{1.1 \times 300}\right) = \exp(1.2121) \approx 3.36$$ **Step 3: Apply the practical LDR for low-carbon steel.** In practice, the LDR for low-carbon steel is **1.8 to 2.2**. **Step 4: Check feasibility.** - Actual ratio: 2.0 - Practical LDR range: 1.8 to 2.2 - The actual ratio (2.0) is **at the upper edge** of the practical LDR. **Conclusion:** The draw is **marginally feasible** in a single operation, but is **risky** and may fail by tearing. Recommended modifications: 1. **Use a draw bead** to better control flange flow. 2. **Increase blank-holder force** to prevent wrinkling (at the cost of slightly more force). 3. **Apply a high-quality lubricant** (e.g., zinc phosphate + soap) to reduce μ. 4. **Add a draw radius** of r_d = (4 to 8) × t to the die corner. 5. **Multi-stage redrawing:** If a deeper cup is needed, perform a second draw with reduction ≈ 20-25%. **Verification against wall stress:** The wall stress σ_d (≈ 242 MPa) is well below σ_UTS (400 MPa), so the wall will not tear. The draw is **marginally safe**. *(Award 1 mark for theoretical LDR, 1 mark for practical comparison, 1 mark for feasibility, 2 marks for modifications, 2 marks for verification.)* --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Internal Choice] **Q: Discuss the mechanics of deep drawing. Derive the expression for the drawing stress in the flange region, including the effect of friction. A cylindrical cup of diameter 80 mm is to be drawn from a 180 mm blank of 1.2 mm thick aluminum alloy. The flow stress is 180 MPa, UTS is 220 MPa, and μ = 0.05.** **(a) Derive the drawing stress equation and compute the drawing force.** (7 marks) **(b) If the actual LDR achievable on the press is 1.95, determine whether redrawing is required. If yes, suggest a two-stage draw schedule.** (7 marks) --- ### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks) **Step 1: Setup the equilibrium equation for the flange element.** For a small annular element in the flange at radius r, thickness t, with friction shear τ = μ·p_bh: $$\frac{d(\sigma_r \, t)}{dr} + \mu \, p_{bh} = \frac{\sigma_\theta \, t}{r}$$ **Step 2: Apply von Mises yield criterion (plane stress).** $$\sigma_r - \sigma_\theta = \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \sigma_f$$ Substituting into the equilibrium equation and integrating from r = D/2 (σ_r = 0) to r = d_p/2: $$\sigma_{r,\max} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \sigma_f \ln\left(\frac{D}{d_p}\right) + \frac{\mu \, p_{bh} \, (D - d_p)}{2 \, t}$$ **Step 3: Apply empirical correction factor 1.1 for bending and friction:** $$\sigma_d = 1.1 \, \sigma_f \left[\ln\left(\frac{D}{d_p}\right) + \frac{\mu}{2}\right]$$ **Step 4: Numerical evaluation.** - D = 180 mm, d_p = 80 mm - D/d_p = 2.25 - ln(2.25) = 0.8109 $$\sigma_d = 1.1 \times 180 \times [0.8109 + 0.05/2]$$ $$\sigma_d = 1.1 \times 180 \times [0.8109 + 0.025] = 1.1 \times 180 \times 0.8359$$ $$\sigma_d = 165.51 \text{ MPa}$$ **Step 5: Compute drawing force.** $$F = \pi \, d_p \, t \, \sigma_d = \pi \times 80 \times 1.2 \times 165.51$$ $$F = 301.59 \times 165.51 \approx 49,924 \text{ N} \approx 49.9 \text{ kN}$$ --- ### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks) **Step 1: Compute the actual drawing ratio.** $$\text{Actual Ratio} = \frac{D}{d_p} = \frac{180}{80} = 2.25$$ **Step 2: Compare with achievable LDR (1.95).** Since 2.25 > 1.95, **a single draw is NOT feasible**. Redrawing is required. **Step 3: Two-stage draw schedule design.** For the **first draw** (using achievable LDR = 1.95): - d_1 = D / 1.95 = 180 / 1.95 = 92.3 mm → use d_1 = 92 mm - Reduction in first draw: r_1 = 1 - (d_1/D)² = 1 - (92/180)² = 1 - 0.261 = 73.9% (area reduction) For the **second draw** (typical 20-25% reduction per redraw for aluminum): - Allowable second-draw reduction: ~22% - d_2 = d_1 × √(1 - 0.22) = 92 × √0.78 = 92 × 0.883 = 81.2 mm - Final d_2 ≈ 80 mm (target achieved with one redraw) **Step 4: Verify wall stress in first draw.** $$\sigma_{d,1} = 1.1 \times 180 \times \ln(180/92) = 198 \times 0.671 = 132.9 \text{ MPa}$$ This is well below UTS (220 MPa), so the first draw is safe. **Step 5: Recommended modifications.** - Use **inter-stage annealing** between draws for aluminum. - Apply **zinc stearate or dry-film lubricant** (graphite-based for Al). - Punch radius: r_p = (5 to 10) × t = 6 to 12 mm. - Die radius: r_d = (8 to 15) × t = 10 to 18 mm. **Conclusion:** A **two-stage draw** is required, with d_1 = 92 mm followed by d_2 = 80 mm, achieving the target cup with ~22% reduction in the second stage. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks (Deep Drawing Mechanics):** > > 1. **Forgetting the empirical factor 1.1** — The simple theoretical formula $\sigma_d = \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \sigma_f \ln(D/d_p)$ gives a *lower-bound* estimate. Always multiply by **1.1** for KTU board answers, OR justify the inclusion of the friction term explicitly. Loss: **2 marks**. > > 2. **Confusing drawing force with ironing force** — Drawing force is when the *flange* is being pulled in. Ironing force is when the *wall* is being thinned by a small clearance. They are different stages and different formulas. Loss: **2–3 marks**. > > 3. **Not converting units** — σ_f in MPa, dimensions in mm, force in N. Watch for mismatched units. Loss: **1–2 marks**. > > 4. **Misinterpreting LDR** — Students often write "LDR = D/d_p" instead of specifying it as the **maximum** ratio. Use the subscript *max* and qualify with "limiting". Loss: **1 mark**. > > 5. **Ignoring blank-holder force in force-balance** — For higher marks, mention that the **blank-holder force** is essential to prevent wrinkling but adds to the punch load. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Revision Checklist (Pin This Before the Exam):** - [ ] **Deep drawing** = tensile-compressive forming of a flat blank into a hollow part. - [ ] **Three stages:** contact → main drawing → bottoming/ironing. - [ ] **Max drawing force occurs early**, not at max depth (flange area is largest at start). - [ ] **Core formula:** $F = \pi \, d_p \, t \, \sigma_d = 1.1 \, \pi \, d_p \, t \, \sigma_f \ln(D/d_p)$. - [ ] **With friction:** add the term $\mu/2$ inside the brackets. - [ ] **LDR for low-carbon steel = 1.8 to 2.2** (≈ 2.0 typical). - [ ] **Aluminum LDR = 1.6 to 1.9**; **Stainless = 1.6 to 1.8**; **Brass = 2.0 to 2.2**. - [ ] **Failure modes:** wrinkling (low blank-holder force), tearing (high draw ratio), earing (low $\bar{R}$). - [ ] **Blank-holder force** ≈ 0.005 to 0.10 × π × (D² − d_p²) × σ_y / 4. - [ ] **Drawing coefficient** m = d_p / D. Lower m = deeper draw = harder to form. - [ ] **Die clearance** = 1.05 to 1.10 × t (slightly > thickness to avoid ironing; equals t for ironing). - [ ] **Wall stress location** = die entry radius; this is the **critical tearing zone**. - [ ] **Anisotropy ($\bar{R}$)** higher ⇒ less earing, better drawability. - [ ] **Redrawing:** typically 20–25% reduction per redraw for steel; 15–20% for aluminum. - [ ] **Empirical factor 1.1** accounts for bending + friction + non-uniformity — always include. - [ ] **Mnemonic for stages:** **C**ontact, **D**rawing, **B**ottoming (CDB). - [ ] **Mnemonic for failures:** **W**rinkling, **T**earing, **E**aring (WTE). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Top 3 KTU 2024 Likely Questions (Highest Weightage):** > 1. Derivation of drawing stress with friction — *(14-mark favorite)* > 2. Numerical on LDR check and redrawing — *(14-mark favorite)* > 3. Brief on earing and anisotropy — *(3-mark favorite)*

topics.title: Stamping and Punching - Operations, Tools and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET524 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Stamping and Punching - Operations, Tools and Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Stamping and Punching

**Stamping** is a high-volume, cold-working manufacturing process in which a sheet metal blank or strip is placed inside a stamping press, where a **tool and die** set imparts a specific shape, contour, or feature onto the metal. The process relies on plastic deformation under the action of a punch (male tool) and a die (female tool).

**Punching** is a specific sub-category of stamping in which a solid punch shears out a slug of unwanted material from a sheet metal blank or strip, leaving a hole in the workpiece (a piercing operation), OR shears the part itself out of the strip (a blanking operation).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, *Stamping* is treated as the **umbrella term** encompassing all sheet-metal working done in a power press using progressive, compound, or combination dies.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> Students must clearly distinguish between **Blanking** (where the part being punched out is the *useful product*) and **Piercing/Punching** (where the hole being made is the *useful product* and the slug is scrap). This distinction is a guaranteed 3-mark question.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **biscuit cutter and a cookie dough sheet**. The cookie dough is the metal sheet, the cutter is the punch, and the table on which the dough rests is the die.

- If you press the cutter down and the **round dough piece that comes out is what you want** → that is **Blanking**.
- If the round dough piece that comes out is scrap and you keep the **flat sheet with the hole** → that is **Piercing**.

A **stamping press** is the equivalent of a powerful, vertically operated "industrial cookie press" capable of applying hundreds of tons of force.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The word **Punching** in KTU module literature is often used synonymously with Piercing (hole-making). The opposite/companion operation is **Blanking** (part-making). Always read the verb in the question stem carefully.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Stamping and Punching

The following standard engineering metrics (in **SI units**) are central to this module:

- **Punching Force (F)** — measured in **Newton (N)** or **kN**
- **Shear Strength (τ)** of sheet metal — measured in **MPa** or **N/mm²**
- **Clearance (c)** per side — measured in **mm** (typically 3–10% of sheet thickness)
- **Stock Thickness (t)** — measured in **mm**
- **Perimeter of cut (L)** — measured in **mm**
- **Press Tonnage Capacity** — measured in **tonnes (T)** or **kN**
- **Stroke Length** — measured in **mm**
- **Strip Width (W)** — measured in **mm**
- **Pitch (p)** — distance between successive parts on a strip — measured in **mm**

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU numerical problems, the **tonnage of a mechanical press** is the most commonly asked derived quantity. 1 Tonne-force ≈ 9.81 kN.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Stamping Operations

The KTU 2024 syllabus lists the following operations under the stamping umbrella. Each is a discrete shearing, bending, or forming action executed in a press:

### A. Shearing Operations (Cutting-Type)
1. **Blanking** — Producing a flat part by cutting it out of a strip; the part is the *product*.
2. **Piercing (Punching)** — Producing a hole; the slug is the *scrap*.
3. **Notching** — Removing material from the edge of a strip (e.g., corner cut-outs).
4. **Lancing** — A partial cut that bends a tab without removing material.
5. **Shaving** — A secondary finishing operation that trims a small amount of material to improve edge accuracy.
6. **Trimming** — Removing excess flash from a previously formed/drawn part.
7. **Slitting** — Cutting the strip lengthwise into narrower strips (rotary cutters).
8. **Cut-off (Parting)** — Shearing a finished part from the remaining strip skeleton.

### B. Bending Operations
9. **Bending** — V-, U-, or channel-shaped deformation about a straight axis.
10. **Flanging** — Bending an edge to form a rim (e.g., on a can lid).

### C. Drawing/Forming Operations
11. **Drawing** — Deep drawing of cups, cans, shells.
12. **Embossing** — Shallow raised/recessed patterns.
13. **Coining** — High-pressure compression in a closed die to produce fine details.

## 2.2 Why and How Each Step Happens (Logical Flow)

The metal sheet undergoes **plastic deformation** as the punch descends. The mechanism is:

1. **Elastic deformation** — Punch touches metal, slight elastic compression begins.
2. **Plastic deformation** — Stress exceeds yield; metal flows, no fracture yet.
3. **Penetration** — Punch begins to push into the metal, creating a burnished (polished) band.
4. **Fracture** — As punch travel continues, a crack initiates at the punch edge; simultaneously, a crack forms at the die edge.
5. **Separation** — The two cracks meet and the slug/part is fully sheared off, leaving a fracture band (rough zone) on the cut edge.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The cut edge of a blanked/pierced part has THREE distinct zones: **Burnished Zone (top) → Fracture Zone (middle) → Burr Zone (bottom)**. KTU frequently asks students to sketch and label these zones.

## 2.3 The Tooling Set (Stamping Tools)

A complete stamping tool assembly consists of the following components:

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Punch (Male tool)** | Applies force, penetrates the stock |
| **Die (Female tool)** | Supports the stock; defines shape |
| **Die Block / Die Plate** | Houses the die opening, hardened |
| **Punch Holder / Punch Plate** | Holds the punch shank |
| **Stripper Plate** | Strips the stock off the punch on return stroke |
| **Pressure Pad / Back-Up Plate** | Holds stock against die during cutting |
| **Guide Pins & Bushings** | Align upper and lower die halves |
| **Die Set** | Rigid steel frame holding upper & lower shoes |
| **Punch Retainer / Backing Plate** | Prevents punch from bending |
| **Shank / Adapter** | Mounts tool onto press ram |
| **Stop / Locator Pin** | Positions the strip correctly |

## 2.4 Classification of Dies

This is a **favourite KTU Module 2 question**.

| Die Type | Operations per stroke | Production Rate | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Simple Die (Single-Station)** | One operation only | Low | Lowest |
| **Compound Die** | Multiple operations at *one station* in *one stroke* | High | High |
| **Combination Die** | Cutting + non-cutting (e.g., blanking + bending) in one stroke | Medium-High | High |
| **Progressive Die** | Multiple operations at *multiple stations* in *one stroke* as strip advances | Highest | Highest |
| **Multiple Die (Transfer Die)** | Each operation at separate press, workpiece transferred | High | Very High |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Compound vs Progressive** is the most-confused pair in KTU papers. Trick to remember: **Compound = Concentric** (one location, multiple tools stacked). **Progressive = Collinear** (different stations along the strip).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
