| 1 | $F = L \cdot t \cdot \tau$ | Punching/Blanking Force (ideal shear) | N |
| 2 | $F = \pi \cdot D \cdot t \cdot \tau$ | Force for piercing a circular hole of diameter D | N |
| 3 | $F = 0.7 \cdot L \cdot t \cdot \tau$ | Pneumatic/Hydraulic press force (no impact factor) | N |
| 4 | $F = 1.30 \cdot L \cdot t \cdot \tau$ | Mechanical press force (with impact/shock factor) | N |
| 5 | $c = 0.05 \cdot t$ (typical) | Clearance per side as a fraction of thickness | mm |
| 6 | $c = K \cdot t$ | General clearance formula; $K$ depends on material (3–10%) | mm |
| 7 | $T = \dfrac{F \cdot s}{1000}$ (or $\dfrac{F}{2000}$) | Press tonnage (1 T ≈ 2000 kgf ≈ 19.62 kN) | Tonnes |
| 8 | $W = D_{\max} + 2c + b$ | Strip width (max part dim + 2·clearance + bridge) | mm |
| 9 | $p = D_{\max} + s$ | Pitch (advance) of strip per stroke | mm |
| 10 | $F_{\text{strip}} = 0.05 F$ to $0.10 F$ | Stripping force (5–10% of punching force) | N |
| 11 | $P_{\text{center}} = \dfrac{\sum L_i \cdot x_i}{\sum L_i}$ | X-coordinate of Center of Pressure (centroidal moment) | mm |
| 12 | $P_{\text{center}} = \dfrac{\sum L_i \cdot y_i}{\sum L_i}$ | Y-coordinate of Center of Pressure | mm |
| 13 | $\sigma_{\text{capacity}} \geq 2 \cdot \dfrac{F}{A_{\text{shank}}}$ | Punch buckling check (rule of thumb) | MPa |
| 14 | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Useful Area}}{\text{Strip Area}}$ | Material utilization / nesting efficiency | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, $L$ = total cut perimeter (mm), $t$ = stock thickness (mm), $\tau$ = ultimate shear strength (MPa = N/mm²), $s$ = feed/pitch, $b$ = bridge/edge allowance. Do NOT confuse $s$ here with stroke length — contextually it is the pitch.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Stamping and punching underpin the **automotive body-in-white production**, **consumer electronics enclosures** (laptop chassis, mobile phone internal frames), **appliance panels** (washing machine, refrigerator), and **electrical laminations** (stator/rotor core sheets for motors). A modern car contains roughly **4,000 stamped parts**, and high-tonnage transfer lines produce a complete body side in under 60 seconds.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations and Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Punching Force Formula

The derivation begins with the basic physics of shearing. The punch must shear the metal along a cylindrical (or prismatic) surface of area equal to the cut perimeter times the sheet thickness.

**Step 1 — Identify the shear surface.**
When the punch pushes into the metal, the shearing takes place along the **vertical wall** of the cut, of area $A_s$.

$$A_s = L \cdot t$$

**Step 2 — Apply the shear stress equation.**
The shear stress on this surface must overcome the ultimate shear strength $\tau$ of the material.

$$\tau = \dfrac{F}{A_s}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to express F (the punching force).**
Solving for the force gives the basic form.

$$F = \tau \cdot A_s = L \cdot t \cdot \tau$$

**Step 4 — Apply the impact factor for press type.**
For a **mechanical press** (which delivers a hammer-like blow), the dynamic impact raises the effective force requirement. The empirical factor is 1.3:

$$F = 1.3 \cdot L \cdot t \cdot \tau$$

For a **hydraulic press** (smooth, slow, no impact), the impact factor drops to 1.0 (or conservatively 0.7 for calculations on the safe side):

$$F_{\text{hyd}} = 0.7 \cdot L \cdot t \cdot \tau$$

**Step 5 — Convert to tonnage for press selection.**
Mechanical presses in industry are rated in tonnes. Using 1 tonne ≈ 9.81 kN:

$$T = \dfrac{F}{9.81 \times 10^3} \text{ tonnes}$$

Or, more commonly in workshop practice (using 1 ton = 2000 lbf approximation converted to kgf):

$$T = \dfrac{F \text{ (in kgf)}}{1000}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Center of Pressure (Critical for Progressive Dies)

The **Center of Pressure (CoP)** is the resultant point at which the total punching force is considered to act on the strip. Misalignment of the press ram with the CoP causes die tilt, premature wear, and breakage.

**Step 1 — Set up the coordinate system.**
Place the origin at the geometric center of the die opening. Identify all the cut segments in the die (each hole and each outline is a "segment"). For each segment $i$, know its perimeter $L_i$ and centroidal location $(x_i, y_i)$.

**Step 2 — Apply the principle of moments.**
The total force is the sum of forces from each segment:

$$F_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} L_i \cdot t \cdot \tau$$

**Step 3 — Sum the moments about the y-axis (for x-coordinate).**
Each segment force contributes a moment equal to $F_i \cdot x_i$. The total moment must equal the total force times the CoP x-coordinate:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} F_i \cdot x_i = F_{\text{total}} \cdot \bar{x}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\bar{x}$.**
Substituting $F_i = L_i \cdot t \cdot \tau$ (where $t$ and $\tau$ are common to all segments and cancel):

$$\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} L_i \cdot x_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} L_i}$$

**Step 5 — Similarly for $\bar{y}$.**

$$\bar{y} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} L_i \cdot y_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} L_i}$$

The press ram center must be aligned to the point $(\bar{x}, \bar{y})$; any deviation produces an unbalanced lateral force.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Worked Numerical Problem

**Problem:** A square washer of side 50 mm with a central hole of diameter 20 mm is to be blanked from a 2 mm thick mild steel strip. Calculate:
1. The blanking force (mechanical press)
2. The stripper force
3. The press tonnage required

**Given:**
- Side $a = 50$ mm, Hole $D = 20$ mm, Thickness $t = 2$ mm
- Ultimate shear strength of mild steel $\tau = 350$ MPa
- Mechanical press impact factor = 1.3

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate perimeter.**
For blanking a washer, the punch must cut *both* the outer square perimeter and the inner circular perimeter. Total cut length:

$$L = 4a + \pi D = 4(50) + \pi(20) = 200 + 62.83 = 262.83 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate blanking force.**

$$F = 1.3 \cdot L \cdot t \cdot \tau = 1.3 \times 262.83 \times 2 \times 350$$

$$F = 1.3 \times 262.83 \times 700 = 239,178.3 \text{ N} \approx 239.2 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate stripper force (assume 5% of blanking force).**

$$F_{\text{strip}} = 0.05 \times 239.2 = 11.96 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to press tonnage (1 T = 9.81 kN).**

$$T = \dfrac{239.2}{9.81} = 24.4 \text{ Tonnes}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Always round up** when selecting a press (e.g., choose a **30 T press** for a 24.4 T requirement) to provide a safety margin of 20–25%.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Center of Pressure Worked Example

**Problem:** A die performs three operations simultaneously:
- Hole A: circular, $D = 20$ mm, centroid at $(30, 40)$ mm
- Hole B: square, $20 \times 20$ mm, centroid at $(-20, 50)$ mm
- Blanking contour: outer circle, $D = 100$ mm, centroid at $(0, 0)$ mm

Find the location of the center of pressure.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Compute perimeters.

$$L_A = \pi D = 62.83 \text{ mm}$$

$$L_B = 4 \times 20 = 80 \text{ mm}$$

$$L_{\text{outer}} = \pi \times 100 = 314.16 \text{ mm}$$

Step 2 — Sum the perimeters.

$$\sum L_i = 62.83 + 80 + 314.16 = 456.99 \text{ mm}$$

Step 3 — Sum the moment products for $\bar{x}$.

$$\sum L_i \cdot x_i = (62.83)(30) + (80)(-20) + (314.16)(0)$$

$$= 1884.9 - 1600 + 0 = 284.9 \text{ mm}^2$$

Step 4 — Compute $\bar{x}$.

$$\bar{x} = \dfrac{284.9}{456.99} = 0.623 \text{ mm}$$

Step 5 — Sum the moment products for $\bar{y}$.

$$\sum L_i \cdot y_i = (62.83)(40) + (80)(50) + (314.16)(0)$$

$$= 2513.2 + 4000 + 0 = 6513.2 \text{ mm}^2$$

Step 6 — Compute $\bar{y}$.

$$\bar{y} = \dfrac{6513.2}{456.99} = 14.25 \text{ mm}$$

**Result:** The press ram center must be aligned at **(0.62, 14.25) mm** from the geometric center of the die layout.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams and Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Stamping Tool Assembly

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Press Ram Top] --> B[Punch Shank Adapter]
    B --> C[Upper Shoe / Bolster Plate]
    C --> D[Punch Retainer]
    D --> E[Punch Plate]
    E --> F[Punches Male Tool]
    F --> G[Guide Pin and Bushing]
    G --> H[Stripper Plate]
    H --> I[Sheet Metal Strip]
    I --> J[Die Block Female Tool]
    J --> K[Lower Shoe]
    K --> L[Press Bed Bottom]
    H -.-> M[Stripper Springs]
    M -.-> C
    J -.-> N[Knockout Pin]
    N -.-> K
```

## 4.2 Operational Sequence Flow (Punching Cycle)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step1 Stock Loading] --> S2[Step2 Locator Pin Engages]
    S2 --> S3[Step3 Ram Descends]
    S3 --> S4[Step4 Punch Penetrates Stock]
    S4 --> S5[Step5 Shear Fracture Complete]
    S5 --> S6[Step6 Stripper Pushes Scrap Off]
    S6 --> S7[Step7 Ram Retracts]
    S7 --> S8[Step8 Strip Advances by Pitch]
    S8 --> S1
```

## 4.3 Die Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    D[Stamping Dies] --> E[Single Station Simple Die]
    D --> F[Compound Die]
    D --> G[Combination Die]
    D --> H[Progressive Die]
    D --> I[Transfer Die]
    F --> F1[All cut operations at ONE station]
    F --> F2[Slug remains in die cavity]
    H --> H1[Multiple stations along strip]
    H --> H2[Operations as strip advances pitch by pitch]
```

## 4.4 Shear Action and Edge Quality Zones

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ZA[Cut Edge of Blanked Part] --> Z1[Zone 1 Burnished Top Surface]
    ZA --> Z2[Zone 2 Fracture Middle Band]
    ZA --> Z3[Zone 3 Burr Bottom Edge]
    Z1 --> Z1d[Caused by initial punch penetration]
    Z2 --> Z2d[Caused by crack propagation from punch and die edges]
    Z3 --> Z3d[Caused by clearance and material ductility]
```

## 4.5 Process Architecture: Strip Layout in a Progressive Die

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Station 1 Piercing Hole] --> P2[Station 2 Notching]
    P2 --> P3[Station 3 Bending]
    P3 --> P4[Station 4 Blanking Final Part]
    P4 --> P5[Station 5 Scrap Skeleton Eject]
    S1[Strip Feed Direction] -.-> P1
    S1 -.-> P2
    S1 -.-> P3
    S1 -.-> P4
    S1 -.-> P5
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank and Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between blanking and piercing operations in sheet metal working.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Blanking | Piercing (Punching) |
|---|---|---|
| Product obtained | The part punched **out** of the strip | The part **remaining** in the strip with a hole |
| Slug/Scrap | The blank is product; the leftover strip is scrap | The slug pushed out is scrap; the workpiece is product |
| Tool design | Die opening is the **shape of the part** | Die opening is **larger**; punch shape defines hole |
| Typical use | Manufacturing flat parts (washers, gaskets) | Producing holes in sheets |
| Edge quality difference | Both produce identical edge zones | — |

The fundamental rule: **In blanking, the die shape = part shape; in piercing, the punch shape = hole shape.**

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition of each + 1 mark for the key differentiator.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is meant by the term "center of pressure" in die design? Why is it important?**

**Model Answer:**

The **center of pressure (CoP)** is the single point on the die face through which the resultant of all individual punching forces is considered to act. Mathematically, it is the perimeter-weighted centroid:

$$\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum L_i \cdot x_i}{\sum L_i}, \quad \bar{y} = \dfrac{\sum L_i \cdot y_i}{\sum L_i}$$

**Importance:**
1. The press ram center must be aligned with the CoP; otherwise, an unbalanced lateral force is generated.
2. Misalignment causes die tilting, uneven wear, broken punches, and poor part accuracy.
3. It is **especially critical in compound and progressive dies** where multiple punches act simultaneously.

*(3 marks: 1.5 for definition + 1.5 for importance.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### CHOICE 1 — Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a **progressive die**. List any **four** operations that can be performed in a single progressive die. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** For a **compound die**, explain its construction, working principle, and state **two** applications with the help of sketches. Compare a compound die with a progressive die in a tabular form. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (a):**

**Construction:**
A progressive die consists of multiple stations arranged sequentially along the path of the strip. At each station, one or more operations (piercing, bending, blanking, etc.) are performed. The strip is advanced by a fixed pitch $p$ after every stroke using a pilot pin or feed mechanism.

**Working Principle:**
1. The strip is fed into the die and positioned by a locator/pilot.
2. On the first stroke, piercing or notching is done at station 1.
3. The strip is advanced by one pitch; the second stroke performs the next operation at station 2.
4. This continues until the final station, where the finished part is blanked out of the strip.
5. The remaining skeleton is ejected as scrap.

**Operations possible in a single progressive die:**
1. Piercing
2. Notching
3. Bending
4. Blanking
5. Embossing
6. Coining
7. Drawing (shallow)

*(4 marks: construction 1.5, working 1.5, operations list 1.)*

**Model Solution (b):**

**Construction of a Compound Die:**
A compound die has multiple cutting operations performed at a single station. The punch, die, and stripper are concentric. Typical arrangement: outer punch + inner punch held in a common punch plate; the die block has two corresponding cavities (outer for blanking, inner for slug ejection).

**Working Principle:**
In a single stroke, the punch descends, and simultaneously: (i) the part is blanked by the outer punch cutting against the die, AND (ii) a hole is pierced in the blank itself by the inner punch. The slug from the inner punch is pushed through the die.

**Applications:**
1. **Gaskets with central holes** — blanking and piercing in one stroke.
2. **Washers** — square/circular blanking with central hole in one stroke.
3. **Shim plates with mounting holes**.

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Compound Die | Progressive Die |
|---|---|---|
| Stations | One (single station) | Multiple (in-line) |
| Operations per stroke | All at one place | One per station |
| Production rate | High | Very High |
| Die cost | High | Highest |
| Operator skill | Lower | Higher (for setup) |
| Material utilization | Better (smaller skeleton) | Slightly lower (carrier needed) |
| Tool complexity | Concentric, intricate | Modular, sequential |

*(7 marks: 3 for construction & working + 2 for applications + 2 for comparison.)*

---

### CHOICE 2 — Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** A circular blank of **60 mm diameter** is to be cut from a **3 mm thick** mild steel sheet using a **mechanical press**. Given the ultimate shear strength of mild steel as **310 MPa**:
1. Calculate the blanking force.
2. Calculate the press tonnage.
3. If the die clearance per side is **0.1 mm**, determine the **die opening diameter**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the concept of **die clearance** in stamping. With a neat sketch, describe the **cutting clearance per side** between punch and die. State the typical clearance values for **mild steel, aluminum, and stainless steel** as a percentage of stock thickness. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (a):**

**Given:**
- Diameter $D = 60$ mm
- Thickness $t = 3$ mm
- $\tau = 310$ MPa
- Impact factor for mechanical press = 1.3
- Clearance per side $c = 0.1$ mm

**Step 1 — Calculate blanking force.**

$$F = 1.3 \cdot L \cdot t \cdot \tau = 1.3 \cdot (\pi D) \cdot t \cdot \tau$$

$$F = 1.3 \times \pi \times 60 \times 3 \times 310$$

$$F = 1.3 \times 565.49 \times 930$$

$$F = 1.3 \times 525,905.7 = 683,677.4 \text{ N} \approx 683.7 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2 — Press tonnage.**

$$T = \dfrac{683,677.4}{9.81 \times 10^3} = \dfrac{683.7}{9.81} = 69.7 \text{ Tonnes}$$

A standard **75 T mechanical press** is selected.

**Step 3 — Die opening diameter.**
For a blanking operation, the **die opening equals the blank size**. Clearance is added *to* the punch (punch is smaller than the die in blanking):

$$D_{\text{die}} = D + 2c = 60 + 2(0.1) = 60.2 \text{ mm}$$

$$D_{\text{punch}} = D = 60 \text{ mm}$$

*(7 marks: 2 for force + 2 for tonnage + 3 for die opening.)*

**Model Solution (b):**

**Concept of Die Clearance:**
Die clearance is the intentional small gap between the cutting edges of the punch and the die. It is provided on **each side** (i.e., total gap = 2c). Clearance is essential because:

1. It allows the fracture cracks initiated at the punch edge and die edge to meet cleanly.
2. Zero clearance causes double-shear, tool binding, and excessive tool wear.
3. Excessive clearance causes large burrs and poor edge quality.

**Sketch (described in text):**
- A cylindrical punch of diameter $D_p$ descends into a die with opening $D_d$.
- $D_d > D_p$ when blanking.
- Radial gap on each side = $c$.
- Total radial gap = $2c$.

**Typical clearance values (% of thickness t):**

| Material | Clearance per side (% of t) |
|---|---|
| Mild Steel | 5–7% |
| Aluminum / Alloys | 4–6% |
| Stainless Steel | 7–10% |
| Copper / Brass | 4–5% |
| High-carbon steel | 8–10% |

*(7 marks: 2 for concept + 2 for sketch/explanation + 3 for the values table.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Losing Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Blanking vs Piercing sign convention** for die/punch sizing. In **blanking**: die size = part size; punch is smaller. In **piercing**: punch size = hole size; die is larger.
> 2. **Forgetting the impact factor** of 1.3 for mechanical presses in force calculations. Hydraulic presses use 0.7 to 1.0.
> 3. **Not rounding up** the press tonnage. Always select a press with at least 20–25% higher tonnage than calculated.
> 4. **In Center of Pressure problems**, students often forget to use the **perimeter $L_i$** as the weighting factor (not the area). This is a guaranteed 2-mark loss.
> 5. **Stripping force is NOT zero** — it is 5–10% of the blanking force. Many students omit it.
> 6. **Clearance in blanking and piercing is opposite**: in blanking the part is smaller than the die opening, in piercing the hole is smaller than the punch.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap: Important Things to Remember

- **Stamping** is the umbrella; **Blanking, Piercing, Notching, Bending, Drawing** are the sub-operations.
- **Blanking** → die shape = part shape. **Piercing** → punch shape = hole shape.
- **Punching force** $F = 1.3 \cdot L \cdot t \cdot \tau$ (mechanical press).
- **Hydraulic press** uses factor 0.7 to 1.0 instead of 1.3.
- **Stripping force** = 5–10% of $F$.
- **Clearance per side** typically **5–10% of stock thickness** (material-dependent).
- **Center of Pressure** = perimeter-weighted centroid of all cut segments.
- **Compound die** = multiple operations at one station, in one stroke.
- **Progressive die** = multiple operations at multiple stations, strip advances per stroke.
- **Cut edge zones** = Burnished → Fracture → Burr.
- **Press tonnage** = $F / 9.81$ (in kN), then divide by 1000 → tonnes; **always round up**.
- **Die set components**: punch, die, stripper, pressure pad, guide pins, die shoes, knockout.
- **Pilot pin** is used in progressive dies to ensure accurate feed.
- **Cut-off / parting** operation separates the part from the strip at the end of progressive sequence.
- **Slitting** is the only stamping operation that uses **rotary cutters** (not a press).
- **Material utilization $\eta$** = useful part area / strip area; aim for $\eta > 70\%$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Defects of Various Metal Forming Processes and their Remedies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET524 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Defects of Various Metal Forming Processes and their Remedies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Metal Forming Defects?

A **metal forming defect** is any undesirable discontinuity, geometric inaccuracy, surface irregularity, or internal discontinuity that develops in a workpiece during or immediately after a plastic deformation process (forging, rolling, extrusion, drawing, sheet metal forming, etc.), which compromises the **dimensional integrity, mechanical performance, surface quality, or service life** of the finished component.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Board Examiner Wording):**
> *Defects in metal forming are deviations from the desired shape, size, microstructure, or surface finish introduced during plastic deformation, arising from incorrect process parameters, poor tooling design, improper lubrication, or unsuitable material condition. Remedies are corrective actions involving process parameter optimization, tooling modification, lubrication improvement, or thermal treatment.*

## 1.2 Classification of Metal Forming Defects (KTU Board Taxonomy)

| S.No | Defect Class | Origin | Nature |
|:----:|:-------------|:-------|:-------|
| 1 | **Geometric / Dimensional** | Improper die design, springback | External |
| 2 | **Surface Defects** | Lubrication failure, die wear | External |
| 3 | **Internal / Structural Defects** | Tensile fracture, ductility exhaustion | Internal |
| 4 | **Microstructural Defects** | Strain hardening, grain growth | Internal |
| 5 | **Thermal Defects** | Improper temperature, overheating | Internal + External |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Clay Play Dough" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy for a KTU Fresher:**
> Imagine pressing a lump of play dough between your palms to make a chapati:
> - If you press **too fast** → it tears at the edges (like **edge cracking in rolling**)
> - If you press **too thin** → a hole opens in the middle (like **center burst in extrusion**)
> - If your palms are **sticky/dry** → the dough surface roughens (like **die lines in extrusion**)
> - If you let go suddenly → it springs back slightly (like **springback in bending**)
> - If you fold it improperly → a fold line remains (like a **lap or fold in forging**)

Every defect in metal forming has a **cause–effect–remedy** triple that we will dissect below.

## 1.4 Why This Topic Carries Heavy Marks in KTU 2024

- Module 2 questions on **"defects and remedies"** appear almost **every semester** in Part A (3-mark) and frequently as a 14-mark essay in Part B.
- Examiner marks are awarded for: **(i) Naming the defect, (ii) Sketching it, (iii) Stating the cause, (iv) Prescribing the remedy** — students who skip the remedy lose ~3 marks.
- The 2024 Scheme emphasizes **application-level** and **diagnostic** questions under **CO2 (Apply)** and **CO3 (Analyze)** of PEIET524.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Defects in Forging (Open-Die & Closed-Die)

### 2.1.1 Unfilled Sections
- **Description:** Portions of the die cavity remain unfilled because the volume of billet is insufficient or flash is excessive.
- **Cause:** Insufficient material; low forging temperature; excessive flash land; incorrect preform shape.
- **Remedy:** Use correct billet volume (≥ 1.05 × part volume); preheat uniformly; design proper preform; reduce flash ratio.

### 2.1.2 Cold Shut
- **Description:** A discontinuity where two metal streams meet but do not fuse properly — appears as a wrinkle or seam on the forging surface.
- **Cause:** Insufficient material flow; low temperature; sharp corners in die; poor draft angles.
- **Remedy:** Increase forging temperature; add fillet radii to die; use proper draft angle (typically 5°–7°); design proper preform to balance metal flow.

### 2.1.3 Die Shift / Mismatch
- **Description:** The two die halves are laterally misaligned, producing a forging with a stepped parting line.
- **Cause:** Worn die guides; improper die setting; flash buildup.
- **Remedy:** Regular die maintenance; proper guide alignment; clean flash traps.

### 2.1.4 Forging Laps and Folds
- **Description:** A lap is a surface discontinuity formed when metal folds back on itself during forging. Folds are similar but occur internally.
- **Cause:** Sharp corners; excessive deformation; improper preform.
- **Remedy:** Increase die fillet radii; reduce strain per blow; use proper preform design.

### 2.1.5 Internal Cracks and Flakes
- **Description:** Internal fissures, often transverse to the forging axis.
- **Cause:** Improper cooling (hydrogen-induced cracking in steels); high strain rate at low temperature; excessive reduction.
- **Remedy:** Controlled cooling (furnace cool for alloy steels); proper soaking time; reduce strain rate; vacuum degassing of steel.

## 2.2 Defects in Rolling

### 2.2.1 Wavy Edges
- **Description:** The longitudinal edges of the rolled sheet are wavy or scalloped.
- **Cause:** Roll camber mismatch; non-uniform roll pressure; incorrect roll gap geometry.
- **Remedy:** Adjust roll camber; use crown on rolls; optimize roll gap distribution.

### 2.2.2 Edge Cracking
- **Description:** Cracks initiate at the edges of the rolled product and propagate inward.
- **Cause:** Low ductility at edges (often from secondary tensile stresses); high reduction per pass; low temperature.
- **Remedy:** Reduce reduction per pass; increase rolling temperature; use edge guards; preheat billet uniformly.

### 2.2.3 Center Burst (Internal Cracking in Rolling)
- **Description:** Internal chevron-shaped fracture at the center plane of the rolled plate.
- **Cause:** Excessive tensile stresses along the central plane due to redundant work.
- **Remedy:** Reduce reduction per pass; use proper lubrication; increase roll radius; control friction.

### 2.2.4 Alligatoring (Fish-Mouth / Splitting)
- **Description:** The rolled product splits open lengthwise, resembling an alligator's mouth.
- **Cause:** Excessive bite angle; poor ductility; too low rolling temperature; wrong roll diameter.
- **Remedy:** Reduce bite angle; increase temperature; use larger diameter rolls; preheat properly.

### 2.2.5 Barrelling
- **Description:** In upsetting, the cylindrical specimen bulges outward at the center, taking a barrel shape.
- **Cause:** Friction at die-workpiece interface; high aspect ratio (height/diameter > 2).
- **Remedy:** Use effective lubrication; reduce aspect ratio by using multiple upsetting steps; use tapered dies.

## 2.3 Defects in Extrusion

### 2.3.1 Center Burst (Chevron Cracking)
- **Description:** A star-shaped or chevron-pattern internal crack at the center of the extruded product.
- **Cause:** Excessive tension at the centerline due to high die angle or excessive reduction.
- **Remedy:** Optimize die angle; reduce extrusion ratio; use proper billet temperature; use a mandrel for hollow sections.

### 2.3.2 Piping Defect
- **Description:** A funnel-shaped cavity forms at the rear of the billet due to inward flow of material.
- **Cause:** Excessive extrusion ratio; non-uniform deformation at billet rear.
- **Remedy:** Reduce extrusion ratio; use proper dummy block pressure; discard the rear portion of billet.

### 2.3.3 Surface Cracking and Die Lines
- **Description:** Longitudinal surface cracks or fine lines parallel to extrusion direction.
- **Cause:** Excessive die friction; high temperature gradient; surface defects on billet.
- **Remedy:** Improve lubrication; polish die; homogenize billet; reduce extrusion speed.

### 2.3.4 Twisting and Curving
- **Description:** Extruded product twists or curves along its length.
- **Cause:** Asymmetric die geometry; non-uniform temperature; uneven friction.
- **Remedy:** Use symmetric die; improve billet temperature uniformity; balance lubrication.

## 2.4 Defects in Wire/Tube Drawing

### 2.4.1 Center Burst (Cuppy Draw)
- **Description:** Internal conical fracture at the center of drawn wire or tube.
- **Cause:** Excessive reduction; high die angle; improper back tension.
- **Remedy:** Limit reduction per pass (≤ 30% for steel); optimize die angle (typically 6°–12°); apply suitable back tension.

### 2.4.2 Surface Seams and Scratches
- **Description:** Longitudinal surface discontinuities.
- **Cause:** Die wear; improper lubrication; dirty wire rod.
- **Remedy:** Polish/rotate dies; use proper lubricant; clean wire surface.

### 2.4.3 Orange Peel
- **Description:** Rough, pebble-grained surface texture.
- **Cause:** Coarse initial grain size; excessive grain growth from overheating.
- **Remedy:** Refine grain size before drawing; control annealing temperature.

## 2.5 Defects in Sheet Metal Forming (Deep Drawing, Bending, Stretching)

### 2.5.1 Springback
- **Description:** Elastic recovery of the sheet after load removal causes deviation from desired geometry.
- **Cause:** Elastic portion of the deformation; high yield strength.
- **Remedy:** Overbend the tool; use bottoming dies; lower yield strength by annealing; compensate in tool design.

### 2.5.2 Wrinkling
- **Description:** Buckling of the flange during deep drawing.
- **Cause:** Insufficient blank holder force; high drawing ratio.
- **Remedy:** Apply sufficient blank holder pressure; use draw beads; reduce drawing ratio (use multi-stage drawing).

### 2.5.3 Tearing (Necking Failure)
- **Description:** Localized thinning and fracture in the wall of the drawn cup.
- **Cause:** Excessive drawing ratio; insufficient material; over-thinning.
- **Remedy:** Optimize drawing ratio (limit: DR ≤ 2.0 for single stage); increase blank thickness; improve lubrication.

### 2.5.4 Earing
- **Description:** Wavy edge of a deep-drawn cup with peaks (ears) at regular intervals.
- **Cause:** Anisotropy in the sheet (preferred crystallographic orientation / texture).
- **Remedy:** Use balanced biaxial texture sheets; control cold rolling reduction; intermediate annealing.

### 2.5.5 Stretcher Strains (Lüders Bands)
- **Description:** Localized banded deformation patterns on the sheet surface.
- **Cause:** Yield point phenomenon in low-carbon steels; aging after temper rolling.
- **Remedy:** Use temper rolling to eliminate yield point; add nitrogen stabilizers; use killed steel.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas are written with `\vert` instead of `|` to maintain table integrity. Always state units in KTU answers.

| Process | Key Formula | Application |
|:--------|:------------|:------------|
| **Forging Upsetting Pressure** | $p \;=\; Y_{f}\,\left(1 + \dfrac{2\,\mu\,D}{3\,h}\right)$ | Average forging pressure on disc |
| **Max. Reduction per Pass (Drawing, Siebel)** | $\Delta A_{max} \;=\; A_{0}\,\bigl[1 - e^{-2f\,\alpha}\bigr]\,\dfrac{\sigma_{y}}{\sigma_{y}+Y}$ | Wire/tube drawing limit |
| **Drawing Stress (Max)** | $\sigma_{d,max} \;=\; \sigma_{f}\,(\,1 + \dfrac{\mu}{\tan\alpha}\,)\,\phi\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{A_{0}}{A_{f}}\right)$ | Wire drawing |
| **Center Burst Criterion (Avitzur, Extrusion)** | $\dfrac{2\,L}{D}\,\ln R \;\geq\; \left(\dfrac{\alpha}{f}\right)$ | Chevron cracking |
| **Rolling Bite Angle** | $\tan\alpha \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta h}{2R} \;\Rightarrow\; \alpha \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta h}{R}}$ | Max. bite condition |
| **Maximum Draft (Rolling)** | $\Delta h_{max} \;=\; \mu^{2}\,R$ | Friction-limited |
| **Deep Draw Ratio Limit** | $DR_{max} \;\approx\; 1.85 \;\text{to}\; 2.10$ (for steel) | Single-stage drawability |
| **Springback Angle (Bending)** | $\dfrac{\theta_{s}}{\theta_{b}} \;=\; \dfrac{3\,(K_{s}/Y)\,-\,1}{3\,(K_{s}/Y)\,+\,1}$ (where $K_s$ is the strain-hardening slope) | Compensation |
| **Strain in Upsetting** | $\varepsilon \;=\; \ln\!\left(\dfrac{h_{0}}{h_{f}}\right)$ | True strain |
| **Friction Hill (Forging)** | $p_{avg} \;=\; Y_{f}\,e^{2\mu R/h}$ | Open-die forging |

**Legend:** $Y_f$ = flow stress, $\mu$ = friction coefficient, $D$ = diameter, $h$ = height, $R$ = reduction ratio, $\alpha$ = die semi-angle, $f$ = friction factor, $L$ = billet length, $D$ = billet diameter, $K_s$ = strength coefficient, $\theta_s$ = springback, $\theta_b$ = bend angle.

## 2.7 Engineering Utility

- **Defect diagnosis** is a core skill for **tool designers** in automotive (forged crankshafts), aerospace (extruded turbine blades), and structural (rolled plates) industries.
- The **Friction Hill** concept explains why forging loads are 2–3× higher than flow stress — directly relevant to press capacity selection.
- **Springback compensation** is critical in **sheet metal industries** — a 0.5° error in bend angle can cause assembly failures.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Center Burst Criterion in Drawing (Avitzur Model)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** This derivation is a "favorite" 7-mark sub-question. Showing each step earns full marks.

**Problem:** Find the condition for the onset of center burst in wire drawing through a conical die.

### Step 1: Set up the geometry
Consider a conical die with semi-angle $\alpha$, friction factor $f$, drawing stress $\sigma_d$, and reduction ratio $R = A_0 / A_f$.

### Step 2: Write the equilibrium of an element

Consider an annular element at radius $r$ inside the deformation zone. The differential force balance along the drawing direction gives:

$$
\frac{d\sigma_{x}}{dx} \;=\; \frac{2\,\tau\,\tan\alpha}{r}
$$

where $\tau = f\,Y_f$ is the shear (friction) stress and $\alpha$ is the die semi-angle.

### Step 3: Integrate along the deformation zone

Assuming homogeneous deformation and $Y_f \approx$ constant, integrating from the entry ($x = 0$, stress = back tension $\sigma_b$) to exit ($x = L$, stress = drawing stress $\sigma_d$):

$$
\int_{\sigma_{b}}^{\sigma_{d}} d\sigma_{x} \;=\; \frac{2\,f\,Y_{f}\,\tan\alpha}{r} \int_{0}^{L} dx
$$

### Step 4: Express L in terms of geometry

From die geometry, the deformation zone length is:

$$
L \;=\; \frac{r_{0} - r_{f}}{2\,\tan\alpha}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\sigma_{d} - \sigma_{b} \;=\; 2\,f\,Y_{f}\,\tan\alpha \cdot \frac{r_{0} - r_{f}}{2\,r\,\tan\alpha}
$$

### Step 5: Recognize that the central element sees maximum tension

For the **central axis** fiber ($r \to 0$), the redundant work component becomes dominant, and the axial stress in the central fiber approaches the maximum possible tensile stress. The **fracture criterion** states that center burst occurs when:

$$
\sigma_{axial,\,center} \;\geq\; \sigma_{fracture}
$$

### Step 6: Final form (Avitzur Criterion)

Combining the redundant work analysis with the homogeneous solution gives the **center burst avoidance condition**:

$$
\frac{2\,L}{D}\,\ln R \;\geq\; \frac{\alpha}{f}
$$

where $L$ is the deformation zone length, $D$ is the wire diameter, $R$ is the reduction ratio, $\alpha$ is the die semi-angle (in radians), and $f$ is the friction factor.

> **Physical interpretation:** A **large reduction ratio** and **long deformation zone** promote center burst, while a **larger die angle** and **higher friction** actually *suppress* it (counter-intuitively, friction at the die surface is what *protects* the center).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Springback in Pure Bending (KTU 14-Mark Standard)

**Problem:** Derive the springback angle for an elastic-perfectly-plastic sheet subjected to pure bending.

### Step 1: Define bending moment and curvature

For a sheet of thickness $t$ bent to radius $\rho$ (curvature $\kappa = 1/\rho$), the bending moment about the neutral axis is:

$$
M \;=\; \int_{-t/2}^{t/2} \sigma_{x}\, y\, dy
$$

### Step 2: Stress distribution — plastic region

For elastic-perfectly-plastic behavior, with yield stress $Y$ and elastic modulus $E$, the elastic core radius is $c$. For $c \leq \vert y \vert \leq t/2$, the stress is constant at $Y$:

$$
\sigma(y) \;=\; \pm Y \quad \text{for } \vert y \vert > c
$$

For the elastic core $\vert y \vert < c$:

$$
\sigma(y) \;=\; E\,\kappa\,y
$$

### Step 3: Determine elastic core radius

The yield criterion at $y = c$ gives:

$$
E\,\kappa\,c \;=\; Y \quad\Rightarrow\quad c \;=\; \frac{Y}{E\,\kappa}
$$

### Step 4: Compute the bending moment

$$
M \;=\; 2\int_{c}^{t/2} Y\,y\,dy \;+\; 2\int_{0}^{c} E\,\kappa\,y^{2}\,dy
$$

Evaluating:

$$
M \;=\; Y\!\left[\frac{t^{2}}{4} - c^{2}\right] \;+\; \frac{2E\,\kappa\,c^{3}}{3}
$$

### Step 5: Substituting c and simplifying

Substituting $c = Y/(E\kappa)$:

$$
M \;=\; \frac{Y\,t^{2}}{4} \;-\; \frac{Y^{3}}{3E^{2}\kappa^{2}}
$$

### Step 6: Apply elastic unloading (Springback)

On unloading, the sheet behaves elastically, with a **constant elastic curvature change** $\Delta\kappa$ given by:

$$
\Delta\kappa \;=\; \frac{12\,M}{E\,t^{3}}
$$

### Step 7: Springback ratio

The **springback ratio** (final curvature / initial curvature) is then:

$$
\frac{\kappa_{f}}{\kappa_{i}} \;=\; 1 \;-\; \frac{12\,M}{E\,t^{3}\,\kappa_{i}}
$$

For small elastic strains (most KTU cases), this is approximated as:

$$
\frac{R_{i}}{R_{f}} \;=\; 1 \;-\; \frac{3\,\sigma_{y}}{E}\,\frac{R_{i}}{t}
$$

> **Result:** Springback **decreases** with thicker sheets, lower yield stress, and smaller bend radius.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Defect-to-Remedy Quick Lookup (for KTU revision)

| Process | Defect | Cause (One Phrase) | Remedy (One Phrase) | KTU Frequency |
|:--------|:-------|:-------------------|:--------------------|:--------------:|
| Forging | Cold shut | Low temp / sharp corner | Increase temp, add fillet | High |
| Forging | Flakes | Hydrogen + fast cool | Vacuum degas, furnace cool | Medium |
| Forging | Die shift | Worn guides | Realign guides | Low |
| Forging | Laps | Sharp corner, fold-over | Add radii, reduce strain | High |
| Rolling | Wavy edge | Roll camber mismatch | Adjust camber / crown | Medium |
| Rolling | Edge crack | Low ductility, high reduction | Reduce draft, raise temp | High |
| Rolling | Center burst | High redundant tension | Reduce draft, lubricate | High |
| Rolling | Alligatoring | Excessive bite | Larger roll radius | Medium |
| Rolling | Barrelling | Friction at die face | Lubricate, multi-step | High |
| Extrusion | Center burst | High die angle, high R | Reduce R, optimize angle | High |
| Extrusion | Piping | Excessive R | Reduce R, use dummy block | Medium |
| Extrusion | Die lines | Die wear, dirty billet | Polish die, clean billet | Medium |
| Drawing | Center burst | High reduction | Limit to ≤ 30% per pass | High |
| Drawing | Orange peel | Coarse grains | Refine grain, control anneal | Low |
| Drawing | Seams | Die wear | Polish / rotate die | Medium |
| Deep draw | Wrinkling | Low blank holder | Increase BHF, draw beads | High |
| Deep draw | Tearing | Excessive DR | Multi-stage drawing | High |
| Deep draw | Earing | Texture / anisotropy | Use balanced sheets | Medium |
| Deep draw | Springback | Elastic recovery | Overbend, lower YS | High |
| Deep draw | Stretcher strains | Yield point | Temper roll, kill steel | Low |
| Bending | Springback | Elastic recovery | Bottoming die, overbend | High |

## 3.4 Worked Example (KTU 14-Mark Standard)

**Problem (KTU Sample):** A cylindrical billet of 50 mm diameter and 60 mm height is upset in closed-die forging to a final height of 30 mm between rough dies. The coefficient of friction is 0.15, and the flow stress at the average strain is 350 MPa. Determine:
1. The forging pressure.
2. Whether barrelling is likely to occur (assume aspect ratio threshold = 2.0).
3. One remedy if barrelling is predicted.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute true strain**

$$
\varepsilon \;=\; \ln\!\left(\frac{h_{0}}{h_{f}}\right) \;=\; \ln\!\left(\frac{60}{30}\right) \;=\; \ln 2 \;\approx\; 0.693
$$

**Step 2: Initial aspect ratio**

$$
AR_{0} \;=\; \frac{h_{0}}{D_{0}} \;=\; \frac{60}{50} \;=\; 1.2 \;\text{<}\; 2.0
$$

**Step 3: Forging pressure (friction hill)**

$$
p \;=\; Y_{f}\!\left(1 + \frac{2\,\mu\,D}{3\,h}\right) \;\approx\; 350\!\left(1 + \frac{2 \times 0.15 \times 0.05}{3 \times 0.03}\right)
$$

$$
p \;=\; 350\,(1 + 0.1667) \;\approx\; 350 \times 1.1667 \;\approx\; 408.3 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 4: Barrelling prediction**

Initial aspect ratio $= 1.2 < 2.0$, hence **barrelling is NOT likely to occur in a single step**, but if aspect ratio grows above 2.0 during deformation (e.g., when $h \to 30$ mm and $D$ expands), barrelling may initiate.

**Step 5: Remedy if barrelling is observed**

Use **effective lubrication** (graphite or MoS₂-based) at the die-workpiece interface, or split the operation into **two or more upsetting-reheating steps** to keep aspect ratio below 2.0.

> **Answer:** $p \approx 408.3$ MPa; barrelling unlikely for this geometry; remedy = improved lubrication or multi-step upsetting.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Defect Diagnosis Decision Tree (KTU Visualization Aid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Workpiece Shows Defect] --> B{Process Type?}
    B --> C1[Forging]
    B --> C2[Rolling]
    B --> C3[Extrusion]
    B --> C4[Drawing]
    B --> C5[Sheet Forming]

    C1 --> D1{Surface or Internal?}
    D1 --> E1[Surface: Cold Shut, Laps]
    D1 --> E2[Internal: Flakes, Cracks]
    E1 --> F1[Remedy: Add fillet, raise temp]
    E2 --> F2[Remedy: Vacuum degas, furnace cool]

    C2 --> D2{Location?}
    D2 --> G1[Edge: Edge Crack, Wavy]
    D2 --> G2[Center: Center Burst]
    D2 --> G3[Surface: Alligatoring]
    G1 --> H1[Remedy: Lower draft, edge guards]
    G2 --> H2[Remedy: Reduce reduction, lubricate]
    G3 --> H3[Remedy: Larger roll radius]

    C3 --> D3{Defect Pattern?}
    D3 --> I1[Chevron: Center Burst]
    D3 --> I2[Funnel Rear: Piping]
    D3 --> I3[Longitudinal: Die Lines]
    I1 --> J1[Remedy: Reduce R, optimize angle]
    I2 --> J2[Remedy: Use dummy block]
    I3 --> J3[Remedy: Polish die, clean billet]

    C4 --> D4{Severity?}
    D4 --> K1[Internal Fracture: Center Burst]
    D4 --> K2[Surface: Seams, Orange Peel]
    K1 --> L1[Remedy: Limit reduction to under 30 percent]
    K2 --> L2[Remedy: Polish die, refine grain]

    C5 --> D5{Symptom?}
    D5 --> M1[Buckle: Wrinkling]
    D5 --> M2[Thin Spot: Tearing]
    D5 --> M3[Wavy Edge: Earing]
    D5 --> M4[Angular Error: Springback]
    M1 --> N1[Remedy: Increase BHF, draw beads]
    M2 --> N2[Remedy: Lower DR, multi-stage]
    M3 --> N3[Remedy: Balanced texture sheet]
    M4 --> N4[Remedy: Overbend, bottoming die]
```

flowchart LR
    P1[Process Parameters] --> Q1[Material Flow]
    P2[Tool Geometry] --> Q1

    P4[Temperature] --> Q1
    Q1 --> R1{Defect Generation}

> 1. **Process geometry** (die, workpiece, deformation zone).
> 2. **Defect location** (arrow pointing to the defective region).
> 3. **Stress direction arrows** (tensile/compressive) to show cause.
