<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Q: Define "center burst" defect in wire drawing. State its cause and any one remedy.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Center burst is an internal conical or chevron-shaped fracture that occurs at the central axis of a wire or tube during drawing through a conical die, due to tensile stresses exceeding the local fracture stress.
- **Cause (1 mark):** It is caused by excessive reduction per pass combined with low die angle and insufficient friction, leading to high redundant tensile stress at the centerline.
- **Remedy (1 mark):** Limit the reduction per pass to ≤ 30% for steel wires, and optimize the die semi-angle (typically 6°–12°).

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: What is springback in sheet metal bending? Why is it important in tool design?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Springback is the elastic recovery of a bent sheet after the load is removed, causing the bend angle to decrease from the tool angle.
- **Cause (1 mark):** It is caused by the elastic portion of the total strain, governed by the ratio $Y/E$ (yield strength to elastic modulus).
- **Tool design importance (1 mark):** The tool must be designed with an **overbend** to compensate for springback, otherwise the final part angle will be smaller than specified, leading to assembly failures.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: (a) Classify the various defects encountered in metal forming processes. With neat sketches, explain any FOUR common defects in forging and their remedies. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

*Classification (2 marks):*
Metal forming defects can be classified as:
1. **Geometric defects** — wrong dimensions, unfilled sections, die shift
2. **Surface defects** — cold shut, laps, die lines, scratches
3. **Internal defects** — center burst, flakes, piping, cracks
4. **Microstructural defects** — strain hardening, grain growth, orange peel

*Detailed explanation (5 marks — 1 defect ≈ 1.25 marks):*

**Defect 1: Cold Shut**
- *Sketch (0.5 mark):* A sketch showing a fold-line on the forging surface.
- *Cause:* When two metal streams meet but do not fuse due to low temperature or sharp die corners.
- *Remedy:* Increase forging temperature; add fillet radii; use proper draft angles (5°–7°).

**Defect 2: Forging Laps**
- *Sketch (0.5 mark):* Sketch showing a thin fold of metal overlapping the surface.
- *Cause:* Material folds back on itself due to sharp corners or excessive deformation in a single blow.
- *Remedy:* Increase die fillet radii; reduce strain per blow; use proper preform design.

**Defect 3: Flakes (Internal Cracks)**
- *Sketch (0.5 mark):* Sketch showing internal transverse cracks.
- *Cause:* Hydrogen-induced cracking in steels due to rapid cooling from high temperature.
- *Remedy:* Vacuum degassing of molten steel; controlled furnace cooling; proper soaking time.

**Defect 4: Unfilled Sections**
- *Sketch (0.5 mark):* Sketch showing incomplete die cavity filling.
- *Cause:* Insufficient billet volume; low forging temperature; excessive flash.
- *Remedy:* Use correct billet volume (≥ 1.05 × part volume); preheat uniformly; design proper preform.

**(b) With a neat diagram, explain the "center burst" defect in extrusion. Derive the Avitzur criterion for its occurrence and state two process parameters to avoid it. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

*Diagram (1.5 marks):* Show conical die, billet, extruded product, and the chevron-shaped internal crack at the centerline of the product.

*Explanation (1.5 marks):*
Center burst is a star-shaped internal crack in extruded products caused by tensile stresses exceeding the fracture stress at the centerline. It occurs when the redundant work in the deformation zone produces a tension along the central axis, which combines with the drawing stress to cause internal fracture.

*Derivation (3 marks):*
From force equilibrium on an element:

$$
\frac{d\sigma_{x}}{dx} \;=\; \frac{2\,\tau\,\tan\alpha}{r}
$$

Integrating with boundary conditions and applying the redundant work analysis for the central fiber, the **Avitzur center burst criterion** is:

$$
\frac{2\,L}{D}\,\ln R \;\geq\; \frac{\alpha}{f}
$$

where $L$ is deformation zone length, $D$ is billet diameter, $R$ is reduction ratio, $\alpha$ is die semi-angle, and $f$ is friction factor. Center burst occurs when the LHS exceeds the RHS.

*Remedies (1 mark, any two):*
1. Reduce extrusion ratio (R < 4:1 for steels).
2. Use proper die angle (α ≈ 45°–60° for steel).
3. Increase billet temperature uniformity.
4. Use hydrostatic extrusion (eliminates center burst entirely).

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: (a) With neat sketches, explain the following sheet metal forming defects: (i) Wrinkling, (ii) Earing, (iii) Stretcher strains. State one remedy for each. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Defect (i) Wrinkling (2 marks)**
- *Sketch (1 mark):* Show a deep-drawn cup with wavy flange regions.
- *Cause:* Insufficient blank holder force causes the flange to buckle under compressive hoop stresses.
- *Remedy (0.5 mark):* Increase blank holder pressure; use draw beads at appropriate locations.

**Defect (ii) Earing (2.5 marks)**
- *Sketch (1 mark):* Show a deep-drawn cup with 4 ears (peaks) and 4 valleys around the rim.
- *Cause:* Crystallographic texture (anisotropy) in the sheet — different directions have different flow strengths, leading to uneven flange drawing.
- *Remedy (0.5 mark):* Use sheets with balanced biaxial texture; intermediate annealing to randomize texture.

**Defect (iii) Stretcher Strains (Lüders Bands) (2.5 marks)**
- *Sketch (1 mark):* Show localized dark bands on the surface of a stretched sheet.
- *Cause:* Yield point phenomenon in low-carbon steels — dislocations pin on interstitial atoms (C, N), causing localized deformation bands.
- *Remedy (0.5 mark):* Apply temper rolling (1–2% reduction) to remove the yield point; use stabilized/killed steels with nitrogen tied up by Al or Ti.

**(b) Explain the phenomenon of springback in bending. Derive an expression for the springback angle in pure bending of an elastic-perfectly-plastic sheet. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

*Phenomenon explanation (1.5 marks):*
Springback is the elastic recovery of a bent sheet after the bending load is released. The bend angle reduces from $\theta_b$ (tool angle) to $\theta_s$ (springback angle). The amount of springback depends on the ratio $Y/E$ — higher yield strength and lower elastic modulus lead to greater springback.

*Derivation (4.5 marks):*
For pure bending of an elastic-perfectly-plastic sheet of thickness $t$:
- Elastic core radius: $c = Y/(E\kappa)$
- Bending moment:

$$
M \;=\; \frac{Y\,t^{2}}{4} \;-\; \frac{Y^{3}}{3E^{2}\kappa^{2}}
$$

- On unloading, the elastic recovery reduces the curvature by $\Delta\kappa = 12M/(Et^3)$.
- Therefore, the **springback angle** is:

$$
\theta_{s} \;=\; \theta_{b} \cdot \left[1 \;-\; \frac{3\,\sigma_{y}}{E}\,\frac{R_{i}}{t}\right]
$$

*Practical compensation (1 mark):*
The tool must be designed with an overbend angle equal to $\theta_s$ to ensure the final part has the desired angle.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU 2024 Mark-Deduction Points — Read Carefully:**
> 1. **Forgetting the remedy** — A 7-mark sub-question with only defect + cause (no remedy) caps at ~4 marks. Always end with a remedy.
> 2. **No sketch** — A "neat sketch" question without a sketch loses 1.5–2 marks instantly. Even a rough line diagram scores partial credit.
> 3. **Wrong terminology** — Saying "orange peel" when it is "alligatoring" (or vice-versa) loses 1 mark. Memorize the exact names.
> 4. **Skipping the formula** — In derivation questions, write the starting equation *before* integrating. Starting with the integrated form loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Mixing up pipe/piping** — "Piping defect" is extrusion-specific (rear of billet), NOT center burst.
> 6. **Forging "scales" vs "flakes"** — Scales are surface oxide; flakes are internal hydrogen cracks. Examiners will deduct for confusion.
> 7. **Springback direction** — Springback *reduces* the bend angle on release, not increases. Wrong direction = 0.5 mark cut.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Revision — 30-Sec Memory Refresh:**

- **Forging:** Cold shut (low temp/sharp corner), Laps (sharp corner), Flakes (H₂), Die shift (guides), Unfilled (billet vol).
- **Rolling:** Wavy edge (camber), Edge crack (draft/temp), Center burst (redundant tension), Alligatoring (bite angle), Barrelling (friction).
- **Extrusion:** Center burst/chevron (R/die angle), Piping (excess R, dummy block), Die lines (die polish), Twisting (asymmetry).
- **Drawing:** Center burst (limit ≤ 30% reduction), Orange peel (grain size), Seams (die wear).
- **Sheet Forming:** Springback (overbend), Wrinkling (↑BHF), Tearing (↓DR, multi-stage), Earing (texture), Stretcher strains (temper roll).
- **Key formulas to memorize:** Friction hill $p = Y_f(1 + 2\mu D/3h)$, Center burst criterion (Avitzur), Springback ratio $R_i/R_f$, Bite angle $\alpha = \sqrt{\Delta h / R}$.
- **Avitzur Rule of Thumb:** "Long, thin, low-angle, low-friction" = center burst prone.
- **Friction Paradox:** Friction *suppresses* center burst in extrusion (counter-intuitive) but *causes* barrelling in upsetting.
- **Deep Draw Ratio Limit:** DR ≤ 2.0 for steel single-stage; use multi-stage beyond.
- **Springback Direction:** Always *opposes* the deformation — bend opens up on release.
- **Springback Compensation Rule of Thumb:** $R_i/t$ ratio governs; thicker sheets = less springback.
- **Stretcher strains** = yield point phenomenon → cure by **temper rolling** (1–2% cold reduction).
- **Earing** = texture-driven → cure by **balanced biaxial texture** sheets or intermediate anneal.
- **Or ange peel** in drawing = coarse grain → cure by **refining grain** before drawing.
- **Cold shut** in forging = surface fold, not crack → cure by **fillet radii** and **higher temperature**.
- **Flakes** = internal transverse cracks from **hydrogen** → cure by **vacuum degassing** and **furnace cooling**.
- **Piping** = rear funnel cavity in extrusion → cure by **dummy block pressure** and **discarding rear portion**.
- **Alligatoring / fish-mouth** = longitudinal splitting in rolling → cure by **larger roll radius** and **higher temperature**.
- **For unified understanding:** Every defect has a **cause (parameter)** and a **remedy (parameter adjustment)** — the examiner looks for this pairing.

> **End of Topic Notes — Defects of Various Metal Forming Processes and their Remedies (PEIET524, Module 2, KTU 2024 Scheme)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Role of Tool Design in Manufacturing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET524 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Role of Tool Design in Manufacturing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Tool Design** is the branch of manufacturing engineering that involves the systematic planning, analysis, and creation of tools, dies, jigs, fixtures, gauges, and other production aids required to transform raw material into a finished product of desired geometry, dimensional accuracy, and surface quality. In the context of **Metal Forming and Tool Design (PEIET524)**, it specifically refers to the engineering of dies, punches, mandrels, and associated tooling used in operations such as **forging, stamping, deep drawing, extrusion, bending, and sheet metal forming**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition Hook**
> *Tool design is the integration of mechanical engineering principles, material science, manufacturing processes, and economics to produce tooling that is functional, durable, economical, and safe.*

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Architect's Blueprint" of Manufacturing

Think of tool design as the **architectural blueprint of a building**. Just as an architect plans every pillar, beam, and window before construction begins, a tool designer plans every contour, clearance angle, and surface finish of a die before a single component is produced.

| Analogy Element | Real-World Equivalent in Tool Design |
| :--- | :--- |
| Architect's blueprint | Engineering drawing of the die/tool |
| Building foundation | Die set, bolster, and frame |
| Pillar/structural member | Punch, die block, stripper |
| Door/window placement | Clearance, draft angle, radii |
| Building occupants (users) | Sheet metal being formed |
| Construction cost | Tool fabrication and maintenance cost |
| Building lifespan | **Tool life** (typically **10,000 – 1,000,000 strokes**) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Tool Design Matters in KTU Context**
> The tool is the **silent partner** of every manufactured product. A poorly designed tool can scrap an entire batch worth lakhs of rupees, while an intelligently designed tool can produce millions of components with micron-level accuracy. In KTU's NEP 2020 framework, tool design is treated as a **multi-disciplinary competency** bridging design, materials, and production engineering.

---

### 1.3 Scope of Tool Design in Manufacturing

The role of tool design in manufacturing is **multi-dimensional**, influencing every Key Performance Indicator (KPI) of a production system. The major dimensions are:

- **Functional Role** — Imparting desired shape, size, and surface finish.
- **Economic Role** — Minimizing cost per component and maximizing tool life.
- **Quality Role** — Ensuring dimensional accuracy and repeatability.
- **Safety Role** — Incorporating fail-safes for operators and machines.
- **Productivity Role** — Enabling high-speed, automated, and continuous production.
- **Innovation Role** — Enabling near-net-shape and net-shape manufacturing.

> [!TIP]
> **Did You Know?**
> The global tooling industry is valued at approximately **USD 200 billion** (as of 2024 estimates). In automotive sheet metal forming, tooling cost can constitute **5–15%** of total product cost, yet it determines **100%** of the product's geometric quality.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Functions Performed by a Tool (Die)

A forming tool performs several simultaneous functions, each contributing to the final product. The following structured logic explains the **"Why"** and **"How"** of each function:

1. **Shaping (Geometric Definition)**
   - *Why?* Raw material has no useful form.
   - *How?* The cavity geometry of the die physically forces the metal to flow into the required shape through plastic deformation.

2. **Sizing (Dimensional Control)**
   - *Why?* Components must interchange and assemble.
   - *How?* Precision machining of die surfaces with **tolerances as tight as ±0.01 mm** for high-grade dies.

3. **Surface Finishing**
   - *Why?* Functional and aesthetic requirements.
   - *How?* Polished die cavities (Ra **0.1 – 0.8 μm**) transfer finish to the workpiece.

4. **Force Transmission and Containment**
   - *Why?* Uncontrolled metal flow causes defects (wrinkles, cracks, thinning).
   - *How?* Draw beads, blank holders, and pressure pads regulate metal flow.

5. **Ejection and Stripping**
   - *Why?* Sticking parts damage tools and halt production.
   - *How?* Stripper plates, ejector pins, and positive knockouts.

6. **Cooling and Lubrication Routing**
   - *Why?* Heat degrades tool hardness; friction causes wear.
   - *How?* Internal coolant channels and lubrication channels.

---

### 2.2 Classification of Tools in Manufacturing

Tools can be broadly classified based on their function:

| Category | Examples | Primary Role |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Cutting Tools** | Single-point turning tool, milling cutter, drill | Material removal by chip formation |
| **Forming Tools** | Forging die, stamping die, extrusion die, drawing die | Shape change without material removal |
| **Holding Tools (Workholding)** | Jigs, fixtures, chucks, vises | Locate and secure workpiece |
| **Inspecting Tools (Gauges)** | Plug gauge, snap gauge, ring gauge, CMM probe | Verify dimensional accuracy |
| **Assembly Tools** | Torque wrenches, jigs for riveting | Joining components |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note**
> In metal forming (Module 3 context), the focus is primarily on **forming tools** — particularly **press tools** for sheet metal operations and **forging dies** for bulk deformation.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table summarizes all key equations relevant to tool design analysis in manufacturing:

| **#** | **Formula** | **Meaning** | **Typical Units** |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $V \cdot T^{n} = C$ | **Taylor's Tool Life Equation** — $V$ is cutting speed, $T$ is tool life, $n$ and $C$ are constants | $V$ in m/min, $T$ in min |
| 2 | $C_{pc} = C_{t} + C_{o} \cdot t_{m}$ | **Cost per component** — $C_t$ is tool cost, $C_o$ is operator/machine rate, $t_m$ is machining time | $C_{pc}$ in ₹/piece |
| 3 | $T_{opt} = \dfrac{1}{n}\left[\dfrac{C_{t}}{C_{o}}(n-1) + t_{ct}\right]$ | **Economic tool life** minimizing cost per piece | $T_{opt}$ in min |
| 4 | $\eta = \dfrac{V_{max} - V_{min}}{V_{max}} \times 100$ | **Tool wear efficiency** / relative wear percentage | Dimensionless (%) |
| 5 | $F_{d} = \pi \cdot d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_{d} \cdot K$ | **Drawing force** in deep drawing — $d$ is blank diameter, $t$ is thickness, $\sigma_d$ is draw stress, $K$ is die geometry factor | $F_d$ in N |
| 6 | $N_{b} = \dfrac{\text{Tool Cost}}{\text{Saving per part}}$ | **Economic batch size** to justify a new tool | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $\text{Productivity} = \dfrac{N_{parts}}{T_{cycle}}$ | **Production rate** — pieces produced per unit time | pieces/hour |
| 8 | $t_{cycle} = t_{s} + t_{f} + t_{h}$ | **Cycle time** = setup + forming + handling time | seconds or minutes |

> [!WARNING]
> **Vertical Pipe Rule** — In KTU answer scripts and in this table, the absolute value $\vert x \vert$ is written using `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid markdown table corruption.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The above framework is used in:

- **Automotive Industry** — Stamping dies for car body panels must produce **6–8 cars per hour** consistently over **5+ years**.
- **Aerospace** — Forging dies for turbine blades operate at elevated temperatures (**900–1100°C** for Ni-superalloys).
- **White Goods (Refrigerators, Washing Machines)** — Progressive dies producing **200–400 parts/minute** for sheet metal panels.
- **Electronics** — Micro-stamping and micro-forming tools for connectors and lead frames.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Taylor's Tool Life Equation (Conceptual)

**Statement:** Tool life $T$ is related to cutting speed $V$ through a power-law relationship.

#### Step 1 — Empirical Observation
Through controlled machining experiments, F. W. Taylor (1907) observed that:
- When cutting speed is **doubled**, tool life does **not** halve linearly; it reduces **drastically** (e.g., to 1/8 or 1/16 of original life).

#### Step 2 — Mathematical Formulation
Taylor proposed the inverse power-law relationship:

$$V \cdot T^{n} = C$$

where:
- $V$ = cutting speed (m/min)
- $T$ = tool life (min)
- $n$ = Taylor's exponent (typically 0.1 to 0.5, often ~0.25 for HSS tools)
- $C$ = constant depending on tool-workpiece combination

#### Step 3 — Solving for Tool Life $T$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V \cdot T^{n} &= C \\
T^{n} &= \frac{C}{V} \\
T &= \left(\frac{C}{V}\right)^{1/n}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** Taking the $n$-th root of both sides gives tool life as a function of cutting speed.

#### Step 4 — Physical Interpretation
- A higher $n$ (e.g., 0.4) means tool life is **less sensitive** to speed changes.
- A lower $n$ (e.g., 0.1) means tool life is **highly sensitive** — small speed increases drastically reduce life.
- This equation is the **backbone of machining parameter selection** and economic tool life analysis.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Economic Tool Life (Minimum Cost Criterion)

**Goal:** Find the tool life $T_{opt}$ that minimizes the cost per component.

#### Step 1 — Define Cost per Component

The cost per component $C_{pc}$ is given by:

$$C_{pc} = C_{t} + C_{o} \cdot t_{m}$$

where:
- $C_t$ = tool cost amortized over its life, i.e., $\dfrac{C_{\text{tool}}}{N_{\text{parts}}}$
- $C_o$ = operator + machine overhead rate (₹/min)
- $t_m$ = machining time per part (min/part)

#### Step 2 — Express Variables in Terms of Tool Life

From Taylor's equation, machining time $t_m$ is inversely proportional to cutting speed $V$, and $V$ is related to $T$ by $V = C \cdot T^{-n}$. Therefore:

$$t_m = \frac{k}{V} = \frac{k \cdot T^{n}}{C}$$

where $k$ is a constant relating feed, depth of cut, and length of cut.

#### Step 3 — Express Number of Parts per Tool Life

The number of parts $N$ produced in tool life $T$ is:

$$N = \frac{T}{t_{ct} + t_{m}}$$

where $t_{ct}$ is the tool change time per part.

#### Step 4 — Cost per Component in Terms of $T$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pc} &= \frac{C_{\text{tool}}}{N} + C_{o} \cdot t_{m} \\
C_{pc} &= \frac{C_{\text{tool}} \cdot (t_{ct} + t_{m})}{T} + C_{o} \cdot t_{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting $t_m \propto T^n$ and simplifying, then differentiating $C_{pc}$ with respect to $T$ and setting $\dfrac{dC_{pc}}{dT} = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dC_{pc}}{dT} &= 0 \\
-\frac{C_{\text{tool}} \cdot (t_{ct} + t_{m})}{T^{2}} + \frac{C_{\text{tool}} \cdot t_{m}'}{T} + C_{o} \cdot t_{m}' &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result (Economic Tool Life):**

$$T_{opt} = \frac{1}{n}\left[\frac{C_{\text{tool}}}{C_{o}}(n - 1) + t_{ct}\right]$$

#### Step 5 — Engineering Insight

- If $C_{\text{tool}}$ (tool cost) is **high**, $T_{opt}$ is **large** — use the tool longer to spread the cost.
- If $C_o$ (machine/operator rate) is **high**, $T_{opt}$ is **small** — replace the tool quickly to save expensive machine time.
- If $n$ is **small** (highly speed-sensitive tools), $T_{opt}$ is **small** — replace tools more frequently.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Economic Batch Size for Tool Investment

**Scenario:** A manufacturer must decide the production volume above which investing in a new dedicated tool is justified.

#### Step 1 — Define Total Cost Functions

Total cost with **existing/shared tool**:

$$C_{1}(N) = C_{\text{setup,1}} + N \cdot c_{1}$$

Total cost with **new dedicated tool**:

$$C_{2}(N) = C_{\text{tool}} + C_{\text{setup,2}} + N \cdot c_{2}$$

where $c_1 > c_2$ (per-part cost is higher with shared tool).

#### Step 2 — Break-Even Point

Set $C_1(N) = C_2(N)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{setup,1}} + N \cdot c_{1} &= C_{\text{tool}} + C_{\text{setup,2}} + N \cdot c_{2} \\
N \cdot (c_{1} - c_{2}) &= C_{\text{tool}} + C_{\text{setup,2}} - C_{\text{setup,1}} \\
N_{b} &= \frac{C_{\text{tool}} + \Delta C_{\text{setup}}}{c_{1} - c_{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision rule:** Produce $N \geq N_b$ parts to justify the new tool.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Tool Design Impact on Manufacturing KPIs

| **KPI** | **Poor Tool Design** | **Excellent Tool Design** | **Improvement Factor** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Production rate (parts/hr) | 50 | 300 | **6×** |
| Scrap rate (%) | 8 | 0.5 | **16×** |
| Tool life (strokes) | 50,000 | 500,000 | **10×** |
| Setup time (min) | 120 | 20 | **6×** |
| Operator skill needed | High | Low | — |
| Energy per part (kWh) | 1.2 | 0.4 | **3×** |
| First-piece inspection | Frequent | Once | — |
| Downtime (hrs/month) | 30 | 5 | **6×** |

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is **exam-relevant** for KTU Part B (14-mark) questions asking for "Discuss the role of tool design in improving manufacturing productivity."
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Systematic Tool Design Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Product Requirement] --> B[Study Engineering Drawing]
    B --> C[Identify Manufacturing Process]
    C --> D[Estimate Production Volume]
    D --> E[Select Tool Material]
    E --> F[Design Tool Geometry]
    F --> G[Calculate Forces and Stresses]
    G --> H[Perform FEA and Validation]
    H --> I[Generate Tool Drawings]
    I --> J[Fabricate Tool]
    J --> K[Tryout and Testing]
    K --> L{Meets Specs?}
    L -->|No| M[Redesign and Iterate]
    M --> F
    L -->|Yes| N[Release for Production]
    N --> O[Monitor Tool Life]
    O --> P[Schedule Maintenance]
    P --> Q[End: Continuous Improvement]
```

**Block-Level Functional Architecture Explanation:**

- **Inputs (Nodes A–B):** Product CAD model, tolerances, material grade, batch size.
- **Processing (Nodes C–H):** Process selection (stamping, forging, etc.), material selection, geometric design, force analysis, finite element validation.
- **Outputs (Nodes I–K):** Engineering drawings, fabricated tool, validated performance.
- **Feedback Loop (L → M → F):** Iterative refinement until specifications are met.
- **Lifecycle Management (N–Q):** Production release, in-process monitoring, and predictive maintenance.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Factors Influencing Tool Design

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Inputs
        A1[Product Geometry]
        A2[Material Properties]
        A3[Batch Size]
        A4[Quality Standards]
    end
    subgraph DesignParameters
        B1[Tool Material Selection]
        B2[Clearance and Draft Angle]
        B3[Force and Stress Analysis]
        B4[Cooling and Lubrication]
    end
    subgraph Outcomes
        C1[Tool Life]
        C2[Product Quality]
        C3[Production Cost]
        C4[Manufacturing Lead Time]
    end
    A1 --> B2
    A1 --> B3
    A2 --> B1
    A2 --> B3
    A3 --> B1
    A3 --> C1
    A4 --> C2
    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C2
    B3 --> C3
    B4 --> C1
    B1 --> C3
    C1 --> C3
    C2 --> C4
```

**Block Architecture Logic:**
The diagram models tool design as a **multi-input, multi-output transformation system** with four key inputs, four design parameters, and four performance outcomes. The arrows show causal dependencies.

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Decision Tree — Tool Material Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Select Tool Material] --> Q1{Workpiece Hardness}
    Q1 -->|Soft, Non-Ferrous| S1[Carbon Steel AISI 1040]
    Q1 -->|Medium Steel| S2[Oil Hardening Steel O1]
    Q1 -->|Hard Steel, High Volume| S3[High Speed Steel M2]
    Q1 -->|Very High Volume, Abrasive| S4[Carbide K20]
    Q1 -->|High Temperature Forming| S5[Hot Work Steel H13]
    Q1 -->|Extreme Wear Conditions| S6[Stellite or Tool Steel with Coating]
    S1 --> End1[Validate Cost vs Life]
    S2 --> End1
    S3 --> End1
    S4 --> End1
    S5 --> End1
    S6 --> End1
    End1 --> Final[Final Material Selection]
```

**Block Architecture Explanation:**
This decision tree reflects the **hierarchical decision logic** used in KTU-level design problems. Each branch corresponds to a tool material family, and the **terminal node (Final)** integrates cost-versus-life trade-off analysis.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Bank]**
*Define tool design. List any four functions performed by a forming tool.*

**Model Answer:**

**Tool Design:** Tool design is the engineering discipline of planning and creating tools, dies, jigs, fixtures, and gauges required to manufacture a product with the desired shape, size, surface finish, and quantity.

**Four functions of a forming tool:**

1. **Shaping** — Imparting the required geometry to the workpiece through plastic deformation.
2. **Sizing** — Maintaining dimensional accuracy within prescribed tolerances.
3. **Surface finishing** — Producing a controlled surface texture on the workpiece.
4. **Force regulation** — Controlling the magnitude and direction of forming forces to prevent defects.

**Marks Distribution:** Definition (1.5 marks) + List with brief explanation (1.5 marks).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected]**
*State Taylor's tool life equation. Explain the significance of the exponent $n$.*

**Model Answer:**

**Taylor's Tool Life Equation:**

$$V \cdot T^{n} = C$$

where $V$ is cutting speed, $T$ is tool life, $n$ is the Taylor exponent, and $C$ is a constant.

**Significance of $n$:**

- The exponent $n$ indicates **how sensitive tool life is to cutting speed**.
- A **low value of $n$** (e.g., 0.1) implies that even a small increase in speed causes a **drastic reduction** in tool life — the tool is highly speed-sensitive (typical of carbide tools).
- A **high value of $n$** (e.g., 0.4) implies tool life is **less affected** by speed variations (typical of HSS tools in mild conditions).

**Marks Distribution:** Equation (1.5 marks) + Significance (1.5 marks).

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** *Explain in detail the role of tool design in improving manufacturing productivity and product quality. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *Discuss the various factors that must be considered during the design of a press tool for sheet metal operations. (7 marks)*

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) Solution

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Tool design acts as the **interface between product design and mass production**. It translates a static engineering drawing into a repeatable, high-volume manufacturing process.

**Role in Productivity (3 marks):**

1. **Cycle time reduction** — Well-designed tools minimize idle strokes, part handling, and tool changeovers.
2. **Higher machine utilization** — Tools with robust design can run at higher press speeds (e.g., **200–600 strokes/min** in progressive dies).
3. **Combined operations** — Progressive dies perform cutting, bending, and piercing in a single stroke, eliminating separate stations.
4. **Automation compatibility** — Properly designed tools integrate with feeders, ejectors, and robotic arms, enabling lights-out manufacturing.

**Role in Product Quality (3 marks):**

1. **Dimensional consistency** — Precision-ground die surfaces ensure every part is within **±0.05 mm** tolerance.
2. **Surface integrity** — Polished die cavities produce parts with **Ra ≤ 0.8 μm** without secondary finishing.
3. **Defect prevention** — Correct draft angles, fillet radii, and clearances eliminate wrinkles, cracks, and spring-back errors.
4. **Repeatability** — Modern tool designs with sensors and in-process monitoring maintain quality over millions of cycles.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
Tool design is the **single largest determinant** of both productivity and quality in mass production. A 10% improvement in tool design can yield a 30–50% improvement in overall manufacturing efficiency.

---

#### Part (b) Solution

**Introduction (1 mark):**
A press tool for sheet metal operations is a complex assembly of punches, dies, strippers, and guide systems. Its design must consider multiple interacting factors.

**Key Design Factors (6 marks — 1 mark each + brief explanation):**

| **#** | **Factor** | **Explanation** |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Material of workpiece** | Determines die material, clearance, and lubrication. Stainless steel (work-hardening) needs different clearances than mild steel. |
| 2 | **Thickness of sheet** | Affects punch-die clearance (typically **5–10% of thickness per side**), press tonnage, and die strength. |
| 3 | **Production volume** | High volume → progressive die; low volume → simple compound or single-operation die. |
| 4 | **Tool material selection** | HSS for low-medium runs; carbide (e.g., **K20**) for high-volume abrasive work; H13 for hot working. |
| 5 | **Clearance and draft angles** | Proper clearance prevents burrs and tool damage; draft (typically **0.5–2°**) enables easy part ejection. |
| 6 | **Spring-back compensation** | Sheet metal springs back after bending; die must be **over-bent by 2–5°** to compensate. |
| 7 | **Lubrication and cooling** | Reduces friction, prevents galling, and extends tool life. |
| 8 | **Safety features** | Guarding, overload protectors, and two-hand controls for operator safety. |

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
A holistic view integrating material behavior, economics, ergonomics, and manufacturing constraints is essential for an effective press tool design.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**

**(a)** *With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the systematic procedure for designing a forming tool. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *A manufacturer is evaluating two tool options for a stamping operation. Tool X costs ₹25,000 with a life of 10,000 parts. Tool Y costs ₹80,000 with a life of 50,000 parts. If the operator and machine cost is ₹3 per minute and tool changeover takes 4 minutes, find the more economical option. (7 marks)*

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) Solution

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Tool design is a **systematic, iterative process** that bridges the product design stage and the production stage.

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

Refer to the Mermaid flowchart in Section 4.1 (Tool Design Process). The student should reproduce the following sequence:

1. **Study product drawing** — Geometry, tolerances, material.
2. **Select manufacturing process** — Stamping, forging, drawing, etc.
3. **Estimate production volume** — Determines die complexity.
4. **Select tool material** — Based on workpiece and volume.
5. **Design tool geometry** — Clearances, draft, radii, force flow.
6. **Calculate forces and stresses** — Press tonnage, die block dimensions.
7. **Validate via FEA / simulation** — Identify weak regions.
8. **Generate detailed drawings** — For fabrication.
9. **Fabricate and tryout** — Test on actual press.
10. **Iterate until approved** — Redesign if defects occur.
11. **Release for production** — With monitoring plan.

**Explanation of Each Step (3 marks — 0.3 mark each):**
Each step should be explained with one or two sentences covering its purpose, inputs, and outputs.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
The systematic procedure ensures that the tool is functional, durable, safe, and economical, while minimizing time-to-production.

---

#### Part (b) Solution

**Given Data:**

- Tool X: Cost $C_X = ₹25,000$, Life $N_X = 10,000$ parts
- Tool Y: Cost $C_Y = ₹80,000$, Life $N_Y = 50,000$ parts
- Operator + machine rate $C_o = ₹3/\text{min}$
- Tool changeover time $t_{ct} = 4$ min

**Step 1 — Tool Cost per Part (Amortized)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost per part (Tool X)} &= \frac{C_X}{N_X} = \frac{25{,}000}{10{,}000} = ₹2.50 \\
\text{Cost per part (Tool Y)} &= \frac{C_Y}{N_Y} = \frac{80{,}000}{50{,}000} = ₹1.60
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Machine Time Cost per Part**

Assuming the changeover time is amortized over the tool life:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Time cost per part (Tool X)} &= \frac{C_o \cdot t_{ct}}{N_X} = \frac{3 \times 4}{10{,}000} = ₹0.0012 \\
\text{Time cost per part (Tool Y)} &= \frac{C_o \cdot t_{ct}}{N_Y} = \frac{3 \times 4}{50{,}000} = ₹0.00024
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Total Cost per Part**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pc, X} &= 2.50 + 0.0012 \approx ₹2.501 \\
C_{pc, Y} &= 1.60 + 0.00024 \approx ₹1.600
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Decision**

Tool Y is more economical by approximately **₹0.90 per part**, despite its higher initial cost. This is because its longer life spreads the tool cost over **5× more parts**.

**Conclusion:** **Tool Y is the more economical choice** for high-volume production.

**Marks Distribution:** Given data identification (1) + Cost-per-part calculation (3) + Time cost (1.5) + Comparison and decision (1.5).

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "tool design" with "product design"** — Tool design is about *how to make* the product, not the product itself. (Loses 1–2 marks)
> 2. **Forgetting to include units** in the Taylor equation or cost calculations. Always specify $V$ in m/min, $T$ in min, cost in ₹/piece. (Loses 0.5–1 mark)
> 3. **Skipping the conclusion line** in 14-mark questions. KTU examiners allocate **1 mark** specifically for a concluding statement summarizing the answer. (Loses 1 mark)
> 4. **In numerical problems, not showing substitution step** — Always substitute the numerical values into the formula *before* simplifying. This is the **valuation key pattern** in KTU answer scripts.
> 5. **Ignoring spring-back and clearance in press tool design** — These are **favourite KTU questions** and frequently tested.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Must Memorize for KTU ESE):**

- [x] **Tool design** = engineering of dies, punches, jigs, fixtures, gauges.
- [x] **Primary functions** = shaping, sizing, surface finishing, force regulation, ejection, cooling.
- [x] **Taylor's equation**: $V \cdot T^{n} = C$ → solve for $T$ as $T = (C/V)^{1/n}$.
- [x] **Economic tool life formula**: $T_{opt} = \frac{1}{n}\left[\frac{C_{t}}{C_{o}}(n-1) + t_{ct}\right]$.
- [x] **Economic batch size**: $N_b = \frac{C_{\text{tool}} + \Delta C_{\text{setup}}}{c_1 - c_2}$.
- [x] **Press tool clearance** = 5–10% of sheet thickness per side.
- [x] **Draft angle** = 0.5–2° (typical) for easy part ejection.
- [x] **Spring-back compensation** = over-bend by 2–5° in bending dies.
- [x] **Tool material families**: AISI 1040 (low), O1 (medium), M2 HSS (high), K20 carbide (very high), H13 (hot work).
- [x] **Cost per part = Tool cost amortized + Machine/operator time cost.**
- [x] **Tool design impacts 4 KPIs**: Productivity, Quality, Cost, Safety.
- [x] **Systematic design process** = 11-step flow (drawing → process → material → geometry → forces → FEA → drawings → fabricate → tryout → iterate → release).
- [x] **Block diagrams and flowcharts** are **mandatory visuals** for full marks in 7-mark sub-questions.

> **High-Yield KTU Key Terms to Use in Answers:**
> *Net-shape manufacturing, near-net-shape, die life, tool wear, spring-back, blank holder force, draw bead, stripper plate, bolster, shut height, die set, progressive die, compound die, combination die.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Tools Used in Metal Forming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET524 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Types of Tools Used in Metal Forming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard Terminology):**
> **Tools used in metal forming** are specialized, high-precision, hardened devices (dies, punches, mandrels, rollers, etc.) designed to apply controlled mechanical forces on a workpiece to cause **plastic deformation** without material removal, thereby producing a desired geometry, size, and surface finish. They are classified according to the forming operation they perform — cutting (blanking, piercing), bending, drawing, forging, extrusion, or rolling.

Tooling in metal forming is broadly grouped under two families:

| Family | Functional Role | Typical Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Deformation (Forming) Tools** | Cause plastic flow of the metal to reshape it | Drawing die, forging die, extrusion die, roll |
| **Cutting (Shearing) Tools** | Separate material along a defined contour | Blanking punch & die, piercing punch & die |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Kitchen Cookie Cutter vs. The Clay Mould**
> - Imagine you have a **flattened ball of dough** (workpiece). If you press a **sharp-edged cookie cutter** (punch + die) into it, the dough gets **cut along a sharp line** and the unwanted part is detached — this is exactly how a **blanking/piercing die** works.
> - Now imagine you press the dough into a **mould cavity** of a specific shape (e.g., a star). The dough flows plastically and takes the cavity's shape **without any cutting** — this is how a **drawing / forging / extrusion die** works.
> - The metal forming shop is essentially an industrial-scale "kitchen," where each tool is a precisely engineered mould or cutter designed for mass production of identical parts at high speed.

### 1.3 Why Tooling is Central to Metal Forming

- A single set of tools can produce **millions of identical parts** with sub-millimeter accuracy.
- Tooling cost is amortized over the production volume, which is why **tool design is the engineering heart** of any sheet-metal or bulk-forming process.
- Tool wear, failure, and material selection directly determine **product quality, machine tonnage, cycle time, and unit cost**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 3):**
> The student is expected to understand the **classification, constructional features, working principles, and selection criteria** of tools used in **sheet-metal cutting (blanking, piercing, trimming)**, **sheet-metal forming (bending, deep drawing, stretch forming)**, and **bulk forming (forging, extrusion)** operations.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Master Classification of Metal Forming Tools

Metal forming tools can be classified along **three independent axes**:

1. **By type of operation performed** (cutting vs. forming).
2. **By constructional complexity** (simple die vs. progressive die).
3. **By the forming process served** (forging die, extrusion die, rolling tool, etc.).

#### 2.1.1 Tools for Sheet-Metal Cutting Operations

| Tool Type | Functional Description | Typical Industries |
|---|---|---|
| **Blanking Die** | Produces a flat part by shearing the contour out of a strip | Auto body panels, washers, gaskets |
| **Piercing Die** | Produces holes in a flat part without removing the part itself | Sheet-metal enclosures, electrical panels |
| **Trimming Die** | Removes excess flash/material from a previously formed part | Drawn cups, forged components |
| **Shaving Die** | Removes a thin sliver (≈ 0.05–0.25 mm) to improve edge finish | Precision gears, motor laminations |
| **Notching Die** | Removes material from the edge of a strip | Electrical laminations, brackets |
| **Lancing Die** | Cuts partially without removing material, then bends | Vent louvers, heat-shield panels |

#### 2.1.2 Tools for Sheet-Metal Bending & Forming Operations

| Tool Type | Functional Description |
|---|---|
| **V-Die and Punch (Press Brake Tooling)** | V-shaped bottom die with matching punch bends the sheet along a straight line |
| **U-Bending / Channel Die** | Produces U-shaped channels in one stroke |
| **Draw Die (Single-Action)** | A punch forces the sheet into a die cavity to produce a cup-like part |
| **Redrawing Die** | Reduces the diameter/wall thickness of an already drawn cup |
| **Stretch-Forming Die** | Sheet is clamped and stretched over a form block |
| **Die Forming (Coining / Embossing)** | Sheet is compressed in a closed die cavity for fine detail or thickness reduction |

#### 2.1.3 Tools for Bulk Forming Operations

| Tool Type | Functional Description | Process |
|---|---|---|
| **Open-Die Hammer Dies (Set of Swage Blocks)** | Flat or simple contoured dies; workpiece is manipulated between strikes | Open-die forging |
| **Closed (Impression) Die Forging Tools** | Multi-impression die set (edger → blocker → finisher) | Closed-die forging |
| **Extrusion Dies (Direct & Indirect)** | Container, dummy block, and die with one or more orifices | Hot/Cold extrusion |
| **Rolling Mill Rolls (Work Rolls, Back-up Rolls)** | Two contra-rotating cylindrical tools reduce thickness | Flat / shape rolling |
| **Drawing Die (Wire/Tube)** | Conical die reduces cross-section through a die orifice | Wire / tube drawing |
| **Swaging Dies** | Reciprocating dies that hammer the workpiece radially | Rotary swaging |
| **Header Dies** | Cut-off and heading dies in a header machine | Cold heading (bolts, screws) |

#### 2.1.4 Tools Classified by Constructional Complexity

| Type | Description | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Simple Die** | One operation per stroke | High-volume single-feature parts |
| **Compound Die** | Two or more cutting operations in a single stroke (e.g., blank + pierce) | Gaskets with holes |
| **Combination Die** | Both cutting AND forming in one stroke | Cup-shaped part cut and formed together |
| **Progressive Die** | Multiple stations along a strip; each station performs one operation | High-volume multi-feature parts |
| **Transfer Die** | Workpiece transferred mechanically between separate stations | Large automotive panels |

### 2.2 Constructional Anatomy of a Generic Sheet-Metal Cutting Die

A standard **piercing/blanking die set** consists of:

- **Upper Shoe / Punch Holder** — clamps the punch to the press ram.
- **Punch** — the hardened male tool that penetrates the sheet.
- **Stripper Plate** — strips the sheet off the punch on the upstroke.
- **Die Block (Matrix)** — the hardened female tool containing the cutting cavity.
- **Lower Shoe / Die Set** — supports the die block on the press bed.
- **Guide Pins & Bushings** — ensure precise alignment between punch and die.
- **Punch Retainer & Back-up Plate** — provide rigid support to the punch shank.
- **Stop / Pilot Pin** — locates the strip accurately for the next pitch.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F = L \cdot t \cdot \tau$ | $F$ = blanking force (N), $L$ = perimeter (mm), $t$ = thickness (mm), $\tau$ = shear strength (MPa) | Blanking & piercing force |
| 2 | $F_p = \pi \cdot d \cdot t \cdot \tau$ | $d$ = hole diameter (mm) | Piercing force for a circular hole |
| 3 | $F_{stripper} = (0.05 \text{ to } 0.10) \cdot F$ | — | Stripper spring design |
| 4 | $F_{press} = \dfrac{F}{\eta}$ | $\eta$ = mechanical efficiency (≈ 0.6–0.8) | Press tonnage selection |
| 5 | $C_h = 0.005 \cdot t \cdot \sqrt{t}$ (approx.) | $C_h$ = clearance per side (mm) | Die clearance for low-carbon steel |
| 6 | $D_d = D_p + 2C_h$ | $D_d$ = die bore, $D_p$ = punch diameter | Die opening dimension |
| 7 | $t_{die} = 3 \cdot F / (\sigma_{allow} \cdot L)$ | $\sigma_{allow}$ = die material allowable (MPa) | Minimum die block thickness |
| 8 | $D_{draw} = m \cdot d_p$ | $m$ = drawing ratio (typically ≤ 2.0 per stage) | Deep drawing blank diameter |
| 9 | $F_{draw} = \pi \cdot d_p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_{flow} \cdot \ln(DR)$ | $\sigma_{flow}$ = flow stress, $DR$ = drawing ratio | Deep-drawing punch force |
| 10 | $F_{forging} = K_f \cdot A \cdot \sigma_{flow}$ | $K_f$ = forging factor (1.5–3.0), $A$ = projected area | Forging press tonnage |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These relations are the bedrock of **tool-design numericals** in KTU examinations. Part-B questions on this topic almost always require computing **blanking force**, **die clearance**, or **press tonnage** using the formulas above. Memorizing them with the units of each variable is the single highest-yield activity for scoring in the ESE (End Semester Examination).

### 2.4 Tool Materials — A Quick Reference

| Material | Typical Hardness (HRC) | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **AISI D2** (Cold-work tool steel) | 58–62 | Blanking, piercing, drawing dies |
| **AISI A2 / D3** | 58–62 | Forming dies, trimming dies |
| **AISI H11 / H13** (Hot-work steel) | 48–52 | Forging, hot extrusion dies |
| **Carbide (WC-Co)** | 70+ (Rockwell C-equiv.) | High-volume stamping, wire drawing |
| **Tool Steel with TiN / CrN coating** | Surface layer | Wear-resistance upgrade |
| **Cast Iron (Meehanite / Ni-hard)** | — | Low-stress forming, low-cost dies |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Blanking & Piercing Force

The **fundamental shearing model** for blanking treats the punch as a wedge that shears the sheet along its perimeter.

**Step 1 — Shear area over the perimeter:**

Consider a punch that cuts a contour of perimeter $L$ through a sheet of thickness $t$. The area of metal that must be sheared is:

$$
A_s = L \cdot t
$$

*Here, the shearing action is concentrated along the cylindrical surface traced by the punch edge — area equals perimeter times thickness.*

**Step 2 — Force required to initiate shear:**

The sheet material resists shear with a strength $\tau$ (the **ultimate shear strength**, not the yield shear). To shear this area, the punch must apply:

$$
F = A_s \cdot \tau
$$

*Substituting the expression for $A_s$:*

$$
F = L \cdot t \cdot \tau
$$

**Step 3 — Specialization to a circular hole (Piercing):**

For a hole of diameter $d$, the perimeter is $L = \pi d$, hence:

$$
F_p = \pi \cdot d \cdot t \cdot \tau
$$

**Step 4 — Practical press tonnage (with safety factor and efficiency):**

Real presses are not 100% efficient, and a design safety margin is standard practice:

$$
F_{press} = \dfrac{F}{\eta}
$$

where $\eta$ is the overall mechanical efficiency of the press (typically 0.6–0.8). Some texts also multiply by an **overload factor** of 1.25 to 1.5 to account for material variability.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Die Clearance (Per-Side)

Die clearance is the **radial gap** between the punch and the die bore on each side.

**Step 1 — Empirical relationship for low-carbon steel:**

Empirical studies (KTU-referenced) yield the rule of thumb:

$$
C_h \approx 0.005 \cdot t \cdot \sqrt{t}
$$

*Here, $t$ is in mm and $C_h$ is in mm per side. For thicker sheets, the clearance scales approximately as $t^{1.5}$.*

**Step 2 — Worked example:**

For a sheet of $t = 4 \text{ mm}$:

$$
C_h = 0.005 \times 4 \times \sqrt{4} = 0.005 \times 4 \times 2 = 0.040 \text{ mm per side}
$$

**Step 3 — Die opening dimension:**

For a punch of diameter $D_p$, the die bore is:

$$
D_d = D_p + 2 \cdot C_h
$$

*Both sides — hence the factor of 2.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Why clearance matters:** Too little clearance causes **secondary shearing**, double burr, and accelerated tool wear. Too much clearance produces a **large rollover zone**, a deep burr, and a tapered (non-vertical) cut edge — which is unacceptable for parts that mate with another surface (e.g., motor laminations).

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Minimum Die Block Thickness

The die block must not fail in **compression / bending** under the punch reaction force.

**Step 1 — Approximate reaction on die block:**

The die must support the full blanking force $F$ across a width equal to the perimeter $L$ (worst case: a long thin blank). The average compressive stress on the die is:

$$
\sigma = \dfrac{F}{L \cdot t_d}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for minimum thickness $t_d$:**

Setting $\sigma = \sigma_{allow}$ (the allowable compressive stress of die material) and solving for $t_d$:

$$
t_d = \dfrac{F}{L \cdot \sigma_{allow}}
$$

**Step 3 — Empirical engineering factor:**

For practical design, an empirical factor of 3 is included to handle stress concentrations at sharp corners:

$$
t_d = \dfrac{3 \cdot F}{L \cdot \sigma_{allow}}
$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Deep Drawing — Maximum Blank Diameter

**Step 1 — Definition of drawing ratio (DR):**

$$
DR = \dfrac{D_b}{d_p}
$$

where $D_b$ = blank diameter, $d_p$ = punch diameter.

**Step 2 — Empirical limit for a single draw (low-carbon steel):**

$$
DR_{max} \approx 2.0
$$

*If the ratio exceeds this, multi-stage redrawing is required.*

**Step 3 — Maximum blank diameter for a given punch:**

$$
D_b = DR_{max} \cdot d_p = 2.0 \cdot d_p
$$

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Tool Selection for Common Operations

| Operation | Workpiece Material | Recommended Die Type | Tool Material | Typical Clearance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Blanking washer (mild steel) | Low-carbon steel | Simple die, progressive die | AISI D2, HSS | 5–10% of $t$ |
| Piercing aluminium can | Aluminium alloy | Progressive die | Carbide, D2 | 4–6% of $t$ |
| Deep-drawing SS cup | Stainless steel | Single-action draw + redraw | AISI D2, coated | 7–10% of $t$ |
| Hot forging a connecting rod | Medium-carbon steel | Closed-die forging (3-impression) | AISI H13 | N/A (bulk) |
| Cold heading a bolt | Medium-carbon steel | Header die (2-die / 3-die) | Carbide / D2 | N/A (bulk) |
| Extruding an aluminium profile | Aluminium alloy | Direct-extrusion die, porthole die | H13, ceramic-coated | N/A (bulk) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Progressive Die

The progressive die is the most exam-relevant compound tool; the following Mermaid diagram shows its functional architecture (strip flow, station sequence, and output).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Coil Stock In] --> A2[Starter Notcher]
    A2 --> A3[Station 1 Pilot Hole Pierce]
    A3 --> A4[Station 2 Pilot Lube Hole]
    A4 --> A5[Station 3 Forming Bend]
    A5 --> A6[Station 4 Restrike]
    A6 --> A7[Station 5 Trim and Pierce]
    A7 --> A8[Station 6 Final Pierce]
    A8 --> A9[Station 7 Blank Out Part]
    A9 --> A10[Finished Part Eject]
    A9 --> A11[Scrap Skeleton Out]

    B1[Upper Shoe Assembly] -.hosts.-> A2
    B1 -.hosts.-> A3
    B1 -.hosts.-> A4
    B1 -.hosts.-> A5
    B1 -.hosts.-> A6
    B1 -.hosts.-> A7
    B1 -.hosts.-> A8
    B1 -.hosts.-> A9

    C1[Lower Shoe Assembly] -.hosts.-> A2
    C1 -.hosts.-> A3
    C1 -.hosts.-> A4
    C1 -.hosts.-> A5
    C1 -.hosts.-> A6
    C1 -.hosts.-> A7
    C1 -.hosts.-> A8
    C1 -.hosts.-> A9

    D1[Die Set with Guide Pins] --> B1
    D1 --> C1
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Forging Die Set

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[Heated Billet] --> F2[Billet Placed in Die]
    F2 --> F3[Upper Die Descends]
    F3 --> F4[Filling Impressions Edger Blocker]
    F4 --> F5[Finishing Impression Closed]
    F5 --> F6[Forged Part with Flash]
    F6 --> F7[Trimming Die Removes Flash]
    F7 --> F8[Final Forged Part]

    G1[Lower Die Half] -.supports.-> F4
    G1 -.supports.-> F5
    G2[Upper Die Half] -.acts as.-> F3
    G2 -.acts as.-> F4
    G2 -.acts as.-> F5
    H1[Die Set and Hammer Press] --> G1
    H1 --> G2
```

### 4.3 Anatomy of a Generic Blanking / Piercing Die

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    T1[Press Ram] --> T2[Punch Shank]
    T2 --> T3[Punch Back-up Plate]
    T3 --> T4[Stripper Plate with Pressure Pads]
    T4 --> T5[Punch Tip Active Cutting Edge]
    T5 --> T6[Workpiece Sheet Metal]
    T6 --> T7[Die Block with Cutting Cavity]
    T7 --> T8[Die Back-up Plate]
    T8 --> T9[Lower Shoe Bolster]
    T9 --> T10[Press Bed]

    T11[Guide Pin] -.aligns.-> T2
    T12[Guide Bushing] -.aligns.-> T11
    T13[Stripper Spring] -.preloads.-> T4
    T14[Stop Pin] -.locates.-> T6
```

### 4.4 Tool-Classification Tree (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1[Metal Forming Tools] --> M2[Sheet Metal Cutting Tools]
    M1 --> M3[Sheet Metal Forming Tools]
    M1 --> M4[Bulk Forming Tools]

    M2 --> N1[Blanking Die]
    M2 --> N2[Piercing Die]
    M2 --> N3[Trimming Die]
    M2 --> N4[Shaving Die]
    M2 --> N5[Notching Die]
    M2 --> N6[Lancing Die]

    M3 --> P1[V Die and Punch]
    M3 --> P2[U Bending Die]
    M3 --> P3[Single Action Draw Die]
    M3 --> P4[Redrawing Die]
    M3 --> P5[Stretch Forming Die]
    M3 --> P6[Coining Die]

    M4 --> Q1[Open Forging Dies]
    M4 --> Q2[Closed Impression Forging Dies]
    M4 --> Q3[Direct Extrusion Die]
    M4 --> Q4[Indirect Extrusion Die]
    M4 --> Q5[Rolling Mill Rolls]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between a blanking die and a piercing die. State any two applications of each.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Blanking Die:** The cut part is the **desired product**, and the remaining strip is scrap. It is used to produce flat components such as **washers, gaskets, and shims**.
- **Piercing Die:** The strip is the **desired product**, and the punched slug is scrap. It is used to produce **holes in sheet metal**, e.g., in **electrical enclosures and motor laminations**.
- *Construction-wise,* both are nearly identical — the difference is **which part is kept as the useful output** and which is rejected.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**What is die clearance? Why is it important in sheet-metal cutting?**

**Model Answer:**

- **Die clearance** is the small **radical gap per side** between the punch and the die bore during blanking/piercing.
- Its magnitude is typically **4%–10% of sheet thickness**, depending on material.
- It is important because it controls the **quality of the cut edge** (rollover, burnish, fracture, burr), the **punching force**, the **tool life**, and the **stripping behaviour** of the sheet off the punch.
- **Insufficient clearance** → double-shear, large burr, accelerated punch wear. **Excessive clearance** → large rollover zone, deep burr, tapered edge.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Question (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Compulsory Attempt**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

> **(a)** Classify the different types of tools used in metal forming with suitable examples. Highlight the differences between **simple, compound, combination, and progressive dies**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A circular washer of **outer diameter 50 mm** and **inner diameter 25 mm** is to be blanked from a **4 mm thick** low-carbon steel strip. The **shear strength of the material is 320 MPa**, the **ultimate tensile strength is 480 MPa**, and the press **mechanical efficiency is 0.75**.
> Calculate:
> 1. The **blanking force** required.
> 2. The **press tonnage** (with a 25% safety factor).
> 3. The **die clearance per side** using the empirical relation $C_h = 0.005 \cdot t \cdot \sqrt{t}$. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Classification of metal forming tools (with examples):**

1. **By Operation Type:**
   - **Sheet-metal cutting tools:** Blanking, piercing, trimming, notching, lancing, shaving dies.
   - **Sheet-metal forming tools:** V-dies, draw dies, redraw dies, stretch-form dies, coining dies.
   - **Bulk-forming tools:** Open and closed forging dies, extrusion dies, rolling rolls, drawing dies, header dies, swaging dies.

2. **By Constructional Complexity:**

| Die Type | Operations per Stroke | Typical Example | Key Advantage |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Simple Die** | One | Pierce a single hole in a strip | Lowest cost, easy to maintain |
| **Compound Die** | Two or more cutting ops in **one stroke** at the **same station** | Blank + hole in a single stroke (e.g., washer) | Excellent concentricity, low part-cost |
| **Combination Die** | One cutting + one forming op in one stroke | Cut blank and coin a recess simultaneously | Reduced handling |
| **Progressive Die** | Each station performs **one op**; workpiece advances by a fixed pitch | Multi-feature automotive bracket | Highest productivity, full automation |

**Differentiating features:**
- A **compound die** performs multiple operations **simultaneously at one station** (e.g., the punch and die cut the outer and inner profile in the same stroke).
- A **progressive die** performs operations **sequentially at multiple stations** (the strip is indexed between hits).
- A **combination die** couples **cutting and forming** in the same stroke.
- A **simple die** is a single-station, single-operation die.

---

##### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- Outer diameter, $D = 50$ mm; Inner diameter, $d = 25$ mm; thickness, $t = 4$ mm
- Shear strength, $\tau = 320$ MPa
- UTS, $\sigma_u = 480$ MPa (not directly required — provided as distractor data)
- Mechanical efficiency, $\eta = 0.75$

**Step 1 — Total perimeter to be sheared:**

The blanking operation must cut **both the outer and inner** contours. The total perimeter is:

$$
L = \pi \cdot D + \pi \cdot d = \pi \cdot (D + d)
$$

$$
L = \pi \cdot (50 + 25) = \pi \cdot 75 = 235.62 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Blanking force:**

$$
F = L \cdot t \cdot \tau
$$

$$
F = 235.62 \times 4 \times 320 = 301{,}593.6 \text{ N} \approx 301.6 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 3 — Press tonnage with efficiency and safety factor:**

First, account for mechanical efficiency:

$$
F_{press, eff} = \dfrac{F}{\eta} = \dfrac{301.6}{0.75} = 402.1 \text{ kN}
$$

Now, apply a 25% safety factor (multiply by 1.25):

$$
F_{press, design} = 402.1 \times 1.25 = 502.6 \text{ kN} \approx 50.3 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**Step 4 — Die clearance per side:**

$$
C_h = 0.005 \cdot t \cdot \sqrt{t} = 0.005 \times 4 \times \sqrt{4} = 0.005 \times 4 \times 2 = 0.040 \text{ mm}
$$

**Final Answers:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Blanking Force, $F$ | **301.6 kN** |
| Press Tonnage (designed) | **502.6 kN ≈ 51 tonnes** |
| Die Clearance per side | **0.040 mm** |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

> **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **constructional features of a forging die set** for closed-die forging. Explain the role of **flash gutter, edger, blocker, and finisher** impressions. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A deep-drawing operation is to produce a **cylindrical cup of diameter 60 mm and height 80 mm** from a **mild-steel sheet of 2 mm thickness**. Assuming a **single-stage draw is permitted**, calculate the **maximum blank diameter** and the **drawing force** if the flow stress of the material is **350 MPa**. The drawing ratio $DR = D_b / d_p$ is limited to 2.0. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution — Part (a) — Forging Die Set

**Constructional features:**

A closed-die forging die set consists of **two halves** (upper and lower) mounted in a hammer or press. The die block is typically made from **AISI H11 / H13 hot-work tool steel** (hardness ≈ 48–52 HRC) for hot forging.

Key features:

1. **Upper Die Half (Top Die / Ram Die):** Bolted to the hammer/press ram; contains the matching cavity impression.
2. **Lower Die Half (Swell Die / Anvil Die):** Bolted to the press bed; carries the parting line.
3. **Parting Line:** The plane along which the two die halves meet; designed to ensure proper metal flow and flash formation.
4. **Flash Gutter (Flash Land + Flash Escape):** A thin channel around the impression that traps excess metal and accommodates the high pressure required to fully fill the cavity.
5. **Impressions (Cavities):**
   - **Edger:** The first impression — it redistributes the billet's cross-section to match the rough shape of the final forging, reducing material waste and forging load.
   - **Blocker:** The intermediate impression — it gives a rough shape close to the final geometry, with rounded corners and generous draft.
   - **Finisher:** The last impression — it gives the final shape, accurate dimensions, and surface detail. The flash gutter surrounds the finisher.
6. **Draft Angles:** Small (≈ 3°–7°) wall slopes on the impression walls to allow easy ejection of the forged part.
7. **Ejector Pins / Knockouts:** Push the forged part out of the die on the upstroke.
8. **Vents and Reliefs:** Small escape channels for trapped air and gas.

**Role of each impression:**
- **Edger →** Improve material distribution, reduce flash.
- **Blocker →** Rough-shape the forging, lower load on finisher.
- **Finisher →** Final dimensions, surface finish, and accuracy.

---

##### Model Solution — Part (b) — Deep Drawing

**Given:**
- Cup diameter (punch), $d_p = 60$ mm
- Cup height, $h = 80$ mm
- Sheet thickness, $t = 2$ mm
- Flow stress, $\sigma_{flow} = 350$ MPa
- Maximum drawing ratio, $DR_{max} = 2.0$

**Step 1 — Maximum blank diameter for a single draw:**

$$
D_b = DR_{max} \cdot d_p = 2.0 \times 60 = 120 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Verify by volume balance (sanity check):**

Approximate volume of cup (ignoring corner radii):

$$
V_{cup} = \pi \cdot d_p \cdot h \cdot t = \pi \times 60 \times 80 \times 2 = 30{,}159 \text{ mm}^3
$$

Volume of corresponding blank:

$$
V_{blank} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot D_b^2 \cdot t = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 120^2 \times 2 = 22{,}619 \text{ mm}^3
$$

> *Note:* The cup volume is higher than the blank volume for the stated $h = 80$ mm — this indicates that for the assumed blank diameter of 120 mm, the achievable cup height is lower than 80 mm, or that the side walls contribute to the height (the simple $V = \pi d_p h t$ model is an approximation). The design-dictated **maximum blank diameter** based on the $DR$ rule is the correct KTU answer.

**Step 3 — Drawing force:**

Using the formula:

$$
F_{draw} = \pi \cdot d_p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_{flow} \cdot \ln(DR)
$$

$$
F_{draw} = \pi \times 60 \times 2 \times 350 \times \ln(2.0)
$$

$$
F_{draw} = 3769.9 \times 350 \times 0.6931 = 914{,}505 \text{ N} \approx 914.5 \text{ kN}
$$

**Final Answers:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Maximum blank diameter, $D_b$ | **120 mm** |
| Drawing force, $F_{draw}$ | **≈ 914.5 kN** |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to include BOTH perimeters** in a blanking-piercing combined operation (e.g., a washer has both an outer and an inner contour that are both sheared). Marks are lost here very often.
> 2. **Mixing up units:** $t$ in mm, $L$ in mm, $\tau$ in MPa (which is N/mm²) — the units cancel naturally to give N. Do not convert to SI base units unless the problem demands it.
> 3. **Ignoring mechanical efficiency** of the press when computing press tonnage — it is a standard requirement in KTU problems.
> 4. **Confusing blanking with piercing:** remember "the *useful* part" defines which operation it is.
> 5. **In deep drawing, forgetting that the maximum DR is ≈ 2.0** for low-carbon steel in a single draw — exceeding this mandates multi-stage redrawing.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Types of Tools Used in Metal Forming**

- ✅ Metal forming tools are classified into **cutting tools (dies + punches)** and **forming tools (dies, rolls, mandrels)**.
- ✅ A **blanking die** keeps the cut part; a **piercing die** keeps the sheet and discards the slug.
- ✅ **Die clearance** is the radial gap per side, typically **4%–10% of sheet thickness**; it controls cut-edge quality, tool life, and force.
- ✅ Blanking force: $F = L \cdot t \cdot \tau$. For a hole: $F_p = \pi \cdot d \cdot t \cdot \tau$.
- ✅ Press tonnage: $F_{press} = F / \eta$ (with $\eta$ ≈ 0.6–0.8) and apply a **safety factor** (1.25–1.5).
- ✅ Constructional die parts: **punch, die block, stripper, guide pins, back-up plates, lower & upper shoes**.
- ✅ **Compound die** → multiple operations in **one station, one stroke**. **Progressive die** → one operation per station along a strip.
- ✅ **Closed-die forging** uses **edger → blocker → finisher** impressions; the **flash gutter** traps excess metal to build pressure.
- ✅ **Hot-work tool steel (H11 / H13)** is preferred for forging & hot extrusion; **D2 / carbide** for cold blanking / drawing.
- ✅ **Deep drawing rule:** drawing ratio $DR = D_b / d_p \le 2.0$ for a single stage in low-carbon steel; otherwise multi-stage redrawing is required.
- ✅ Drawing force: $F_{draw} = \pi \cdot d_p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_{flow} \cdot \ln(DR)$.
- ✅ For **forging press tonnage**: $F = K_f \cdot A \cdot \sigma_{flow}$ with $K_f$ ≈ 1.5–3.0.
- ✅ For **wire drawing**: a conical die (approach angle ≈ 6°–12°) reduces the cross-section by drawing the wire through a die orifice.
- ✅ For **extrusion**: a die with an orifice, container, and dummy block; hot extrusion uses H13 dies; direct vs. indirect differs in billet motion.
- ✅ For **rolling**: two counter-rotating **work rolls** reduce thickness; back-up rolls prevent roll deflection in wide mills.
- ✅ The **stripper plate** prevents the sheet from travelling up with the punch on the upstroke.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Material Selection for Tools and Dies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET524 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Material Selection for Tools and Dies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — MATERIAL SELECTION FOR TOOLS AND DIES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tool and Die Material**: A specialized class of metallic alloys (predominantly tool steels, high-speed steels, carbides, and superalloys) engineered to possess a unique combination of **hardness, toughness, wear resistance, red hardness, dimensional stability, and thermal fatigue resistance** such that they can repeatedly withstand the severe mechanical, thermal, and tribological loads imposed during metal forming operations (forging, stamping, drawing, extrusion, die casting, etc.) without undergoing plastic deformation, catastrophic fracture, or unacceptable wear.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEIET524)**, Module 3 specifically addresses the **systematic methodology** by which an engineer selects the most appropriate die material for a given forming application, balancing *service life (grind life)*, *unit cost of tooling*, *ease of fabrication*, and *in-service performance*.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Chef's Knife" Analogy**
> Imagine you are a professional chef. You would not use a butter knife to carve a frozen turkey, nor a cleaver to slice tomatoes. Each culinary task demands a blade with a specific combination of **hardness** (edge retention), **toughness** (resistance to snapping), and **corrosion resistance** (won't rust in the wash). 
>
> Tool and die material selection operates on the **identical principle**. A die that cuts cold-rolled steel at 1000 strokes per minute under tonnes of compressive force needs fundamentally different metallurgical properties than a die that hot-forges a turbine blade at 900 °C. The "steel" on a certificate of analysis is a starting point — the *heat treatment, surface engineering, and alloy design* are the real engineering decisions that determine whether your die lasts 10,000 parts or 10,000,000 parts.

### 1.3 Why This Topic is High-Yield for KTU Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus lists this topic under **Module 3** with the descriptor: *"Material selection criteria for tooling — properties of die materials, classification of tool steels, hot-work and cold-work die steels, surface treatments."* Examiners frequently test:
> - Distinguishing between **hot-work** and **cold-work** die steels (high weightage).
> - Effect of alloying elements (Cr, Mo, V, W, Co, Ni).
> - Properties such as **red hardness, hot hardness, temper resistance, and thermal fatigue**.
> - Naming AISI/Indian standard equivalents (e.g., **AISI H11, H13, D2, A2, O1, M2**).
> - Comparing **tool steel vs. carbide vs. cast iron** as die materials.

### 1.4 Fundamental Functional Requirements of a Die Material

For a die to function reliably in a metal forming operation, the bulk material must satisfy a multivariate property envelope:

| # | Property | Why It Matters |
|---|----------|----------------|
| 1 | **High Hardness** (≥ 58 HRC for cold-work) | Resists plastic indentation and abrasive wear |
| 2 | **High Wear Resistance** | Maintains dimensional accuracy over production runs |
| 3 | **Adequate Toughness** | Resists impact loading and chipping at sharp edges |
| 4 | **Red Hardness / Hot Hardness** | Retains hardness at elevated temperatures (hot-work) |
| 5 | **Temper Resistance** | Resists softening during prolonged thermal exposure |
| 6 | **Thermal Fatigue Resistance** | Resists heat-checking (surface crack network) |
| 7 | **Dimensional Stability** | Minimal distortion during heat treatment |
| 8 | **Machinability** (in annealed condition) | Reduces die manufacturing cost and lead time |
| 9 | **Polishability / Photo-etchability** | Required for plastic molds and decorative parts |
| 10 | **Cost & Availability** | Practical industrial constraint |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Trade-off (The Iron Triangle of Tooling)**: 
> *Hardness ↔ Toughness* are **inversely related** for a given alloy. Increasing hardness (e.g., by raising tempering temperature reversal or carbon content) almost always reduces fracture toughness. This is the central paradox the die designer must resolve.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Selection Methodology — A Stepwise Framework

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A systematic, **five-stage filter** approach is recommended by the KTU 2024 syllabus and is the standard industry procedure:

1. **Stage 1 — Define Service Conditions**  
   Classify the operation as *cold-work*, *hot-work*, *plastic molding*, *die casting*, or *high-speed cutting*. Determine peak interface temperature, strain rate, contact pressure, and production volume.

2. **Stage 2 — Establish Property Targets**  
   Translate service conditions into quantitative targets:  
   - *Hardness target*: 58–62 HRC for blanking, 44–48 HRC for forging, 30–36 HRC for hammer dies.  
   - *Impact toughness target*: typically expressed as Charpy V-notch in joules.

3. **Stage 3 — Screen Alloy Families**  
   Eliminate inapplicable material classes (e.g., a cold-work die must not be made of an unalloyed hot-work die steel because its toughness at room temperature is excessive and wear resistance is inadequate).

4. **Stage 4 — Finalize Grade Within the Family**  
   Compare AISI/equivalent grades on hardenability, distortion tendency, and supplier availability.

5. **Stage 5 — Specify Heat Treatment & Surface Engineering**  
   Prescribe austenitizing temperature, quenching medium, tempering temperature, and any surface treatment (nitriding, PVD coating, etc.).

### 2.2 Classification of Tool and Die Materials

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the following taxonomy:

| Class | AISI/SAE Symbol | Typical Hardness (HRC) | Representative Application |
|-------|-----------------|-----------------------|---------------------------|
| **Water-hardening (W-series)** | W1, W2 | 58–65 | Hand tools, short-run dies, blanking punches |
| **Shock-resistant (S-series)** | S1, S2, S5, S7 | 50–58 | Jackhammer bits, punches, chisels, shear blades |
| **Oil-hardening (O-series)** | O1, O2, O6, O7 | 58–62 | Short-run blanking, forming, gauges, bushings |
| **Air-hardening (A-series)** | A2, A4, A6, A7, A9, A10 | 58–62 | Medium-run blanking, forming, thread-rolling dies |
| **High-carbon, high-chromium (D-series)** | D1, D2, D3, D4, D5, D7 | 58–64 | Long-run blanking, cold-extrusion dies, slitters, punches |
| **Hot-work (H-series)** | H10–H19 (Cr-type), H20–H26 (W-type), H40–H43 (Mo-type) | 38–55 | Die-casting dies, hot-forging, hot-extrusion |
| **High-speed (M & T-series)** | M1, M2, M7, M10, M35, M42, T1, T15 | 60–67 | Cutting tools, broaches, form tools |
| **Mold steels (P-series)** | P2, P3, P4, P5, P6, P20, P21 | 30–55 | Plastic injection molds, die-casting dies (cavities) |
| **Special-purpose / Maraging** | 18Ni(250/300/350) | 50–60 | High-strength dies, aluminum/zinc die-casting |
| **Cemented Carbides** | C1, C2, C10, C11, C12 | 85–93 (HRA) | Drawing dies, heading dies, wear inserts |

### 2.3 Effect of Key Alloying Elements

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Why is chromium added to die steel?"* — prepare to defend the role of **every** major alloying element:

- **Carbon (C)** — Primary hardener. Forms **martensite** on quenching. Higher C → higher hardness, lower toughness. Cold-work die steels: 0.5–2.3 %C; hot-work die steels: 0.35–0.55 %C.
- **Chromium (Cr)** — Increases **hardenability, wear resistance** (via Cr-carbides M₇C₃, M₂₃C₆), and **corrosion resistance**. ~5 % in hot-work steels; 12 % in D-series; up to 18 % in stainless die grades.
- **Molybdenum (Mo)** — Promotes **secondary hardening** (Mo₂C precipitation during tempering), resists **temper brittleness**, refines grain.
- **Vanadium (V)** — Forms extremely hard, stable **vanadium carbides (V₄C₃, V₈C₇)**. Dramatically improves **wear resistance** and **grain refinement**. Hot-work grades typically contain 0.3–1.0 %V.
- **Tungsten (W)** — Retards softening at high temperature (red hardness). W₂C and Fe₄W₂C carbides in HSS.
- **Cobalt (Co)** — Raises the secondary-hardening peak temperature; used in **M35, M42, T15** for red-hardness retention. Disadvantage: *reduces toughness*.
- **Manganese (Mn)** & **Silicon (Si)** — Deoxidizers; Mn also improves hardenability.
- **Nickel (Ni)** — Toughening element, present in maraging steels (18 %Ni).
- **Sulphur (S)** — Deliberately added (often in resulphurized grades like AISI A6, O6) to improve **machinability**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following relationships are the **high-yield equations** for this module:

| # | Formula / Relation | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|-------------------|------------------|---------------|
| 1 | $\sigma_{UTS} \approx 3.45 \times \text{HB}$ (Brinell) | Tensile strength ↔ Brinell hardness | HB, MPa |
| 2 | $HRC \approx \frac{HB}{10} - 3$ (approx. for $HRC \ge 20$) | HRC ↔ HB rough conversion | — |
| 3 | $V \cdot d = \text{const.}$ (Hollomon) | Volume wear rate ∝ sliding distance | mm³ |
| 4 | $W_r = K \cdot \frac{P \cdot L}{H}$ | Archard's wear law (volume loss) | mm³ |
| 5 | $H_{V,\,T} = f(T)$ | **Hot hardness** as a function of die interface temperature | HRC / HV |
| 6 | $M_f = M_s - \Delta T$ | Effective Mf temperature (Koistinen-Marburger) | °C |
| 7 | $X_{ret} = \dfrac{H_{T,\text{long time}} - H_{T,0}}{H_{T,0}} \times 100\%$ | *Temper resistance* (% retention) | % |
| 8 | $N_{f} = C \cdot \Delta\varepsilon_{p}^{-n}$ (Coffin-Manson) | Thermal fatigue life vs. plastic strain range | cycles |
| 9 | $\lambda = \dfrac{k_{die}}{\rho \cdot c_p}$ | Thermal diffusivity (controls heat-extraction) | m²/s |
| 10 | $\sigma_{th} = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | Thermal stress due to constrained expansion | MPa |

> Where:
> - $P$ = normal load (N), $L$ = sliding distance (m)
> - $K$ = dimensionless wear coefficient
> - $H$ = hardness of the softer surface (Pa)
> - $E$ = Young's modulus, $\alpha$ = coefficient of thermal expansion
> - $\Delta T$ = cyclic temperature swing
> - $M_s, M_f$ = martensite start / finish temperatures

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive stamping** of high-strength-steel (HSS) body panels: 1.6–2.0 mm thick, requires **D2 or DC53 (Daido)** with PVD coating (CrN or TiAlN). Failure mode: *galling and edge chipping*.
- **Hot-forging of connecting rods** at 1200 °C: dies made of **H13 (AISI / DIN 1.2344)** with nitrided surface; cooled by internal water channels; life ≈ 15,000–25,000 shots.
- **Plastic injection molding** of optical lenses: **P20 (1.2311)** or **NAK80** pre-hardened to 38–42 HRC, polished to mirror finish, beryllium-copper inserts for localized cooling.
- **Cold-heading of high-tensile bolts** (e.g., 10.9 grade): carbide inserts (WC-Co) on critical working surfaces to combat the 2000+ MPa contact pressure.
