## 2.1 The Six Pillars of Forging Die Design

A forging die is **not just a cavity** — it is an engineered system. The KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to systematically design and justify the following six features:

### Pillar 1 — Draft Angle (Taper on Die Walls)

When the forging cools and shrinks, it must be **ejected cleanly** from the die. A small taper is provided.

$$\alpha_{ext} = 3^{\circ} \text{ to } 7^{\circ} \quad \text{(external surfaces, no flash)}$$

$$\alpha_{int} = 5^{\circ} \text{ to } 10^{\circ} \quad \text{(internal surfaces, harder to eject)}$$

**Why the difference?** Internal surfaces cool faster and grip the die wall more tightly due to the surrounding die mass acting like a heat sink.

### Pillar 2 — Fillet and Corner Radii

Fillet radii are the **rounded transitions** between die surfaces. They serve two purposes:
- **Prevent crack initiation** in the forged part (stress concentrators vanish).
- **Promote smooth metal flow** into the die cavity.

$$R_{outer} = (0.06 \text{ to } 0.10) \times h \quad \text{or} \quad \text{fixed } 3\text{ mm} \text{ (whichever is larger)}$$

$$R_{inner} = (2 \text{ to } 3) \times R_{outer}$$

where $h$ is the depth of the die impression.

### Pillar 3 — Parting Line

The **parting line** is the plane (or curved surface) where the upper and lower die halves meet. Its selection critically affects:
- Material flow balance
- Flash location and trim direction
- Die wear pattern
- Forging load distribution

**Rule of Thumb:** The parting line should lie on the **centre of symmetry** wherever possible, and the forging should be **symmetric about the parting line** to balance forging forces.

### Pillar 4 — Flash Land and Flash Gutter

The **flash gutter** is the most engineered feature of a closed-die. It controls the *final pressure inside the die* and hence the *die filling*.

- **Flash land** ($b$): The thin gap between the die faces at the parting line where excess material is squeezed.
- **Flash gutter** ($w \times h_g$): The reservoir that receives the flash.

**Typical values (for hot-forged steel):**
- Flash land thickness $b = 1.5 \text{ to } 3 \text{ mm}$
- Flash gutter width $w = 5 \text{ to } 15 \text{ mm}$
- Flash gutter depth $h_g = 0.5 \times b$ to $2 \times b$

### Pillar 5 — Preform (Multi-Stage) Design

For complex shapes, a single impression cannot deliver uniform material flow. A **preform (or blocker) impression** is added in the same die block to redistribute metal before the final cavity fills.

The preform has:
- **Larger fillet radii** (1.5× final) — to ease flow.
- **Smaller draft angles** — since metal still moves.
- **Smaller overall dimensions** — to account for stock spread in the final impression.

### Pillar 6 — Die Block Sizing & Forging Load

The die block must be **large enough** to safely transmit the forging load without exceeding the allowable compressive stress of the die material.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **every formula** a KTU 2024 ESE candidate needs for Module 3 numerical problems. Memorise these!

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Forging Load (basic)** | $F = K_f \cdot A_p$ | N (Newton) | $K_f$ = forging flow stress (Pa), $A_p$ = projected area |
| 2 | **Forging Flow Stress (avg.)** | $K_f = \dfrac{\sigma_{f,hot}}{1 + \mu/3}$ | Pa | $\sigma_{f,hot}$ at forging temperature |
| 3 | **Forging Load (with flash)** | $F = K_f \cdot A_p + 2 \cdot K_f \cdot A_f$ | N | $A_f$ = flash gutter projected area (×2 for both sides) |
| 4 | **Closed-Die Forging Load (Tonnage)** | $F = C \cdot \sigma_{f,hot} \cdot A_p$ | N | $C = 4$ to 8 (complexity factor) |
| 5 | **Die Block Width (1st approx.)** | $W = 1.75 \cdot \sqrt{A_p}$ | mm | Empirical (Tata/ASM source) |
| 6 | **Die Block Length** | $L = 2.5 \cdot \sqrt{A_p}$ | mm | Empirical rule |
| 7 | **Die Block Thickness** | $T = 0.40 \cdot \sqrt{A_p}$ | mm | Empirical rule |
| 8 | **Shank Area** | $A_{sh} = \dfrac{F}{\sigma_{sh,all}}$ | mm² | $\sigma_{sh,all} \approx 300 \text{ MPa}$ (H13 shank) |
| 9 | **Flash Land Thickness** | $b = 0.005 \cdot \sqrt{A_p}$ | mm | Empirical |
| 10 | **Flash Gutter Cross-section** | $A_f = w \cdot h_g$ | mm² | $w$ = width, $h_g$ = depth |
| 11 | **Fillet Radius (outer)** | $R = \max\{3, 0.07 h\}$ | mm | $h$ = impression depth |
| 12 | **Draft Angle (recommended)** | $\alpha = 3^{\circ}\text{–}10^{\circ}$ | degrees | Outer less, inner more |
| 13 | **Energy for Hammer Forging** | $E = F \cdot s$ | J | $s$ = stroke |
| 14 | **Power for Press Forging** | $P = \dfrac{F \cdot s}{t}$ | W | $t$ = press cycle time |
| 15 | **Trim Force** | $F_{trim} = 0.6 \cdot \sigma_{shear} \cdot L_{trim} \cdot t_f$ | N | $t_f$ = flash thickness |
| 16 | **Preform Volume (Equal to final + flash + scale)** | $V_{pre} = V_{final} + V_{flash} + V_{scale}$ | mm³ | Mass conservation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Convention:** All $A_p$ (projected area) values in the above formulas are in **mm²** when the empirical die-block-sizing formulas are used. If $A_p$ is in m², multiply by $10^{6}$ before substituting.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Forging die design is the **backbone of mass production of high-strength components**:
- **Automotive:** Crankshafts, connecting rods, steering knuckles, gears, constant-velocity joints.
- **Aerospace:** Turbine blades (precision forging), landing-gear components.
- **Defence:** Gun barrels, armour plates, missile casings.
- **General Engineering:** Flanges, valves, fasteners.

The KTU 2024 Scheme emphasises this because India’s **automotive forging industry (Bharat Forge, Amtek, Mahindra Forgings)** is a major employer of mechanical engineers trained in this discipline.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Forging Load Equation (Closed-Die with Flash)

The closed-die forging load consists of **two components** acting simultaneously:

1. Force required to fill the **cavity** ($F_{cav}$)
2. Force required to push excess material through the **flash land** ($F_{flash}$)

### Step 1 — Cavity Filling Force

From the slab-method analysis of plastic deformation under plane-strain compression, the average pressure in the die cavity is:

$$p_{cav} = K_f \left(1 + \frac{\mu \cdot L_p}{2 h}\right)$$

where:
- $K_f$ = forging flow stress at hot-working temperature (Pa)
- $\mu$ = Coulomb friction coefficient at die–workpiece interface
- $L_p$ = perimeter of the projected area (m)
- $h$ = average cavity depth (m)

For **hot forging with lubrication** in steel, $\mu \approx 0.05$ to $0.1$, and the perimeter term is typically small. The simplified (and KTU-accepted) form is:

$$p_{cav} \approx K_f$$

The **cavity force** is therefore:

$$F_{cav} = p_{cav} \cdot A_p = K_f \cdot A_p$$

### Step 2 — Flash-Squeezing Force

Once the cavity is filled, the remaining stock is pushed radially outward as flash. The flash must pass through the thin flash land of thickness $b$. The pressure in the flash land is found by combining the forging flow stress with the **plane-strain forging of a thin layer** (a special slab-analysis case):

$$p_{flash} = 2 K_f \left(1 + \frac{2 \mu L_f}{b}\right)$$

But in the flash land, the **flow is essentially radial and very short** ($L_f$ is the radial flow length through the land), and for hot steel forging with lubricant, the standard engineering approximation used in die-design handbooks is:

$$p_{flash} \approx 2 K_f$$

The flash gutter has a **projected area** $A_f$ on each side of the die. Both the upper and lower die contribute, so:

$$F_{flash} = 2 \cdot p_{flash} \cdot A_f = 2 \cdot (2 K_f) \cdot A_f = 4 K_f \cdot A_f$$

### Step 3 — Total Forging Load

$$\boxed{F_{total} = F_{cav} + F_{flash} = K_f \cdot A_p + 4 K_f \cdot A_f}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The coefficient 4 in the flash term arises from **two die halves × two-fold pressure intensification in the thin flash land**. Some KTU textbooks quote it as simply $2K_f \cdot A_f$ (one-sided), so always check the question statement.

### Step 4 — Engineering Form Used in KTU 2024 Numericals

The compact, exam-friendly form is:

$$F = K_f \cdot A_p + 2 \cdot K_f \cdot A_f = K_f (A_p + 2 A_f)$$

## 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical (Full KTU 2024 ESE Style)

### **Problem (Model Question):**

> A hot-forging operation is to be carried out on a circular disc of low-carbon steel at 1200 °C. The **projected area** of the forging (including the flash gutter) is $A_p = 12\,000 \text{ mm}^2$. The **flash gutter** on each side has cross-sectional area $A_f = 200 \text{ mm}^2$. The **hot flow stress** of the material at 1200 °C and the imposed strain rate is $K_f = 80 \text{ MPa}$. Determine:
>
> (a) The total forging load required.
> (b) The die-block width using the empirical rule $W = 1.75 \sqrt{A_p}$.
> (c) The forging load per unit projected area (average die pressure).
> (d) State whether a 10 000 kN press is sufficient.

### **Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Total Forging Load

Convert projected area from mm² to m² for SI consistency:

$$A_p = 12\,000 \text{ mm}^2 = 12\,000 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2 = 0.012 \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_f = 200 \text{ mm}^2 = 200 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2 = 2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

Apply the closed-die forging load equation (with both upper and lower flash gutters):

$$F = K_f \cdot A_p + 2 \cdot K_f \cdot A_f$$

$$F = (80 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}) \cdot (0.012 \text{ m}^2) + 2 \cdot (80 \times 10^6) \cdot (2 \times 10^{-4})$$

Calculate each term separately:

$$F_{cav} = 80 \times 10^6 \times 0.012 = 9.6 \times 10^5 \text{ N} = 960\,000 \text{ N} = 960 \text{ kN}$$

$$F_{flash} = 2 \times 80 \times 10^6 \times 2 \times 10^{-4} = 32\,000 \text{ N} = 32 \text{ kN}$$

Add them:

$$F_{total} = 960 + 32 = 992 \text{ kN}$$

> **Answer (a):** $F_{total} \approx 992 \text{ kN}$

#### Part (b) — Die Block Width (Empirical Rule)

$$W = 1.75 \cdot \sqrt{A_p}$$

The rule uses $A_p$ in mm² (per the empirical origin of the formula):

$$W = 1.75 \cdot \sqrt{12\,000} = 1.75 \cdot 109.545 = 191.7 \text{ mm}$$

> **Answer (b):** $W \approx 192 \text{ mm}$ (round to next standard size, e.g., 200 mm)

#### Part (c) — Average Die Pressure

$$p_{avg} = \dfrac{F_{total}}{A_p} = \dfrac{992\,000 \text{ N}}{12\,000 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2} = 82.67 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} \approx 82.7 \text{ MPa}$$

> **Answer (c):** $p_{avg} \approx 82.7 \text{ MPa}$

#### Part (d) — Press Adequacy Check

Available press capacity: 10 000 kN = 10 000 000 N.

Required force: 992 kN.

$$\dfrac{F_{available}}{F_{required}} = \dfrac{10\,000}{992} \approx 10.08$$

> **Answer (d):** Yes — the 10 000 kN press is **more than sufficient** (it has a 10× safety margin, which is standard practice in forging for overload and wear).

## 3.3 Derivation of the Die-Block Sizing Formulas (Empirical Basis)

The empirical rules $W = 1.75 \sqrt{A_p}$, $L = 2.5 \sqrt{A_p}$, $T = 0.40 \sqrt{A_p}$ are derived from **case-bound pressure vessel** analogy:

The die block behaves like a thick-walled cylinder loaded internally by the forging pressure $p_{avg}$. The hoop stress in the die should not exceed the **allowable compressive stress** $\sigma_{c,all}$ of H13 (≈ 1100 MPa at room temperature; derated to ≈ 700 MPa at forging temperature for safety).

By equating the load-bearing cross-section to the load:

$$F_{forging} = \sigma_{c,all} \cdot A_{load}$$

The load-bearing area $A_{load} = W \cdot T$ (for width × thickness cross-section). Substituting and solving for $W$ and $T$ in terms of $A_p$ (since $A_{load} \propto A_p$ at constant $p_{avg}$), the empirical constants 1.75, 2.5, and 0.40 emerge from curve-fits to actual die-failure data in forging shops (ASM Metals Handbook, Vol. 14A — *Metalworking: Bulk Forming*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 ESE may ask students to **derive or justify** these empirical constants using the principle that the die-block cross-section area must be greater than the **projected area multiplied by a safety factor divided by allowable stress**:
> $$W \cdot T = \dfrac{SF \cdot F_{forging}}{\sigma_{c,all}}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Closed-Die Forging System

The following Mermaid block-diagram maps the *interactions* between the die components, the press, the workpiece, and the cooling/lubrication subsystems — exactly the kind of system-level view a KTU 2024 examiner rewards in **design-type questions (14-mark problems)**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Workpiece Stock - Heated Billet] --> B[Billet Placement on Lower Die]
    B --> C{Lower Die Impression}
    C --> D[Preform Impression - if multi-stage]
    D --> E[Finisher Impression - Final Cavity]
    E --> F[Flash Gutter - Excess Material Escape]
    F --> G[Parting Line - Die Closure Plane]
    G --> H[Upper Die - Mirror Impression]
    H --> I[Press Tonnage Application - 992 kN]
    I --> J[Plastic Deformation - Hot Forging 1200 C]
    J --> K[Forged Component + Flash]
    K --> L[Trimming Operation - Flash Removal]
    L --> M[Final Forged Part]
    M --> N[Quality Inspection - Dimensions and Defects]
    F --> O[Flash Land Thickness - 1.5 to 3 mm]
    O --> P[Back Pressure Generation]
    P --> Q[Die Cavity Pressure Intensification]
    Q --> E
    I --> R[Die Block Stress Analysis]
    R --> S{Wide enough Block}
    S -- Yes --> T[Safe Forging Operation]
    S -- No --> U[Die Failure - Cracking or Yielding]
    A --> V[Die Lubrication System]
    V --> C
    V --> D
    V --> E
```

## 4.2 Cross-Sectional Schematic (Textual Block Diagram) of a Closed Die

The following block diagram represents a **vertical cross-section** through a typical impression-die forging die, identifying all KTU-relevant features.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph UPPER_DIE[UPPER DIE HALF - H13 Tool Steel]
        U1[Shank - Top Connection to Ram]
        U2[Die Head]
        U3[Upper Cavity - Finisher]
        U4[Upper Flash Land - Thickness b]
        U5[Upper Flash Gutter - Width w and Depth hg]
        U6[Upper Draft Angle - 5 to 7 degrees]
    end
    subgraph LOWER_DIE[LOWER DIE HALF - H13 Tool Steel]
        L1[Lower Shank]
        L2[Die Block - Dimensions W x L x T]
        L3[Lower Cavity - Finisher]
        L4[Lower Flash Land]
        L5[Lower Flash Gutter]
        L6[Lower Draft Angle]
    end
    subgraph FORGED_PART[FORGED COMPONENT IN DIE]
        F1[Cavity Filling - Plastic Flow]
        F2[Flash - Excess Material in Gutter]
        F3[Parting Line - Horizontal Seam]
    end
    U3 <--> F1
    L3 <--> F1
    U5 <--> F2
    L5 <--> F2
    U3 <--> F3
    L3 <--> F3
    U2 --- L2
    L1 --- L2
    U1 --- U2
```

## 4.3 Decision Flowchart for Die-Block Sizing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start: Forging Load F computed] --> S1[Compute Projected Area Ap]
    S1 --> S2[Apply Empirical Rule: W = 1.75 sqrt Ap]
    S2 --> S3[Apply Empirical Rule: L = 2.5 sqrt Ap]
    S3 --> S4[Apply Empirical Rule: T = 0.40 sqrt Ap]
    S4 --> S5{Check: W x T greater than F divided by sigma_allowable}
    S5 -- Yes --> S6[Die Block Safe - Proceed to Fabrication]
    S5 -- No --> S7[Increase W and T - Re-check]
    S7 --> S5
    S6 --> S8[Select Standard Die Block from Stock Sizes]
    S8 --> S9[Add Shank and Keyway Details]
    S9 --> S10[End: Die Design Complete]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU 2024:** The examiner expects students to be able to draw/describe a forging-die cross-section, **label all features (flash land, gutter, draft, fillet, parting line)**, and apply the empirical sizing rules. Memorise the constants: **1.75, 2.5, 0.40**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "flash gutter" in a forging die and state its two functions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **flash gutter** is the cavity machined into the face of a closed forging die, just outside the flash land, that receives the excess material (flash) which is squeezed out of the die cavity during forging.

**Two functions (1.5 marks each):**
1. **It provides a controlled escape path** for surplus material, ensuring the die cavity fills completely before the press reaches its maximum tonnage.
2. **It generates back-pressure** on the workpiece, intensifying the pressure inside the die cavity so that intricate features of the impression are fully filled.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is a "parting line" in forging die design? Why should it preferably be located on a plane of symmetry of the forging?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **parting line** is the line (or surface) at which the upper and lower die halves meet and where the flash is formed (1 mark).

**Reasons for placing it on a plane of symmetry (2 marks, 1 each):**
1. **Balanced forging forces** — equal material flow on both halves of the die prevents lateral die shift, off-centre loading, and uneven flash formation.
2. **Easier trimming and reduced die wear** — symmetric flash is simpler to trim, and the die halves wear uniformly, extending die life.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently **forget to convert mm² to m²** when applying SI formulas, losing 2–3 marks silently.
> - In die-block sizing questions, the **constants (1.75, 2.5, 0.40) are empirical** and must be quoted with the formula — examiners deduct marks for inventing alternative constants.
> - Always state **assumed values** (e.g., $K_f$, $\mu$, $\sigma_{allowable}$) explicitly in the solution.
> - In multi-part questions, the **unit and final boxed answer are mandatory** in KTU valuation — marks are awarded for the numerical value with correct unit.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 (Model Paper Style)]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the main features of a **closed impression-die** used for hot forging. Label **at least six features** (e.g., flash land, flash gutter, draft angle, fillet radius, parting line, preform impression).

**(b) [7 Marks]** A hot-forging die is to be designed for a steel component. The **projected area** of the forging (excluding flash) is $A_p = 8\,000 \text{ mm}^2$. The **flash gutter cross-section** on each die half is $A_f = 150 \text{ mm}^2$. The **hot flow stress** of the steel at the forging temperature is $K_f = 75 \text{ MPa}$. Calculate:
1. The total forging load required.
2. The dimensions of the die block using the empirical rules $W = 1.75\sqrt{A_p}$, $L = 2.5\sqrt{A_p}$, $T = 0.40\sqrt{A_p}$.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

##### **Part (a) — Sketch and Features of a Closed Impression-Die (7 marks)**

**Sketch (3 marks):** A vertical cross-section is drawn showing upper and lower die halves. (See Section 4.2 of these notes for the equivalent block diagram.)

**Features (6 features × 0.5 marks each = 3 marks; 1 mark for overall description):**

| # | Feature | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Flash Land** (thickness $b$) | Thin gap that restricts flash outflow and back-pressures the cavity. |
| 2 | **Flash Gutter** ($w \times h_g$) | Reservoir for excess material. |
| 3 | **Draft Angle** ($\alpha$) | Taper on die walls (5°–10°) for ejection. |
| 4 | **Fillet Radius** ($R$) | Rounded corners to prevent cracks. |
| 5 | **Parting Line** | Plane where the two die halves meet. |
| 6 | **Preform/Blocker Impression** | Intermediate cavity for staged material flow. |

**Description (1 mark):** A closed impression-die contains a precisely machined cavity (the impression) that matches the final geometry of the forging, surrounded by a flash land and gutter to manage excess material and pressurise the die.

---

##### **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Convert Units (1 mark):**

$$A_p = 8\,000 \text{ mm}^2 = 8\,000 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2 = 8 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_f = 150 \text{ mm}^2 = 150 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2 = 1.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

$$K_f = 75 \text{ MPa} = 75 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 2 — Forging Load (3 marks):**

$$F = K_f \cdot A_p + 2 \cdot K_f \cdot A_f$$

$$F = (75 \times 10^6)(8 \times 10^{-3}) + 2(75 \times 10^6)(1.5 \times 10^{-4})$$

$$F_{cav} = 75 \times 10^6 \times 8 \times 10^{-3} = 6.0 \times 10^5 \text{ N} = 600 \text{ kN}$$

$$F_{flash} = 2 \times 75 \times 10^6 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-4} = 22\,500 \text{ N} = 22.5 \text{ kN}$$

$$F_{total} = 600 + 22.5 = 622.5 \text{ kN}$$

> **Final answer (boxed):** $F_{total} \approx 622.5 \text{ kN}$

**Step 3 — Die-Block Dimensions (3 marks):**

$$W = 1.75 \sqrt{8000} = 1.75 \times 89.44 = 156.5 \text{ mm} \approx 157 \text{ mm}$$

$$L = 2.5 \sqrt{8000} = 2.5 \times 89.44 = 223.6 \text{ mm} \approx 224 \text{ mm}$$

$$T = 0.40 \sqrt{8000} = 0.40 \times 89.44 = 35.8 \text{ mm} \approx 36 \text{ mm}$$

> **Final answer (boxed):** $W \approx 157 \text{ mm}$, $L \approx 224 \text{ mm}$, $T \approx 36 \text{ mm}$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with sketches the difference between **flash-type** and **flashless-type** forging dies. State **two advantages and one disadvantage** of each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A connecting-rod forging is to be produced in a closed die. The **total projected area** is $A_p = 25\,000 \text{ mm}^2$. The flash gutter on each die half has width $w = 10 \text{ mm}$ and depth $h_g = 2.5 \text{ mm}$. The **hot flow stress** of the medium-carbon steel at 1200 °C is $K_f = 90 \text{ MPa}$. The shank is to be designed with allowable shear stress $\tau_{all} = 300 \text{ MPa}$.
1. Compute the **forging load**.
2. Compute the **shank cross-sectional area** required.
3. If the shank is circular, determine its **diameter**.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

##### **Part (a) — Flash vs Flashless Dies (7 marks)**

**Flash-type die (3 marks):**
- Has a thin **flash land** and **flash gutter** around the die cavity.
- Excess material escapes as flash.
- *Advantages:* Lower forging load tolerance required, easier die fill, accommodates stock-volume variation.
- *Disadvantage:* Wasted material in flash; requires a separate trimming operation.

**Flashless die (4 marks):**
- No flash gutter; the cavity is **enclosed** by upper and lower die walls.
- Stock volume must be **precisely controlled** (e.g., by weighing or preforming).
- *Advantages:* No flash, no trimming, better material utilisation, closer tolerances.
- *Disadvantage:* Requires very precise billet volume, higher press tonnage, sensitive to lubrication and temperature variations.

---

##### **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Compute Flash Gutter Cross-Section (1 mark):**

$$A_f = w \times h_g = 10 \text{ mm} \times 2.5 \text{ mm} = 25 \text{ mm}^2 = 25 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_p = 25\,000 \text{ mm}^2 = 25\,000 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2 = 2.5 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Forging Load (2 marks):**

$$F = K_f \cdot A_p + 2 \cdot K_f \cdot A_f$$

$$F_{cav} = 90 \times 10^6 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-2} = 2.25 \times 10^6 \text{ N} = 2\,250 \text{ kN}$$

$$F_{flash} = 2 \times 90 \times 10^6 \times 25 \times 10^{-6} = 4\,500 \text{ N} = 4.5 \text{ kN}$$

$$F_{total} = 2\,250 + 4.5 = 2\,254.5 \text{ kN}$$

> **Final answer:** $F_{total} \approx 2\,254.5 \text{ kN} \approx 2.25 \text{ MN}$

**Step 3 — Shank Area (2 marks):**

$$A_{sh} = \dfrac{F_{total}}{\tau_{all}} = \dfrac{2\,254\,500 \text{ N}}{300 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}} = 7.515 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_{sh} = 7\,515 \text{ mm}^2$$

> **Final answer:** $A_{sh} \approx 7\,515 \text{ mm}^2$

**Step 4 — Shank Diameter (Circular) (2 marks):**

$$A_{sh} = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} \implies d = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 A_{sh}}{\pi}}$$

$$d = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 7\,515}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{30\,060}{3.1416}} = \sqrt{9\,567.5} \approx 97.8 \text{ mm}$$

> **Final answer:** $d \approx 98 \text{ mm}$ (round up to standard 100 mm)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these 15 high-yield points before walking into the KTU ESE hall:**

- [ ] **Forging load** with flash: $F = K_f A_p + 2 K_f A_f$. The factor 2 accounts for **upper + lower flash gutters**.
- [ ] **Projected area $A_p$** must be in **m²** when using SI units (Pa, N). Convert mm² → m² by multiplying by $10^{-6}$.
- [ ] **Empirical die-block sizing constants**: $W = 1.75\sqrt{A_p}$, $L = 2.5\sqrt{A_p}$, $T = 0.40\sqrt{A_p}$ — these are in **mm when $A_p$ is in mm²**.
- [ ] **Flash land thickness** $b = 1.5$ to $3$ mm for hot-forged steel.
- [ ] **Flash gutter depth** $h_g = (0.5 \text{ to } 2) \times b$.
- [ ] **Fillet radius outer** $R = \max\{3 \text{ mm}, 0.07 h\}$; **inner fillet** $R_i = (2 \text{ to } 3) R_{outer}$.
- [ ] **Draft angle** outer 3°–7°; **inner** 5°–10°. Always **greater for inner surfaces** (heat-sink effect).
- [ ] **Parting line** must be on the **plane of symmetry** for balanced loading and uniform die wear.
- [ ] **Standard die steel** for hot forging: **AISI H11 / H13**; allowable compressive stress ≈ **1000–1200 MPa** at forging temperature (derated).
- [ ] **Shank design** uses allowable shear stress $\tau_{all} \approx 300$ MPa for H13.
- [ ] **Preform (blocker) impression** uses **larger fillets (1.5× final)**, **smaller draft**, and **smaller overall dimensions** to account for stock spread.
- [ ] **Trim force** for removing flash: $F_{trim} = 0.6 \sigma_{shear} L_{trim} t_f$.
- [ ] **Forging temperature** for low/medium-carbon steel: **1100 °C to 1250 °C**.
- [ ] **Flash-type dies** are robust to stock-volume variation; **flashless dies** are precise but require tight stock-volume control.
- [ ] **Always draw/describe** the die cross-section with **all six features labelled** in 14-mark design questions — examiners reward this heavily.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lubrication and Cooling of Forging Dies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET524 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Lubrication and Cooling of Forging Dies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Die Lubrication** in forging is defined as the controlled application of a lubricating medium at the die–workpiece interface to minimize interfacial friction, prevent metallic adhesion (galling/seizure), control heat transfer, and extend die life. **Die Cooling** refers to the systematic removal of accumulated thermal energy from forging dies through conduction, convection, and radiation pathways, maintaining die surface temperature within permissible design limits (typically **below 550 °C for hot-work die steels** like H11, H13, and H21).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Lubrication and cooling are not optional auxiliaries — they are integral parameters governing **die life, dimensional accuracy, surface finish, and production economy** in forging operations. Questions frequently appear under CO3: *Apply forming processes and tool design principles for industrial components.*

---

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **sliding a heavy iron skillet across a hot stovetop** with your bare palm (terrifying, right?). Your hand seizes, burns, and tears. Now coat your palm with **oil (lubricant) and wave it under a fan (cooling)** — sliding becomes smooth, and heat dissipates quickly. A forging die experiences **exactly** this situation: enormous contact pressure (200–1000 MPa), surface temperatures approaching **950–1200 °C** for hot forging, and continuous cyclic loading. The lubricant acts as the "oil on your palm," and die cooling is the "fan." Together, they keep the die alive, accurate, and productive.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Industrial Metric:** A typical hot-forging die without proper lubrication–cooling fails in **5,000–10,000 cycles**; with optimized lubrication–cooling, life extends to **50,000–100,000+ cycles**. This is a 5×–10× productivity multiplier.

### Standard Physical Constants and Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Die surface temperature (hot forging) | 300 – 650 | °C |
| Workpiece temperature (steel) | 1100 – 1250 | °C |
| Interface contact pressure | 200 – 1000 | MPa |
| Coefficient of friction (with lubricant) | 0.05 – 0.20 | dimensionless |
| Coefficient of friction (dry forging) | 0.30 – 0.50 | dimensionless |
| Heat flux at die surface | 10 – 50 | MW/m² |
| Die hardness (H13 tempered) | 48 – 54 | HRC |
| Safe die surface temperature | < 550 | °C |

---

### Three Pillars of Die Performance in Forging

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every forging die must satisfy the **Tribo-Thermal-Mechanical Triangle** simultaneously:
> 1. **Tribology** (Lubrication) — controls friction and wear.
> 2. **Thermals** (Cooling) — controls temperature and heat flux.
> 3. **Mechanics** (Die material & design) — controls stress and fatigue.
>
> Neglecting any one pillar collapses the other two. This topic focuses on **Pillars 1 and 2**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Functions of Lubricants in Forging

Lubricants in forging serve **seven concurrent functions**. A KTU examiner will award marks only if you list at least 4–5:

1. **Friction Reduction:** Lowers the coefficient of friction (μ) at the die–workpiece interface from 0.4 (dry) to 0.05–0.1 (lubricated), reducing forging load.
2. **Anti-Welding / Anti-Galling:** Prevents micro-welding of workpiece material to die surface, which causes die surface failure.
3. **Thermal Insulation:** Creates a thin film barrier reducing heat flow from hot workpiece to die.
4. **Die Surface Protection:** Shields die from oxidation and thermal shock.
5. **Metal Flow Enhancement:** Promotes uniform material flow, reducing forging defects (laps, folds, underfills).
6. **Surface Finish Improvement:** Produces smoother forged surfaces (lower Ra values).
7. **Ejection Assistance:** Helps in releasing the forged part from die cavity.

---

### 2.2 Classification of Forging Lubricants

```
FORGING LUBRICANTS
│
├── SOLID LUBRICANTS
│   ├── Graphite (colloidal graphite in water/oil carrier)
│   ├── Molybdenum Disulfide (MoS₂)
│   ├── Boron Nitride (h-BN)
│   └── Mica, Talc (limited use)
│
├── LIQUID LUBRICANTS
│   ├── Mineral oils
│   ├── Synthetic oils
│   ├── Emulsions (oil-in-water)
│   └── Fatty oils (palm, tallow, lard)
│
├── VISCOUS / SEMI-SOLID
│   ├── Greases
│   ├── Soap-based compounds
│   └── Wax-based compounds
│
└── CONVERSION / REACTIVE COATINGS
    ├── Phosphate coatings (Zn, Mn, Fe phosphate)
    ├── Oxalate coatings
    ├── Borax coatings
    └── Glass lubricants (for high-temp forging of Ti, Ni superalloys)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Cold Forging:** Uses **phosphate + soap (stearate)** coating — this is the gold standard.
> **Warm Forging:** Uses **MoS₂/graphite in oil** or **soap-based emulsions**.
> **Hot Forging:** Uses **water-based graphite suspensions** (5–25% graphite by weight) — most common industrially.

---

### 2.3 Selection Criteria for Lubricants

A KTU-level answer should justify lubricant choice based on:

- **Working temperature range** (cold / warm / hot)
- **Workpiece material** (steel, Al, Ti, Ni-alloy)
- **Type of forging** (open-die, closed-die, extrusion, upset)
- **Compatibility with die material**
- **Environmental / health regulations** (e.g., elimination of heavy metals)
- **Ease of application** (spray, brush, dip)
- **Cost-effectiveness**

---

### 2.4 Mechanisms of Lubrication (Tribological Regimes)

Lubrication in forging operates predominantly in the **Boundary Lubrication Regime** and **Extreme Pressure (EP) Regime**, with the Stribeck curve governing the transition.

**Stribeck Curve Concept:**
$$ \mu = f\left(\frac{\eta \cdot V}{P}\right) $$

where $\eta$ = lubricant viscosity, $V$ = sliding velocity, $P$ = contact pressure.

| Regime | Operating Condition | μ Value | Applicable To |
|---|---|---|---|
| Boundary | High P, low V, thin film | 0.05 – 0.15 | **Most forging** |
| Mixed | Moderate P and V | 0.10 – 0.20 | Some warm forging |
| Hydrodynamic | Low P, high V, thick film | 0.001 – 0.01 | Not in forging |
| Extreme Pressure (EP) | Very high P, asperity contact | 0.05 – 0.10 | Heavy hot forging |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Forging NEVER operates in hydrodynamic regime due to extreme pressures that squeeze out the lubricant film. **Boundary and EP regimes dominate.**

---

### 2.5 Die Cooling: Why It Is Essential

During hot forging, the die absorbs **80–90%** of the heat from the workpiece in the first few milliseconds of contact. Without cooling:
- Die surface temperature rises above tempering temperature (~600 °C for H13).
- Die undergoes **softening, plastic deformation, thermal fatigue cracking, and gross failure**.
- Production rate (parts/hour) drops because natural cooling is too slow.

**Modes of Heat Transfer in Die:**
- **Conduction** — through die body to internal cooling channels.
- **Convection** — coolant (water/oil/air) carries heat away.
- **Radiation** — die surface radiates heat to surroundings (minor at 300–500 °C).

---

### 2.6 Methods of Die Cooling

| Cooling Method | Mechanism | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Natural air cooling | Convection + radiation between cycles | Slow, low-volume forging |
| Forced air cooling | Blower-directed air jets | Hammers, small presses |
| Water spray / mist | Direct water impingement | Hot forging of steel |
| Internal water channels | Conduction to coolant | Hammer dies, press dies |
| Oil circulation | Heat transfer oil in channels | Warm forging (precision) |
| Heat pipes | Two-phase heat transfer | Advanced / research systems |
| Cryogenic cooling | Liquid N₂ / CO₂ | Specialized tooling |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Direct water spray on hot die (>300 °C) causes **thermal shock cracking**. Always pre-cool or use water-mist systems with controlled temperature.

---

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = h \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat transfer by convection | W (J/s) | Coolant heat removal |
| 2 | $q = k \cdot \frac{\Delta T}{\Delta x}$ | Heat conduction (Fourier) | W/m² | Heat flow in die body |
| 3 | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Coolant enthalpy change | W | Water/oil cooling capacity |
| 4 | $\tau_f = \mu \cdot \sigma_n$ | Friction shear stress | Pa | Friction force on die |
| 5 | $P = P_0 \cdot e^{\mu \cdot \frac{2L}{h}}$ | Forging load (slip-line) | N | Effect of μ on load |
| 6 | $\mu = \frac{\eta V}{P}$ | Stribeck parameter | dimensionless | Regime identification |
| 7 | $L_{die} = f(T, \sigma, \mu)$ | Die life function | cycles | Empirical models |
| 8 | $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot A \cdot v$ | Coolant mass flow rate | kg/s | Pipe sizing |
| 9 | $Nu = \frac{h D}{k}$ | Nusselt number | — | Convection correlation |
| 10 | $Bi = \frac{h L_c}{k_{die}}$ | Biot number | — | Lumped heat analysis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Examination Insight:** Equation 5 shows forging load depends **exponentially** on friction coefficient μ. A reduction in μ from 0.4 to 0.1 can reduce forging load by **50–70%**. This single fact makes lubrication economically indispensable.

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Hot forging of connecting rods, crankshafts — water-based graphite lubricants are standard.
- **Aerospace:** Isothermal forging of Ti-6Al-4V and Inconel uses **glass lubricants** because conventional lubricants vaporize at 1100 °C+.
- **Cold Forging of Fasteners:** Zinc phosphate + sodium stearate (soap) is the universal system for bolts, screws, and nuts.
- **Press Forging vs. Hammer Forging:** Press forging has longer die contact time → demands **superior internal cooling channels**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Heat Transfer Through Die Wall to Cooling Channel

This is a **high-yield KTU derivation** that frequently appears in Part B (14-mark) questions. We model the die as a slab of thickness $L$ with surface temperature $T_s$ (at die–workpiece interface) and coolant temperature $T_c$ at depth $L$.

**Step 1: State the governing equation (1-D steady-state conduction through die wall)**

$$ q = k_{die} \cdot \frac{T_s - T_{inner}}{L_{wall}} $$

where $k_{die}$ is the thermal conductivity of the die material (H13 steel ≈ **28 W/m·K**), $L_{wall}$ is the distance from die surface to the cooling channel wall, and $T_{inner}$ is the temperature at the cooling channel inner wall.

**Step 2: Convection from channel inner wall to coolant**

The heat reaching the channel wall is then transferred to the coolant by forced convection:

$$ q = h_{cool} \cdot (T_{inner} - T_c) $$

where $h_{cool}$ is the convective heat transfer coefficient of the coolant (water ≈ **3000–10,000 W/m²·K** for turbulent flow; oil ≈ **500–2000 W/m²·K**).

**Step 3: Eliminate $T_{inner}$ by combining the two equations**

From the conduction equation:

$$ T_{inner} = T_s - \frac{q \cdot L_{wall}}{k_{die}} $$

Substitute into the convection equation:

$$ q = h_{cool} \cdot \left( T_s - \frac{q \cdot L_{wall}}{k_{die}} - T_c \right) $$

**Step 4: Expand and collect terms in $q$**

$$ q = h_{cool} \cdot (T_s - T_c) - h_{cool} \cdot \frac{q \cdot L_{wall}}{k_{die}} $$

$$ q + h_{cool} \cdot \frac{q \cdot L_{wall}}{k_{die}} = h_{cool} \cdot (T_s - T_c) $$

$$ q \left( 1 + \frac{h_{cool} \cdot L_{wall}}{k_{die}} \right) = h_{cool} \cdot (T_s - T_c) $$

**Step 5: Final combined heat flux expression**

$$ q = \frac{T_s - T_c}{\dfrac{1}{h_{cool}} + \dfrac{L_{wall}}{k_{die}}} $$

This is the classic **thermal resistance in series** analogy.

$$ q = \frac{\Delta T_{overall}}{R_{convection} + R_{conduction}} $$

where $R_{convection} = \dfrac{1}{h_{cool}}$ and $R_{conduction} = \dfrac{L_{wall}}{k_{die}}$, both in units of **m²·K/W**.

**Step 6: Total heat removed by coolant (per unit time)**

For a die with total surface area $A$ in contact with workpiece, and average heat flux $q$ during contact time $t_c$ per cycle:

$$ Q_{cycle} = q \cdot A \cdot t_c \quad \text{(J per cycle)} $$

The coolant must remove this in the inter-cycle time $t_{gap}$:

$$ \dot{m} = \frac{Q_{cycle}}{c_p \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})} $$

where $\dot{m}$ is coolant mass flow rate, $c_p$ is coolant specific heat, and $(T_{out} - T_{in})$ is the coolant temperature rise (typically limited to **5–10 °C** for water, **10–20 °C** for oil).

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 7-Mark Problem)

> **Problem:** A hot-forging die (H13 steel) has a working surface area of $A = 0.02 \text{ m}^2$ exposed to the workpiece. The die surface temperature during forging is $T_s = 500$ °C. A water-cooling channel is located at $L_{wall} = 15 \text{ mm}$ from the die surface. Coolant water enters at $T_c = 25$ °C with $h_{cool} = 5000 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$. Thermal conductivity of H13 = **28 W/m·K**. Calculate: (a) the steady-state heat flux $q$ through the die wall, (b) the total heat removed per forging cycle if contact time is $t_c = 2$ seconds.

**Solution:**

**(a) Heat flux calculation**

Using the combined resistance equation derived in Section 3.1:

$$ q = \frac{T_s - T_c}{\dfrac{1}{h_{cool}} + \dfrac{L_{wall}}{k_{die}}} $$

Substitute numerical values:

$$ T_s - T_c = 500 - 25 = 475 \text{ °C} $$

$$ \frac{1}{h_{cool}} = \frac{1}{5000} = 2.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W} $$

$$ \frac{L_{wall}}{k_{die}} = \frac{0.015}{28} = 5.357 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W} $$

$$ R_{total} = 2.0 \times 10^{-4} + 5.357 \times 10^{-4} = 7.357 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W} $$

$$ q = \frac{475}{7.357 \times 10^{-4}} $$

$$ q = 6.456 \times 10^{5} \text{ W/m}^2 \approx 0.646 \text{ MW/m}^2 $$

**(b) Total heat per cycle**

$$ Q_{cycle} = q \cdot A \cdot t_c $$

$$ Q_{cycle} = 6.456 \times 10^{5} \times 0.02 \times 2 $$

$$ Q_{cycle} = 2.582 \times 10^{4} \text{ J} = 25.82 \text{ kJ per cycle} $$

**Interpretation:** At a forging rate of 60 parts/min (1 part/sec), the cooling system must remove **≈ 25.82 kW** of heat continuously. This justifies the design of robust internal cooling channels.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Lubricant Selection by Forging Type

| Forging Type | Temperature | Recommended Lubricant | Cooling Method | Die Life Expectancy |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cold Forging (steel)** | Room temp | Zn/Mn phosphate + stearate soap | Minimal (air) | 100,000 – 500,000 |
| **Warm Forging (steel)** | 200 – 850 °C | MoS₂/graphite + oil carrier | Light oil circulation | 30,000 – 80,000 |
| **Hot Forging (steel)** | 850 – 1250 °C | Water-based graphite (10–25%) | Water channels/spray | 10,000 – 50,000 |
| **Hot Forging (Al, Mg)** | 350 – 500 °C | Graphite emulsion, oil-based | Air / water-mist | 50,000 – 100,000 |
| **Hot Forging (Ti)** | 900 – 1100 °C | Glass lubricants (e.g., borosilicate) | Inert atmosphere | 5,000 – 20,000 |
| **Hot Forging (Ni superalloy)** | 1050 – 1200 °C | Glass + ceramic coating | Isothermal die heating | 2,000 – 10,000 |
| **Extrusion (hot Al)** | 400 – 500 °C | Oil + graphite | None / air | 20,000 – 50,000 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of Diagram 4.3:** The temperature drop is **non-linear** across the die cross-section. The largest drop occurs at the **die–workpiece interface** and at the **coolant–channel wall interface**, not within the die body itself. This is why **surface treatments** (e.g., nitriding) and **channel placement** (as close to surface as possible) are critical design decisions.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Define die lubrication. List any four functions of a forging lubricant.*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Die lubrication in forging is the controlled application of a lubricating medium at the die–workpiece interface to minimize friction, prevent metallic adhesion, control heat transfer, and extend die service life.

**Four functions of a forging lubricant:**
1. Reduces the coefficient of friction between workpiece and die, thereby lowering forging load.
2. Prevents die wear, galling, and micro-welding of workpiece material to die surfaces.
3. Acts as a thermal barrier, reducing the rate of heat flow from the hot workpiece to the die.
4. Improves the surface finish of the forged component and assists in ejection from the die cavity.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Mention the various methods used for cooling forging dies. Which method is most commonly used for hot forging of steel?*

**Model Answer:**

**Various methods of die cooling:**
1. Natural air cooling
2. Forced air cooling (blower jets)
3. Direct water spray / mist cooling
4. Internal water-circulation channels
5. Oil circulation cooling
6. Cryogenic cooling (liquid nitrogen)
7. Heat pipe cooling

**Most common for hot forging of steel:** Internal water-cooling channels combined with intermittent water-mist spray is the most widely used method, as water has high specific heat (4.18 kJ/kg·K) and a high convective heat transfer coefficient (3000–10000 W/m²·K), making it highly effective in removing large heat loads from hot-work die steels.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. Question A (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**

(a) **Explain in detail the classification of forging lubricants with suitable examples for cold, warm, and hot forging processes. (7 marks)**

(b) **Derive the expression for steady-state heat flux through a forging die wall with internal cooling. A hot-forging die has surface temperature 480 °C, with a cooling channel located 12 mm below the surface. Water at 30 °C flows in the channel with $h = 4500 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$. The die material is H13 steel ($k = 28 \text{ W/m·K}$). Determine the heat flux through the die wall. (7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

Forging lubricants are broadly classified into the following categories, each suited to a specific temperature range and forming process:

**1. Solid Lubricants**
- **Graphite** — Most widely used in hot forging as a colloidal suspension in water. Stable up to **600 °C in air**; provides excellent boundary lubrication.
- **Molybdenum Disulfide (MoS₂)** — Used in warm and cold forging; stable up to **400 °C**; effective under high contact pressure.
- **Boron Nitride (h-BN)** — Used in high-temperature forging of reactive metals (Ti, Zr) due to chemical inertness.
- **Mica and Talc** — Used in limited applications as solid film lubricants.

**2. Liquid Lubricants**
- **Mineral oils** and **synthetic oils** — Used in cold forging; provide good boundary lubrication.
- **Emulsions** (oil-in-water) — Used in warm forging; balance cooling and lubrication.
- **Fatty oils** (tallow, palm oil) — Used in cold forging of steel; biodegradable.

**3. Viscous / Semi-Solid Lubricants**
- **Greases** and **soap-based compounds** (e.g., sodium stearate) — Used in cold forging.
- **Wax-based compounds** — Provide thick, adherent films.

**4. Conversion Coatings (Reactive Lubricants)**
- **Phosphate coatings** (Zn, Mn, Fe phosphate) — Combined with soap, this is the standard cold-forging lubricant system. The phosphate layer is porous and absorbs the soap, providing excellent boundary lubrication under extreme pressure.
- **Oxalate and Borax coatings** — Used in some warm forging applications.
- **Glass lubricants** — Used in hot forging of titanium and nickel superalloys above 1000 °C; molten glass forms a viscous film that protects the die.

**Application Mapping:**
- **Cold forging (room temperature):** Phosphate + stearate soap (gold standard)
- **Warm forging (200–850 °C):** MoS₂ in oil or graphite emulsion
- **Hot forging (850–1250 °C):** Water-based colloidal graphite (10–25%)

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Derivation (refer to Section 3.1 for full derivation):**

The steady-state heat flux through a die wall with internal cooling is given by:

$$ q = \frac{T_s - T_c}{\dfrac{1}{h_{cool}} + \dfrac{L_{wall}}{k_{die}}} $$

**Numerical substitution:**

Given:
- $T_s = 480$ °C
- $T_c = 30$ °C
- $L_{wall} = 12 \text{ mm} = 0.012 \text{ m}$
- $h_{cool} = 4500 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$
- $k_{die} = 28 \text{ W/m·K}$

$$ \Delta T = 480 - 30 = 450 \text{ °C} $$

$$ R_{conv} = \frac{1}{4500} = 2.222 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W} $$

$$ R_{cond} = \frac{0.012}{28} = 4.286 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W} $$

$$ R_{total} = 2.222 \times 10^{-4} + 4.286 \times 10^{-4} = 6.508 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W} $$

$$ q = \frac{450}{6.508 \times 10^{-4}} = 6.915 \times 10^{5} \text{ W/m}^2 $$

$$ \boxed{q \approx 0.692 \text{ MW/m}^2} $$

**Conclusion:** The heat flux of 0.69 MW/m² is within the safe range for hot-forging dies with internal water cooling, confirming that the design is thermally adequate.

---

**Q3. Question B (14 Marks) – Internal Choice Alternative**

(a) **Discuss the mechanism of lubrication in forging with the help of the Stribeck curve. Explain boundary lubrication and extreme-pressure (EP) regimes. (7 marks)**

(b) **Explain the various methods of die cooling used in forging. Compare the effectiveness of water cooling, oil cooling, and air cooling. (7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Mechanism of Lubrication in Forging:**

The Stribeck curve describes the relationship between the coefficient of friction (μ) and a dimensionless parameter $\eta V / P$ (viscosity × velocity / pressure). It identifies three primary lubrication regimes:

$$ \mu = f\left(\frac{\eta \cdot V}{P}\right) $$

**1. Boundary Lubrication Regime (μ = 0.05 – 0.15)**
- In this regime, the lubricant film is **thinner than the asperities** of the contacting surfaces.
- The load is supported by **asperity contact**, and the lubricant forms a thin adsorbed film (1–10 molecular layers) on the die surface.
- Friction is determined by the **shear strength of the lubricant film**, not by the bulk viscosity.
- **Dominant in cold and warm forging** because the high contact pressure (500–2000 MPa) squeezes out most of the lubricant.

**2. Extreme-Pressure (EP) Regime (μ = 0.05 – 0.10)**
- Occurs under **very high contact pressures** where even boundary films may fail.
- **EP additives** in the lubricant (e.g., sulfur, phosphorus, chlorine compounds) react chemically with the metal surface to form low-shear-strength compounds (e.g., FeS, FeCl₂).
- These sacrificial layers prevent metal-to-metal welding.
- **Dominant in hot forging** of steel where temperatures are very high and pressures are extreme.

**3. Hydrodynamic / Full Film Regime (μ = 0.001 – 0.01)**
- A thick, continuous lubricant film **fully separates** the two surfaces.
- The load is supported by the **pressure generated within the fluid film**.
- **Not applicable in forging** because the extreme pressures in forging cannot be sustained by hydrodynamic films.

> **Forging operates primarily in the Boundary and EP regimes**; the Stribeck curve confirms this by placing forging operations at the high-pressure, low-velocity end of the diagram.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Methods of Die Cooling:**

The choice of cooling method depends on the forging temperature, production rate, die complexity, and economic considerations.

**1. Air Cooling**
- **Mechanism:** Natural or forced convection using ambient air.
- **$h$ (heat transfer coefficient):** 10–200 W/m²·K (natural to forced).
- **Application:** Cold forging, light warm forging, low production rates.
- **Advantages:** Simple, inexpensive, no fluid handling systems.
- **Disadvantages:** Slow, inadequate for hot forging.

**2. Oil Cooling**
- **Mechanism:** Forced circulation of heat-transfer oil (synthetic or mineral) through internal die channels.
- **$h$:** 500–2000 W/m²·K.
- **Application:** Warm forging, isothermal forging, precision dies.
- **Advantages:** Uniform cooling, no corrosion, no thermal shock.
- **Disadvantages:** Lower heat capacity than water; oil degrades over time.

**3. Water Cooling**
- **Mechanism:** Forced circulation of water through internal channels and/or external spray/mist.
- **$h$:** 3000–10,000 W/m²·K (turbulent flow).
- **Application:** Hot forging of steel — **the industry standard**.
- **Advantages:** Highest heat removal rate; abundant; inexpensive.
- **Disadvantages:** Risk of thermal shock; corrosion of die steel; scaling in channels.

**Comparative Effectiveness:**

| Parameter | Air | Oil | Water |
|---|---|---|---|
| Specific heat (kJ/kg·K) | 1.0 | 1.8 – 2.5 | 4.18 |
| Density (kg/m³) | 1.2 | 850 | 1000 |
| $h$ value (W/m²·K) | 10 – 200 | 500 – 2000 | 3000 – 10000 |
| Effectiveness rank | 3 | 2 | 1 |
| Typical use | Cold | Warm | Hot |

**Conclusion:** For hot forging of steel, water cooling is the most effective due to its high specific heat and convective heat transfer coefficient. However, **oil cooling is preferred for warm and isothermal forging** where thermal stability and surface quality are critical.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Lubrication and Cooling Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "lubricant" with "coolant":** They are different systems. Lubricants act at the die–workpiece interface; coolants act inside die channels. Examiners will **deduct 1 mark** if these terms are used interchangeably.
>
> 2. **Forgetting units in formula substitution:** A correct formula with **wrong or missing units** earns only 2 of 7 marks in derivation questions. Always write units explicitly.
>
> 3. **Listing functions without examples:** When asked "classify lubricants," do not just list "solid, liquid, gas." You must give **at least one example per category** (e.g., graphite, MoS₂, phosphate, glass).
>
> 4. **Ignoring die material limitations:** Never recommend water cooling for a die at >300 °C surface temperature without acknowledging **thermal shock** — examiners expect this awareness.
>
> 5. **Stribeck Curve Misuse:** Many students draw the curve but fail to identify which **regime** applies to forging. Always state explicitly: *Forging operates in the boundary and EP regimes.*
>
> 6. **Skipping assumptions in derivation:** For the heat flux derivation, always state the assumptions: *1-D steady-state, constant k, uniform h, no heat generation in die body.*

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Bullet Summary for Last-Minute Revision:**

- **Definition:** Lubrication = friction/wear control at die–workpiece interface; Cooling = heat removal from die body.
- **Safe die surface temperature:** **< 550 °C** for H13 die steel.
- **Functions of lubricant (memorize 5+):** friction reduction, anti-wear, thermal insulation, surface finish, ejection aid, anti-welding, metal flow enhancement.
- **Lubricant types:** Solid (graphite, MoS₂, BN), Liquid (oils, emulsions), Viscous (greases, soaps), Conversion (phosphate, oxalate, glass).
- **Cold forging lubricant:** **Zinc phosphate + sodium stearate soap** (universal system).
- **Hot forging lubricant:** **Colloidal graphite in water (10–25%)** (industry standard).
- **Stribeck curve parameter:** $\eta V / P$. Forging is in **boundary/EP regime**, not hydrodynamic.
- **Friction coefficient with lubricant:** 0.05 – 0.20. Without lubricant: 0.30 – 0.50.
- **Die cooling methods:** Air, oil, water (spray/channels), cryogenic, heat pipes.
- **Best coolant for hot forging steel:** **Water** (highest $h$ and $c_p$).
- **Critical derivation:** $q = \dfrac{\Delta T}{\dfrac{1}{h} + \dfrac{L}{k}}$ — memorize and practice.
- **Forging load vs. friction:** $P \propto e^{\mu}$ — friction reduction is **economically exponential**, not linear.
- **Die life extension:** Proper lubrication + cooling can extend die life **5× to 10×**.
- **Thermal shock risk:** Direct water on >300 °C die surface causes cracking — always use mist or pre-cool.
- **Glass lubricant use:** Hot forging of **Ti, Ni superalloys** above 1000 °C.
- **Biot number usage:** If $Bi < 0.1$, lumped heat capacity analysis is valid for die thermal modeling.
- **KTU Exam Focus Topics (highest weightage):** Classification of lubricants, Stribeck curve interpretation, heat flux derivation, comparison of cooling methods, lubricant selection rationale.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Die Design for Extrusion Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET524 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Die Design for Extrusion Processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Extrusion Die Design** is the engineering discipline concerned with the geometric, metallurgical, and tribological specification of a tooling element (the *die*) that forces a confined billet of ductile metal to flow through a shaped orifice under compressive load, thereby producing a continuous product of uniform cross-section.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** terminology, the die assembly is the heart of the extrusion tooling system. It transforms a cylindrical billet of cross-sectional area **A₀** (in the container) into a product of cross-sectional area **A_f** (after the die). The fundamental parameter governing die design is the **Extrusion Ratio**:

$$R = \frac{A_0}{A_f}$$

and the corresponding **True (Logarithmic) Strain**:

$$\varepsilon = \ln(R) = \ln\!\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right)$$

The die therefore performs three coupled functions:
1. **Geometric Shaping** — defines the cross-sectional profile.
2. **Flow Control** — governs the velocity field of the deforming billet.
3. **Load Transfer** — converts ram pressure into useful plastic deformation work.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Toothpaste Tube Analogy**
> Imagine squeezing a tube of toothpaste. Your thumb acts as the *ram*, the tube walls act as the *container*, and the nozzle acts as the *die*. As you press, the paste is forced to flow through a small opening, taking up the shape of the nozzle. If the nozzle is long and rough (long *die land*, high friction), the paste emerges slowly and the effort is greater. If the nozzle is short and smooth, the paste gushes out easily. A die in metal extrusion behaves identically — its **land length**, **die angle**, and **surface finish** dictate both the **force** required and the **quality** of the extruded product.

A better industrial analogy is the **hydraulic press squeezing modelling clay through a shaped die** to make decorative borders. The clay comes out continuously, taking the cross-section of the die opening.

## 1.3 Classification of Extrusion Processes (Syllabus Highlight)

| Process Type | Relative Ram Motion | Container Friction | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Direct (Forward) Extrusion** | Ram & billet move together | **Present** (significant) | General rods, bars, architectural sections |
| **Indirect (Backward) Extrusion** | Die moves into stationary billet | **Eliminated** | Tubes, high-strength alloys, magnesium |
| **Hydrostatic Extrusion** | Fluid pressure acts on billet | **Eliminated** | Brittle materials, high reduction ratios |
| **Impact (Cold) Extrusion** | High-velocity single blow | Minimal (slug) | Cartridge cases, automotive pistons |
| **Continuous Extrusion** | Friction-wheel feed (Conform) | Variable | Electrical conductors, clad products |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU Module 3, the **die design considerations differ** for each class. Direct extrusion is the *baseline case*; indirect and hydrostatic are treated as friction-modified or frictionless variants of the same theoretical model.

## 1.4 Key Engineering Metrics (In Bold)

- **Extrusion Ratio (R)** — dimensionless, typically 4 to 400 in industrial practice.
- **Mean Flow Stress (Y_f)** — average of yield stress in deformed and undeformed states, in **MPa**.
- **Die Pressure (P)** — peak pressure on the die face, in **MPa**.
- **Bearing Length (L_b)** — straight cylindrical portion of the die land, in **mm**.
- **Die Semi-Angle (α)** — half-angle of the approach cone, in **degrees (typically 20°–60°)**.
- **Bearing Pressure (p_b)** — pressure on the die bearing surface, in **MPa**.
- **Ram Velocity (V_r)** — speed of the press ram, in **mm/s**.
- **Billlet Temperature (T)** — typically 350 °C to 500 °C for aluminum, in **°C**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of an Extrusion Die

The die is not a single flat plate. It is a precision-engineered component composed of several distinct zones, each serving a specific purpose:

1. **Entry / Approach Zone** — the converging conical or streamlined entry that guides the billet from the container diameter (D₀) to the die orifice diameter (D_f). The half-angle **α** here is critical.
2. **Die Land (or Bearing)** — a short, parallel-walled cylindrical land of length **L_b** that stabilizes the flow and imparts the final dimensional accuracy.
3. **Bearing Surface** — the cylindrical wall of the die land that contacts the extruded product. The friction here is *useful* — it resists the tendency of the central flow to outrun the surface flow, which otherwise causes **die-line defects**.
4. **Relief / Back-Extrusion Zone** — a small back-taper (typically 1°–3°) immediately after the bearing, to prevent the extruded product from scoring the die as it leaves.
5. **Die Backer / Shrink-Fit Ring** — a multi-ring forged steel assembly that supports the working die against bursting stresses.

## 2.2 Why Each Design Parameter Exists — The "Why & How" Logic

- **Die Angle (2α)**: A *very small* α (sharp die) gives near-homogeneous deformation but causes *dead-metal zones* and *excessive redundant work* → higher pressure. A *very large* α (blunt die) reduces redundant work but creates *flow lines* that can cause center-burst and surface cracking. The optimum is **45°–60°** for steel and **20°–30°** for aluminum.
- **Bearing Length (L_b)**: Too short → poor dimensional control and low die life. Too long → excessive frictional heat, sticking, and product surface tearing. Empirical rule: **L_b = (0.5 to 2.0) × D_f** depending on product.
- **Bearing Profile**: Often *conical* (tapered 1°–2° along its length) to balance the velocity profile — slightly tighter at the exit to compensate for the central material moving faster.
- **Die Material**: Hot-work tool steel **AISI H11 / H13** (or **H21** for very high temperatures), through-hardened to **48–54 HRC**, then **nitrided** for surface hardness (>1000 HV) and abrasion resistance.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains every equation you must memorize for KTU ESE questions on this topic. Master these and you will solve any numerical.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables / Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Extrusion Ratio | $R = A_0 / A_f$ | $A_0$ = billet area, $A_f$ = product area, dimensionless | Often 4–400 |
| 2 | True Strain | $\varepsilon = \ln R$ | dimensionless | Used in work calculations |
| 3 | Ideal (Frictionless) Pressure | $P_i = Y_f \cdot \ln R$ | $Y_f$ = mean flow stress (MPa) | Lower bound (no friction, no redundant work) |
| 4 | Johnson's Empirical Pressure | $P = Y_f (a + b \ln R)$ | $a$ = friction–redundant factor, $b$ ≈ 0.8 | Direct extrusion, working value |
| 5 | Container Friction Term | $a = 1 + \dfrac{2 L_0}{D_0 \cdot R}$ | $L_0$ = remaining billet length, $D_0$ = billet diameter | Disappears in indirect extrusion |
| 6 | Redundant Work Factor (Slab) | $\Phi \approx 0.8 + 1.5 \alpha$ | $\alpha$ = die semi-angle (rad) | Empirical from upper-bound analysis |
| 7 | Peak Pressure (Combined) | $P_{\text{peak}} = Y_f \, \Phi \, \ln R + p_f \, \dfrac{2 L_0}{D_0}$ | $p_f$ = interface friction stress (MPa) | KTU preferred form |
| 8 | Work per Unit Volume (Homogeneous) | $W = Y_f \, \ln R$ | $W$ in J/m³ | Energy to deform uniformly |
| 9 | Bearing Length (Practical) | $L_b = K \cdot D_f$ | $K$ = 0.5 to 2.0 (empirical) | $D_f$ = die orifice diameter |
| 10 | Extrusion Constant (Extrudability) | $K_{\text{ex}} = \dfrac{\ln R}{P/Y_f}$ | dimensionless | Measures how easily metal extrudes |
| 11 | Exit Temperature Rise (Approx.) | $\Delta T = \dfrac{\eta \cdot P \cdot \ln R}{\rho \, c}$ | $\eta$ = efficiency, $\rho$ = density, $c$ = specific heat | Used to avoid overheating |
| 12 | Bursting Stress on Die | $\sigma_{\text{burst}} = p_d \cdot \dfrac{D_i}{t}$ | $D_i$ = inner die dia, $t$ = wall thickness | Lame-type equation for shrink-fit die |
| 13 | Sticking Friction Stress | $p_f = \dfrac{Y_f}{\sqrt{3}}$ (Tresca) | MPa | Maximum interface shear |
| 14 | Optimum Die Semi-Angle | $\alpha_{\text{opt}} \approx \arctan\!\left(0.1 + 0.01 \, R\right)$ | degrees | Empirical for steel dies |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Tip:** When asked *"why is the extrusion pressure higher than the ideal value?"*, your answer must mention **two** phenomena: (1) *Redundant work* in the deformation zone, and (2) *Frictional work* at the billet–container interface (direct extrusion only). Examiners will deduct 1 mark for each omitted cause.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Extrusion die design principles govern the manufacture of:
- **Aluminium architectural sections** (window frames, structural channels) — 60 % of global extruded aluminium.
- **Copper conductor wire** (continuous Conform extrusion).
- **Steel and titanium aerospace structural sections**.
- **Aluminium and copper heat-sink profiles** for electronics cooling.
- **Bimetallic coins and clad nuclear fuel rods** via coextrusion.

A well-designed die can produce **10,000 to 100,000 billets** before requiring re-nitriding or refurbishment. A poorly designed die fails within **50 billets** due to heat-checking, abrasion, or bursting.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The derivations below are the **exact analytical route** that KTU valuators expect in a 14-mark question. Every algebraic step is shown; no intermediate algebra is skipped.

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Homogeneous (Ideal) Extrusion Pressure

We begin with the **slab method**, treating the deforming material as a stack of thin coaxial disks moving together.

**Step 1 — Define the slab element.**
Consider a thin slab of the billet, of thickness $dz$ along the extrusion axis, at axial position $z$ inside the deformation zone.

**Step 2 — Apply force equilibrium in the z-direction.**
The slab is acted on by:
- Compressive axial stress $\sigma_z$ on its leading face (area $A_z$).
- Compressive axial stress $(\sigma_z + d\sigma_z)$ on its trailing face.
- Shear friction $\tau$ along the conical die wall (area $dA_w$).

Equilibrium of the slab (sign convention: compressive stresses are positive):

$$
\sigma_z \, A_z - (\sigma_z + d\sigma_z)(A_z + dA_z) - \tau \, dA_w = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Geometric relation between $A_z$ and $z$.**
Let the die semi-angle be $\alpha$ and the entry radius be $r_0$. At a depth $z$ into the deformation zone, the current radius is:

$$
r(z) = r_0 - z \tan\alpha
$$

Therefore the cross-sectional area is:

$$
A_z = \pi \, r(z)^2 = \pi \, (r_0 - z \tan\alpha)^2
$$

Differentiating with respect to $z$:

$$
\frac{dA_z}{dz} = -2\pi (r_0 - z \tan\alpha) \tan\alpha
$$

**Step 4 — Express the wall area in terms of $dA_z$.**
For a conical die of semi-angle $\alpha$, the slant length per unit axial length is $1/\cos\alpha$. The wall area for an axial advance $dz$ is:

$$
dA_w = \frac{2\pi r(z)\, dz}{\cos\alpha}
$$

Using $r(z) = r_0 - z \tan\alpha$ and the geometric identity, the wall area can be related to $dA_z$ by:

$$
dA_w = -\frac{dA_z}{2 \sin\alpha \cos\alpha} = -\frac{dA_z}{\sin 2\alpha}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the **Tresca yield criterion** with homogeneous deformation.**
For ideal (frictionless, no redundant shear) deformation, the material yields in pure compression with a uniform flow stress $Y_f$. The axial stress required to push the slab forward equals the local pressure:

$$
\sigma_z = Y_f \quad \text{(yield under uniaxial compression, plane strain included via } Y_f\text{)}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute into the equilibrium equation.**
With $\tau = 0$ (ideal case):

$$
-d\sigma_z \cdot A_z = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; d\sigma_z = 0
$$

This naïve result tells us the equilibrium alone is *insufficient* — we must consider the **radial stress** required to expand the material sideways into the die cone.

**Step 7 — Use the von Mises / Tresca yield condition in the deformation cone.**
At the conical wall, the material is constrained by the die. The radial stress $\sigma_r$ acts perpendicular to the wall, and the axial stress is $\sigma_z$. From Tresca, the principal stress difference equals $Y_f$:

$$
\sigma_z - \sigma_r = Y_f
$$

**Step 8 — Integrate across the deformation zone.**
The die pressure at entry (i.e. the ram pressure $P$) is found by integrating the equilibrium equation from the entry of the deformation zone to the exit (die land):

$$
P_{\text{ideal}} = Y_f \, \ln\!\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right) = Y_f \ln R
$$

Thus the **ideal homogeneous extrusion pressure is**:

$$
\boxed{P_i = Y_f \ln R}
$$

This is the celebrated result first derived by **Siebert and Lueg** (slab method) and later confirmed by **Hill's slip-line field theory**. The result is *independent of die angle* — meaning the ideal work is the same regardless of the geometry of the deformation zone.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Johnson's Empirical Pressure Formula (Direct Extrusion)

The ideal pressure is *never* achieved in real direct extrusion because of:
- **Redundant work** inside the deformation cone.
- **Frictional work** between the billet and the container wall.

**Step 1 — Decompose the actual pressure into additive terms.**

$$
P_{\text{actual}} = P_{\text{ideal}} + P_{\text{redundant}} + P_{\text{friction}}
$$

**Step 2 — Quantify the redundant-work contribution.**
Upper-bound analysis (Avitzur) gives the redundant work as a fraction $\Phi$ of the ideal work, where $\Phi$ depends on die angle $\alpha$ and extrusion ratio $R$:

$$
P_{\text{redundant}} = Y_f \, (\Phi - 1) \ln R
$$

For typical conical dies, $\Phi \approx 0.8 + 1.5 \alpha$ (with $\alpha$ in radians), so $P_{\text{redundant}} \approx 0.8 \, Y_f \ln R$ for a 45° die.

**Step 3 — Quantify the container-friction contribution.**
The container wall is at $D_0$ and the billet length remaining is $L_0$. The shear traction on the container wall is at its sticking value $\tau_s = Y_f / \sqrt{3}$ (Tresca). The additional axial force is:

$$
F_{\text{friction}} = \tau_s \cdot \pi D_0 L_0
$$

Dividing by the die exit area $A_f$:

$$
P_{\text{friction}} = \frac{\tau_s \pi D_0 L_0}{A_f} = \frac{Y_f}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \frac{\pi D_0 L_0}{A_f}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to the canonical Johnson form.**
Noting that $A_0 = \pi D_0^2 / 4$, we have $\pi D_0 / A_0 = 4 / D_0$. Substituting $A_0 = R \cdot A_f$:

$$
P_{\text{friction}} = Y_f \cdot \frac{4 L_0}{D_0 R \sqrt{3}} \approx Y_f \cdot \frac{2 L_0}{D_0 R}
$$

(using the engineering approximation $4/\sqrt{3} \approx 2$.)

**Step 5 — Assemble the total pressure.**

$$
P = Y_f \ln R + Y_f (\Phi - 1) \ln R + Y_f \frac{2 L_0}{D_0 R}
$$

Combining the first two terms with $b = \Phi$ and defining $a = (2 L_0)/(D_0 R)$:

$$
\boxed{P = Y_f \left(a + b \ln R\right)} \quad \text{(Johnson formula, 1957)}
$$

**Step 6 — Special case: Indirect extrusion.**
In indirect (backward) extrusion, the die moves *into* a stationary billet, so the billet does not slide against the container wall. The friction term vanishes, and:

$$
P_{\text{indirect}} = Y_f \, b \ln R \quad \text{with} \quad b \approx 0.8
$$

Hence, indirect extrusion requires **20–30 % less force** than direct extrusion for the same reduction.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Die Bearing Length (Wear-Based Criterion)

The bearing length $L_b$ is set by two competing requirements:
- *Wear life:* $L_b$ must be large enough to keep bearing pressure below a critical value.
- *Product surface:* $L_b$ must be small enough to avoid sticking and tearing.

**Step 1 — Define bearing pressure.**
Bearing pressure $p_b$ is the radial stress on the cylindrical die land:

$$
p_b = \frac{F_{\text{ram}}}{A_b} = \frac{P \cdot A_f}{\pi D_f L_b}
$$

where $D_f$ is the die orifice diameter and $A_b = \pi D_f L_b$ is the bearing area.

**Step 2 — Apply wear criterion.**
Archard's wear equation (volume of wear $\propto$ bearing pressure $\times$ sliding distance) gives an empirical rule:

$$
p_b \leq [p_b]_{\text{allow}} \approx 200 \text{ MPa} \text{ (for nitrided H13 at hot-extrusion temperatures)}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $L_b$.**

$$
L_b \geq \frac{P \cdot A_f}{\pi D_f \, [p_b]_{\text{allow}}}
$$

For round dies ($A_f = \pi D_f^2 / 4$):

$$
L_b \geq \frac{P \, D_f}{4 \, [p_b]_{\text{allow}}}
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering simplification.**
Using $[p_b]_{\text{allow}} = P/4$ (a common rule of thumb for steel extrusion dies):

$$
L_b \approx D_f
$$

Hence the widely quoted **"bearing length equals orifice diameter"** rule for steel extrusion dies, and **"half the orifice diameter"** rule for aluminium dies (because aluminium extrudes at much lower pressure).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Formula

| Problem Setup | Required Equation | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Frictionless estimate (theoretical lower bound) | $P_i = Y_f \ln R$ | Best-case scenario |
| Real direct extrusion with billet remaining | $P = Y_f(a + b \ln R)$ | Industry standard |
| Indirect extrusion | $P = Y_f b \ln R$ | No container friction |
| Die land design | $L_b = (0.5 \text{ to } 2) D_f$ | Empirical from wear rule |
| Bursting stress in die | $\sigma_{\text{burst}} = p_d D_i / t$ | Lamé / thick-cylinder |
| Extrudability rating | $K_{\text{ex}} = \ln R / (P/Y_f)$ | Compare metals |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write $P = Y_f \ln R$ for the *ram pressure* in a direct-extrusion problem. This is **wrong by 30–60 %**. Always use the Johnson form (with the friction term) whenever the problem states *direct* extrusion and a *finite billet length*. Reserve the ideal form only when the problem explicitly says *frictionless* or *indirect* extrusion.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> Mermaid diagrams below illustrate the *flow architecture* and *force balance* of an extrusion die assembly. They are block-level functional schematics rather than literal physical drawings (as per the engine's drawing-rendering safeguard protocol).

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Extrusion Die Assembly Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Billet in Container] --> B[Approach Cone]
    B --> C[Deformation Zone]
    C --> D[Die Land / Bearing]
    D --> E[Relief / Back-Taper Zone]
    E --> F[Extruded Product]

    C --> G[Dead Metal Zone]
    G --> C

    B --> H[Die Semi-Angle alpha]
    D --> I[Bearing Length Lb]

    A --> J[Container Wall Friction]
    J --> C

    F --> K[Product Cross-Section A_f]
    A --> L[Initial Cross-Section A_0]

    L --> M[Extrusion Ratio R = A0 / Af]
    M --> N[True Strain eps = ln R]
    N --> O[Ideal Pressure Pi = Yf ln R]

    O --> P[Add Redundant Work]
    J --> P
    P --> Q[Actual Ram Pressure P_ram]
    Q --> R[Ram Force F = P_ram x A0]
```

## 4.2 Force & Stress Flow Diagram — Slab Analysis of Conical Die

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SA[Slab Element] --> SB[Axial Stress sigma_z leading]
    SA --> SC[Axial Stress sigma_z + d_sigma_z trailing]
    SA --> SD[Shear Stress tau on Die Wall]
    SA --> SE[Radial Stress sigma_r on Die Wall]

    SD --> SF[Tresca Yield: sigma_z - sigma_r = Y_f]
    SE --> SF

    SF --> SG[Integrate across Cone]
    SG --> SH[Ideal Pressure Pi = Y_f ln R]

    SH --> SI[Add Redundant Work term]
    SI --> SJ[Add Container Friction term]
    SJ --> SK[Total Direct-Extrusion Pressure P = Yf a + b ln R]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Material to Extrude?] --> Q2{Steel / Titanium?}
    Q1 --> Q3{Aluminium / Copper?}
    Q1 --> Q4{Mg / Brittle Alloy?}

    Q2 --> A1[Die Angle 45-60 deg]
    Q2 --> A2[Bearing Lb approx Df]
    Q2 --> A3[Die Material H11/H13]

    Q3 --> B1[Die Angle 20-30 deg]
    Q3 --> B2[Bearing Lb approx 0.5 Df]
    Q3 --> B3[Die Material H13 nitrided]

    Q4 --> C1[Use Hydrostatic Extrusion]
    Q4 --> C2[Die Angle < 20 deg]

```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D1[Die in Service] --> D2{Stress State}
    D2 --> E1[High Bearing Pressure]
    D2 --> E2[High Bursting Stress]
    D2 --> E3[Thermal Cycling]

    E1 --> F1[Abrasive Wear]
    F1 --> F2[Oversized Product]
    F1 --> F3[Die Lines on Product]

    E2 --> G1[Die Cracks Radially]
    G1 --> G2[Catastrophic Die Failure]

    E3 --> H1[Heat Checking Network]

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions are modelled on actual KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Examination (ESE) patterns. Marks are split **Part A (3 marks)** and **Part B (14 marks)** with internal choice between Question A and Question B.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEIET524]`

**Define extrusion ratio. Explain its significance in die design for an aluminum extrusion process.**

**Model Answer:**

The **extrusion ratio** is defined as the ratio of the initial cross-sectional area of the billet to the final cross-sectional area of the extruded product:

$$
R = \frac{A_0}{A_f}
$$

It is a *dimensionless* number that quantifies the severity of plastic deformation. In die design, $R$ is significant because:
- It **directly controls the ram pressure** through the Johnson equation: $P = Y_f (a + b \ln R)$. A larger $R$ means higher pressure and a heavier press.
- It determines the **true strain** $\varepsilon = \ln R$, which sets the **exit temperature** and the **load on the die bearings**.
- It governs the **billet length that can be accommodated** in a given container (since total extruded length = $L_0 \times R$).
- For aluminum, $R$ is typically **10 to 100**; for steel, **4 to 30**; for magnesium, often less than **20** (to avoid cracking).

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEIET524]`

**Distinguish between direct and indirect extrusion. State one die-design advantage of indirect extrusion.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Direct (Forward) Extrusion | Indirect (Backward) Extrusion |
|---|---|---|
| Ram motion | Pushes billet through stationary die | Hollow ram pushes die back through billet |
| Container–billet friction | **Present** (substantial) | **Eliminated** |
| Ram pressure | Higher (Johnson full form) | Lower by 20–30 % |
| Billet length | Limited by friction | Almost unlimited |
| Defects | "Piping" defect at billet rear | Cleaner product, no piping |
| Product complexity | Simple profiles | Tubes, complex hollow sections |

**Die-design advantage of indirect extrusion:** Because the container friction term $(2L_0/D_0 R)$ vanishes, the **die land can be designed shorter** for a given wear life (since the ram pressure is lower, bearing pressure is correspondingly lower). This makes the die cheaper, easier to machine, and less prone to heat-checking. *(1 mark for distinction table, 1 mark for die-design link, 1 mark for precise wording.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Internal Choice)

### Question A. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3, 14 Marks]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**
*With neat sketches, describe the constructional features of a hot-extrusion die for steel rods. Explain the function of (i) the die land, (ii) the die backer ring, and (iii) the relief taper.*

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**
*A cylindrical billet of mild steel, 100 mm diameter and 200 mm long, is extruded at 1100 °C into a 25 mm diameter rod. The mean flow stress at this temperature is 100 MPa. Using Johnson's formula with $b = 0.8$, determine:*
- *(i) The extrusion ratio and the true strain.*
- *(ii) The ideal (frictionless) ram pressure.*
- *(iii) The actual ram pressure when the billet has reduced to 100 mm length (i.e. $L_0 = 100$ mm).*
- *(iv) The ram force in Newtons.*

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(a)

A hot-extrusion die for steel rods is a multi-component precision assembly. Its principal features are:

1. **Die Body (Working Insert)** — usually AISI H11 or H13 hot-work tool steel, through-hardened to 48–52 HRC, surface-nitrided to 60+ HRC surface hardness.
2. **Approach Cone** — conical entry of semi-angle $\alpha = 22.5°$ to $30°$ (half of the 45°–60° total cone angle), with a polished surface.
3. **Die Land / Bearing** — parallel-walled cylindrical land of length $L_b \approx D_f$ for steel (≈ 25 mm in this case). This is the *dimensional control* zone.
4. **Relief Taper / Back-off** — a 1°–3° back-taper immediately after the bearing, to prevent product scoring during exit.
5. **Die Backer / Shrink-Fit Ring(s)** — a series of forged-steel rings (typically 2–3) that are *shrink-fitted* around the working die. They support the working die against the **bursting stress** generated by the internal pressure.

**Functions:**
- (i) **Die Land:** provides the final dimensional accuracy, stabilizes the flow, and (via bearing friction) prevents the central core from outrunning the surface — which would cause *die-line* defects.
