- (ii) **Die Backer Ring:** carries the hoop (tensile) bursting stress in the working die. By being in compression (shrink-fit), it pre-stresses the die so the working tensile stress is reduced. Without backer rings, the die would crack radially within a few billets.
- (iii) **Relief Taper:** provides a clearance gap (1°–3°) so that the hot product does not drag on the die exit, preventing scratching and reducing the die-exit sticking tendency.

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(b)

**Given:**
- $D_0 = 100$ mm, $L_0^{\text{initial}} = 200$ mm
- $D_f = 25$ mm
- $Y_f = 100$ MPa
- $b = 0.8$
- $L_0^{\text{current}} = 100$ mm (after some extrusion)

**Step (i) — Extrusion ratio and true strain:**

$$
A_0 = \frac{\pi}{4} D_0^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (100)^2 = 7853.98 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$$
A_f = \frac{\pi}{4} D_f^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (25)^2 = 490.87 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$$
\boxed{R = \frac{A_0}{A_f} = \frac{7853.98}{490.87} = 16.0}
$$

$$
\boxed{\varepsilon = \ln R = \ln 16.0 = 2.773}
$$

**Step (ii) — Ideal (frictionless) ram pressure:**

$$
\boxed{P_i = Y_f \ln R = 100 \times 2.773 = 277.3 \text{ MPa}}
$$

**Step (iii) — Actual ram pressure (Johnson formula):**

Compute the friction–redundant factor $a$:

$$
a = \frac{2 L_0}{D_0 \cdot R} = \frac{2 \times 100}{100 \times 16.0} = \frac{200}{1600} = 0.125
$$

(Note: This is the *container friction term only*; the *die-zone redundant work* is embedded in the constant $b = 0.8$.)

Now apply Johnson:

$$
P = Y_f (a + b \ln R) = 100 \times (0.125 + 0.8 \times 2.773)
$$

$$
= 100 \times (0.125 + 2.218) = 100 \times 2.343
$$

$$
\boxed{P = 234.3 \text{ MPa}}
$$

**Step (iv) — Ram force:**

$$
F = P \times A_0 = 234.3 \text{ N/mm}^2 \times 7853.98 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$$
\boxed{F = 1.840 \times 10^6 \text{ N} = 1840 \text{ kN}}
$$

*(7 marks distribution: 2 marks for (i), 1 for (ii), 3 for (iii) including the $a$ term, 1 for (iv).)*

---

### Question B. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3, 14 Marks]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**
*Derive the Johnson formula for direct extrusion pressure. Clearly state the assumptions and the role of the parameters $a$ and $b$.*

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**
*For a hot-extrusion die of orifice diameter 30 mm, the peak ram pressure is 280 MPa. The allowable bearing pressure for the nitrided H13 die material is 200 MPa. Calculate the required bearing length. Comment on whether this length is feasible, and if not, suggest two die-design modifications.*

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(a)

*See the full derivation in Section 3.2 of these notes.* The examiner expects the following outline:

1. **Start with ideal (homogeneous) pressure** $P_i = Y_f \ln R$ — derived from slab method (1 mark).
2. **Add the redundant-work term** $P_r = Y_f (\Phi - 1) \ln R$, where $\Phi = b$ (0.8) is the redundant-work efficiency factor (2 marks).
3. **Add the container friction term** $P_f = Y_f \cdot \dfrac{2 L_0}{D_0 R}$ — derived from integrating the interface shear stress over the remaining billet length (3 marks).
4. **Assemble and simplify** to $P = Y_f (a + b \ln R)$ with $a = \dfrac{2 L_0}{D_0 R}$ (1 mark).

**Assumptions to state explicitly (1 mark):**
- Steady-state, axisymmetric deformation.
- Homogeneous material with constant $Y_f$.
- Sticking friction at the container wall (Tresca, $\tau_s = Y_f / \sqrt{3}$).
- Conical die with average efficiency factor $b = 0.8$.

**Role of parameters:**
- $a$ quantifies **container friction** — depends on remaining billet length $L_0$; *vanishes* in indirect extrusion and at the very start of the stroke.
- $b$ quantifies **redundant work** in the deformation cone — depends on die angle and lubrication.

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(b)

**Given:**
- $D_f = 30$ mm
- $P = 280$ MPa (peak ram pressure)
- $[p_b]_{\text{allow}} = 200$ MPa (allowable bearing pressure for nitrided H13)

**Step 1 — Compute the ram force on the die exit section.**

$$
A_f = \frac{\pi}{4} D_f^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (30)^2 = 706.86 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$$
F = P \cdot A_f = 280 \times 706.86 = 197,920 \text{ N} \approx 198 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the bearing-pressure constraint.**

The bearing pressure is the radial stress on the die land:

$$
p_b = \frac{F}{A_b} = \frac{F}{\pi D_f L_b} \leq [p_b]_{\text{allow}}
$$

Solving for $L_b$:

$$
L_b \geq \frac{F}{\pi D_f \, [p_b]_{\text{allow}}} = \frac{197,920}{\pi \times 30 \times 200}
$$

$$
L_b \geq \frac{197,920}{18,849.6} = 10.5 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\boxed{L_b \geq 10.5 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Step 3 — Feasibility check.**
The rule of thumb for steel dies is $L_b \approx D_f = 30$ mm. A required $L_b$ of only 10.5 mm is *shorter* than the rule of thumb — meaning the bearing pressure constraint is easily satisfied. **The design is feasible**, and in fact, the die is over-designed with respect to wear life (the bearings will last very long).

**Step 4 — Two die-design modifications (in case the design were *not* feasible):**

1. **Reduce the ram pressure** by changing to **indirect extrusion** (eliminates container friction, reducing $P$ by 20–30 %). This in turn reduces $F$ and the required $L_b$.
2. **Increase the bearing area** by using a **tapered (conical) bearing profile** of length $L_b$ that flares out toward the exit, thereby reducing the bearing pressure at the most critical (exit) zone.
3. **Use a higher-grade die material** with a higher $[p_b]_{\text{allow}}$ (e.g., powder-metallurgy H13 with PVD coating), which raises the allowable bearing pressure.
4. **Increase the orifice diameter** (if the product specification allows) — a larger $D_f$ spreads the force over a larger bearing area.

*(Any two of the above modifications are acceptable. 4 marks for numerical work, 2 marks for feasibility comment, 1 mark for two modifications.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Die-Design Questions:**
> 1. **Using $P = Y_f \ln R$ for direct extrusion** — this omits both redundant work and container friction. Lose **2–3 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert $L_0$** — many students use the *initial* billet length throughout. The friction term uses the *current* (remaining) length, which decreases as the ram advances. Lose **1 mark** if you do not mention this.
> 3. **Mixing units** — $Y_f$ in MPa with $D_0$ in cm. The KTU answer sheet has no mercy; lose **1 mark** for unit errors.
> 4. **Confusing *die land* with *bearing*** — they are the *same* feature, not two different features. Lose **1 mark** for terminology.
> 5. **Skipping the *sketch* in part (a) questions** — the word *"neat sketch"* in the question is worth **1–2 marks** all by itself. Always draw a labelled diagram.
> 6. **Not stating assumptions** in derivation questions. KTU's CO-aligned marking requires explicit identification of *steady-state*, *isothermal*, and *sticking-friction* assumptions. Lose **1 mark** per missing assumption.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Die Design for Extrusion Processes":**

- ☐ **Extrusion ratio** $R = A_0 / A_f$ and **true strain** $\varepsilon = \ln R$ are the *master variables* of every calculation.
- ☐ **Ideal pressure** $P_i = Y_f \ln R$ is the *theoretical minimum*; real direct extrusion always exceeds this.
- ☐ **Johnson's formula** $P = Y_f (a + b \ln R)$ is the *industry-standard* equation for direct extrusion; remember $b \approx 0.8$.
- ☐ **Container friction term** $a = 2 L_0 / (D_0 R)$ is *zero* for indirect and hydrostatic extrusion — this is the key reason for choosing them.
- ☐ **Die semi-angle** $\alpha$: $20°$–$30°$ for aluminum, $45°$–$60°$ for steel; small $\alpha$ → dead-metal zone, large $\alpha$ → redundant work minimum.
- ☐ **Die land / bearing** length $L_b \approx D_f$ (steel) or $\approx 0.5 D_f$ (aluminium); controls dimensional accuracy and product surface quality.
- ☐ **Bearing pressure** $p_b = F / (\pi D_f L_b)$ must be kept below the allowable value for the die material.
- ☐ **Die materials**: H11, H13, H21 tool steels; nitrided for surface hardness.
- ☐ **Bursting stress** in the die is resisted by **shrink-fit backer rings** — die design is *not* just the working insert.
- ☐ **Relief taper** of 1°–3° after the bearing prevents product scoring at the die exit.
- ☐ **Defects to recognize**: piping (direct extrusion), center-burst (improper die angle), die-lines (worn bearings), heat-checking (thermal fatigue).
- ☐ **Indirect extrusion** has 20–30 % lower ram pressure and produces *cleaner* products (no piping) — but is mechanically more complex.
- ☐ For KTU 14-mark questions, *always* state assumptions, draw a labelled diagram, and end with a numerical answer in SI units (N, MPa, mm).
- ☐ Memorize: ideal pressure formula, Johnson formula, bearing-length formula, bursting-stress formula — these are the *four* equations that appear in 90 % of KTU exam problems on this topic.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tooling for Tube and Wire Drawing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEIET524 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Tooling for Tube and Wire Drawing # TOOLING FOR TUBE AND WIRE DRAWING ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Wire Drawing > [!IMPORTANT] > **Wire Drawing** is a cold working metal forming process in which a wire (or rod) is pulled through a tapered die to reduce its cross-sectional area and increase its length, producing a product with improved surface finish, dimensional accuracy, and mechanical strength. The tooling primarily consists of the **die**, which is a hardened tool with a precisely machined tapered hole that performs the deformation. Wire drawing is essentially a **tension-dominated** process because the only driving force is the tensile pull applied at the exit side of the die. > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy — The Play-Dough Extruder** > Imagine squeezing play-dough through a plastic nozzle to make long, thin spaghetti strands. The dough is pushed at one end, but in wire drawing, the metal is *pulled* through the die from the other end. The die hole acts like a "stencil" that shapes the metal into the desired cross-section (round, square, hexagonal), and the metal is plastically compressed and elongated as it squeezes through. The stretching that occurs simultaneously hardens the metal, much like repeatedly bending a paperclip makes it harder to bend further. ### 1.2 Tube Drawing > [!IMPORTANT] > **Tube Drawing** is a cold working process used to reduce the diameter and wall thickness of a hollow cylindrical workpiece (billet or previously drawn tube) by pulling it through a die. Depending on whether the inside diameter is supported during deformation, it is classified into **Bearing (Plug/Mandrel) Drawing**, **Sinking (Without Internal Support)**, or **Rod Drawing**. The tooling system for tube drawing consists of a **die** plus an **internal mandrel, plug, or float** that controls the inner diameter, along with a **swaging bell** for end preparation. > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy — The Icing Piping Tip** > When you fit a star-shaped nozzle onto an icing bag and squeeze, the icing emerges with the shape of the nozzle. Now imagine an icing nozzle with a central pin sticking out from the middle — the icing that flows around the pin would come out as a hollow tube whose wall thickness is governed by the gap between the pin and the nozzle wall. This is exactly how **plug tube drawing** works. If the pin can slide freely (a *floating plug*), it self-centers, much like a ball bearing spinning in a groove. ### 1.3 Tooling Components — The Big Picture | Component | Function | Typical Material | |---|---|---| | **Die** | Forms the outer profile of the wire/tube | Tool Steel, Tungsten Carbide, Single-Crystal Diamond | | **Plug / Mandrel** | Controls the inner diameter of tubes | Tool Steel, Tungsten Carbide | | **Draw Bench / Bull Block** | Provides tensile pulling force | Hardened Steel Capstans | | **Pointing / Swaging Tool** | Reduces the leading end of the stock to insert through the die | Carbide / Steel Anvils | | **Lubricant Applicator** | Reduces friction and die wear | Soap, Oil, Phosphate Coatings | | **Coilers / Spoolers** | Wind the drawn product | — | ### 1.4 Stock Standards (KTU Reference Data) - **Wire diameter range:** **0.01 mm to 25.4 mm (1 inch)** - **Tube diameter range:** **3 mm to 400 mm OD** - **Drawing temperature:** **Cold working (room temperature)** for most metals; warm drawing occasionally used for refractory metals like tungsten and molybdenum - **Typical die approach angle (α):** **6° to 20°** for wires, **12° to 15°** for tubes - **Maximum reduction per pass:** **20 % to 45 %** depending on material ductility --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Die Geometry and Stress Zones A wire/tube drawing die consists of **four distinct functional zones** that are machined sequentially into a single block: 1. **Entry / Bell Zone (Back Relief):** A wide, tapered opening that guides the stock into the die and prevents scratching. Cone angle: typically **30°–60°** from the die axis. This region does *no* deformation. 2. **Approach (Reduction) Zone:** The conical tapering region where plastic deformation occurs. The half-angle is the **approach angle (α)**. The metal flows from initial diameter $D_i$ to final diameter $D_f$. 3. **Bearing (Land) Zone:** A short, parallel cylindrical section (length $L_b$) that provides the final dimensional accuracy and stabilizes the product. It is also the region of highest die wear. 4. **Exit / Back Relief (Exit Bell):** A slight taper (typically **2°–3°**) that prevents the drawn wire from rubbing against the die exit shoulder. > [!NOTE] > The **approach angle (α)** controls the *drawing stress* and *die life*. A small α gives a long deformation zone, high frictional work, and high drawing force, but a smooth surface. A large α gives lower force but risks central bursting (chevron cracking) because the redundant shear strain is excessive. ### 2.2 Die Materials — Selection Hierarchy | Die Material | Hardness (HV) | Max Service Temp | Typical Application | |---|---|---|---| | **Tool Steel (D2, H11, H13)** | 600–800 | 200 °C | Large diameter rods, occasional jobs | | **Cemented Tungsten Carbide (WC-Co)** | 1500–1800 | 800 °C | Medium-to-high volume wire & tube production | | **Polycrystalline Diamond (PCD)** | 6000–8000 | 700 °C | Fine wire (< 2 mm), non-ferrous metals | | **Single-Crystal Diamond (SCD)** | 8000–10000 | 600 °C | Ultra-fine wire, optical fibers, precious metals | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why diamond dies for fine wire?** > A die hole of, say, 0.1 mm diameter has an extremely small surface area. Any wear or distortion of the bearing zone ruins dimensional accuracy. Diamond's near-perfect hardness and isotropic single-crystal structure allow **die life** exceeding **10,000 km of wire** for copper, compared to a few hundred km for tungsten carbide. ### 2.3 Drawing Force — Ideal and Real The **ideal drawing stress** (no friction, no redundant work) for a perfectly plastic material is given by the **Sachs equation**: $$\sigma_d^{ideal} = Y_f \ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right)$$ where: - $Y_f$ = **average flow stress** (≈ yield stress after strain hardening) - $A_0$ = initial cross-sectional area - $A_f$ = final cross-sectional area The **real drawing stress** includes contributions from friction, redundant work (due to non-uniform deformation), and the back tension (if applied): $$\sigma_d^{real} = Y_f \left[ \ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right) + \frac{2L_b}{D_f}\mu \cdot \cot\alpha + \frac{2\alpha}{\sqrt{3}} \right] - q$$ where: - $L_b$ = bearing length - $D_f$ = final diameter - $\mu$ = coefficient of friction - $\alpha$ = approach (half) angle - $q$ = back-pull stress ### 2.4 Optimum Die Angle — The Hill (1945) Criterion The drawing force is **minimized** when the approach angle $\alpha^*$ satisfies: $$\alpha^* = \sqrt{\frac{3\mu \cdot \ln(A_0/A_f)}{1 + \mu \cot\alpha}}$$ A more practical KTU form is: $$\alpha^* \approx \sqrt{\frac{3 \cdot \mu \cdot \varepsilon}{2}}$$ where $\varepsilon = \ln(A_0/A_f)$ is the **true strain**. ### 2.5 Maximum Reduction per Pass The **maximum allowable reduction per pass** is governed by the **no-breakage condition**: the drawing stress must not exceed the **ultimate tensile strength (UTS)** of the work-hardened material at the exit of the die. The empirical rule is: $$r_{max} = \frac{UTS - Y_f}{UTS + Y_f} \times 100\%$$ For most annealed low-carbon steels, $r_{max} \approx 30\%$; for annealed copper, $r_{max} \approx 40\%$; for aluminum and its alloys, $r_{max} \approx 45\%$. ### 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Meaning / Use | |---|---|---| | 1 | $\sigma_d^{ideal} = Y_f \ln(A_0/A_f)$ | Minimum (frictionless) drawing stress | | 2 | $\sigma_d^{real} = Y_f [\ln(A_0/A_f) + 2L_b \mu \cot\alpha / D_f + 2\alpha/\sqrt{3}] - q$ | Actual drawing stress with friction, redundant work, back tension | | 3 | $\alpha^* = \sqrt{3\mu \cdot \ln(A_0/A_f)}$ | Optimum die angle minimizing drawing force | | 4 | $r_{max} = (UTS - Y_f)/(UTS + Y_f)$ | Maximum reduction per pass (no breakage) | | 5 | $\sigma_d \leq \sigma_{UTS}$ | Stability condition: wire must not fracture | | 6 | $P = \sigma_d \cdot A_f$ | Total drawing load (Newtons) | | 7 | $Y_{avg} = (Y_0 + Y_f)/2$ | Average flow stress for strain-hardening material | | 8 | $\varepsilon = \ln(A_0/A_f) = 2\ln(D_0/D_f)$ | True strain in wire drawing | | 9 | $\mu = \tan\beta$ (Eytelwein) | Coulomb friction in capstan (block-on-belt) | | 10 | $T_2 = T_1 \cdot e^{\mu\phi}$ | Euler-Eytelwein capstan equation | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility** > These equations are used to **select the correct die angle, the number of drawing passes, the capstan diameter for accumulation blocks, and the die material** in industries producing electrical wires, fasteners, orthodontic wires, hypodermic needles, and seamless tubes for hydraulic and aerospace applications. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Ideal Drawing Stress (Sachs Equation) We derive the **ideal drawing force** by considering an infinitesimal element of metal passing through the die, subjected to axial tension only (no friction, no redundant work). **Step 1 — Free Body Diagram Setup** Consider a small element of wire inside the die at axial position $x$, with instantaneous cross-sectional area $A(x)$. The element has length $dx$, and the diameter changes from $D$ to $D + dD$ across the element. **Step 2 — Force Equilibrium** Tension on the upstream face = $F$, and on the downstream face = $F + dF$. The die exerts a normal pressure $\sigma_n$ on the conical surface. $$F + dF - F = -\sigma_n \cdot \sin\alpha \cdot dA_{contact}$$ **Step 3 — Geometric Relations** The contact area between die and wire (circumferential) over length $dx$ is: $$dA_{contact} = \pi D \cdot \frac{dx}{\sin\alpha}$$ The change in cross-sectional area is: $$dA = \frac{\pi}{2} D \cdot dD \quad \text{and} \quad dD = 2 \tan\alpha \cdot dx$$ **Step 4 — Apply Yield Condition (Slab Method)** In the conical deformation zone, the radial compressive stress is balanced by axial tension. The Tresca yield criterion gives: $$\sigma_{axial} - \sigma_{radial} = Y_f \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma_{axial} = Y_f - \sigma_{radial}$$ For **simplified analysis** (assuming die pressure is small compared to $Y_f$, i.e., low friction): $$dF = Y_f \cdot dA = Y_f \cdot \frac{\pi}{2} D \cdot dD$$ **Step 5 — Integration** Substituting and integrating from $D_0$ to $D_f$: $$\int_{0}^{F_d} dF = \int_{D_0}^{D_f} Y_f \cdot \frac{\pi}{2} D \cdot dD$$ The left side gives $F_d$. The right side requires recognizing that $A = \pi D^2/4$, so $dA = (\pi/2) D \, dD$. $$\begin{aligned} F_d &= Y_f \int_{A_0}^{A_f} dA \\ F_d &= Y_f (A_0 - A_f) \quad \text{... (this is for rigid-plastic only)} \end{aligned}$$ But for **strain-hardening material**, we use the incremental plastic work method. Consider an element in the die where the area is $A$ and the flow stress is $Y_f$ (constant for small increment). The work done by the tensile force is $F \cdot dL$ where $dL$ is elongation. Using volume constancy $A \cdot L = A_0 \cdot L_0$: $$dL = -\frac{L_0 \cdot A_0}{A^2} dA = -\frac{L}{A} dA$$ Setting the work of plastic deformation equal to the integrated external work: $$F_d \cdot dL = Y_f \cdot A \cdot d\varepsilon$$ The **ideal drawing force per unit final area** is then: $$\boxed{\sigma_d^{ideal} = \frac{F_d}{A_f} = Y_f \ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right)}$$ **This is the Sachs Ideal Drawing Stress Formula.** ### 3.2 Effect of Friction — Modified Sachs Now include **Coulomb friction** $\mu$ acting on the conical die wall. The frictional shear stress is $\tau = \mu \cdot p$, where $p$ is the normal pressure. The total axial force balance becomes: $$dF = \left(Y_f \cdot dA + \mu \cdot p \cdot dA_{contact} \cdot \cos\alpha\right)$$ For small $\alpha$, and approximating $p \approx Y_f/2$: $$\sigma_d^{real} = Y_f \ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right) + \frac{\mu Y_f}{\sin\alpha} \cdot \frac{A_{contact}}{A_f}$$ Recognizing $A_{contact} \approx \pi D_f \cdot L_b$ and $A_f = \pi D_f^2/4$: $$\sigma_d^{real} = Y_f \ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right) + \frac{4\mu Y_f L_b}{D_f} \cdot \cot\alpha \cdot (\text{approx factor})$$ Final form (including redundant work $2\alpha/\sqrt{3}$): $$\sigma_d^{real} = Y_f \left[ \ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right) + \frac{2\mu L_b}{D_f}\cot\alpha + \frac{2\alpha}{\sqrt{3}} \right] - q$$ ### 3.3 Worked Example — KTU 2024 Pattern > **Problem [KTU University Exam - June 2023, adapted]:** > A copper wire of initial diameter $D_0 = 5.0$ mm is drawn through a die to a final diameter $D_f = 3.5$ mm. The average flow stress of the work-hardened copper is $Y_f = 280$ MPa. The die has an approach angle $\alpha = 8°$, bearing length $L_b = 2.0$ mm, and coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.05$. Calculate: > (a) The ideal drawing stress (no friction). > (b) The actual drawing stress with friction and redundant work. > (c) The total drawing load in kN. > (d) The percentage increase in drawing stress due to friction and redundant work. **Solution:** **Part (a) — Ideal Drawing Stress** True strain: $$\varepsilon = 2 \ln\left(\frac{D_0}{D_f}\right) = 2 \ln\left(\frac{5.0}{3.5}\right) = 2 \times 0.3567 = 0.7133$$ Ideal stress: $$\sigma_d^{ideal} = Y_f \cdot \ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right) = Y_f \cdot \varepsilon = 280 \times 0.7133 = 199.7 \text{ MPa}$$ **Part (b) — Real Drawing Stress** Friction term: $$\sigma_{friction} = Y_f \cdot \frac{2\mu L_b}{D_f} \cot\alpha = 280 \times \frac{2 \times 0.05 \times 2.0}{3.5} \times \cot(8°)$$ $$\sigma_{friction} = 280 \times 0.05714 \times 7.1154 = 280 \times 0.4066 = 113.85 \text{ MPa}$$ Redundant work term: $$\sigma_{red} = Y_f \cdot \frac{2\alpha}{\sqrt{3}} = 280 \times \frac{2 \times 8 \times \pi/180}{\sqrt{3}} = 280 \times \frac{0.2793}{1.732}$$ $$\sigma_{red} = 280 \times 0.1612 = 45.13 \text{ MPa}$$ Total real stress: $$\sigma_d^{real} = 199.7 + 113.85 + 45.13 = 358.7 \text{ MPa}$$ **Part (c) — Total Drawing Load** Final area: $$A_f = \frac{\pi}{4} D_f^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (3.5)^2 = 9.621 \text{ mm}^2$$ $$F_d = \sigma_d^{real} \cdot A_f = 358.7 \times 9.621 = 3451 \text{ N} = 3.45 \text{ kN}$$ **Part (d) — Percentage Increase** $$\%\text{ increase} = \frac{358.7 - 199.7}{199.7} \times 100\% = \frac{159.0}{199.7} \times 100\% = 79.6\%$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Note:** Students often forget to convert $\alpha$ from **degrees to radians** in the redundant work term $\frac{2\alpha}{\sqrt{3}}$. Always write $\alpha_{rad} = \alpha_{deg} \times \pi/180$ before substituting. Marks will be deducted if degrees are used directly. ### 3.4 Optimum Die Angle Calculation For the same problem, with friction coefficient $\mu = 0.05$ and strain $\varepsilon = 0.7133$: $$\alpha^* = \sqrt{3 \mu \varepsilon} = \sqrt{3 \times 0.05 \times 0.7133} = \sqrt{0.1070} = 0.3271 \text{ rad}$$ $$\alpha^* = 0.3271 \times \frac{180}{\pi} = 18.74°$$ So the **optimum die angle** is about **18.7°**, but in the given problem $\alpha = 8°$ is sub-optimal, which is why the friction term is so large. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Wire Drawing Die — Functional Architecture The following Mermaid diagram maps the **four stress zones** of a wire drawing die and the corresponding metallurgical phenomena occurring in each. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Wire Entry D0] --> B[Entry Bell Zone
30 to 60 deg back relief
Function: Guide stock, prevent scratching] B --> C[Approach Zone
alpha 6 to 20 deg half angle
Function: Plastic deformation] C --> D[Bearing Land Zone
Length Lb 0.1 to 0.5 Df
Function: Final sizing, accuracy] D --> E[Exit Bell Zone
2 to 3 deg back taper
Function: Prevent exit rubbing] E --> F[Drawn Wire Df] C --> C1[Phenomena: Redundant shear strain] C --> C2[Phenomena: Strain hardening] C --> C3[Phenomena: Hydrostatic pressure rise] D --> D1[Phenomena: Maximum die wear] D --> D2[Phenomena: Frictional heat generation] style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 style B fill:#e6f2ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 style C fill:#b3d9ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 style D fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#660000,color:#000 style E fill:#e6f2ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 style F fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 style C1 fill:#fff5cc,stroke:#806600,color:#000 style C2 fill:#fff5cc,stroke:#806600,color:#000 style C3 fill:#fff5cc,stroke:#806600,color:#000 style D1 fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#804000,color:#000 style D2 fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#804000,color:#000 ``` ### 4.2 Tube Drawing Process Variants — Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Tube Billet or Hollow Shell] --> B{Which Tube Drawing Method} B -->|Sinking| C[SINKING
No internal tool
OD reduced, wall may thicken
No internal accuracy] B -->|Fixed Plug| D[PLUG DRAWING
Plug fixed at die throat
OD and ID both controlled
Best surface inside] B -->|Floating Plug| E[FLOATING PLUG
Plug self-centers in die
Long mandrel not required
Used for long tubes] B -->|Mandrel| F[ROD OR MANDREL DRAWING
Long steel rod supports ID
Best for heavy reductions
Thin wall tubes] B -->|Drawing over fixed bar| G[BAR DRAWING
Tube is drawn over a solid bar
Brazed to bar at start
Used for very long thin tubes] C --> H[Drawn Tube] D --> H E --> H F --> H G --> H style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 style B fill:#ffffcc,stroke:#806600,color:#000 style C fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#660000,color:#000 style D fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,color:#000 style E fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,color:#000 style F fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,color:#000 style G fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,color:#000 style H fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 ``` ### 4.3 Multi-Die Wire Drawing Machine (Bull Block & Accumulator) ```mermaid flowchart LR R[Payoff Reel] --> P1[Pointing and Swaging] P1 --> D1[Die 1 alpha 12 deg] D1 --> C1[Capstan 1 Accumulator] C1 --> D2[Die 2 alpha 12 deg] D2 --> C2[Capstan 2 Accumulator] C2 --> D3[Die 3 alpha 10 deg] D3 --> C3[Capstan 3 Accumulator] C3 --> D4[Die 4 alpha 10 deg] D4 --> SP[Takeup Spool] C1 -.->|Stores wrap tension| T1[T1 gt T2 Euler capstan] C2 -.->|Stores wrap tension| T2[T2 gt T3 Euler capstan] C3 -.->|Stores wrap tension| T3[T3 gt T4 Euler capstan] style R fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 style P1 fill:#ffffcc,stroke:#806600,color:#000 style D1 fill:#b3d9ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 style D2 fill:#b3d9ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 style D3 fill:#b3d9ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 style D4 fill:#b3d9ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 style C1 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#660000,color:#000 style C2 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#660000,color:#000 style C3 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#660000,color:#000 style SP fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,color:#000 style T1 fill:#fff5cc,stroke:#806600,color:#000 style T2 fill:#fff5cc,stroke:#806600,color:#000 style T3 fill:#fff5cc,stroke:#806600,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > The **Euler-Eytelwein equation** $T_2 = T_1 e^{\mu\phi}$ governs the capstan wrap tension, where $\phi$ is the wrap angle. Accumulator capstans have multiple wraps to build up back tension to prevent wire slippage in the die. ### 4.4 Process Flow — Wire Production from Rod ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Hot Rolled Rod Coil
5 to 12 mm diameter] --> B[Descaling
Pickling H2SO4 or HCL] B --> C[Surface Coating
Phosphate or Borax] C --> D[Lubricant Application
Sodium or Calcium Soap] D --> E[Pointing and Swaging] E --> F[Bull Block Multi Die Drawing
4 to 12 dies in series] F --> G[Intermediate Annealing
If total reduction exceeds 60 to 80 percent] G --> H[Final Drawing Passes
Fine wire 0.1 to 2 mm] H --> I[In Line Annealing
Electrical resistance] I --> J[Takeup Spool] style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 style B fill:#e6f2ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 style C fill:#e6f2ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 style D fill:#ffffcc,stroke:#806600,color:#000 style E fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#804000,color:#000 style F fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#660000,color:#000 style G fill:#b3d9ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 style H fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#660000,color:#000 style I fill:#ffffcc,stroke:#806600,color:#000 style J fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,color:#000 ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** > *List the four functional zones of a wire drawing die and state the primary purpose of each zone.* **Model Answer:** | Zone | Primary Purpose | |---|---| | **1. Entry / Bell Zone** | Guides the stock into the die cone and prevents surface scratching. No deformation occurs here. | | **2. Approach (Reduction) Zone** | Performs the plastic deformation; the diameter is reduced from $D_0$ to $D_f$. | | **3. Bearing (Land) Zone** | Provides final dimensional accuracy and a stable bearing surface; the region of maximum die wear. | | **4. Exit / Back Relief Zone** | Prevents the drawn wire from rubbing against the die exit shoulder; provides clearance. | --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]** > *Distinguish between **sinking** and **plug drawing** of tubes, with one engineering application of each.* **Model Answer:** | Feature | Sinking | Plug Drawing | |---|---|---| | **Internal support** | None | A stationary plug is positioned at the die throat | | **Wall thickness change** | May *increase* (since ID is unconstrained) | *Decreases* predictably (gap is fixed) | | **Dimensional accuracy (ID)** | Poor | Excellent | | **Application** | Long-length welded tubes for furniture, automotive exhaust | Hydraulic / pneumatic cylinder tubes, hypodermic needles, condenser tubes | --- ### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice) > **Module Choice: Students answer EITHER Question A OR Question B. Each carries 7 + 7 = 14 marks.** --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **Q3. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]** > A cylindrical brass rod of initial diameter $D_0 = 8$ mm is drawn through a die to a final diameter $D_f = 6$ mm. The average flow stress is $Y_f = 350$ MPa. The die approach angle is $\alpha = 10°$, bearing length $L_b = 3$ mm, and coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.08$. > > **(a) [7 Marks]** Calculate the **ideal drawing stress**, the **actual drawing stress** (with friction and redundant work), and the **total drawing load** in kN. > > **(b) [7 Marks]** Determine the **optimum die angle** for minimum drawing force. Comment on whether the given die is over- or under-designed in terms of die angle, and state **two practical consequences** of operating at the optimum angle. **Model Solution — Part (a):** **Step 1 — Compute true strain:** $$\varepsilon = 2\ln(D_0/D_f) = 2\ln(8/6) = 2 \times 0.2877 = 0.5754$$ **Step 2 — Ideal drawing stress:** $$\sigma_d^{ideal} = Y_f \cdot \varepsilon = 350 \times 0.5754 = 201.4 \text{ MPa}$$ **Step 3 — Friction contribution:** $$\sigma_{fric} = Y_f \cdot \frac{2\mu L_b}{D_f} \cdot \cot\alpha = 350 \times \frac{2 \times 0.08 \times 3}{6} \times \cot(10°)$$ $$\sigma_{fric} = 350 \times 0.08 \times 5.671 = 350 \times 0.4537 = 158.8 \text{ MPa}$$ **Step 4 — Redundant work contribution:** Convert $\alpha$ to radians: $\alpha = 10° \times \pi/180 = 0.1745$ rad $$\sigma_{red} = Y_f \cdot \frac{2\alpha}{\sqrt{3}} = 350 \times \frac{2 \times 0.1745}{1.732} = 350 \times 0.2016 = 70.5 \text{ MPa}$$ **Step 5 — Real drawing stress:** $$\sigma_d^{real} = 201.4 + 158.8 + 70.5 = 430.7 \text{ MPa}$$ **Step 6 — Final area and drawing load:** $$A_f = \frac{\pi}{4}(6)^2 = 28.27 \text{ mm}^2$$ $$F_d = 430.7 \times 28.27 = 12180 \text{ N} = 12.18 \text{ kN}$$ **Model Solution — Part (b):** **Step 1 — Optimum die angle (Hill's criterion):** $$\alpha^* = \sqrt{3 \mu \varepsilon} = \sqrt{3 \times 0.08 \times 0.5754} = \sqrt{0.1381} = 0.3716 \text{ rad}$$ $$\alpha^* = 0.3716 \times \frac{180}{\pi} = 21.29°$$ **Step 2 — Comment:** Since the actual die angle $\alpha = 10°$ is **smaller** than the optimum $\alpha^* = 21.3°$, the die is **under-designed (angle too small)**. A small approach angle causes: - Excessively long die–workpiece contact → high frictional work → high drawing force. - Increased risk of die wear and surface scoring. **Step 3 — Practical consequences of operating at optimum angle (21.3°):** 1. **Lower drawing force** → reduced power consumption on the bull block; possibility of larger reduction per pass. 2. **Better die life** → because friction force and die–workpiece contact length are both lower. > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake to Avoid (KTU Valuation):** > In Part (b), students often compute the *optimum angle in degrees* directly using $\sqrt{3\mu\varepsilon}$ and forget that the formula is in **radians**. Always convert to degrees **after** the square root, not before. Marks lost in this conversion have been a recurring KTU issue. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **Q4. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Adapted]** > **(a) [7 Marks]** With the aid of a labelled schematic, explain the **four functional zones** of a wire drawing die. Discuss how the **approach angle (α)** affects the drawing stress, surface finish, and die life. > > **(b) [7 Marks]** A steel wire with initial diameter $D_0 = 4$ mm is drawn to a final diameter $D_f = 2.5$ mm in **two passes**, with an intermediate annealing restoring the original yield strength. The flow stress for the first pass averages $Y_{f1} = 500$ MPa, and for the second pass $Y_{f2} = 600$ MPa. Assuming the friction and redundant terms contribute 40 % of the ideal stress in each pass, calculate the **total drawing load** in N at the exit of each pass, and identify which pass is the **critical (highest load) pass**. **Model Solution — Part (a):** The four zones are: 1. **Entry Bell** (back relief, 30°–60°): guides stock, prevents scratching. 2. **Approach Cone** (α = 6°–20°): plastic deformation. 3. **Bearing Land** ($L_b$): final sizing, max die wear. 4. **Exit Bell** (2°–3°): prevents exit rubbing. (Refer to Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 for the schematic.) **Effect of approach angle α:** | α Small (≤ 8°) | α Large (≥ 15°) | α Optimum (≈ 18°) | |---|---|---| | Long die–workpiece contact → **higher frictional force** | Short contact → **lower friction** | Balance of friction and redundant work | | **Lower redundant shear strain** → less central burst risk | **Higher redundant shear** → risk of chevron cracks | Minimum drawing force | | **Smoother surface** (gradual deformation) | **Rougher surface** | Acceptable finish | | **Shorter die life** (more wear area) | **Longer die life** | Optimal life | Thus the **optimum α minimizes drawing stress** but is not always the smallest; selection balances force, surface, and die life. **Model Solution — Part (b):** **Pass 1: $D_0 = 4$ mm → $D_{f1} = ?$** Since two passes total, an optimum split gives each pass the same strain. We assume equal strain split: $$\varepsilon_{total} = 2\ln(4/2.5) = 2 \times 0.4700 = 0.9400$$ Per pass: $\varepsilon_1 = \varepsilon_2 = 0.4700$ Diameter after Pass 1: $$\ln(D_0/D_{f1}) = 0.4700/2 = 0.2350 \Rightarrow D_{f1} = 4 e^{-0.235} = 3.146 \text{ mm}$$ **Ideal stress, Pass 1:** $$\sigma_{ideal,1} = 500 \times 0.4700 = 235.0 \text{ MPa}$$ **Real stress, Pass 1 (with 40 % extra):** $$\sigma_{real,1} = 1.40 \times 235.0 = 329.0 \text{ MPa}$$ **Final area, Pass 1:** $$A_{f1} = \frac{\pi}{4}(3.146)^2 = 7.776 \text{ mm}^2$$ **Drawing load, Pass 1:** $$F_{d1} = 329.0 \times 7.776 = 2558 \text{ N}$$ --- **Pass 2: $D_{f1} = 3.146$ mm → $D_{f2} = 2.5$ mm** **Ideal stress, Pass 2:** $$\sigma_{ideal,2} = 600 \times 0.4700 = 282.0 \text{ MPa}$$ **Real stress, Pass 2:** $$\sigma_{real,2} = 1.40 \times 282.0 = 394.8 \text{ MPa}$$ **Final area, Pass 2:** $$A_{f2} = \frac{\pi}{4}(2.5)^2 = 4.909 \text{ mm}^2$$ **Drawing load, Pass 2:** $$F_{d2} = 394.8 \times 4.909 = 1938 \text{ N}$$ --- **Critical pass identification:** | Pass | Drawing Load (N) | Drawing Stress (MPa) | |---|---|---| | 1 | **2558** | 329.0 | | 2 | 1938 | 394.8 | The **drawing load is highest in Pass 1 (2558 N)**, so the **bull block must be rated for at least 2.6 kN pull capacity** at Pass 1. However, the **highest stress is in Pass 2 (394.8 MPa)**; this is the critical pass for **wire fracture risk**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students confuse *drawing load* with *drawing stress*. A smaller cross-section at Pass 2 gives a lower load even though stress is higher. Always check both quantities: load governs **machine capacity**; stress governs **wire breakage**. Marks are deducted for failing to distinguish. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision) > [!IMPORTANT] > **1. Die Geometry is Critical** > - Four zones: entry bell, approach, bearing, exit bell. > - **Approach angle α** controls friction, redundant work, and die life. > - **Optimum α** (Hill) minimizes drawing force: $\alpha^* = \sqrt{3\mu\varepsilon}$ (radians). > [!IMPORTANT] > **2. Drawing Force Formulas** > - **Ideal:** $\sigma_d^{ideal} = Y_f \ln(A_0/A_f)$ > - **Real:** Add friction term $\left(2\mu L_b/D_f\right)\cot\alpha$ and redundant work term $2\alpha/\sqrt{3}$. > - **Stability:** Drawing stress $\sigma_d$ **must remain < UTS** of the work-hardened material. > [!IMPORTANT] > **3. Die Material Selection** > - **Tool Steel** → large diameter rods, low volume. > - **Tungsten Carbide (WC-Co)** → standard for most wires/tubes. > - **Polycrystalline Diamond (PCD)** → non-ferrous fine wire. > - **Single-Crystal Diamond (SCD)** → ultra-fine wire, optical fibers. > [!IMPORTANT] > **4. Tube Drawing Variants** > - **Sinking** — no internal tool; wall may thicken. > - **Plug drawing** — fixed plug; both OD and ID controlled. > - **Floating plug** — self-centers; long mandrel not required. > - **Rod/mandrel drawing** — long bar inside tube; best for thin walls. > - **Bar drawing** — tube brazed onto bar; used for very long thin tubes. > [!IMPORTANT] > **5. Maximum Reduction per Pass** > - $r_{max} = (UTS - Y_f)/(UTS + Y_f)$ > - Typical: ~30 % steel, ~40 % copper, ~45 % aluminum. > - If total reduction > 60–80 %, **intermediate annealing** is required. > [!IMPORTANT] > **6. Common Defects in Drawn Products** > - **Chevron / central burst** — too large approach angle. > - **Surface scoring** — too small approach angle or poor lubrication. > - **Die lines** — worn bearing zone; die needs re-polishing. > - **Broken wire** — drawing stress exceeded UTS; reduce $r$ per pass. > - **Wall thickening (tube sinking)** — geometry, not a tool defect. > [!IMPORTANT] > **7. Multi-Die Bull Block Operation** > - Each successive die does ~20–25 % area reduction. > - **Capstan (accumulator)** between dies builds back-tension per $T_2 = T_1 e^{\mu\phi}$. > - Total reduction can be 80–90 % before intermediate annealing. > [!IMPORTANT] > **8. Lubrication Matters** > - Soap (sodium/calcium stearate) for ferrous wire. > - Phosphate + soap coating for heavy-duty steel wire. > - Mineral oil + graphite for tube drawing on a mandrel. > - Inadequate lubrication → scoring, high friction, die failure. > [!IMPORTANT] > **9. Numerical Pitfalls (Most Lost Marks)** > - Always use **radians** in $2\alpha/\sqrt{3}$ and $\sqrt{3\mu\varepsilon}$. > - Use **average flow stress** $Y_{avg} = (Y_0 + Y_f)/2$ if material hardens significantly. > - Distinguish **drawing load** (governs machine) from **drawing stress** (governs breakage). > - **Cross-sectional area** in mm² × **stress** in MPa = **load** in Newtons. > [!IMPORTANT] > **10. Industrial Applications to Quote in Answers** > - Wire drawing: electrical cables, fasteners, springs, orthodontic wires, tire bead wire, music strings. > - Tube drawing: hydraulic lines, hypodermic needles, heat exchanger tubes, boiler tubes, bicycle frames, refrigeration coils.

topics.title: Design of Dies and Punches for Blanking and Piercing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET524 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design of Dies and Punches for Blanking and Piercing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Blanking** is a shearing operation in which a **punch** is used to cut out a desired shape from a sheet metal (strip/sheet), and the cut-out piece (the **blank**) is the **useful product**. **Piercing** is a similar operation, but the punched-out slug is **scrap**, and the remaining strip is the useful product.

**Die** = A specialized tool with a matching cavity that holds the sheet stock and provides the **fixed cutting edge** (die opening) which works in conjunction with the punch to perform the shearing action.

**Punch** = The moving member attached to the press ram that carries the **upper cutting edge** and applies the shearing force to the strip placed over the die.

In the KTU 2024 scheme, the design of dies and punches for blanking/piercing involves the systematic calculation of **die clearances, cutting forces, strip layout, die opening dimensions, punch profile dimensions, shank, shank holder, die set, guide pins, strippers, and back-plates**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **biscuit cutter and rolling pin**.

- The **biscuit cutter (die)** is held firmly on the table (fixed).
- The **roller/punch** presses down from above and forces the dough through the cutter.
- The dough inside the cutter becomes a **biscuit (blank)** — useful product (Blanking).
- If you instead punch a hole into the dough sheet, the punched-out piece is **scrap (slug)**, and the remaining dough sheet is the **useful product (Piercing)**.

The **small gap between the cutter's inner wall and the roller's outer wall** is the **clearance (c)**. Too tight = the dough gets crushed and tears. Too loose = the biscuit edge is ragged and burred. This gap is the **single most critical parameter** in blanking/piercing die design.

## 1.3 Key Components of a Blanking/Piercing Tool

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Components (Must Memorize for KTU)**
> 1. **Punch** (upper moving tool) — does the shearing
> 2. **Die** (lower fixed tool) — provides the supporting cutting edge
> 3. **Stripper Plate** — strips the stock off the punch on the return stroke
> 4. **Punch Holder / Punch Plate** — holds the punch shank to the press ram
> 5. **Backing Plate / Backing Plate for Punch** — supports the punch
> 6. **Shank** — connects the upper assembly to the press ram
> 7. **Die Block (Die Plate)** — holds the die opening
> 8. **Die Shoe** — supports the die block
> 9. **Guide Pins & Bushes** — ensures alignment between punch and die
> 10. **Pilot Pin** — locates the strip using the previously pierced hole
> 11. **Stop Pin / Locator** — positions the strip in correct pitch
> 12. **Bolster / Bolster Plate** — additional support beneath die

## 1.4 Single-Cut vs Progressive Die Terminology

> [!NOTE]
> - **Single Stage / Single Cut Die**: One operation per press stroke.
> - **Progressive Die (Compound Die)**: Two or more stations; the strip advances through each station per stroke. The pilot pin in the second station locates the strip using the hole pierced in the first station.
> - **Compound Die**: Two or more cutting operations (e.g., blank + pierce) performed simultaneously in a single press stroke.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Shearing Phenomenon

When the punch descends and the punch tip pressure exceeds the shear strength of the stock, the material does **not** shear in one clean line. The shearing actually proceeds in **three distinct zones** (this is the foundation of why we need clearance):

| Zone | Description |
|---|---|
| **Zone I – Plastic Deformation** | Punch tip indents the material; plastic flow begins (≈ 5–8 % of stroke) |
| **Zone II – Penetration / Fracture Initiation** | Punch penetrates roughly ½ the stock thickness, micro-cracks initiate at both edges |
| **Zone III – Fracture / Separation** | The two micro-cracks meet and join, completing the cut |

The **clearance (c)** is the small perpendicular gap per side between the punch and die. Without proper clearance, the cracks cannot meet cleanly → **secondary shear, excessive burr, doubled cut edge, and rapid punch-die wear**.

## 2.2 Classification of Clearance

| Clearance Type | Value (Per Side) | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Zero Clearance** | c = 0 | Not used industrially; used only for fine foil work |
| **Close / Low Clearance** | 2 – 4 % of t | Hard / brittle material, thin gauge, precise finish |
| **Normal / Standard Clearance** | **4 – 6 % of t** (most KTU problems use 5 %) | Mild steel, soft brass, general engineering |
| **Large / Wide Clearance** | 6 – 10 % of t | Ductile material (aluminium, copper) |
| **Extra Large Clearance** | > 10 % of t | Nibbling type operations |

Where **t** = stock thickness in mm.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Convention (Pinning this for the exam):**
> In numerical problems, KTU expects the **total diametrical clearance** (also called the **die-punch gap per piece** = 2c) to be computed and then applied as:
> - **For Blanking:** Die Opening = Punch Size + 2c ; Punch = Required Blank Size
> - **For Piercing:** Punch Size = Die Opening − 2c ; Die = Required Hole Size

## 2.3 The Centre of Pressure (CP) — Strip Layout

The **Centre of Pressure (CP)** is the point on the press ram face where the **resultant cutting force** is considered to act. For a die to perform without tilting, the CP of all operations in a single stage must coincide with the **shank centre**. Otherwise, the press ram experiences an unbalanced side load, accelerating wear.

**Assumption (Shigley's/Kalpakjian standard used in KTU textbook):** Treat each segment of the cut outline as a **concentrated load** acting at the **midpoint of that segment**, with magnitude proportional to the **length of the cut**.

If there are **n** segments of cut lengths $\ell_1, \ell_2, \ldots, \ell_n$ with segment midpoints at $(x_1, y_1), (x_2, y_2), \ldots, (x_n, y_n)$:

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{\sum \ell_i \cdot x_i}{\sum \ell_i}
$$

$$
\bar{y} = \frac{\sum \ell_i \cdot y_i}{\sum \ell_i}
$$

These $(\bar{x}, \bar{y})$ coordinates are the **Centre of Pressure** of the die layout.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total Clearance (per side, c) | $c = K \cdot t$ | mm | $K = 0.04$ to $0.10$ (use **0.05** for mild steel) |
| 2 | Total Diametrical Clearance | $C = 2c = 2Kt$ | mm | 2 × single-side clearance |
| 3 | Cutting Force (Flat Edge Punch) | $F = 0.40 \cdot t \cdot \text{UTS} \cdot L$ | N | 0.40 = practical shear factor (not 1.0) |
| 4 | Shear Strength (approx.) | $\tau = 0.8 \cdot \text{UTS}$ | N/mm² | Empirical |
| 5 | Stripping Force | $F_s = 0.05 \cdot \text{UTS} \cdot t \cdot L$ | N | 5 % of cut force rule |
| 6 | Stock Strip Width (rectangle) | $w = D + 2F$ | mm | $F$ = side allowance (usually 1.5–2.5 × t) |
| 7 | Pitch (distance between centres of two adjacent parts) | $p = D + s$ | mm | $s$ = side gap (1.0 – 1.5 × t) |
| 8 | Feed / Strip Advance | depends on layout | mm | Equals pitch for single row |
| 9 | Centre of Pressure (x) | $\bar{x} = \frac{\sum \ell_i x_i}{\sum \ell_i}$ | mm | |
| 10 | Centre of Pressure (y) | $\bar{y} = \frac{\sum \ell_i y_i}{\sum \ell_i}$ | mm | |
| 11 | Press Tonnage Selection | $P_{press} = F \cdot n_s$ | N | $n_s$ = safety factor (1.3 – 1.5) |
| 12 | Stock length per blank (rectangle) | $\ell_{stock} = D + a$ | mm | $a$ = edge-trim allowance |
| 13 | Blank Area (rectangle L × B) | $A = L \cdot B$ | mm² | For force calculations |
| 14 | Perimeter of cut (rectangle) | $L = 2(L+B)$ | mm | Used in cutting force |
| 15 | Punch Land Height | $h_{land} = (1.0 \text{ to } 2.0)\,t$ | mm | The narrow relief below cutting edge |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Convention:** When clearance is asked, write it as **"per side" = c** and **"total / diametrical" = 2c**. Examiners deduct marks when this distinction is missing.

## 2.5 The Two Clearance Rules (Mental Anchor)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule 1 — Blanking:** The **punch is the master**. Punch size = the **exact required blank size**. The die is made **bigger** by 2c.
>
> **Rule 2 — Piercing:** The **die is the master**. Die opening = the **exact required hole size**. The punch is made **smaller** by 2c.
>
> **Why?** In blanking, you want a clean blank of known dimension → size the punch exactly. In piercing, the hole is what matters → size the die exactly.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility

This formula set is the backbone of the **tool room, stamping shop, automotive sheet metal industry, electrical stamping of laminations, washing machine panel production, and PCB punching**. Every blanking die from a paper clip to a car body panel uses these same five formulas with adjusted constants.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Cutting Force Formula

### Statement

Derive the expression $F = 0.40 \cdot t \cdot \text{UTS} \cdot L$ for the maximum shearing force required to blank/pierce a perimeter of length $L$ through a stock of thickness $t$ with ultimate tensile strength UTS.

### Step 1 — Identify the Shear Plane

When the punch begins to cut, the material resists shear along a **cylindrical (or prismatic) surface** whose area equals the perimeter of the cut multiplied by the stock thickness. The shear plane area $A_s$ is:

$$
A_s = L \cdot t
$$

where $L$ is the total perimeter of the cut profile (mm) and $t$ is the stock thickness (mm).

### Step 2 — Estimate Shear Strength from UTS

Empirically, for most sheet metals, the ultimate shear strength is approximately **80 % of the ultimate tensile strength** (UTS). This arises because the Tresca yield criterion gives $\tau_{max} = 0.5 \cdot \sigma_y$, but for true shearing-to-fracture the empirical factor is closer to **0.8 × UTS**:

$$
\tau_u = 0.8 \cdot \text{UTS}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply Force = Stress × Area

The theoretical shear force would then be:

$$
F_{ideal} = \tau_u \cdot A_s = 0.8 \cdot \text{UTS} \cdot L \cdot t
$$

### Step 4 — Apply Practical Correction Factor

In practice, the cut is **not clean shear**. The punch must also overcome:
- Friction between punch and stock (≈ 5–10 %)
- Plastic bending and extrusion through the die
- Local work-hardening

This reduces the **effective** shear strength that the press must deliver. The **empirical factor that the KTU textbook uses is 0.40 × UTS** (which corresponds to ½ of the empirical 0.8 shear strength, accounting for the practical efficiency of shearing with clearance):

$$
F = 0.40 \cdot \text{UTS} \cdot L \cdot t
$$

### Step 5 — Final Dimensional Check

- UTS in N/mm²
- $L$ in mm
- $t$ in mm
- Therefore $F$ in $\dfrac{\text{N}}{\text{mm}^2} \cdot \text{mm} \cdot \text{mm} = \text{N}$ ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why 0.40 and not 0.8?** KTU follows the textbook convention where the **practical (lower) factor** is used because: (a) the punch is **not perfectly sharp**, (b) **friction** is at play, and (c) the material **strain-hardens** during the cut. The factor 0.40 gives realistic press tonnage numbers consistent with industrial practice. **Use 0.40 unless the problem explicitly states "ideal shear".**

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Worked Example for a Rectangular Blank

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup (Standard KTU Type):**
> Blank a rectangular washer of outer dimensions **60 mm × 40 mm** with a central hole of **20 mm diameter** from a **2 mm thick mild steel strip**. Given: UTS of stock = **400 N/mm²**. Take clearance factor K = 5 %, and edge-trim/side allowance F = 1.5 t. Find:
> (i) Die and punch dimensions for both blanking and piercing
> (ii) Cutting force for each operation
> (iii) Strip width and pitch
> (iv) Centre of pressure

### Step 1 — Clearance Computation

Single-side clearance:

$$
c = K \cdot t = 0.05 \times 2 = 0.10 \text{ mm}
$$

Total diametrical clearance:

$$
C = 2c = 0.20 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 2 — Blanking Tool Sizes (Punch = Master)

For the outer rectangular blank (60 × 40 mm):

$$
\text{Punch}_L = 60 \text{ mm}, \quad \text{Punch}_W = 40 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\text{Die}_L = 60 + 0.20 = 60.20 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\text{Die}_W = 40 + 0.20 = 40.20 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 3 — Piercing Tool Sizes (Die = Master)

For the central hole (20 mm diameter):

$$
\text{Die}_{\text{hole}} = 20 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\text{Punch}_{\text{hole}} = 20 - 0.20 = 19.80 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 4 — Cutting Force (Blanking)

Perimeter of rectangular outer cut:

$$
L_{blank} = 2(60 + 40) = 200 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
F_{blank} = 0.40 \times 400 \times 200 \times 2 = 64\,000 \text{ N} = 64 \text{ kN}
$$

### Step 5 — Cutting Force (Piercing)

Perimeter of circular hole:

$$
L_{pierce} = \pi \cdot 20 = 62.832 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
F_{pierce} = 0.40 \times 400 \times 62.832 \times 2 = 20\,106 \text{ N} \approx 20.1 \text{ kN}
$$

### Step 6 — Strip Width and Pitch

Side allowance F (given as 1.5 t):

$$
F = 1.5 \times 2 = 3.0 \text{ mm}
$$

Strip width:

$$
w = 60 + 2(3.0) = 66 \text{ mm}
$$

Side gap s (use 1.5 t as standard):

$$
s = 1.5 \times 2 = 3.0 \text{ mm}
$$

Pitch:

$$
p = 60 + 3.0 = 63 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 7 — Stripping Force (Bonus Quantity Often Asked)

$$
F_s = 0.05 \times 400 \times 200 \times 2 = 8000 \text{ N} = 8 \text{ kN}
$$

### Step 8 — Centre of Pressure (One Simple Approach)

By symmetry (rectangle is centred, piercing hole is centred), the **CP lies at the geometric centre of the rectangle** = (30, 20) measured from one corner. **In KTU, always state this symmetry argument first** before doing the long summation.

### Step 9 — Required Press Tonnage

With safety factor $n_s = 1.3$:

$$
P_{press} = 1.3 \times (64 + 20.1) = 109.33 \text{ kN} \approx 11 \text{ tonnes}
$$

(1 tonne ≈ 10 kN; in exam you may write the value in kN directly.)

## 3.3 Punch Profile Design Rules

| Parameter | Typical Range | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **Punch Land (relief below cutting edge)** | (1.0 to 2.0) × t | Provides support to cutting edge, prevents chipping |
| **Punch Taper / Back Relief** | 0.5° to 1° per side | Reduces friction during stock stripping |
| **Punch Shank Diameter** | Standard ISO fit (h6) | To fit punch holder |
| **Punch Head Height** | 8 – 12 mm for light duty, more for heavy | For rigidity |
| **Punch Material** | Tool steel (AISI D2/SKD11) or HSS | Wear resistance |

## 3.4 Die Profile Design Rules

| Parameter | Typical Range | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **Die Land (relief below cutting edge)** | (1.5 to 3.0) × t | Must be **greater than** punch land to prevent binding |
| **Die Taper (Draw Angle)** | 0.5° to 1.5° per side | Allows slug/blank to fall through freely |
| **Die Plate Thickness** | ≥ 3 × (max cut length) and ≥ 20 mm | For rigidity |
| **Die Material** | Tool steel (AISI D2, H11, H13) | Wear + impact resistance |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Single-Stage Blanking Die

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Press Ram - Downward Motion] --> B[Shank Connects Ram to Upper Shoe]
    B --> C[Punch Holder Plate]
    C --> D[Backing Plate - Prevents Punch Punch Through]
    D --> E[Punch - The Cutting Member]
    F[Strip / Sheet Stock] --> G[Locates on Die using Pilot Pin or Stop Pin]
    E --> H[Shears Material through Die Opening]
    H --> I[Blank Drops into Die Cavity - Useful Product]
    H --> J[Slug / Scrap Falls Through - If Piercing]
    K[Stripper Plate - Presses Strip Off Punch on Return Stroke] --> E
    L[Die Block - Holds the Die Opening Profile] --> H
    M[Die Shoe - Bolster Supporting Die Block] --> L
    N[Guide Pins and Bushes - Ensures Alignment] -.-> E
    N -.-> L
    O[Bolster Plate - Additional Lower Support] --> M
```

## 4.2 Progressive Die Architecture — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Station 1 - Pierce Pilot Hole] --> S2[Station 2 - Pierce Internal Holes]
    S2 --> S3[Station 3 - Partial Blank Outline]
    S3 --> S4[Station 4 - Final Blank Separation]
    S4 --> SK[Skeleton - Scrap Carrier]
    subgraph ALIGN[Alignment and Location]
        P1[Pilot Pin at Station 2] -.->|Locates strip using Station 1 hole| S2
        P2[Pilot Pin at Station 3] -.->|Locates strip using Station 2 hole| S3
    end
```

## 4.3 Cross-Sectional Block View of the Shearing Action

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Press Ram] --> P1[Shank]
    P1 --> P2[Punch Holder]
    P2 --> P3[Backing Plate]
    P3 --> P4[Punch - Sharp Edge]
    STK[Stock Strip - Thickness t] --> P4
    P4 --> D1[Die Block - Sharp Edge with Clearance c Per Side]
    D1 --> D2[Slug or Blank - Falls Through Die]
    SP[Stripper Plate - Fixed to Lower Shoe via Springs] --> P4
    P4 -->|After Cut| CUT[Stock Strip Lifted Off by Stripper on Return]
```

## 4.4 The Three Zones of Shear — Conceptual Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Z1[Zone 1 - Elastic and Plastic Indentation - About 5 to 8 Percent of Stroke] --> Z2[Zone 2 - Penetration and Microcrack Initiation]
    Z2 --> Z3[Zone 3 - Fracture and Separation]
    Z3 --> ED[Final Cut Edge with Rollover, Burnish, and Burr Zones]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reading Tip:** In the KTU board exam, if a question asks *"Explain the shearing phenomenon"*, the answer is a direct re-statement of the three zones above (Zone I → Zone II → Zone III). The examiner allocates ~3–4 marks for this section.

## 4.5 Strip Layout — Block Diagram of Pitch, Side Gap, and Edge Trim

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E1[Edge Trim Allowance a] --> A[Part Width D]
    A --> SG[Side Gap s]
    SG --> A2[Next Part Width D]
    A2 --> SG2[Side Gap s]
    E1 --> |Strip Width w| A
    A2 --> |Pitch p Equals D plus s| A2
    PITCH[Pitch p = D + s] --- SG
    WIDTH[Strip Width w = D + 2F] --- E1
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "clearance" as applied to blanking and piercing dies. State its typical value as a percentage of stock thickness for mild steel.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Clearance is the small intentional gap per side between the punch and the die opening, which allows the fractured surfaces of the stock to meet cleanly. For mild steel, the standard single-side clearance is **4 % to 6 % of stock thickness**, with the most commonly used value being **5 % of t** in KTU textbook problems. Total diametrical clearance is therefore $2c = 10\%$ of $t$.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
*State and justify the rule: "In blanking, the punch is the master."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
In blanking, the **cut-out piece is the useful product (blank)**, and its size must be controlled accurately. Therefore, the **punch is sized to the exact required blank dimension**, and the die is made **larger by the total diametrical clearance 2c** to allow the blank to drop through freely. Because the punch determines the blank's size, it is called the *master*. In piercing, this rule reverses: the die is the master, because the hole dimension is the critical size.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 | Model Paper – Module 3]**
*(a)* A washer is to be blanked and pierced from a **2 mm thick** mild steel strip (UTS = 410 N/mm²). The **outer blank diameter is 50 mm** and the **inner hole diameter is 25 mm**. Take clearance factor K = 0.05 and side allowance F = 1.5 t.
*Determine:*
(i) Punch and die sizes for the blanking operation (3 marks)
(ii) Punch and die sizes for the piercing operation (3 marks)
(iii) Cutting force for each operation, with safety factor 1.3 for press selection (4 marks)

*(b)* Describe the **function of a pilot pin** and **stripper plate** in a progressive die. Why is the centre of pressure important? (4 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Given:** t = 2 mm, UTS = 410 N/mm², K = 0.05, F = 1.5 t, Safety factor $n_s = 1.3$, $D_{outer} = 50$ mm, $D_{inner} = 25$ mm.

**Step 1 — Clearance**

$$
c = K \cdot t = 0.05 \times 2 = 0.10 \text{ mm per side}
$$

$$
2c = 0.20 \text{ mm total}
$$

**Step 2 (i) — Blanking Tool Sizes (Punch = Master)**

$$
\text{Punch diameter} = 50.00 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\text{Die opening diameter} = 50.00 + 0.20 = 50.20 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 (ii) — Piercing Tool Sizes (Die = Master)**

$$
\text{Die opening diameter} = 25.00 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\text{Punch diameter} = 25.00 - 0.20 = 24.80 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 (iii) — Cutting Force (Blanking)**

Perimeter:

$$
L_{blank} = \pi \cdot D_{outer} = \pi \times 50 = 157.08 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
F_{blank} = 0.40 \times 410 \times 157.08 \times 2 = 51\,522 \text{ N} \approx 51.52 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 5 — Cutting Force (Piercing)**

$$
L_{pierce} = \pi \times 25 = 78.54 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
F_{pierce} = 0.40 \times 410 \times 78.54 \times 2 = 25\,761 \text{ N} \approx 25.76 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 6 — Press Tonnage (with safety factor)**

$$
P_{press} = n_s \times (F_{blank} + F_{pierce}) = 1.3 \times (51.52 + 25.76) = 100.46 \text{ kN}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{press} \approx 100.5 \text{ kN} \approx 10.05 \text{ tonnes}}
$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (4 marks)

- **Pilot Pin:** A small hardened pin that enters the previously pierced hole in the strip to **accurately locate** the strip before the next operation. It **eliminates the cumulative pitch error** in a progressive die. (2 marks)
- **Stripper Plate:** A plate held by springs that **presses the stock off the punch** on the return stroke. It prevents the stock from lifting with the punch. (1 mark)
- **Centre of Pressure:** The point on the die face where the **resultant cutting force** is considered to act. It should be **aligned with the shank centre** to avoid side loads on the press, which cause premature wear, tilting, and dimensional inaccuracy. (1 mark)

---

### Question B — Option 2 (Alternative Choice for Internal Selection)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2022 | University Question Paper]**
*(a)* A circular disc of **diameter 75 mm** is to be blanked from a **1.6 mm thick** aluminium strip (UTS = 180 N/mm²). For the blanking operation, find:
(i) Punch and die diameters assuming a clearance of 6 % of stock thickness per side (4 marks)
(ii) Cutting force and press tonnage with safety factor of 1.25 (3 marks)

*(b)* With neat sketches, explain the **three zones of shearing** during a blanking operation. (4 marks)
Also explain the difference between **progressive die and compound die** with one example each. (3 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Given:** $D = 75$ mm, $t = 1.6$ mm, UTS = 180 N/mm², K = 0.06, $n_s = 1.25$.

**Step 1 — Clearance**

$$
c = 0.06 \times 1.6 = 0.096 \text{ mm per side} \approx 0.10 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
2c = 0.192 \text{ mm} \approx 0.20 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 (i) — Blanking Tool Sizes (Punch = Master)**

$$
\text{Punch diameter} = 75.00 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\text{Die opening diameter} = 75.00 + 0.20 = 75.20 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 (ii) — Cutting Force**

Perimeter:

$$
L = \pi \times 75 = 235.62 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
F = 0.40 \times 180 \times 235.62 \times 1.6 = 27\,143 \text{ N} \approx 27.14 \text{ kN}
$$

Press tonnage:

$$
P_{press} = 1.25 \times 27.14 = 33.93 \text{ kN} \approx 3.4 \text{ tonnes}
$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Three Zones of Shearing (4 marks):**
1. **Zone I — Plastic Deformation:** Punch indents the stock; localized plastic flow occurs; punch penetrates about 5–8 % of the stroke.
2. **Zone II — Penetration and Fracture Initiation:** Punch penetrates roughly half the stock thickness; micro-cracks initiate at both the punch edge and die edge.
3. **Zone III — Fracture and Separation:** The two cracks meet and join, completing the cut; a small rollover, burnish, and burr region is produced on the cut edge.

**Progressive Die vs Compound Die (3 marks):**
- **Progressive Die:** Multiple stations; the strip advances from station to station with a **pilot pin** locating it; each station performs a different operation (e.g., pierce in station 1, blank in station 2). Example: Washing machine panel production.
- **Compound Die:** Two or more operations performed **simultaneously in the same stroke** at the same station (e.g., blanking and piercing a washer in one stroke). Example: Coin blanking.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Reasons for Losing Marks (compiled from KTU board examiner reports)**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor 0.40** and using 0.8 or 1.0 — leads to a force value 2× to 2.5× higher → wrong tonnage → 2 marks lost.
> 2. **Confusing "per side" and "total" clearance** — KTU problem statements may say "5 % clearance"; students must write $c = 0.05t$ per side and $2c = 0.10t$ total. Writing only one leads to a half-mark deduction.
> 3. **Inverting the master rule** — writing die = master for blanking. This is the most common error. **Mnemonic: B-P (Blank-Punch), P-D (Pierce-Die).**
> 4. **Missing safety factor** in press tonnage. Always multiply by 1.3 (or as given).
> 5. **Forgetting to mention Centre of Pressure** when the question asks for "die design" in a multi-operation layout. Examiners allocate 2 marks specifically for CP.
> 6. **Not stating the stock material assumption** when UTS is not given — assume mild steel, UTS = 400 N/mm² unless specified.
> 7. **Writing strip width as w = D + 2s** (using side gap) instead of w = D + 2F (using **side allowance F**). F > s because F includes edge trim allowance.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> ### 🔑 Single-Glance Revision Block
>
> **Definitions (must-memorize):**
> - Blanking → blank is the **product**; Punch is the **master**.
> - Piercing → slug is the **scrap**; Die is the **master**.
> - Clearance c = K·t (K = 5 % for mild steel, K = 6–10 % for ductile materials).
>
> **Core Formulas (memorize exactly):**
> - $F = 0.40 \cdot \text{UTS} \cdot L \cdot t$
> - $c = K \cdot t$ ; $2c$ = total diametrical clearance
> - Strip width $w = D + 2F$ where F = 1.5–2.5 × t
> - Pitch $p = D + s$ where s = 1.0–1.5 × t
> - $F_s = 0.05 \cdot \text{UTS} \cdot L \cdot t$ (stripping force)
> - $P_{press} = n_s \cdot F_{total}$, $n_s = 1.3$ to $1.5$
> - CP: $\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum \ell_i x_i}{\sum \ell_i}$, $\bar{y} = \dfrac{\sum \ell_i y_i}{\sum \ell_i}$
>
> **The Two Golden Rules (impossible to forget):**
> - **B-P** = **B**lank → **P**unch is master
> - **P-D** = **P**ierce → **D**ie is master
>
> **Design Sequence (always follow this order in numericals):**
> 1. Compute clearance c and 2c.
> 2. Decide master → apply 2c to the other member.
> 3. Compute perimeter L.
> 4. Compute cutting force F.
> 5. Compute stripping force $F_s$.
> 6. Apply safety factor → press tonnage.
> 7. Layout strip → compute pitch and strip width.
> 8. Check Centre of Pressure alignment.
>
> **Critical Numbers to Anchor in Memory:**
> - **0.40** → practical shear force factor (not 0.8, not 1.0)
> - **5 %** → standard clearance for mild steel
> - **1.5 t** → side gap & side allowance default
> - **1.3** → press safety factor default
> - **0.05** → stripping-to-cutting ratio
>
> **Real-World Application Anchors (use these for viva + situational questions):**
> - Automotive panels → progressive dies
> - Electrical laminations → fine blanking with low clearance
> - Washers (the standard textbook example) → compound die
> - PCB hole punching → piercing with high-precision die
>
> **Trap Question Pattern (KTU favourite):** *"If the punch diameter is 50 mm and clearance is 0.10 mm per side, what is the die opening diameter?"* — Answer: **50.20 mm** (if blanking) or **50.00 mm** (if piercing — then punch would be 49.80 mm).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Deep Drawing Dies and Tooling for Stamping Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET524 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design of Deep Drawing Dies and Tooling for Stamping Operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Deep Drawing — Formal KTU 2024 Definition

**Deep Drawing** is a sheet metal forming process in which a flat metal blank is plastically deformed into a die cavity by means of a punch to produce a hollow, cup-shaped, box-shaped, or contoured part of substantial depth without intentionally changing the sheet thickness. The process relies on controlled radial metal flow into the die, constrained by a **blank holder** to suppress wrinkling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Deep drawing is the principal manufacturing route for **cylindrical cups, automotive panels, fuel tanks, kitchen sinks, and battery casings**. KTU board questions frequently test the **drawing ratio, drawing force, blank-holder force, and die clearance** — these are non-negotiable fundamentals.

## 1.2 Stamping Operations — Scope

**Stamping (Press Working)** is a class of cold-working processes performed on a power press using hardened tooling. The principal operations are:
- **Blanking** — shearing a part outline from a strip (the part is the desired product).
- **Piercing** — punching a hole inside a blank (the slug is scrap).
- **Bending** — plastic deformation about a straight axis.
- **Drawing** — producing hollow sections (subset of stamping).
- **Coining, Embossing, Lancing, Shaving** — secondary operations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (favourite KTU question!):** In **Blanking** the **part is the desired output** and the surrounding strip is scrap. In **Piercing** the **hole is the desired output** and the slug is scrap. The metal-flow mechanics and clearance direction reverse between the two.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "Pushing Dough into a Muffin Tray"

Imagine pressing a flat circular piece of dough into a muffin cavity using your thumb:
- The **dough = sheet metal blank**
- Your **thumb = punch**
- The **muffin cavity = die**
- The **rim of the tray that holds dough in place = blank holder**
- If the rim pressure is too low → dough wrinkles (analogue: wrinkling defect).
- If the rim pressure is too high → dough tears (analogue: tearing/creasing defect).
- If the tray (die) is too tight → dough gets squeezed thinner (analogue: **ironing** — intentional thinning of the wall).

This single mental picture captures **the entire deep-drawing physics** KTU expects you to master.

## 1.4 Standard Terminology (used verbatim in KTU answer scripts)

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Range (steel) |
|---|---|---|
| **D** | Initial blank diameter | — |
| **d** | Final cup diameter (punch diameter) | — |
| **t** | Initial sheet thickness | 0.5 – 3.0 mm |
| **h** | Cup depth | — |
| **DR** | Drawing ratio D/d | 1.5 – 2.2 |
| **LDR** | Limiting drawing ratio | 2.0 – 2.4 (steel) |
| **r_p** | Punch nose radius | (5–10)t |
| **r_d** | Die entry radius | (5–10)t |
| **c** | Die–punch clearance (per side) | (1.0–1.07)t |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Constants to Memorise:**
> - **UTS of mild steel (CRCA)** ≈ **350 – 410 MPa**
> - **Yield strength of CRCA** ≈ **210 – 250 MPa**
> - **Strain-hardening exponent (n) for steel** ≈ 0.18 – 0.22
> - **LDR for first draw, steel** ≈ **2.0 – 2.2**
> - **LDR for aluminum (annealed)** ≈ **1.8 – 2.0**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanics of the Deep Drawing Process

The process unfolds in **four sequential physics stages**, each of which KTU examiners expect you to articulate in a 7-mark answer:

1. **Stage 1 — Initial Contact:** The punch tip touches the centre of the blank. A small region of the blank bends over the punch nose radius **r_p**. The bending moment here is small but critical for stress concentration.
2. **Stage 2 — Wrinkling Tendency:** As the punch descends, the outer annular region (between blank-holder and die entry) is in **compressive hoop (circumferential) stress**. If this compressive stress exceeds the critical buckling load, the flange **wrinkles** radially. The **blank-holder pressure p_bh** must be tuned to be just sufficient to suppress wrinkling without crushing the sheet.
3. **Stage 3 — Drawing (Steady State):** The metal in the flange flows radially inward over the die shoulder. The flange region experiences **biaxial tension-compression**: radial tensile stress (drawing the metal) + circumferential compressive stress (which causes thickening). The wall, having passed over **r_d**, is now in **pure axial tension** carried by the punch.
4. **Stage 4 — Bottom Sizing / Ironing (Optional):** If the die–punch clearance **c < t**, the wall is squeezed between the die wall and the punch, deliberately thinning it. This is called **ironing** and is used to make food-can bodies and battery cans.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Logic Hook:** The reason the wall **does not** thicken (despite the flange thickening) is that the wall is constrained by the clearance gap. The wall undergoes **axial tensile drawing stress** only — no circumferential constraint.

## 2.2 Stress State in Each Zone

| Zone | Stress State | Failure Mode if Mismanaged |
|---|---|---|
| **Flange (annular ring)** | Radial tension + circumferential compression | **Wrinkling** (if p_bh too low) or **Tearing at edge** (if p_bh too high) |
| **Die shoulder (r_d)** | Bending over radius + tension | **Folding / Burnishing** |
| **Wall** | Pure axial tension | **Wall tearing (splitting)** |
| **Punch nose (r_p)** | Reverse bending + tension | **Bottom corner cracking** |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Board-Exam Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM TECHNIQUE:** For every numerical problem, state the formula symbolically first, then substitute values with units, then present the final answer with the **correct SI unit and 2–3 significant figures**. KTU examiners deduct marks for missing units.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables & Units | Notes / KTU Tip |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Drawing Ratio** | $\text{DR} = \dfrac{D}{d}$ | D, d in mm | Unitless; must be $\le$ LDR |
| 2 | **Percentage Reduction** | $\%r = \dfrac{D-d}{D} \times 100$ | D, d in mm | First-draw limit $\approx$ 40–45% for steel |
| 3 | **Limiting Drawing Ratio (1st draw, steel)** | $\text{LDR} \approx 2.0$ to $2.2$ | — | Use 2.0 if not given |
| 4 | **Number of draws required** | $n = \dfrac{\log(\text{DR}_{\text{overall}})}{\log(\text{DR}_{\text{per draw}})}$ | — | Round UP to next integer |
| 5 | **Approximate Drawing Force (Sachs / KTU standard)** | $F_d = \pi \, d_p \, t \, \sigma_d$ | F_d in N, d in mm, t in mm, σ_d in MPa | $\sigma_d \approx (0.7 \text{ to } 1.0)\,\sigma_{uts}$ |
| 6 | **Blank-Holder Force** | $F_{bh} = 0.015 \, \dfrac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4} \, \sigma_y$ | All in N, mm, MPa | Empirical; coefficient 0.010–0.020 |
| 7 | **Die Clearance (per side, no ironing)** | $c = t$ | mm | Loose — no wall thinning |
| 8 | **Die Clearance (per side, with ironing)** | $c = (1.05 \text{ to } 1.10)\, t$ | mm | Slight wall thinning |
| 9 | **Die Clearance (blanking/piercing, per side)** | $c = k \sqrt{t}$ | k = 0.03–0.10 | k depends on material |
| 10 | **Punch nose radius (1st draw)** | $r_p = (5 \text{ to } 10)\,t$ | mm | Higher value reduces tearing |
| 11 | **Die entry radius (1st draw)** | $r_d = (5 \text{ to } 10)\,t$ | mm | Higher value reduces folding |
| 12 | **Ironing Force** | $F_i = \pi \, d \, t_i \, \sigma_i$ | N | $\sigma_i \approx$ yield after ironing |
| 13 | **Strip width (stamping)** | $B = D + 2W$ | mm | W = 1.5t to 2.5t edge allowance |
| 14 | **Pitch (distance between successive parts)** | $p = D + s$ | mm | s = 1.0t to 1.5t web between |
| 15 | **Material Utilization** | $\eta = \dfrac{\pi D^2 / 4}{B \times p}$ | — | Expressed as % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Table-Break Safeguard:** The vertical pipe symbol `\vert` is used wherever a magnitude or modulus is meant (e.g., $\vert \sigma \vert$ or "given that..."). NEVER type the bare `\|` character inside a table cell.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive:** Door panels, fenders, fuel tanks — drawn in 4–6 stages on transfer or tandem press lines with LDR strategy.
- **Packaging:** Two-piece aluminum cans — drawn + ironed (DWI) at 200 cans/minute; the wall is ironed from 0.30 mm to 0.10 mm.
- **Cookware:** Stainless-steel pressure cookers — multi-draw with intermediate annealing.
- **Electronics:** Battery cans, capacitor housings — single-draw, high-precision.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Heuristic:** A "design of deep drawing die" question typically accounts for **14 marks** split into (a) procedure/description = 7 marks and (b) numerical problem = 7 marks. The numerical part demands the **drawing force, blank-holder force, and clearance** — you must show all three with units.

## 3.1 Derivation: Drawing Force in the Flange (E Siebel / K Sachs Approach)

The KTU board expects the following physical derivation of $F_d = \pi \, d_p \, t \, \sigma_d$. We work from the **force equilibrium of the flange** under the assumption of plane stress and isotropic hardening.

**Step 1 — Free Body of the Cup Wall (axial equilibrium)**

Consider the wall of the cup, cross-section area $A_w = \pi \, d_p \, t$. The axial drawing stress acting on this section is $\sigma_d$. The punch must supply an equal and opposite force to draw the metal:

$$
F_d = \sigma_d \cdot A_w
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the wall cross-sectional area**

$$
F_d = \sigma_d \cdot (\pi \, d_p \, t)
$$

**Step 3 — Express $\sigma_d$ in terms of material strength**

Empirically, $\sigma_d$ is the **average drawing stress** sustained by the wall, lying between the yield strength $\sigma_y$ and ultimate tensile strength $\sigma_{uts}$:

$$
\sigma_d = (0.7 \text{ to } 1.0)\, \sigma_{uts}
$$

Therefore:

$$
F_d = \pi \, d_p \, t \, (0.7 \text{ to } 1.0)\, \sigma_{uts}
$$

**Step 4 — More refined: Sachs equation including friction and bending**

A complete Sachs-form equation is:

$$
F_d = \pi \, d_p \, t \, \sigma_{uts} \left[ 1.1 \, \text{DR}^{0.25} \right]
$$

For the KTU board, **the simpler form $F_d = \pi d_p t \sigma_d$ is sufficient and is what is rewarded**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the factor 0.7–1.0?** Because $\sigma_d$ is not the peak stress but the *average* stress in the wall. For **low drawing ratios** (DR near 1), $\sigma_d$ is close to $\sigma_y \approx 0.6\,\sigma_{uts}$. For **high drawing ratios** (DR near LDR), $\sigma_d$ approaches $\sigma_{uts}$. The 0.7–1.0 envelope is therefore a **safe upper bound** the board expects.

## 3.2 Derivation: Blank-Holder (Blank-Holding) Force
