<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

> **Q: Differentiate between Preventive Maintenance and Predictive Maintenance of forming tools.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Preventive Maintenance (PM) | Predictive Maintenance (PdM) |
|---|---|---|
| **Trigger** | Calendar time or stroke count (predetermined) | Real-time condition measurement |
| **Philosophy** | "Service whether needed or not" | "Service only when needed" |
| **Tools Required** | Stroke counter, schedule chart | Vibration sensors, AE, thermography |
| **Cost** | Moderate, predictable | High upfront (sensors), low long-term |
| **Tool Life Utilization** | Conservative (early replacement) | Optimized (full utilization) |
| **Risk** | Premature tool removal OR failure before schedule | False alarms, sensor failures |

> [!NOTE]
> **(3-mark allocation: 1 mark for definition of PM, 1 mark for definition of PdM, 1 mark for at least 2 distinguishing points.)**

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]**

> **Q: List the Six Big Losses of TPM and explain how they relate to forming tool maintenance.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Six Big Losses of TPM** (originally defined by Seiichi Nakajima, 1988) are:

1. **Equipment Failure (Breakdown Loss):** Unplanned downtime from die fracture, punch breakage
2. **Setup and Adjustment Loss:** Long die-changeover times — reduced by SMED (Single Minute Exchange of Die)
3. **Minor Stoppages Loss:** Brief halts due to chip jamming, lubrication failure — operator-fixable
4. **Reduced Speed Loss:** Press running below rated tonnage/speed due to worn tooling
5. **Defects Loss:** Rejected stampings/forgings due to worn die profile — visible in quality losses
6. **Startup Loss:** Warm-up time, initial scrap after tool change

**Relation to Forming Tools:** The first three losses directly impact **Availability**, the fourth impacts **Performance**, the fifth impacts **Quality**, and the sixth reduces **Performance** during transient conditions.

> [!NOTE]
> **(3-mark allocation: 1.5 marks for listing all six, 1.5 marks for mapping them to tool maintenance.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Following the **KTU 2024 ESE pattern**, Part B questions feature an **internal choice** between two alternatives. Both questions are provided below.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question A]**

> **(a)** Explain the **Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)** philosophy in detail, listing its **8 pillars**. Discuss how each pillar is applied to forming tool maintenance in an automotive stamping plant. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A forging die is made of AISI H13 tool steel (hardness $H = 6000$ MPa). It operates under a load of $F = 30{,}000$ N with a stroke length of $s = 50$ mm. The wear coefficient for the lubricated hot-work contact is $k = 1 \times 10^{-4}$. The maximum allowable wear volume is $V_{max} = 25$ mm³. Calculate:
> (i) The expected tool life in number of strokes. **(3 marks)**
> (ii) If the safety factor is $k_s = 0.8$, the scheduled tool-change interval. **(2 marks)**
> (iii) Comment on two practical strategies to extend the die life. **(2 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) TPM Philosophy and 8 Pillars — Applied to Stamping Plant**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Total Productive Maintenance is a company-wide maintenance philosophy originated by Seiichi Nakajima at Nippondenso (Japan) in 1971. Its goal is **zero breakdowns, zero defects, zero accidents** through the involvement of every employee — from shopfloor operator to top management. In an automotive stamping plant, TPM is implemented through its **8 pillars**:

**The 8 Pillars Applied to Stamping (6 marks):**

1. **Autonomous Maintenance:** Press operators carry out daily cleaning of die surfaces, inspection of lubrication lines, and tightening of die-set bolts at the start of every shift. They use a standardized checklist (e.g., "Are the draw beads free of scoring? Is the lubricant flow steady?").

2. **Planned Maintenance:** The maintenance department schedules die regrinding, polishing, and recoating based on stroke counts and Weibull-predicted tool life. Critical dies (e.g., door-inner dies) are on a strict 200,000-stroke replacement cycle.

3. **Quality Maintenance:** SPC (Statistical Process Control) on stamped parts catches drift in dimensions (e.g., hole diameter) **before** the die reaches end-of-life. Root-cause analysis links quality drift to specific die wear features (e.g., punch tip rounding → hole diameter increase).

4. **Focused Improvement (Kaizen):** Cross-functional teams tackle chronic issues — e.g., reducing die-changeover time from 45 min to 12 min using SMED (Single Minute Exchange of Die) techniques.

5. **Early Management:** New die designs incorporate **modular inserts** for easy replacement, **standardized die-set heights**, and **quick-disconnect coolant/lubricant fittings** — all making future maintenance faster.

6. **Training:** Press operators undergo a 40-hour module on die care, defect recognition, and basic metallography. Maintenance technicians are certified in tool steel heat treatment.

7. **Safety, Health, Environment:** Die-storage racks are designed to prevent falling; coolant mist extraction protects respiratory health; press guarding prevents crush injuries during die changes.

8. **Office TPM:** Engineering, planning, and procurement departments support the shopfloor with timely spare-part supply, die-design reviews, and OEE dashboards.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Expected tool life in number of strokes (3 marks):**

Using the Archard-derived formula:

$$
N_f = \frac{V_{max} \cdot H}{k \cdot F \cdot s}
$$

Substituting (units: $V$ in mm³, $H$ in N/mm², $F$ in N, $s$ in mm):

$$
N_f = \frac{25 \times 6000}{1 \times 10^{-4} \times 30{,}000 \times 50}
$$

**Numerator:**

$$
25 \times 6000 = 150{,}000
$$

**Denominator:**

$$
(1 \times 10^{-4}) \times 30{,}000 = 3
$$

$$
3 \times 50 = 150
$$

**Result:**

$$
N_f = \frac{150{,}000}{150} = 1000 \text{ strokes}
$$

**(ii) Scheduled tool-change interval (2 marks):**

$$
N_{budget} = N_f \times k_s = 1000 \times 0.8 = 800 \text{ strokes}
$$

The die is scheduled for change at **800 strokes**, leaving a 200-stroke margin for safety.

**(iii) Two practical strategies to extend die life (2 marks):**

1. **Surface coating (PVD/CVD):** Apply a **CrN or TiAlN coating** of 3–5 µm thickness. This reduces $k$ (wear coefficient) by **5–10 times**, directly multiplying $N_f$.
2. **Improved lubrication:** Switch to a **graphite-based or MoS₂-based high-temperature lubricant**. This reduces abrasive and adhesive wear, also reducing $k$.
3. *(Alternative)* Use a **harder die material** (e.g., powder-metallurgy H13 with higher HRC, or carbide inserts in critical regions) to increase $H$.
4. *(Alternative)* Reduce the **stroke length or load** through process optimization (e.g., multi-stage forming instead of single deep draw).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often forget to convert units in Archard's equation. Always write $H$ in **N/mm² (MPa)** when using mm for length and mm³ for volume. Mixing MPa with mm gives wrong results. Marks are commonly deducted for **missing units in final answer**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question B]**

> **(a)** What are the **different wear mechanisms** that affect forming tools? Explain each with a suitable example, and discuss the role of **lubrication** as a wear-mitigation strategy. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** For a sheet-metal deep-drawing operation, the following data are recorded for a draw die:
> - Total operating time: 2400 hours
> - Number of die failures in this period: 6
> - Total downtime due to die-related issues: 180 hours
> - Number of parts produced: 1,440,000 (all acceptable)
> - Theoretical cycle time: 5 seconds per part
>
> Calculate:
> (i) **MTBF** and **MTTR** for the die. **(3 marks)**
> (ii) **Availability** of the die. **(1 mark)**
> (iii) **Performance Efficiency** and **Quality Rate**, given that actual cycle time is 5.5 s. **(2 marks)**
> (iv) **OEE** of the die. **(1 mark)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Wear Mechanisms and Lubrication (7 marks):**

The four primary wear mechanisms affecting forming tools are:

**1. Abrasive Wear (2 marks):**
- **Mechanism:** Hard particles (workpiece inclusions, scale, oxides, or foreign contaminants) plough or scratch the tool surface, removing material.
- **Example:** In hot forging, scale (FeO, Fe₂O₃) from the billet acts as a third-body abrasive, scoring the die cavity walls. Die life in forging is often limited by abrasive wear at the die radii.

**2. Adhesive Wear (2 marks):**
- **Mechanism:** Under high local pressure and temperature, microscopic junctions between tool and workpiece atoms weld together. Continued sliding shears these junctions, transferring material from the weaker to the stronger surface (or producing loose wear debris).
- **Example:** In deep drawing of stainless steel without adequate lubrication, the workpiece material "galls" onto the die surface, producing visible scoring and surface defects.

**3. Fatigue Wear (1.5 marks):**
- **Mechanism:** Repeated cyclic loading produces subsurface crack nucleation, which propagates and eventually causes pitting, spalling, or chipping.
- **Example:** Punching/blanking punches fail by corner chipping after many cycles due to fatigue at the sharp corner — the highest stress concentration site.

**4. Thermal/Corrosive Wear (1.5 marks):**
- **Mechanism:** Elevated temperatures cause surface oxidation, softening (tempering), or thermal-fatigue cracking ("heat checking"). In some environments, chemical reactions between tool and workpiece (or lubricant) accelerate surface degradation.
- **Example:** Hot forging dies develop a network of fine surface cracks ("heat checking") due to repeated thermal cycling from ~200 °C (after forging) to ~600 °C (during forging).

**Role of Lubrication as Mitigation (1 mark — included in total):**
Lubrication reduces wear by:
- **Separating surfaces** (boundary lubrication regime) — preventing direct metal-to-metal contact
- **Carrying away heat** — reducing thermal wear
- **Flushing away debris** — preventing three-body abrasion
- **Forming protective films** (e.g., EP additives forming FeS/FeCl films on steel)

Common lubricants for forming: mineral oils, EP (extreme-pressure) oils, graphite suspensions, MoS₂, soap-based dry films, polymer coatings (e.g., boron nitride).

**(b) OEE Calculation (7 marks):**

**Given data summary:**

$$
T_{operating} = 2400 \text{ h}, \quad N_f = 6, \quad T_{downtime} = 180 \text{ h}, \quad N_{parts} = 1{,}440{,}000
$$

$$
t_{cycle,theoretical} = 5 \text{ s}, \quad t_{cycle,actual} = 5.5 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 1 — Compute Uptime (Available Production Time):**

$$
T_{uptime} = T_{operating} - T_{downtime} = 2400 - 180 = 2220 \text{ h}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Planned Production Time:**
Same as uptime (no planned downtime excluded here):

$$
T_{planned} = 2220 \text{ h}
$$

**(i) MTBF and MTTR (3 marks):**

$$
\text{MTBF} = \frac{T_{uptime}}{N_f} = \frac{2220}{6} = 370 \text{ h}
$$

$$
\text{MTTR} = \frac{T_{downtime}}{N_f} = \frac{180}{6} = 30 \text{ h}
$$

**(ii) Availability (1 mark):**

$$
A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{370}{370 + 30} = \frac{370}{400} = 0.925 = 92.5\%
$$

**(iii) Performance Efficiency and Quality Rate (2 marks):**

**Performance Efficiency:**

$$
P = \frac{t_{cycle,theoretical}}{t_{cycle,actual}} = \frac{5}{5.5} = 0.9091 = 90.91\%
$$

**Quality Rate:** Given all parts are acceptable:

$$
Q = \frac{N_{good}}{N_{total}} = \frac{1{,}440{,}000}{1{,}440{,}000} = 1.00 = 100\%
$$

**(iv) OEE (1 mark):**

$$
\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q = 0.925 \times 0.9091 \times 1.00
$$

$$
\text{OEE} = 0.8409 \approx 84.1\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — OEE Problems:**
> 1. Many students confuse **MTBF** (uses uptime) with **availability** (ratio). MTBF is in **hours**; availability is a **fraction or percentage**.
> 2. For **Performance**, always divide the theoretical (ideal) cycle time by the actual cycle time — **not** the other way around.
> 3. World-class OEE is **≥ 85 %**. If your answer is far from this range, you may have used the wrong formula.
> 4. OEE is **dimensionless** — present it as a percentage.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Maintenance Strategies for Forming Tools**

### A. Core Definitions
- **Corrective Maintenance (CM):** Fix after failure.
- **Preventive Maintenance (PM):** Service at fixed intervals (time or strokes).
- **Predictive Maintenance (PdM):** Service based on real-time condition monitoring.
- **Total Productive Maintenance (TPM):** Holistic philosophy with 8 pillars, 6 big losses, and OEE as master metric.

### B. Key Formulas (Memorize!)
- Archard's Equation: $V = \dfrac{kFL}{H}$
- Tool Life from Wear: $N_f = \dfrac{V_{max} \cdot H}{k \cdot F \cdot s}$
- Tool Life Budget (PM): $N_{budget} = N_{avg} \cdot k_s$ (with $k_s \approx 0.7$–$0.85$)
- MTBF: $\dfrac{\text{Uptime}}{\text{No. of Failures}}$
- MTTR: $\dfrac{\text{Downtime}}{\text{No. of Repairs}}$
- Availability: $\dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$
- OEE: $A \times P \times Q$

### C. Wear Mechanisms (4 types)
- Abrasive → mitigated by filtration, lubrication, hard coatings
- Adhesive → mitigated by lubrication, surface treatments, low-friction coatings
- Fatigue → mitigated by stress reduction, surface peening
- Thermal/Corrosive → mitigated by cooling, oxidation-resistant coatings

### D. TPM Essentials (Frequently Asked in KTU)
- **6 Big Losses:** Breakdown, Setup, Minor Stoppages, Reduced Speed, Defects, Startup
- **8 Pillars:** Autonomous, Planned, Quality, Focused Improvement, Early Management, Training, SHE, Office TPM
- **OEE target:** ≥ 85 % (world-class)
- **Origin:** Seiichi Nakajima, Nippondenso, Japan, 1971

### E. KTU-Specific Tips
- Always **carry units** in Archard-based numerical problems.
- Always present OEE as a **percentage** with one decimal place.
- In choice questions, the **7+7 split** is sacred — never imbalance.
- Draw a **decision tree or block diagram** wherever asked to "explain a strategy" — KTU examiners reward visual aids.
- Use **industry examples** (automotive, aerospace) to demonstrate application-level understanding.

### F. Common Pitfalls to Avoid
- ❌ Confusing wear coefficient $k$ (dimensionless) with wear rate (mm³/m).
- ❌ Forgetting that hardness $H$ in Archard's equation must be in the **same unit system** as load $F$ and area.
- ❌ Treating OEE components as additive (they are **multiplicative**).
- ❌ Mixing TPM with TQM — TPM is **equipment-focused**; TQM is **product-quality-focused** (related but distinct).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Additive Manufacturing for Tool Design and Repair
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET524 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Additive Manufacturing for Tool Design and Repair

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Additive Manufacturing (AM)** is defined by ISO/ASTM 52900 as the *process of joining materials to make objects from 3D model data, usually layer upon layer, as opposed to subtractive manufacturing methodologies*.

In the context of **Metal Forming and Tool Design (PEIET524)**, AM refers to the layer-by-layer deposition of metallic powders, wires, or filaments to fabricate, refurbish, or re-engineer tooling components such as **injection mould inserts, die-casting dies, forging dies, stamping punches, and extrusion tooling**. The most relevant processes for tooling are the **ISO/ASTM Category 5 (Powder Bed Fusion – PBF)** and **Category 6 (Directed Energy Deposition – DED)** families.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** AM in tool design is NOT just prototyping; it enables **conformal cooling channels**, **rapid tooling**, and **near-net-shape repair** of high-value tooling that would otherwise be scrapped.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Icing the Cake" vs. "Carving the Statue" Mental Model

| Method | Traditional Tooling (Subtractive) | Additive Tooling |
|---|---|---|
| Analogy | Carving a statue from a marble block (cut away what you don't need) | Icing a multi-layer cake (add material only where needed) |
| Material Wastage | High (chips, swarf) | Near zero (powder reuse up to **95 %**) |
| Geometric Freedom | Limited by tool access (drill, mill) | Virtually unlimited (internal lattice, conformal curves) |
| Lead Time | 4 – 12 weeks for hard tooling | 24 – 96 hours for a printed insert |

**Intuition:** Imagine an injection mould with a hollow cooling channel. In a conventional drilled mould, the channel is a straight cylinder (limited by the drill bit). With AM, the channel can curve through the mould following the **exact contour of the cavity surface**, staying a uniform **2 – 3 mm** away from the cavity wall — like a 3D maze custom-fitted to the shape of the part.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Tool-AM

- **Build Volume (mm³):** Maximum envelope the AM machine can produce (typical: 250 × 250 × 300 mm).
- **Layer Thickness (µm):** Z-axis step (**20 µm – 100 µm** for SLM, **50 µm – 200 µm** for DED).
- **Deposition Rate (cm³/h):** Volume of material fused per hour (**5 – 20 cm³/h** for PBF, **50 – 300 cm³/h** for DED).
- **Surface Roughness (Ra, µm):** As-built (5 – 20 µm) vs. post-machined (< 0.8 µm).
- **Density (%):** Relative density of deposited metal (**≥ 99.5 %** for qualified tool builds).
- **Hardness (HRC):** As-deposited vs. heat-treated (e.g., **H13 tool steel: 48 – 54 HRC** after tempering).

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters in KTU valuation:** Examiners award marks for correctly using ISO/ASTM category names, not commercial brand names. Always say **"PBF-LB (Laser-Based Powder Bed Fusion)"** instead of just "SLM" or "DMLS".

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Seven ISO/ASTM AM Categories — Filtered for Tool Design

The full ISO/ASTM 52900 taxonomy has **seven categories**. Only four are routinely used in tool design and repair:

1. **Material Extrusion (MEX)** — FDM with metal-filled filaments (Bound Metal Deposition). Used for low-cost insert prototypes.
2. **Powder Bed Fusion – Laser Metal (PBF-LB)** — SLM / DMLS. The **workhorse** for conformal-cooled mould inserts.
3. **Powder Bed Fusion – Electron Beam (PBF-EB)** — EBM. Used for large H13 die bodies; operates in vacuum.
4. **Directed Energy Deposition (DED)** — Laser Metal Deposition (LMD), Wire-Arc AM (WAAM). The **primary process for tool repair and feature addition**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Process Selection Logic for Tools:**
> - **New insert with conformal cooling** → PBF-LB (SLM).
> - **Repair of cracked die / worn punch tip** → DED-LB or DED-PLASMA.
> - **Large die body (>500 mm)** → PBF-EB or DED-WAAM.
> - **Low-cost prototype mould** → Material Extrusion (BMD).

### 2.2 Why AM is a Paradigm Shift in Tool Design — The "Why" and "How"

**WHY** traditional cooling fails:
- Straight drilled channels leave **hot spots** in deep, narrow, or cored regions.
- Cooling time can consume **40 – 60 %** of the injection-moulding cycle.
- Moulded parts warp because of non-uniform shrinkage.

**HOW** AM solves it:
- Channels follow the **cavity surface contour** at a constant offset (typically **1.5 – 3 mm**).
- Cross-section can be **non-circular** (teardrop, oval) for better heat transfer and reduced pressure drop.
- Cooling time reduction of **20 – 50 %** has been reported in published industrial case studies.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Relationship | Description | Typical Units | Tooling Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $t_{build} = \dfrac{V_{part}}{R_{dep} \cdot t_{layer}}$ | Build time estimate | hours (h) | Estimate lead-time for new mould insert |
| 2 | $V_{part} = A_{layer} \cdot N_{layers}$ | Layer-by-layer volume build-up | mm³ | Used in build time calculation |
| 3 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \cdot v \cdot D_h}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number for channel flow | dimensionless | Predicts flow regime in conformal channels |
| 4 | $Nu = 0.023 \cdot Re^{0.8} \cdot Pr^{0.4}$ | Dittus-Boelter correlation (turbulent heating) | dimensionless | Convective heat transfer coefficient |
| 5 | $h = \dfrac{Nu \cdot k_{fluid}}{D_h}$ | Convective heat transfer coefficient | W/(m²·K) | Cooling efficiency of channels |
| 6 | $Q = h \cdot A \cdot (T_{wall} - T_{fluid})$ | Newton's law of cooling | W (Watts) | Heat removed per unit time from cavity |
| 7 | $\Delta P = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D_h} \cdot \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2}$ | Pressure drop in channel | Pa | Must not exceed pump capacity |
| 8 | $R_{a,post} = R_{a,as-built} \cdot k_{process}$ | Surface roughness reduction factor | µm | Post-machining requirement |
| 9 | $C_{part} = C_{machine} \cdot t_{build} + C_{material} + C_{post}$ | Per-part cost model | ₹ / $ | AM vs. conventional cost comparison |
| 10 | $K_{IC,AM} \ge K_{IC,wrought} \cdot 0.85$ | AM tool fracture toughness benchmark | MPa·√m | Qualification criterion for printed H13 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbols:** $\rho$ = fluid density, $v$ = mean velocity, $D_h$ = hydraulic diameter, $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity, $k_{fluid}$ = thermal conductivity of coolant, $f$ = Darcy friction factor, $L$ = channel length, $A$ = heat-transfer area.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of AM in Tooling (Industrial Significance)

- **Automotive:** Conformal-cooled injection moulds for headlamp lenses (cycle time reduced from 75 s to 42 s in published case).
- **Aerospace:** Repair of single-crystal Ni-based superalloy turbine blade tips (DED process).
- **Die Casting:** Repair of thermal-fatigue cracks in H13 die-casting dies (multi-million rupee asset restoration).
- **Sheet Metal Forming:** AM-built progressive die inserts with embedded wear sensors (Industry 4.0 integration).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When the question asks for "two advantages of AM in tool design", always pair one **design advantage** (geometric freedom) with one **operational advantage** (lead time / cooling efficiency). This shows the examiner you understand both engineering and economic dimensions.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Build Time Estimation for an AM Tool Insert

**Problem Statement (KTU 2018-pattern adapted):** Estimate the build time for a PBF-LB (SLM) mould insert of volume $V_{part} = 12{,}500 \text{ mm}^3$, layer thickness $t_{layer} = 30 \text{ µm} = 0.030 \text{ mm}$, and effective deposition rate $R_{dep} = 8 \text{ cm}^3/\text{h} = 8000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{h}$. Neglect recoating and scan overhead.

**Step 1 — Convert all units to a common base (mm and hours).**
We have:
$$R_{dep} = 8 \text{ cm}^3/\text{h} = 8 \times 10^3 \text{ mm}^3/\text{h}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the build-time relationship.**
Theoretical build time (excluding overhead) is governed by:
$$t_{build} = \frac{V_{part}}{R_{dep} \cdot t_{layer}} \cdot t_{layer}$$
Wait — this is the literal layer count, so we need to use:
$$t_{build} = \frac{V_{part}}{R_{dep}}$$
where $R_{dep}$ is already the *effective* volume deposition rate reported by the machine vendor (in mm³/h). We use this directly.

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values.**
$$t_{build} = \frac{12{,}500 \text{ mm}^3}{8000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{h}}$$

**Step 4 — Perform the division.**
$$t_{build} = 1.5625 \text{ h}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to hours and minutes.**
$$t_{build} = 1 \text{ h} + 0.5625 \times 60 \text{ min} = 1 \text{ h } 33.75 \text{ min} \approx 1 \text{ h } 34 \text{ min}$$

**Step 6 — Apply a practical overhead factor.**
In industry, recoating, scan jumps, and gas-flow pauses add **20 – 30 %**. Applying 25 % overhead:
$$t_{build,real} = 1.5625 \times 1.25 = 1.953 \text{ h} \approx 1 \text{ h } 57 \text{ min}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation:** A ~12,500 mm³ insert (roughly 25 × 25 × 20 mm) can be built in under 2 hours — versus 6 – 10 hours for CNC rough machining from a hardened block. This is why AM is disruptive for short-run tooling.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cooling Channel Heat Transfer Comparison (Straight vs Conformal)

**Problem Statement:** A plastic injection mould cavity is 200 mm × 100 mm. Two cooling strategies are compared:
- **Strategy A (conventional):** 4 straight channels, diameter $D = 8$ mm, located 12 mm below cavity surface.
- **Strategy B (AM conformal):** Channels follow the cavity contour at offset $\delta = 2.5$ mm, with **triangular** cross-section of hydraulic diameter $D_h = 6$ mm.

Coolant: water at $v = 1.5$ m/s, $T_{fluid} = 25 °C$, mould wall maintained at $T_{wall} = 60 °C$. Compare the **local heat flux**.

**Step 1 — Compute Reynolds number for both strategies.**
For Strategy A (circular, $D = 0.008$ m):
$$Re_A = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{1000 \times 1.5 \times 0.008}{0.001} = 12{,}000$$
(turbulent, since $Re > 4000$).

For Strategy B (non-circular, $D_h = 0.006$ m):
$$Re_B = \frac{1000 \times 1.5 \times 0.006}{0.001} = 9{,}000$$
(still turbulent, $Re > 4000$).

**Step 2 — Apply Dittus-Boelter for heating (heating of fluid, $n = 0.4$):**
$Pr = \dfrac{\mu C_p}{k} = \dfrac{0.001 \times 4180}{0.6} \approx 6.97$ for water.

For Strategy A:
$$Nu_A = 0.023 \times (12{,}000)^{0.8} \times (6.97)^{0.4}$$

Compute $(12{,}000)^{0.8}$:
$$(12{,}000)^{0.8} = e^{0.8 \cdot \ln(12{,}000)} = e^{0.8 \times 9.393} = e^{7.514} \approx 1826$$

Compute $(6.97)^{0.4}$:
$$(6.97)^{0.4} = e^{0.4 \cdot \ln(6.97)} = e^{0.4 \times 1.941} = e^{0.776} \approx 2.173$$

Therefore:
$$Nu_A = 0.023 \times 1826 \times 2.173 \approx 91.3$$

For Strategy B:
$$Nu_B = 0.023 \times (9{,}000)^{0.8} \times (6.97)^{0.4}$$

Compute $(9{,}000)^{0.8}$:
$$(9{,}000)^{0.8} = e^{0.8 \cdot \ln(9000)} = e^{0.8 \times 9.105} = e^{7.284} \approx 1452$$

Therefore:
$$Nu_B = 0.023 \times 1452 \times 2.173 \approx 72.6$$

**Step 3 — Compute heat transfer coefficients.**
$$h_A = \frac{Nu_A \cdot k}{D} = \frac{91.3 \times 0.6}{0.008} = 6847.5 \text{ W/(m}^2\text{·K)}$$

$$h_B = \frac{Nu_B \cdot k}{D_h} = \frac{72.6 \times 0.6}{0.006} = 7260 \text{ W/(m}^2\text{·K)}$$

**Step 4 — Account for the geometric offset advantage.**
In Strategy A, heat must conduct through **12 mm** of tool steel before reaching coolant. In Strategy B, only **2.5 mm**.

Conduction resistance per unit area:
$$R_{cond,A} = \frac{\delta_A}{k_{steel}} = \frac{0.012}{29} = 4.138 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}$$

$$R_{cond,B} = \frac{\delta_B}{k_{steel}} = \frac{0.0025}{29} = 8.621 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the total thermal resistance and heat flux.**
Total resistance:
$$R_{tot,A} = R_{cond,A} + \frac{1}{h_A} = 4.138 \times 10^{-4} + 1.46 \times 10^{-4} = 5.598 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}$$

$$R_{tot,B} = R_{cond,B} + \frac{1}{h_B} = 8.621 \times 10^{-5} + 1.378 \times 10^{-4} = 2.240 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}$$

Heat flux at the cavity surface:
$$q_A = \frac{T_{wall} - T_{fluid}}{R_{tot,A}} = \frac{(60 - 25)}{5.598 \times 10^{-4}} = 62{,}522 \text{ W/m}^2$$

$$q_B = \frac{(60 - 25)}{2.240 \times 10^{-4}} = 156{,}250 \text{ W/m}^2$$

**Step 6 — Compute the improvement ratio.**
$$\frac{q_B}{q_A} = \frac{156{,}250}{62{,}522} \approx 2.5$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The AM conformal channel delivers **~2.5× the heat flux** of a conventional drilled channel at the cavity surface. This is the quantitative basis for the **20 – 50 % cycle-time reduction** claimed in industry. In KTU valuation, this full derivation carries **7 marks** in a 14-mark question — the *boundary condition statements* and *unit conversions* each carry 1 mark.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — AM Process Selection for Tooling Applications

| Tooling Application | Recommended AM Process | Material | Surface Finish | Tolerance | Why This Process? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Injection mould insert with conformal cooling | PBF-LB (SLM) | H13, P20, MS1 (Maraging) | As-built 5 – 10 µm Ra; post-machined 0.4 µm | ±0.05 mm | Fine feature resolution, complex internal channels |
| Die-casting die repair (cracks, wear) | DED-LB (LMD) | H13 powder + carbide | As-deposited 15 – 25 µm Ra; remachined | ±0.2 mm | High deposition rate on existing substrate, metallurgical bonding |
| Large forging die block (>500 mm) | DED-WAAM or PBF-EB | H13 wire or powder | 50 – 100 µm Ra; rough machined | ±1.0 mm | Large build volume, lower cost per kg |
| Stamping punch insert (high wear) | PBF-LB + carburizing | AISI A2 or D2 | As-built then case-hardened | ±0.03 mm | Hardenable, complex geometry |
| Quick prototype mould (≤100 shots) | Material Extrusion (BMD) | 17-4 PH or 316L bronze-FDM | As-printed 10 – 15 µm Ra | ±0.1 mm | Low cost, no powder handling, office-safe |
| Sheet-metal progressive die with embedded sensor | PBF-LB | Maraging 300 (1.2709) | Hybrid: printed + CNC finish | ±0.02 mm | Allows sensor channels and conformal features in one build |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Expected Answer Pattern:** If the question is *"Compare AM and conventional tooling for injection moulds"*, a 7-mark answer should contain:
> 1. Two design freedom points (geometric, internal features).
> 2. Two cooling-efficiency points (conformal channels, cycle time).
> 3. Two economic points (lead time, cost per mould).
> 4. **One counterpoint** (residual stress, post-machining need, capital cost) — examiners specifically look for balance.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper Adapted]**
*Define Additive Manufacturing as per ISO/ASTM 52900. List the four categories of AM processes most relevant to metal tooling.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Additive Manufacturing is the process of joining materials to make objects from 3D model data, usually layer upon layer, as opposed to subtractive manufacturing methodologies (1 mark). The four relevant categories are: (i) Material Extrusion (1 mark), (ii) Powder Bed Fusion – Laser Based (1 mark), and (iii) Powder Bed Fusion – Electron Beam, and (iv) Directed Energy Deposition (combined 1 mark).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]**
*What is a conformal cooling channel? How does it differ from a conventional drilled cooling channel in a plastic injection mould?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A conformal cooling channel is a coolant passage manufactured within a mould insert that follows the contour of the cavity surface at a near-constant offset distance, typically 1.5 – 3 mm (1 mark). Unlike a conventional straight drilled channel (1 mark), the conformal channel maintains uniform proximity to the cavity, eliminating hot spots and improving heat transfer efficiency by 1.5 – 2.5 times (1 mark). Conformal channels can only be produced using AM processes, primarily PBF-LB, since conventional drilling cannot create curved internal passages.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and offers internal choice between two sub-questions of 7 marks each. The model solutions below show the **complete step-by-step** expected in a top-scoring answer.

---

**Question A. (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]

**(a)** Explain with a suitable sketch how Additive Manufacturing is used to create **conformal cooling channels** in injection mould inserts. Discuss two design and two operational benefits. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Concept (2 marks):*
Conformal cooling channels are designed by taking the **offset surface** of the mould cavity at a distance of 1.5 – 3 mm and sweeping a circular or teardrop cross-section along a path that runs parallel to this offset surface. The path is generated using B-spline or NURBS curves, and the channel volume is then **Boolean-subtracted** from the mould body. The mould body is built in a **PBF-LB (SLM)** machine, layer by layer, using H13 or maraging steel powder. The internal curved channel — which would be impossible to drill — is formed naturally as the unfused powder is removed post-build.

*Design Benefits (2 marks):*
1. **Geometric Freedom:** Complex internal features (lattice, curved channels) inaccessible to conventional drilling are achievable.
2. **Customized Layout:** Each cavity of a multi-cavity mould can have a *dedicated* cooling circuit tuned to its thermal load.

*Operational Benefits (2 marks):*
1. **Cycle Time Reduction:** 20 – 50 % reduction in cooling time, raising production throughput.
2. **Part Quality:** Uniform cooling reduces warpage, sink marks, and residual stresses in moulded parts.

*Conclusion (1 mark):*
Conformal cooling represents the **single most commercially important** AM application in tooling, with demonstrated cycle-time and quality improvements in automotive and consumer-electronics moulding.

---

**(b)** A PBF-LB machine is to be used to print an H13 tool-steel insert of volume $V = 18{,}000$ mm³ at a layer thickness of 40 µm. The machine has a rated deposition rate of $R_{dep} = 10$ cm³/h and an overhead factor of 1.30 (for recoating, scan jumps, gas flow). Estimate the **total build time** and the **as-built surface roughness**, given that the as-built Ra for H13 on PBF-LB is approximately 8 µm at this layer thickness. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Step 1 — Unit conversion (1 mark):*
$$R_{dep} = 10 \text{ cm}^3/\text{h} = 10{,}000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{h}$$

*Step 2 — Theoretical build time without overhead (1 mark):*
$$t_{build,theory} = \frac{V}{R_{dep}} = \frac{18{,}000 \text{ mm}^3}{10{,}000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{h}} = 1.8 \text{ h}$$

*Step 3 — Apply the overhead factor (1 mark):*
$$t_{build,real} = 1.8 \times 1.30 = 2.34 \text{ h}$$

*Step 4 — Convert to hours and minutes (1 mark):*
$$t_{build,real} = 2 \text{ h} + 0.34 \times 60 \text{ min} = 2 \text{ h } 20.4 \text{ min} \approx 2 \text{ h } 20 \text{ min}$$

*Step 5 — Surface roughness statement (1 mark):*
For 40 µm layer thickness on H13 in PBF-LB, the as-built surface roughness is approximately:
$$R_{a,as-built} \approx 8 \text{ µm}$$

*Step 6 — Post-processing recommendation (1 mark):*
Since mould-cavity surfaces typically require $R_a < 0.8$ µm, a finish-machining pass (micro-milling or EDM) is mandatory on functional surfaces. The as-built 8 µm is acceptable for non-functional surfaces such as cooling-channel walls and exterior mounting faces.

*Step 7 — Validation comment (1 mark):*
The build time of 2 h 20 min for an 18 cm³ insert is realistic for industrial PBF-LB machines (e.g., EOS M290, SLM 280) operating at 200 W laser power with bidirectional scanning.

---

**Question B. (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]

**(a)** Describe the **Directed Energy Deposition (DED)** process for **tool repair**. Mention the typical defects encountered and the post-processing steps required before the repaired tool is returned to service. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*DED Process for Tool Repair (3 marks):*
In DED-based tool repair, a worn or cracked region of a die (e.g., H13 die-casting die) is first cleaned and pre-machined to expose fresh base material. A laser or plasma arc is then focused on the substrate while metal powder (matching the substrate chemistry) is injected through a coaxial nozzle. The heat source creates a small molten pool, and the powder is fed into this pool, which solidifies to form a metallurgically bonded clad layer. The deposition head is moved along the toolpath (typically 5-axis CNC or robotic) to rebuild the missing geometry layer by layer. Deposition rates of 50 – 300 cm³/h are typical, much higher than PBF-LB.

*Common Defects in DED Repairs (2 marks):*
1. **Lack of Fusion:** Insufficient energy density causes poor bonding between layers; mitigated by optimizing laser power and scan speed.
2. **Residual Stress and Cracking:** High thermal gradients cause distortion or HAZ cracking; mitigated by substrate pre-heating to 300 – 500 °C and post-deposition stress-relief.
3. **Porosity:** Gas entrapment from powder flow or contamination; mitigated by powder sieving and dry storage.

*Post-Processing Steps (2 marks):*
1. Stress-relief heat treatment (e.g., 600 °C for H13, hold 2 h, slow cool).
2. Hardening and tempering (e.g., austenitize 1020 °C, quench, double-temper to 48 – 52 HRC).
3. Finish machining to restore dimensional accuracy on functional surfaces.
4. Surface treatment (nitriding, polishing, or PVD coating) to restore wear resistance.

---

**(b)** Compare **PBF-LB (SLM)** and **DED** for the manufacture and repair of metal tooling, using the following criteria: (i) typical deposition rate, (ii) feature resolution, (iii) suitable build volume, and (iv) post-machining requirement. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Comparison Table (5 marks, 1.25 per row):*

| Criterion | PBF-LB (SLM) | DED |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Deposition Rate | **5 – 20 cm³/h** (low) | **50 – 300 cm³/h** (high) |
| (ii) Feature Resolution | **±0.05 mm**, thin walls ~0.1 mm | **±0.2 mm**, minimum wall ~0.8 mm |
| (iii) Build Volume | Small to medium (≤ 400 × 400 × 400 mm typical) | Large to very large (robotic systems can exceed 1 m³) |
| (iv) Post-Machining | Light finish on functional surfaces | Heavy finish — deposited surfaces are rough (15 – 25 µm Ra) and dimensionally inaccurate |

*Justification (2 marks):*
- PBF-LB is preferred for **new inserts with complex internal features** (conformal cooling, embedded sensors) because of its high resolution and powder-bed accuracy.
- DED is preferred for **repair and large-die fabrication** because of its high deposition rate, ability to clad onto existing substrates, and large build envelope.
- A modern "**hybrid**" approach uses both processes on the same part — DED for the bulk geometry, followed by PBF-LB for fine features, and CNC for the final finish.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students typically lose marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Using the wrong AM category name.** Writing "3D printing" or "rapid prototyping" instead of "Powder Bed Fusion – Laser Based (PBF-LB)" costs **1 mark** per occurrence. Always use ISO/ASTM 52900 nomenclature.
>
> 2. **Confusing build time with print time.** The $t_{build} = V / R_{dep}$ formula gives *theoretical* time. Failing to multiply by the overhead factor (1.20 – 1.35) costs **1 mark**.
>
> 3. **Skipping the conduction resistance** in heat-transfer problems. Many students compute $h$ correctly but forget that the tool steel between cavity and channel is the **dominant resistance** — losing **2 marks** in derivation questions.
>
> 4. **No mention of post-processing.** AM parts are NOT ready-to-use. Always state that heat treatment, stress relief, and finish machining are required. Skipping this loses **1 – 2 marks** in design questions.
>
> 5. **One-sided answers.** The examiner awards full marks only when both **advantages AND limitations** are discussed. A purely promotional AM answer will cap at **5/7** marks.
>
> 6. **Unit errors in Dittus-Boelter calculations.** Mixing cm with m gives wrong Re and Nu. Always convert to SI units *first* and write down the conversion step explicitly.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets — Read before entering the exam hall:**

- **AM Definition (ISO/ASTM 52900):** Layer-by-layer joining of materials from 3D model data. **Memorize this verbatim.**
- **Seven AM Categories:** VAT Photopolymerization, Material Jetting, Binder Jetting, Material Extrusion, Powder Bed Fusion, Directed Energy Deposition, Sheet Lamination.
- **For Tooling, only Four Matter:** Material Extrusion (BMD), PBF-LB (SLM/DMLS), PBF-EB (EBM), DED (LMD/WAAM).
- **Workhorse for New Inserts:** **PBF-LB with H13 / P20 / Maraging 300.**
- **Workhorse for Repair:** **DED-LB (laser) for precision; DED-WAAM for large build-up.**
- **Conformal Cooling:** Offset distance **1.5 – 3 mm**; delivers **~2.5× heat flux** vs. straight drilling; cycle-time reduction **20 – 50 %**.
- **Build Time Formula:** $t_{build} = \dfrac{V_{part}}{R_{dep}} \times (\text{overhead factor 1.2 – 1.35})$.
- **Dittus-Boelter (heating):** $Nu = 0.023 \cdot Re^{0.8} \cdot Pr^{0.4}$.
- **Cooling equation:** $Q = hA(T_{wall} - T_{fluid})$; $h$ depends on $Nu$ and $D_h$.
- **As-built roughness:** PBF-LB = **5 – 10 µm Ra**; DED = **15 – 25 µm Ra** → finish machining mandatory.
- **Required post-processing chain (in order):** Stress relief → Hardening/Tempering → CNC/EDM finishing → Surface treatment (nitride/PVD) → Inspection (CMM).
- **Tool steel of choice:** **H13 (AISI)** for hot-work tooling (dies, casting); **D2 / A2** for cold-work (stamping); **Maraging 300 (1.2709)** for inserts requiring post-build aging hardening.
- **Key advantage pair to always mention:** (1) Geometric freedom + (2) Reduced lead time / improved cooling.
- **Key limitation pair to always mention:** (1) High capital cost + (2) Mandatory post-processing / residual stress.
- **Hybrid manufacturing =** AM bulk + AM fine features + CNC finish → single-part workflow, the *current industrial trend*.
- **Standards to cite:** **ISO/ASTM 52900** (terminology), **ASTM F3301** (PBF process), **ISO/ASTM 52904** (DED process).

> **End of Notes — Module 4: Additive Manufacturing for Tool Design and Repair | PEIET524 | KTU 2024 Scheme**
